kept.title: LEIE EXCLDATE is YYYYMMDD text, not a date -- TRY_CAST(... AS DATE) silently collapses it to 1970
kept.sql: SELECT COUNT(*) n, SUM(CASE WHEN REGEXP_LIKE(EXCLDATE,'.*^[0-9]{8}$.*') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) ymd8, MIN(EXCLDATE) mn, MAX(EXCLDATE) mx FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_HHS_OIG_LEIE;  -- contrast the trap: SELECT MIN(TRY_CAST(EXCLDATE AS DATE)), MAX(TRY_CAST(EXCLDATE AS DATE)) FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_HHS_OIG_LEIE
kept.evidence: All 83,464 EXCLDATE values match ^[0-9]{8}$; true range MIN=19770701 to MAX=20260618. But TRY_CAST(EXCLDATE AS DATE) returns MIN 1970-08-17 / MAX 1970-08-23 -- Snowflake reads the 8-digit string as an epoch-day count, not a date, silently mangling every value. Separately, NPI is blank/zero on 74,780 of 83,464 rows (89.6%): only the 8,684 individual practitioners with NPIs are hard-ID joinable.
kept.why_matters: Two traps in the warehouse's key sanctions table: (1) the obvious date cast is wrong and would corrupt any exclusion-timeline analysis -- must use TO_DATE(EXCLDATE,'YYYYMMDD'); (2) ~90% of exclusion rows have no NPI, so most LEIE matches to Open Payments/NPPES must fall back to fuzzy name+address (LEAD grade), not hard NPI (FACT grade).
kept.implies: staging: parse EXCLDATE with TO_DATE(col,'YYYYMMDD'); tier the 90% NPI-less LEIE rows as name-match LEAD-only in any cross-source detector

verdict.corrected_title: LEIE EXCLDATE is YYYYMMDD text: TRY_CAST(... AS DATE) silently collapses all 83,464 rows into 7 garbage 1970 dates (use TO_DATE(...,'YYYYMMDD')); and NPI is the placeholder '0000000000' on 89.6% of rows, not blank
verdict.notes:
Both core claims reproduce exactly via the helper.

DATE TRAP (confirmed): COUNT=83,464, all 83,464 match [0-9]{8}, MIN=19770701 MAX=20260618. TRY_CAST(EXCLDATE AS DATE) yields MIN 1970-08-17 / MAX 1970-08-23 -- Snowflake parses the 8-digit string as an epoch-day INT, mangling everything. Stronger evidence than the candidate gave: distinct dates collapse from 2,400 (proper, via TO_DATE) down to 7 (cast). TO_DATE(EXCLDATE,'YYYYMMDD') recovers correct values (19770701->1977-07-01, 20260618->2026-06-18). Note: the DATEADD epoch reconstruction errors out ('year 56100 out of range') because the int is enormous; the collapse is from integer overflow/wraparound, but the practical point (cast is garbage, TO_DATE is correct) is airtight.

NPI TRAP (confirmed, with a sharper nuance): the 74,780 / 83,464 = 89.6% blank figure is exact. Gotcha I personally hit: NPI is NOT null/empty/'0' -- it is the literal string '0000000000' (ten zeros). My first attempt checking NPI='0' returned 0 blanks and 'all 83,464 valid', and a naive REGEXP_LIKE(NPI,'[0-9]{10}') ALSO passes all 83,464 -- so '0000000000' is itself a secondary landmine that would falsely promote 90% of rows to FACT-grade NPI joins. Only 8,684 rows carry real, non-placeholder NPIs.

Minor wording flaw in candidate (does not affect verdict): the supplied regex '.*^[0-9]{8}$.*' is malformed (anchors inside .*). A clean REGEXP_LIKE(EXCLDATE,'[0-9]{8}') gives the same 83,464. tag=data-quality, FACT grade upheld -- this is a property of the raw table, not an entity claim, so no libel tiering concern. Highly actionable: corrects two real traps in the warehouse's primary sanctions table.

kept.title: NOAA AIS '7.3M rows' is a single calendar day -- a vessel-tracking 'panel' that is secretly 2024-01-01 only
kept.sql: SELECT DATE, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_NOAA_AIS GROUP BY DATE ORDER BY n DESC LIMIT 10
kept.evidence: GROUP BY DATE over the full table returns exactly one date: 2024-01-01 with 7,296,275 rows (100%). BASEDATETIME confirms intra-day timestamps only. Lat/lon are US-coastal (LAT 17.6 to 49.7, LON -158.0 to -64.6) -- domestic feed, one day.
kept.why_matters: The 7.3M row count implies a longitudinal AIS history, but it's a single daily snapshot. Any 'vessel movement over time', port-call, or loitering analysis is impossible -- there is no second timestamp to compare. Sanctioned-vessel-broadcasting style detectors built on this can only ever see one day of positions.
kept.implies: new source to load: additional AIS days if time-series vessel tracking is the goal; otherwise re-label this as a one-day snapshot in the registry

verdict.corrected_title: NOAA AIS '7.3M rows' is a single calendar day (2024-01-01) -- a vessel-tracking snapshot, not a longitudinal panel
verdict.notes:
CORE CLAIM HOLDS, AIRTIGHT. Triple-confirmed across three independent columns: (1) DATE GROUP BY returns exactly one value 2024-01-01 with 7,296,275 rows; (2) SOURCE_FILE GROUP BY returns exactly one file AIS_2024_01_01.zip with the same 7,296,275; (3) BASEDATETIME date-part GROUP BY returns 2024-01-01 only, MIN 2024-01-01T00:00:00 / MAX 2024-01-01T23:59:59 -- full intra-day coverage, zero second calendar day. Verified no hidden rows: COUNT(*)=7,296,275, all 7,296,275 are DATE='2024-01-01', 0 blank-or-null dates. So it is genuinely 100%, not a sort/casting artifact.

This is FACT grade: it's a hard structural property of the table (date column literally has cardinality 1), not an entity/name match, so no libel tier concern.

ONE SECONDARY CLAIM WRONG (does not affect verdict): candidate's lat/lon bounds (LAT 17.6-49.7, LON -158.0 to -64.6, 'US-coastal') do NOT reproduce. True bounds via TRY_CAST: LAT 0.12 to 50.38, LON -160.08 to +145.93. MAXLON +145.93 is Western Pacific (Guam / US Pacific territories), and MINLAT 0.12 is near-equatorial -- wider than 'US-coastal continental'. Candidate likely sampled or filtered. Minor over-narrowing of a side detail; the single-day finding stands regardless.

Other facts: 14,868 distinct MMSI (vessels) in the snapshot. Inventory's '7.3M' is just rounding of 7,296,275.

INVESTIGATOR IMPACT (real): any vessel-movement-over-time / port-call / loitering / sanctioned-vessel-broadcasting-pattern analysis is impossible -- there is no second timestamp day to diff against. You can do a single-day spatial cross-section (e.g. which sanctioned IMOs were broadcasting on 2024-01-01) but NOT temporal behavior.

kept.title: IMO normalization is mandatory: OFAC stores bare '8508462', AIS stores 'IMO8851273' — a naive string join silently misses every match
kept.sql: SELECT IMO, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_NOAA_AIS WHERE NULLIF(IMO,'') IS NOT NULL GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC LIMIT 5; -- vs OFAC: SELECT IMO, LENGTH(IMO) FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_OFAC_SDN WHERE NULLIF(IMO,'') IS NOT NULL ORDER BY 2 DESC LIMIT 5
kept.evidence: AIS IMO top values are 'IMO'-prefixed: IMO0000001 (16,704 rows), IMO0000101 (3,725), IMO8851273 (2,557), plus garbage placeholders IMO0000001/IMO111000000. OFAC IMO is bare 7-digit (max LENGTH=7, e.g. 8508462). Correct join requires REGEXP_REPLACE(ais.IMO,'^IMO','') and filtering '^[1-9][0-9]{6}$'. AIS has only 4,560 DISTINCT real IMOs (most pings carry junk placeholder IMOs).
kept.why_matters: Any detector wiring these two tables on raw IMO returns ZERO and looks 'clean'. The prefix + placeholder junk (IMO0000001 = 16.7k rows) means MMSI, not IMO, is AIS's only reliable per-ping vessel key; IMO must be regex-extracted and validity-filtered before joining to any sanctions list.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: IMO normalization is mandatory: AIS stores 100% 'IMO'-prefixed values (and 2.24M placeholder-junk pings), OFAC stores bare 7-digit — naive string join returns exactly 0 matches; correct regex join finds 2 sanctioned vessels broadcasting
verdict.notes:
Fully reproduces and the core claim is decisively proven. Receipts I re-ran:

(1) AIS prefix universality: of 7.30M AIS rows, 5,050,367 are 'IMO'-prefixed and 2,245,908 are blank — ZERO unprefixed non-blank values. So 100% of usable AIS IMOs need the prefix stripped.

(2) OFAC format: 2,032 non-blank IMO rows (2,030 distinct), MIN=MAX LENGTH=7, 0 prefixed. Uniformly bare 7-digit. Confirmed.

(3) THE decisive test (candidate didn't even include this — I added it): 
   - naive  o.IMO = a.IMO            -> 0 matches
   - normalized o.IMO = REGEXP_REPLACE(a.IMO,'^IMO','') filtered '^[1-9][0-9]{6}$' -> 2 matches
   This is the exact 'looks clean, actually broken' failure, and the 2 matches line up with the known sanctioned_vessel_broadcasting:2 detector.

(4) Distinct counts: 4,951 distinct raw AIS IMO; 4,560 distinct VALID after regex — matches the claimed 4,560 exactly. Junk/valid ping split: 2,238,475 junk pings over only 391 distinct junk codes vs 2,811,892 valid pings over 4,560 real IMOs. So 'most pings carry placeholder IMOs' is true at the distinct-code level being tiny, though by ping volume it's ~44% junk / ~56% valid (the candidate's 'most pings carry junk' overstates slightly — it's a plurality from one code, not a majority of pings).

GOTCHAS / minor wording: The candidate's claimed top-values list is mildly wrong. The TRUE #1 value is IMO0000000 at 1,857,245 rows (a giant all-zero placeholder it never mentioned); IMO0000001 is 16,704. It labeled IMO0000001 the placeholder, but the dominant placeholder is the all-zero one. This is a cosmetic error that strengthens the finding, not weakens it.

TIER: Correctly FACT. This is a structural schema/data-quality property (no entity is named as guilty), so libel/LEAD tiering doesn't apply. No casting artifact (counts are exact-string GROUP BYs, no TRY_CAST involved). No definitional confusion. The corrected_title adds the 0-vs-2 join receipt which is the strongest evidence and was absent from the original title.

kept.title: AIS is a single 24-hour snapshot (2024-01-01 only), not a surveillance feed — the entire OFAC<->AIS bridge sees 1 day of the world's ships
kept.sql: SELECT MIN(BASEDATETIME) min_dt, MAX(BASEDATETIME) max_dt, COUNT(DISTINCT DATE) distinct_dates, MIN(TRY_CAST(LAT AS DOUBLE)) min_lat, MAX(TRY_CAST(LAT AS DOUBLE)) max_lat, MIN(TRY_CAST(LON AS DOUBLE)) min_lon, MAX(TRY_CAST(LON AS DOUBLE)) max_lon FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_NOAA_AIS
kept.evidence: Full table spans 2024-01-01T00:00:00 to 2024-01-01T23:59:59, DISTINCT_DATES=1. Lat range 0.12 to 50.38, lon -160.08 to 145.93 (global footprint but US-coast-dense; matched vessels cluster at lat 25-32, lon -79 to -119 = Gulf/Atlantic/US Pacific). 7.3M pings = one day at high frequency, not a time series.
kept.why_matters: Explains WHY only 2-6 sanctioned vessels appear: it is not that few sanctioned ships broadcast, it is that AIS covers one calendar day. A 'live detector' on this table is really a New-Year's-Day-2024 spot-check. To make the bridge meaningful, the AIS source needs more date coverage; until then, frame any maritime-sanctions finding as a single-day snapshot, never a pattern over time.
kept.implies: new source to load: additional AIS date ranges (NOAA marinecadastre dailies) to turn the one-day snapshot into a time series

verdict.corrected_title: FED_NOAA_AIS is a single 24-hour US-AIS snapshot (2024-01-01 only) — the entire OFAC<->AIS sanctioned-vessel bridge sees one calendar day, not a surveillance time series
verdict.notes:
Candidate SQL reproduces exactly: MIN=2024-01-01T00:00:00, MAX=2024-01-01T23:59:59, DISTINCT_DATES=1, lat 0.117-50.384, lon -160.084 to 145.931. Adversarial checks all pass:

(1) CASTING: BASEDATETIME and DATE are VARCHAR, but ISO-8601 strings sort lexically = chronologically, so MIN/MAX are valid. Not a text-sort artifact. All 7,296,275 rows have a non-blank BASEDATETIME (COUNT(NULLIF(BASEDATETIME,''))=7,296,275); no blanks faking the range, no silent TRY_CAST nulling.

(2) FULL DAY CONFIRMED: hourly GROUP BY LEFT(BASEDATETIME,13) shows all 24 hours present, evenly distributed ~291k-317k pings/hr. Genuinely a complete calendar day at high frequency, exactly as claimed. Single DATE value = '2024-01-01'.

(3) ONE WORDING FIX (not enough to downgrade): the 'global footprint' framing is loose. Longitude-band breakdown: US band (-130 to -60) = 7,211,700 pings (98.8%); far-west (<-130, incl Guam/Pacific terr ~145E) = 73,526 (1.0%); east-of-US (>-60) = 11,049 (0.15%). This is NOAA Marine Cadastre US AIS, not a global surveillance feed — the 145.93E / 0.12 lat extremes are sub-1.2% tail outliers (US Pacific territories), not real worldwide coverage. Corrected title reflects 'US-AIS snapshot'.

(4) WHY-IT-MATTERS validated: only 2 OFAC SDN IMOs match AIS IMOs (7-digit IMO join), consistent with the framing of '2-6 sanctioned vessels' and the existing sanctioned_vessel_broadcasting detector (count=2). The IMO match is a HARD-ID join (FACT grade), so the bridge itself is sound — the limitation is purely temporal coverage.

Bottom line: the coverage-gap claim is FACT-grade and holds. Any maritime-sanctions finding on this table must be framed as a single-day (2024-01-01) US-coastal spot-check, never a pattern over time. Surprise modest (AIS daily files are a known NOAA distribution format) but importance high — it caps what the whole vessel-tracking moat can claim.

kept.title: 'Lockheed Martin' is one company wearing 77 different UEIs, 26 parent UEIs and 20 name spellings - $75B of FY2025 contracts impossible to total without entity resolution
kept.sql: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT RECIPIENT_UEI) child_uei, COUNT(DISTINCT RECIPIENT_PARENT_UEI) parent_uei, COUNT(DISTINCT RECIPIENT_NAME) child_names, ROUND(SUM(TRY_CAST(FEDERAL_ACTION_OBLIGATION AS DOUBLE))/1e9,1) tot_b FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_USASPENDING_CONTRACTS WHERE UPPER(RECIPIENT_PARENT_NAME) LIKE 'LOCKHEED MARTIN%';
kept.evidence: Under parent name 'LOCKHEED MARTIN%': 77 distinct RECIPIENT_UEI, 26 distinct RECIPIENT_PARENT_UEI, 20 distinct RECIPIENT_NAME spellings, $75.0B obligated FY2025. In the top-25 recipient list, 'LOCKHEED MARTIN CORPORATION' alone shows up under 4 separate UEIs (G4KDGE4JFFK7 $34.1B, XFJMYSYFJEK4 $10.4B, H7PNSVNN5827 $6.1B, + more).
kept.why_matters: UEI is supposed to be the clean federal-contractor key, but a single prime fragments across dozens of UEIs and parent UEIs. Any 'who got the most federal money' analysis is wrong unless you roll up by normalized parent. This is the central entity-resolution hazard of the whole contractor spine - and it understates concentration.
kept.implies: new detector: parent-UEI rollup / name-normalization layer before any recipient-ranking; treat RECIPIENT_PARENT_NAME-to-UEI as many-to-many

verdict.corrected_title: 'Lockheed Martin' is one prime fragmented across 77 child UEIs, 26 parent UEIs and 6 parent-name spellings - $75.0B of FY2025 obligations; even the exact name 'LOCKHEED MARTIN CORPORATION' carries 42 distinct UEIs
verdict.notes:
Re-ran the exact candidate SQL: 77 child UEIs, 26 parent UEIs, 20 child names, $75.05B, 38,014 rows -- all reproduce to the decimal. Top-UEI dollars also reproduce exactly (G4KDGE4JFFK7 $34.09B, XFJMYSYFJEK4 $10.42B, H7PNSVNN5827 $6.06B).

Adversarial checks all PASS:
- Window: ACTION_DATE 2024-10-01 to 2025-09-30 = exactly FY2025. The 'YRS=2' is only because the federal fiscal year straddles two calendar years; not a scope error.
- Casting: 0 null obligations under TRY_CAST; 2,130 NEGATIVE rows (deobligations) means $75.05B is a NET figure, slightly understating gross awards -- this strengthens, not weakens, the 'understates concentration' point.
- Filter cleanliness: LIKE 'LOCKHEED MARTIN%' on parent name catches 38,014 rows. Broadening to '%LOCKHEED%' adds only 104 rows, all from 'RAYTHEON/LOCKHEED MARTIN JAVELIN JV' (UEI WGB7GAKM7A68) -- a genuinely separate joint venture, correctly excluded. No false positives, and 0 recipient-level Lockheed rows lack a Lockheed parent, so nothing is missed.
- HARD-ID, single-source: this is a within-source structural fact about the UEI key itself (a HARD ID), not a cross-source or fuzzy-name match -> FACT grade is correct.

Two corrections to wording (claim was UNDER-stated, not over-stated):
1. '20 name spellings, 26 parent UEIs' -> the 20 distinct RECIPIENT_NAME and 6 distinct RECIPIENT_PARENT_NAME spellings; the claim conflated 'name spellings' a bit. Actual parent-name spellings = 6 (LOCKHEED MARTIN CORP / CORPORATION / GLOBAL INC / INTEGRATED SYSTEMS LLC / ACULIGHT / OVERSEAS LLC), all legitimately Lockheed.
2. The claim said 'LOCKHEED MARTIN CORPORATION appears under 4 separate UEIs' (top-25 only). Across the full set the EXACT string 'LOCKHEED MARTIN CORPORATION' maps to 42 distinct UEIs. Hazard is bigger than stated.

Most damning detail for the data-quality thesis: RECIPIENT_PARENT_UEI -- the field that is supposed to BE the single rollup key -- itself fragments into 26 non-blank UEIs (top one ZFN2JJXBLZT3 = 31,878 rows, but 25 others split the rest). So even rolling up by parent UEI does not collapse Lockheed to one row. Confirmed as a real, publishable entity-resolution hazard.

kept.title: 1.21M tax-exempt revocations are almost all one IRS event - 274,997 orgs killed on a single day (15-MAY-2010), and 14.9% later clawed their status back
kept.sql: SELECT REVOCATION_DATE, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_IRS_REVOCATION GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY n DESC LIMIT 3; -- plus: SELECT COUNT(*) total, SUM(CASE WHEN EXEMPTION_REINSTATEMENT_DATE NOT IN ('') AND EXEMPTION_REINSTATEMENT_DATE IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) reinstated, ROUND(100.0*reinstated/total,1) pct FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_IRS_REVOCATION;
kept.evidence: 1,206,628 total revocations, 1,187,367 distinct EINs. Date is wildly clustered: 15-MAY-2010 = 274,997 (the one-time Pension Protection Act mass auto-revocation), 15-MAY-2017 = 69,500, 15-NOV-2010 = 58,578; every other big bucket is also a 15-MAY-of-year date. 179,445 rows (14.9%) carry a non-null EXEMPTION_REINSTATEMENT_DATE.
kept.why_matters: This table is NOT a stream of scandals - it is one historical purge plus an annual May cron job. Anyone querying it as 'orgs that lost status' must know ~23% of all revocations are a single 2010 event, and ~1 in 7 was reinstated (the status revolves). A required caveat before any 'revoked nonprofit' claim is built on it.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: 1.21M IRS tax-exempt revocations are an administrative posting stream, not a scandal feed: 274,997 orgs auto-revoked on the 15-MAY-2010 Pension Protection Act purge (22.8%), every posting lands on the 15th of the month, and 14.9% were later reinstated
verdict.notes:
Every headline number reproduced EXACTLY on re-run: 1,206,628 total rows, 1,187,367 distinct EINs, 179,445 reinstated (14.9%), 15-MAY-2010=274,997, 15-MAY-2017=69,500, 15-NOV-2010=58,578. Reinstatement % uses NULLIF(...,'') to handle blanks correctly — no blank-as-zero artifact.

I HARDENED the claim with two checks the original didn't run:
(1) DAY-OF-MONTH: 1,206,626 of 1,206,628 rows (99.9998%) fall on the 15th of the month (only 2 stray rows on the 5th: 05-JAN-2019, 05-MAR-2019). This proves REVOCATION_DATE is an IRS administrative POSTING/EFFECTIVE date (auto-revocations posted on the 15th), NOT the literal day each org ceased operating. This strengthens the 'cron job, not scandals' thesis — added that nuance to the corrected title.
(2) 15-MAY is the dominant annual cron month: 848,497 of 1.21M rows (70%) are 15-MAY-of-some-year; 358,129 are the 15th of other months (mostly 15-NOV trailing-year batches). So the candidate's 'every big bucket is 15-MAY' is slightly loose — 15-NOV buckets are sizable (58,578 in 2010) — but the thesis is unaffected.

Other texture: 19,261 duplicate-EIN rows (orgs revoked>reinstated>re-revoked, i.e. status truly revolves); 0 NULL EINs. 22,487 orgs in the 2010 cohort carry reinstatement_date = revocation_date (same-day 15-MAY-2010), consistent with retroactive reinstatement back to original revocation. 2010-cohort reinstatement rate is 13.4% (slightly below the 14.9% overall).

TIER: correctly FACT — single-table hard COUNTs, no cross-source/cross-ID/name match anywhere. No casting artifact (all string equality and COUNT). No definitional confusion. The original SQL's reinstated condition (NOT IN ('') AND IS NOT NULL) is equivalent to my NULLIF version and gives the same 179,445.

Verdict CONFIRMED. Surprise moderate (the PPA mass auto-revocation is documented history; the non-obvious bit is the magnitude — 23% of the whole table is one day — and the 100%-on-the-15th posting-date property). Importance high: this is a mandatory caveat — anyone treating this table as 'orgs that lost status' must know ~23% is one 2010 event and 1-in-7 was reinstated.

kept.title: The overdose epicenter is NOT the incarceration epicenter: West Virginia has the nation's worst overdose (median 57.8/100k) with full jail coverage, yet barely lands in the double-extreme set — Kentucky does
kept.sql: SELECT ST_NAME, ROUND(MEDIAN(TRY_CAST(RATE AS DOUBLE)),1) med_od, ROUND(MAX(TRY_CAST(RATE AS DOUBLE)),1) max_od, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CDC_INJURY_VIOLENCE_COUNTY WHERE PERIOD='2023' AND INTENT='Drug_OD' AND TRY_CAST(RATE AS DOUBLE) >= 0 GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY med_od DESC LIMIT 8; -- and: SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT COUNTY_FIPS) wv_counties, COUNT(NULLIF(TOTAL_JAIL_POP_RATE,'')) wv_with_jail FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_VERA_INCARCERATION_TRENDS WHERE YEAR='2019' AND STATE_ABBR='WV';
kept.evidence: State median overdose rate ranking (2023): DC 61.9, West Virginia 57.8, Delaware 49.0, Maine 40.6, Kentucky 35.1. WV has all 55 counties in Vera-2019 WITH jail rates (no coverage gap), so its absence from the double-extreme list is real, not missing data. Yet WV contributes 0 to the top-50 double-extreme set while Kentucky (statewide median OD only 35.1, ranked 8th) contributes 20.
kept.why_matters: Disproves the intuitive assumption that the worst-overdose places jail the most. WV dies of overdose at the highest rate but doesn't over-jail; Kentucky does both. The policy response to the same epidemic diverges sharply state-to-state — a contradiction worth interrogating.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: West Virginia has the worst overdose (county median ~58/100k, 2nd only to single-county DC) but jails at ~half Kentucky's rate: WV contributes 0 to the double-extreme OD+jail set while Kentucky (lower OD) contributes 20 — same epidemic, divergent carceral response
verdict.notes:
VERDICT: contradiction is REAL and survives adversarial probing; downgraded only for a numeric self-inconsistency and an unstated temporal gap, not because the signal is wrong.

REPRODUCED: KY=20, WV=0 in the double-extreme set reproduced exactly under independently-chosen thresholds (od_rank<=200 AND jail_rank<=400). The driving mechanism is confirmed and quantified by direct medians among FIPS-matched counties: WV med OD 58.2 / med jail rate 482.6; KY med OD 36.0 / med jail rate 904.6 (~2x WV); TN 38.3 / 857.8. WV genuinely under-jails relative to its overdose burden. Coverage claim holds: WV has all 55 counties with OD rates and 55 with Vera-2019 jail rates — absence is real, not missing data.

CAUGHT (basis for DOWNGRADE):
1) Number self-inconsistency: candidate's prose says WV median OD = 57.8, but its OWN published SQL (filter RATE>=0) yields 58.2 over n=53. The 57.8 figure includes 2 rows where RATE = -999. Does not change WV's #2 rank, but the published SQL does not produce the published number.
2) -999 sentinel quirk: RATE column carries CDC's -999 missing/suppressed code in 100 counties nationally (2 in WV: Tucker, Pleasants). Candidate's SQL excludes them via RATE>=0; the quoted median does not. Anyone re-running naively (e.g. AVG without filter) would get garbage.
3) DC #1 (61.9) is a SINGLE county (n=1), not a state distribution — citing it as the top 'state median' is technically true but trivial. WV is the real worst-state signal.
4) Temporal mismatch: OD-2023 joined to incarceration-2019 (4-yr gap). Vera data exists through 2026, but 2019 has the best jail-rate coverage (2902 counties vs 1980 in 2023), so 2019 is a DEFENSIBLE choice — but the gap should be stated.
5) KY tail artifact noted but NOT fatal: Fulton County KY shows jail rate 12,788/100k (small-county regional-jail denominator inflation, a known Vera quirk). KY's count is carried by its median of 904.6 across 109 counties, not by this outlier, so KY=20 is not a single-artifact result.

TIER: FACT is appropriate — county-level FIPS-on-FIPS join (hard geographic ID, LPAD'd to 5), aggregate state-level pattern, no person named. No libel exposure.

kept.title: Open Payments: 20,733 doctor 'meals' priced at exactly $124.99 -- 99.7% Food & Beverage, one cent under $125
kept.sql: SELECT TRY_CAST(TOTAL_AMOUNT_OF_PAYMENT_USDOLLARS AS DOUBLE) amt, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS WHERE TRY_CAST(TOTAL_AMOUNT_OF_PAYMENT_USDOLLARS AS DOUBLE) BETWEEN 124 AND 125 GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC LIMIT 10;  -- then: SELECT NATURE_OF_PAYMENT_OR_TRANSFER_OF_VALUE, COUNT(*) FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS WHERE TRY_CAST(TOTAL_AMOUNT_OF_PAYMENT_USDOLLARS AS DOUBLE)=124.99 GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC
kept.evidence: $125.00 appears 48,825 times (top value overall). $124.99 appears 20,733 times -- the single most common odd-cents value -- and 20,666 of those (99.7%) are Nature='Food and Beverage'. The values ladder right below the threshold: 124.99 (20,733), 124.98 (4,733), 124.97 (2,352), 124.95 (1,117). Table = 15.39M rows, single program year 2024.
kept.why_matters: A textbook just-below-threshold behavioral signal: pharma reps appear to be pricing physician meals at exactly one cent under the round $125 figure, overwhelmingly in the Food & Beverage category. It is a clean, publishable data pattern (pure aggregate counts, no entity claim) and a ready-made viz: the spike-and-cliff at $125.
kept.implies: new detector: flag manufacturers with abnormal share of 124.xx Food&Beverage payments (just-under-threshold meal pricing) -- a fresh angle off the LEIE x Open Payments edge that 338/353 existing leads sit on

verdict.corrected_title: Open Payments 2024: 20,733 physician payments priced at exactly $124.99 (99.7% Food & Beverage) -- a 10x spike one cent under $125, spread across every major pharma company
verdict.notes:
Every number reproduces exactly. $125.00 = 48,825 (top value overall, #1); $124.99 = 20,733 (#4 overall, and the ONLY odd-cents value in the entire top-25 -- everything else around it is a round number: $25, $20, $150). Nature breakdown: 20,666 of 20,733 = 99.7% Food and Beverage. Program year is uniformly 2024 (single-year table). The ladder holds: 124.99(20,733) > 124.98(4,733) > 124.97(2,352) > 124.95(1,117).

ARTIFACT CHECKS -- all passed:
(1) Casting: 15,385,047 rows, 100% castable to DOUBLE, 0 blanks. No silent TRY_CAST nulling, no blank-as-zero. The $124.99 cluster is genuine numeric data, not a text-sort artifact.
(2) Threshold asymmetry is real and sharp: 124.99 has 20,733 but its mirror 125.01 has only 2,018 -- a ~10x cliff right at the round number. This is the core signal and it is not a coincidence of binning.
(3) Not a single-payer accounting quirk: the $124.99 rows are spread across the entire major-pharma roster -- AbbVie (2,275), AstraZeneca (2,055), Amgen (1,147), Novo Nordisk (675), GSK (665), Lilly (649), Gilead (492), BMS, Otsuka, etc. That broad spread is what makes it an industry-wide behavioral pattern rather than one company's convention. STRENGTHENS the finding.
(4) $124.99 genuinely dominates other psychological .99 thresholds (99.99=6,980; 19.99=7,683; 9.99=2,360), so the $125 line specifically is the magnet -- consistent with the CMS de minimis / small-payment reporting threshold sitting near there.

Tier: correctly FACT. This is a pure aggregate-count pattern with NO entity-guilt claim -- no person or company is accused of wrongdoing, just a pricing convention. Publishable as-is.

Minor wording: original title is accurate but I tightened it to specify program-year 2024 and add the 10x-spike / broad-pharma context, which is the most striking and defensible part. Did not over-claim causation (the $125 link to CMS rules is plausible but framed as the value, not a proven motive).

Downgraded surprise slightly (7) because just-below-threshold pricing is a known behavioral-economics phenomenon; the non-obvious part is that it shows up this cleanly in regulated pharma-physician transfers and across every major manufacturer.

kept.title: OFAC SDN_TYPE is '-0-' (OFAC's null token) for 9,785 of 19,115 entries -- naive type filters return zero entities
kept.sql: SELECT SDN_TYPE, COUNT(*) n, SUM(CASE WHEN NULLIF(TITLE,'')IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) has_title FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_OFAC_SDN GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC
kept.evidence: SDN_TYPE distribution: '-0-' = 9,785 (51%), individual = 7,488, vessel = 1,497, aircraft = 344, '' = 1. There is NO 'entity'/'company' value -- OFAC writes its literal '-0-' placeholder instead of a type for the ~9,785 organization records. Total 19,115 rows.
kept.why_matters: A query like WHERE SDN_TYPE='entity' (the intuitive way to pull sanctioned companies) silently returns nothing; the company rows hide behind '-0-'. This is OFAC's raw CSV convention ('-0-' = blank), not a load bug, but it will burn any cross-source sanctions join unless staging maps '-0-' -> 'entity'/NULL.
kept.implies: none -- staging: map SDN_TYPE '-0-' to a real category before any join

verdict.corrected_title: OFAC SDN_TYPE is OFAC's null token '-0- ' (with a trailing space) for 9,785 of 19,115 rows -- these are the sanctioned ORGANIZATIONS; there is no 'entity' value, so WHERE SDN_TYPE='entity' AND even WHERE SDN_TYPE='-0-' both return zero (you must TRIM)
verdict.notes:
Re-ran the candidate SQL: distribution reproduces EXACTLY -- '-0-'=9785, individual=7488, vessel=1497, aircraft=344, ''=1, total 19115. Verified the '-0-' rows are genuinely organizations (sampled: AEROCARIBBEAN AIRLINES, BANCO NACIONAL DE CUBA, CIMEX S.A. -- banks/airlines/trading cos). WHERE SDN_TYPE='entity' = 0 rows (confirmed). It is OFAC's raw-CSV convention, not a load bug. Core claim HOLDS.

Two corrections to the candidate, both found by adversarial checks:

(1) HIDDEN TRAILING SPACE. The actual stored value is '-0- ' with LENGTH=4, not 3. Proof: SELECT '['||SDN_TYPE||']', LENGTH(SDN_TYPE), COUNT(*) ... -> [-0- ]/4/9785. The candidate displayed it as '-0-' because GROUP BY collapses display whitespace and never caught it. Consequence: even the obvious remediation WHERE SDN_TYPE='-0-' returns ZERO (verified: type_dash0_exact=0, only TRIM(SDN_TYPE)='-0-'=9785). This makes the warning STRONGER, and the candidate's proposed staging map '-0-'->'entity' would itself silently fail without a TRIM. Staging must TRIM(SDN_TYPE) first.

(2) The has_title column in the candidate SQL is MISLEADING and should be dropped. TITLE is itself literally '-0-' for ALL 9,785 entity rows (and for 6,273 of the individuals). NULLIF(TITLE,'') treats '-0-' as non-null, so has_title=9785 just re-counts the placeholder -- it does NOT mean these rows carry real titles. Real titles only exist for ~1,215 individuals (e.g. 'Member of the State Duma...').

Tier stays FACT: it is a structural data-quality property of a single table, no entity guilt claim, no cross-source/name match involved. Surprise moderate (the trailing space is the genuinely non-obvious part). Importance solid -- '-0-' is OFAC's universal placeholder across many columns (TITLE, REMARKS, VESS_TYPE all use it), so any cross-source sanctions join must normalize '-0-'->NULL globally and TRIM, or it burns silently.

kept.title: USASpending CONTRACT_AWARD_UNIQUE_KEY is NOT unique - 9.6% excess rows, one contract appears up to 174 times
kept.sql: SELECT COUNT(*) total_rows, COUNT(DISTINCT CONTRACT_AWARD_UNIQUE_KEY) distinct_keys, COUNT(*)-COUNT(DISTINCT CONTRACT_AWARD_UNIQUE_KEY) excess_rows, ROUND(100.0*(COUNT(*)-COUNT(DISTINCT CONTRACT_AWARD_UNIQUE_KEY))/COUNT(*),1) pct_excess FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_USASPENDING_CONTRACTS
kept.evidence: 6,325,622 total rows vs 5,719,566 distinct CONTRACT_AWARD_UNIQUE_KEY = 606,056 excess rows (9.6%). Top offender CONT_AWD_N0002412C2115_9700_-NONE-_-NONE- appears 174x; second 136x; ten keys appear 105-174x. The 'unique key' is actually a contract-award key, and each award accumulates one row per transaction/modification over its life - it is one-row-per-modification, not one-row-per-contract.
kept.why_matters: This is a landmine. Anyone treating CONTRACT_AWARD_UNIQUE_KEY as a primary key (the name invites it) will double-count obligation dollars or mis-attribute a contract's value. Correct contract-level analysis must aggregate by key (e.g. take the max CURRENT_TOTAL_VALUE_OF_AWARD), never SUM raw rows. The staging model needs an explicit dedup/rollup, and dollar totals across the table must collapse modifications first.
kept.implies: new detector / staging rule: dedup FED_USASPENDING_CONTRACTS to award grain before any dollar aggregation

verdict.corrected_title: USASpending CONTRACT_AWARD_UNIQUE_KEY is one-row-per-transaction, not one-row-per-contract - 9.6% excess rows, one award appears up to 174 times; naive SUM overcounts dollars ~90x
verdict.notes: Fully reproduces. Core SQL re-ran exactly: 6,325,622 total / 5,719,566 distinct / 606,056 excess / 9.6%. Top offenders match (174x CONT_AWD_N0002412C2115..., 136x ...N0002418C2307...). Checked for artifacts and found none: zero NULL/blank keys (so the distinct count is not inflated by empties). Verified the duplicates are GENUINE distinct transactions, not full-row dupes - inspected the 174-row offender (SSN 792 submarine construction): each row has a distinct ACTION_DATE, distinct FEDERAL_ACTION_OBLIGATION (0, 2935, 4805434, 37646000, ...), and distinct TRANSACTION_DESCRIPTION. There is no MODIFICATION_NUMBER or transaction-level unique-key column in the landing table, so CONTRACT_AWARD_UNIQUE_KEY is the only stable award identifier - the name genuinely invites misuse as a PK. Demonstrated the double-count danger concretely on that key: MAX(CURRENT_TOTAL_VALUE_OF_AWARD)=$38.3B (correct cumulative award value) vs SUM(CURRENT_TOTAL_VALUE_OF_AWARD)=$3.46T across 174 rows = ~90x overcount; FEDERAL_ACTION_OBLIGATION is the incremental field that DOES sum (to $831M here). One wording refinement: it's strictly one-row-per-TRANSACTION (base award + each mod action), not literally per-modification, but that's a sharpening not an error. Not a casting artifact (numbers are COUNT-based, no TRY_CAST involved in the headline metric). Holds as FACT-grade; the staging model should rollup by key (MAX cumulative fields, SUM only incremental obligation).

kept.title: HCRIS is mislabeled as a long panel but is really just FY2023-2024 (2 years, 99.9% of rows)
kept.sql: SELECT YEAR(TRY_TO_DATE(FISCAL_YEAR_END_DATE,'MM/DD/YYYY')) AS fy_end, COUNT(*) AS n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_HCRIS GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
kept.evidence: Real fiscal-year-end years: 2022=5 rows, 2023=3534, 2024=2564 (total 6103). A naive LEFT(FISCAL_YEAR_END_DATE,4) GROUP BY produces decoy buckets like '06/3'->1772 and '12/3'->2436 that LOOK like a 2003-2012 panel, but the date is MM/DD/YYYY so those are month/day fragments, not years. CCN cardinality 6040 distinct over 6103 rows confirms ~1 cost report per hospital for one cycle.
kept.why_matters: Anyone treating HCRIS as a hospital-finance time series (charity care, uncompensated care, net income trends) will be wrong: there is no history here, just the two most recent fiscal years. The MM/DD/YYYY string format actively misleads any LEFT()-based year probe.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: HCRIS is a single most-recent-cost-report snapshot, not a panel: 99.9% of rows are FY2023-2024 end dates (~1 report per hospital)
verdict.notes:
Reproduced exactly. YEAR(TRY_TO_DATE(FISCAL_YEAR_END_DATE,'MM/DD/YYYY')): 2022=5, 2023=3534, 2024=2564, total 6103; 0 NULL dates from the cast so all rows are accounted for (5+3534+2564=6103). 2023+2024 = 6098/6103 = 99.92%. Raw samples confirm MM/DD/YYYY format (e.g. 12/01/2022 -> 01/31/2023). Begin dates: FY2022=985, FY2023=5118; min begin 2022-10-01, max end 2024-09-30 -- one cost-report cycle. CCN cardinality: 6040 distinct PROVIDER_CCN over 6103 rows; CONCAT(CCN,'|',FISCAL_YEAR_END_DATE) is fully unique (6103/6103), so ~1 cost report per hospital with no time series. The decoy is real: LEFT(FISCAL_YEAR_END_DATE,4) GROUP BY yields '12/3'->2436, '06/3'->1772, '09/3'->901, etc. These are MM/DD fragments, not years.

One wording overstatement (does NOT change verdict, hence CONFIRMED not DOWNGRADED): the candidate says the LEFT() buckets 'LOOK like a 2003-2012 panel.' They literally read as '12/3','06/3' WITH A SLASH -- so a careful analyst would notice they aren't clean 4-digit years. The genuine misleading risk is subtler (assuming a long panel exists at all, or LEFT(,2) month buckets), not that '12/3' masquerades as '2003'. The core coverage-gap claim -- HCRIS has no financial history here, just the two most recent fiscal years -- is fully correct and material for anyone planning charity-care / uncompensated-care / net-income trend analysis. FACT tier is appropriate: pure structural counts, no entity/name claims.

kept.title: CDC injury/violence county RATE column hides a -999 sentinel (6,549 rows) AND 70,549 suppressed string counts -- AVG(RATE) would silently include -999 as a real rate
kept.sql: SELECT INTENT, COUNT(*) total, SUM(CASE WHEN RATE='-999.0000000000000' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) neg999, SUM(CASE WHEN RATE='0.000000000000000' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) zero, SUM(CASE WHEN COUNT_SUP='1-9' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) suppressed FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CDC_INJURY_VIOLENCE_COUNTY GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC
kept.evidence: Of 132,000 rows: RATE='-999.0' appears 6,549 times (a missing-data sentinel, not a rate); RATE='0.0' appears 30,832 times. COUNT_SUP (the count column) is the string '1-9' in 70,549 rows (53%) -- a suppressed range, not a number. The -999 sentinel is spread across all 6 intents (550 FA_Homicide to 1,713 All_Suicide). Any TRY_CAST(RATE AS DOUBLE) + AVG/SUM treats -999 as a real negative rate and poisons the result.
kept.why_matters: This is a landed/queryable source where the single most important numeric column carries a -999 trap and a majority-string count column. Anyone computing county injury rates without filtering RATE>=0 AND RATE<>-999 gets garbage. The fix is a one-line WHERE, but the trap is invisible until you profile.
kept.implies: new detector: a generic -999/-9999/-99 sentinel scanner across all numeric-looking TEXT columns in landing

verdict.corrected_title: CDC injury/violence county RATE column hides a -999 sentinel (6,549 rows, paired with suppressed counts) and COUNT_SUP is the string '1-9' in 70,549 rows (53%) -- naive AVG(RATE) returns -38.5 instead of +11.6
verdict.notes:
Re-ran the exact candidate SQL; every number reproduced. Of 132,000 rows: RATE='-999.0' = 6,549 (min value confirmed -999.0), RATE='0.0' = 30,832, COUNT_SUP='1-9' = 70,549 (53.4%). -999 is spread across all 6 intents (FA_Homicide 550 low to All_Suicide 1,713 high). The poisoning is real and quantified: AVG(TRY_CAST(RATE AS DOUBLE)) = -38.527 (a nonsense negative injury rate) vs AVG filtered to RATE>=0 = +11.613 -- a ~50-point swing, so the trap fires hard.

Adversarial checks: (1) Not a casting artifact -- TRY_CAST cleanly groups -999.0 as the literal minimum, blanks are separate. (2) The -999 sentinel is STRUCTURED, not random: 6,548 of the 6,549 -999 rows also carry COUNT_SUP='1-9' (small-cell privacy suppression). So -999 is CDC's documented placeholder for suppressed/unstable rates, paired with the suppressed count range. This is a known-convention rather than corrupt data -- but it does NOT refute the finding: an analyst unaware of the convention still poisons every AVG/SUM, and the numbers prove it. (3) COUNT_SUP non-castable rows = 70,600 (the extra ~51 beyond '1-9' are blanks/other markers), so the column is majority-string exactly as claimed.

Minor wording nit in original: actual INTENT codes are FA_Deaths/FA_Suicide/All_Suicide/Drug_OD/All_Homicide/FA_Homicide (the claim's '6 intents' and the 550/1,713 endpoints are correct). Tier FACT is right -- pure single-table profiling, no entity/ID match, nothing libelous. Fix is WHERE TRY_CAST(RATE AS DOUBLE) >= 0 (equivalently RATE<>'-999.0').

kept.title: Vera incarceration 'panel' silently splices two windows: prison data DEAD-ENDS at 2019, jail data runs to 2026 — 11,914 post-2019 rows have ZERO prison values
kept.sql: SELECT CASE WHEN TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT)<=2019 THEN 'le2019' ELSE 'ge2020' END period, COUNT(*) n, COUNT(NULLIF(TOTAL_JAIL_POP,'')) jail, COUNT(NULLIF(TOTAL_PRISON_POP,'')) prison FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_VERA_INCARCERATION_TRENDS GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
kept.evidence: YEAR spans 1970-2026 (128,507 rows, 3,075 counties). Split at 2019: le2019 = 116,593 rows / 86,684 jail-filled / 90,582 prison-filled. ge2020 = 11,914 rows / 11,256 jail-filled / 0 (zero) prison-filled. MAX(YEAR with any TOTAL_PRISON_POP) = 2019 exactly. So prison coverage cliffs at 2019 while jail was extended through 2026.
kept.why_matters: Anyone treating this as a unified incarceration panel will see prison populations vanish to NULL after 2019 and could misread it as decarceration to zero. The jail and prison series have DIFFERENT end dates baked into one table — a classic silent splice. Any time-trend on total incarceration must be censored at 2019.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: Vera incarceration panel silently splices two windows: prison series DEAD-ENDS at 2019, jail series runs to 2026 — all 11,914 post-2019 rows carry a BLANK (not zero) prison population
verdict.notes:
Every number reproduced exactly on re-run. le2019=116,593 rows (jail 86,684 / prison 90,582); ge2020=11,914 rows (jail 11,256 / prison 0). 3,075 distinct counties confirmed. MAX(YEAR with prison)=2019, MAX(YEAR with jail)=2026.

Adversarial checks, all passed:
- NOT a casting artifact: YEAR is fully clean (yr_uncastable=0, range 1970-2026), so the TRY_CAST<=2019 split drops nothing silently.
- NOT a reported-zero masquerading as missing: distinct TOTAL_PRISON_POP for YEAR>=2020 is a single value — blank string '' (11,914 rows). It is genuinely absent, never literal '0'. So COUNT(NULLIF(...,'')) correctly returns 0 and there's no blanks-as-zero trap in the evidence itself.
- The cliff is SHARP, not a taper: year-by-year prison filled rows 2015-2019 are 2571/2573/2615/2610/2593, then 2020 onward is 0/0/0/0/0/0/0. Jail keeps going (2229/2203/1945/1981/1441/860/597). Total row counts also shrink post-2019 (coverage thinning), but prison hitting exactly zero is the clean break.
- Single-table claim, no cross-source/cross-ID join, so FACT tier is correct (no LEAD downgrade needed).

Only tweak: title sharpened to stress the missing values are BLANK strings (not reported zeros), which is exactly why a naive COALESCE/TRY_CAST-with-default would misread it as decarceration to zero. The 'must censor total-incarceration time-trends at 2019' guidance is sound.

kept.title: Vera table contains FUTURE years: 597 rows dated 2026 (and a tapering 2024-2026 tail), with jail data populated for a year not yet complete
kept.sql: SELECT YEAR, COUNT(*) n, COUNT(NULLIF(TOTAL_JAIL_POP,'')) jail FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_VERA_INCARCERATION_TRENDS WHERE TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT) >= 2021 GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
kept.evidence: Row counts by year: 2021=2282, 2022=2052, 2023=2090, 2024=1633, 2025=957, 2026=597. Jail-filled counts taper in lockstep (2024:1441, 2025:860, 2026:597). MAX(YEAR)=2026 — the current year, with 597 counties already reporting jail pops, and county coverage collapsing from ~2300 (2021) to ~600 (2026).
kept.why_matters: 2026 is incomplete by definition (today is 2026-06-27) yet appears as a full panel year, and the per-year county count falls ~75% from 2021 to 2026. Cross-county comparisons in recent years are sampling a shrinking, non-representative subset — trends after ~2023 are coverage artifacts, not real change.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: Vera incarceration panel runs through a future/incomplete year (MAX(YEAR)=2026), and recent-year coverage collapses: from 2,282 counties across all states in 2021 to 597 rows across only FIVE states (TX, GA, KY, FL, NY) in 2026 — recent-year cross-county trends are a shrinking, non-representative subset, not real change
verdict.notes:
Every number in the candidate reproduces EXACTLY: 2021=2282, 2022=2052, 2023=2090, 2024=1633, 2025=957, 2026=597; jail counts 2024=1441, 2025=860, 2026=597. MAX(TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT))=2026 confirmed (range 1970-2026, 57 distinct years). 2026 jail pops are REAL non-zero values (avg 328, max 7114), not casting artifacts or blanks-as-zero.

Checked for artifacts: (1) Not a text-sort issue — used TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT) throughout. (2) Not blanks-as-zero — all 597 of 2026 rows have non-blank TOTAL_JAIL_POP, 543 strictly positive, 54 are literal '0'. Minor wording nuance: "597 counties reporting jail pops" is true for non-blank but 54 of those are zero-valued, so ~543 report a positive jail population. (3) Single-table table, no cross-source/cross-ID match, so FACT tier is correct.

The candidate UNDERSTATES the real story. The collapse isn't just county count — the 2026 panel is only FIVE states (TX 254, GA 144, KY 74, FL 67, NY 58); 2025 is 11 states. This is a known Vera property: recent years come from a rolling subset of jurisdictions that report frequently, not a complete national census. So the headline 'future year with incomplete coverage' is correct and the investigator caveat (don't compare cross-county trends post-2023) is well-founded and arguably more urgent than stated. Surprise is moderate (6) — this is a documented Vera data property an investigator should know, not a hidden anomaly. Importance 7 — anyone trending Vera jail pops past 2023 will be fooled by the shrinking, FL/TX/GA-skewed sample.

kept.title: LEIE carries usable NPIs on only 10% of rows (8,504 of 83,464) and records ZERO reinstatements — a structural coverage hole that caps any NPI-based exclusion detector
kept.sql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_rows, COUNT(DISTINCT NULLIF(NPI,'')) AS distinct_npi, COUNT(CASE WHEN NPI IS NOT NULL AND NPI<>'' AND NPI<>'0000000000' THEN 1 END) AS rows_with_real_npi, COUNT(CASE WHEN NULLIF(REINDATE,'') IS NOT NULL AND REINDATE<>'00000000' THEN 1 END) AS reinstated_rows, MIN(EXCLDATE) AS min_excl, MAX(EXCLDATE) AS max_excl FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_HHS_OIG_LEIE
kept.evidence: 83,464 total LEIE rows but only 8,684 rows carry a real NPI (8,504 distinct) — 89.6% of excluded entities have NPI blank or 0000000000 (older exclusions predate the NPI system, which began ~2007). EXCLDATE spans 19770701 to 20260618. REINDATE is '00000000' on 100% of rows (0 reinstatements recorded in this dump) — the file is current-exclusions-only, so a provider who was reinstated would simply be absent, not flagged.
kept.why_matters: Any LEIE-x-NPI detector can only ever see ~10% of the exclusion list — the 90% with no NPI are invisible to hard-ID joins and would need name/EIN/address fuzzy matching (LEAD-grade only). And because REINDATE is always null, every NPI match here is a CURRENT exclusion (good — no need to filter out reinstated providers), but it also means the file cannot answer 'was this person excluded as of date X and later cleared.' Sets the honest ceiling and the matching strategy for the whole entity track.
kept.implies: to expand coverage beyond 10%, add a name+EIN+ZIP fuzzy-match path (LEAD-grade) for the 75k NPI-less exclusions

verdict.corrected_title: LEIE carries a real NPI on only 10.4% of rows (8,684 rows / 8,503 distinct NPIs of 83,464) and records ZERO reinstatements — a structural coverage hole that caps any NPI-based exclusion detector; and the gap is NOT mainly the pre-2007 NPI era (42,077 of the 74,780 blank-NPI rows are post-2007 exclusions)
verdict.notes:
Re-ran candidate SQL verbatim — reproduces exactly: 83,464 total / 8,684 rows_with_real_npi / 0 reinstated_rows / EXCLDATE 19770701-20260618. The coverage hole, the 100% REINDATE='00000000' (current-exclusions-only file), and the ~10% NPI ceiling all hold. Two corrections:

(1) CASTING/COUNT artifact in headline: candidate says 8,504 distinct NPIs. The real distinct count of valid NPIs is 8,503 (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT NPI) WHERE NPI<>'0000000000' = 8503). The 8,504 came from COUNT(DISTINCT NULLIF(NPI,'')), which still treats '0000000000' as one extra distinct value (8,503 + the all-zero token = 8,504). Off by one. The blank token is literally '0000000000' on all 74,780 blank rows — there are NO ''/NULL rows, so NULLIF(NPI,'') does nothing useful here.

(2) Causal explanation overstated. Candidate attributes the 89.6% blank-NPI to exclusions predating the NPI system (~2007). Era breakdown refutes that as the main driver: of the 74,780 NO_NPI rows, only 32,703 are pre-2007 — 42,077 are POST-2007 exclusions with no NPI. So most blank-NPI rows are modern exclusions where OIG simply never captured an NPI (entities, or individuals never linked to one), not legacy pre-NPI records. The HAS_NPI side is, as claimed, almost entirely modern (8,635 post-2007 vs 49 pre-2007).

Positives that strengthen it: all 8,684 NPIs are clean 10-digit values in the valid 1xxxxxxxxx range, zero bad-length — so the hard-ID join surface is trustworthy (FACT-grade for any LEIE x NPI match). The detector-ceiling and matching-strategy implication (90% needs name/EIN/address fuzzy = LEAD-grade) is sound. Tier stays FACT (it's all hard-ID/structural facts on one table). Downgraded for the off-by-one headline number and the incorrect 'pre-NPI-era' rationale, not for the core claim, which is solid and useful.

kept.title: Sanctioned-vessel AIS detector is 3x undercounting: OpenSanctions finds 6 broadcasting ships vs OFAC's 2 — for zero extra data
kept.sql: WITH os AS (SELECT NAME, REGEXP_SUBSTR(IDENTIFIERS,'IMO([0-9]{7})',1,1,'e') AS imo7 FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_OPENSANCTIONS WHERE SCHEMA='Vessel' AND IDENTIFIERS ILIKE '%IMO%') SELECT o.imo7, o.name AS sanctioned_name, a.vesselname AS ais_name, a.mmsi, COUNT(*) AS pings, MIN(a.basedatetime) first_seen, MAX(a.basedatetime) last_seen FROM os o JOIN LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_NOAA_AIS a ON REGEXP_REPLACE(a.IMO,'^IMO','')=o.imo7 WHERE REGEXP_LIKE(o.imo7,'^[1-9][0-9]{6}$') GROUP BY 1,2,3,4 ORDER BY pings DESC
kept.evidence: OFAC-only normalized join = 2 distinct IMOs broadcasting (9259317 FEDOR, 9379698 ADVANTAGE VIRTUE). OpenSanctions Vessel schema join = 6 IMOs broadcasting on 2024-01-01: 9259317 FEDOR (514 pings), 9266877 SEA VINE/PIERRE (942), 9439383 PS GENOVA/BHILVA (753), 9389095 MINDORO/MINION (497), 9379698 ADVANTAGE VIRTUE/LAFIT (133), 9308833 LOVINA/KUSTO (3). 4 of 6 are NOT in OFAC (EU/UK/Canada/Australia Russia-shadow-fleet listings). All hard-ID (IMO) matches = FACT.
kept.why_matters: The existing OFAC<->AIS detector only sees US-sanctioned ships. OpenSanctions ingests OFAC + EU + UK + Canada + Australia + UN lists (1,905 vessel IMOs vs OFAC's 1,942) and triples the catch with the same AIS data. Wiring OpenSanctions (not OFAC alone) as the sanction side of the bridge is a free 3x.
kept.implies: new detector: sanctioned_vessel_broadcasting should join AIS to INTL_OPENSANCTIONS Vessel schema, not OFAC SDN alone

verdict.corrected_title: On the 2024-01-01 AIS snapshot, OpenSanctions catches 6 broadcasting sanctioned ships vs OFAC's 2 (4 extra from Australia/Switzerland Russia-shadow-fleet lists) — but OpenSanctions is NOT an OFAC superset (only 1,486 of OFAC's 1,942 vessel IMOs overlap)
verdict.notes:
VERIFIED / REPRODUCES EXACTLY: OFAC-only normalized join = 2 broadcasting IMOs (9259317 FEDOR/EDOR 514 pings, 9379698 ADVANTAGE VIRTUE/LAFIT 133). OpenSanctions Vessel join = 6 IMOs, pings match the candidate to the digit (9266877 942, 9439383 753, 9259317 514, 9389095 497, 9379698 133, 9308833 3). All on the hard IMO ID -> FACT grade is justified. Adversarial checks all passed: OS IDENTIFIERS are clean single 'IMOnnnnnnn' tokens (no multi-IMO regex ambiguity); each AIS IMO maps to exactly 1 vesselname + 1 MMSI (no join blowup, no IMO reuse); the 4 extra IMOs return 0 rows from OFAC (genuinely absent). OFAC vessel-IMO universe = 1,942, OS = 1,905 (1,909 rows / 1,905 distinct) — both confirmed.

WHY DOWNGRADED (not rejected): the 6-vs-2 is real and publishable, but two claims in the 'why it matters' are overstated. (1) The framing 'OpenSanctions ingests OFAC + EU/UK/Canada/Australia... 1,905 vs 1,942' implies OS supersets OFAC. IT DOES NOT — IMO overlap is only 1,486; OFAC has 456 vessel IMOs OS lacks, OS has 419 OFAC lacks. So 'wiring OS as the sanction side is a free 3x' is true for THIS AIS day but is not a structural guarantee, and you'd LOSE 456 OFAC-only IMOs if you swapped OFAC out entirely — the right move is UNION OFAC+OS, not replace. (2) The named authorities are imprecise: the 4 extra broadcasters actually trace to Australia (Autonomous Vessels) and Switzerland (Ordinance of 4 March 2022 re: Ukraine/Russia), not the 'EU/UK/Canada/Australia' stated. (3) DATA PROPERTY to disclose: FED_NOAA_AIS is a single-day snapshot — 100% of 7.296M pings are 2024-01-01 — so 'broadcasting' means 'seen on one calendar day in US waters'; the 6-vs-2 count is snapshot-specific, not a steady-state rate. Cosmetic: the SDN_NAME/regex-extracted names are truncated ('EDOR','LAFIT','MINION') in both OFAC and the OS regex output — display artifact only, IMOs are correct.

kept.title: Every matched sanctioned vessel is broadcasting AIS under a DIFFERENT name than its sanctions listing — and 4 of 6 sit anchored off the Texas coast
kept.sql: SELECT REGEXP_REPLACE(IMO,'^IMO','') imo7, VESSELNAME, ROUND(TRY_CAST(LAT AS DOUBLE),3) lat, ROUND(TRY_CAST(LON AS DOUBLE),3) lon, BASEDATETIME, ROUND(TRY_CAST(SOG AS DOUBLE),1) speed_kn FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_NOAA_AIS WHERE REGEXP_REPLACE(IMO,'^IMO','') IN ('9266877','9439383','9259317','9389095','9379698','9308833') QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY IMO ORDER BY BASEDATETIME DESC)=1 ORDER BY imo7
kept.evidence: Last-known positions 2024-01-01: 9259317 FEDOR lat25.67/lon-79.51 underway 14.8kn (OFAC sanctioned name 'FEDOR'); 9266877 SEA VINE lat32.53/lon-119.28 0.1kn (sanctioned name 'PIERRE'); 9308833 LOVINA lat25.08/lon-89.21 13.0kn ('KUSTO'); 9379698 ADVANTAGE VIRTUE lat28.75/lon-94.71 0.3kn ('LAFIT'); 9389095 MINDORO lat28.47/lon-94.64 0.7kn ('MINION'); 9439383 PS GENOVA lat30.01/lon-94.01 0.0kn ('BHILVA'). 4 of 6 sit near-stationary at lon~-94, lat 28-30 = Galveston/Beaumont TX approaches; IMO is the hard ID so name mismatch is FACT-grade.
kept.why_matters: Vessel renamed-vs-listed is the canonical shadow-fleet evasion tell, and here it is in clean hard-ID form: IMO ties the broadcast hull to the sanctioned hull regardless of the name on the transponder. Four sanctioned ships idling in the same Texas Gulf approach on one day is a tight, publishable, map-ready geographic cluster.
kept.implies: new detector: name_mismatch flag (AIS VESSELNAME != sanctions NAME on identical IMO) as a renaming/evasion signal across all maritime sources

verdict.corrected_title: All 6 sanctioned vessels broadcasting in AIS carry an IMO that hard-matches OpenSanctions; 5 of 6 broadcast a name with zero overlap to the sanctions listing (the 6th, EDOR/FEDOR, is a source truncation, not a rename) — 3 sit near-stationary at the Galveston/Beaumont TX approaches on the single day of AIS coverage
verdict.notes:
CORE HOLDS (FACT): SQL reproduces exactly — all 6 IMOs, positions, speeds verified. All 6 IMOs genuinely match SCHEMA='Vessel' entities in OpenSanctions on the hard IMO ID (real OFAC/EU/CA/AU/UK sanctions datasets). A full join confirms exactly 6 matches total, so 'every matched vessel' is accurate. Each IMO broadcasts exactly ONE consistent name across all pings (514/942/3/133/497/753), and none appear in the sanctions NAME or ALIASES fields — name mismatch is robust, not a last-ping fluke. IMO is a hard ID so the renamed-vs-listed tell is FACT-grade for 5 vessels.

THREE PROBLEMS that force a downgrade:
(1) NAME ERROR in the receipts. Candidate says 9259317's 'OFAC sanctioned name [is] FEDOR'. The OpenSanctions NAME is literally 'EDOR' (ID NK-atJ5u67bb3wi3aNBhL5W2s, IDENTIFIERS '538011855;IMO9259317;V7A3569'). AIS broadcasts 'FEDOR'. EDOR vs FEDOR = a one-letter leading truncation in the OFAC source, almost certainly the SAME name, NOT an evasion rename. So it's 5/6 clean renames, not 6/6, and a stated 'fact' is wrong.

(2) GEOGRAPHIC/TEMPORAL CLUSTER IS A SNAPSHOT ARTIFACT. The ENTIRE FED_NOAA_AIS table is ONE day: MIN/MAX BASEDATETIME = 2024-01-01 00:00 to 23:59, 1 distinct day, 7.30M rows. 'Four sanctioned ships idling in the same Texas Gulf approach on one day' sounds like a temporal coincidence but 2024-01-01 is the ONLY day present — every 'last-known position' is just the last ping on the sole available day. The temporal framing is hollow.

(3) CLUSTER OVERSTATED. Only 3 vessels sit near lon -94 / lat 28-30 (9379698 28.75/-94.71 sog0.3 status0; 9389095 28.47/-94.64 sog0.7 status3; 9439383 30.01/-94.01 sog0.0 status5) — Galveston/Beaumont approaches, plausibly anchored. The other 3 are NOT there: 9259317 off Florida (-79.5) underway 14.8kn; 9266877 in the Pacific off CA (-119.3); 9308833 mid-Gulf (-89.2) underway 13.0kn. So 'anchored' is an inference and '4 off the Texas coast' should be 3.

COVERAGE NOTE: 2.25M of 7.30M AIS rows (31%) have null/blank IMO and can't be matched — limits coverage but creates no false positives. All non-null IMOs are uniformly 'IMO'-prefixed, so the join missed nothing matchable. Bottom line: real, hard-ID, publishable shadow-fleet rename signal for 5 vessels, but the 'beautiful Texas cluster on one day' narrative and the FEDOR name are overstated/wrong.

kept.title: $29.8B of FY2025 federal contracts went to foreign-OWNED firms - and $24.9B of it is hidden behind US recipient addresses
kept.sql: SELECT CASE WHEN RECIPIENT_COUNTRY_NAME='UNITED STATES' THEN 'US_addr' ELSE 'foreign_addr' END loc, COUNT(*) n, ROUND(SUM(TRY_CAST(FEDERAL_ACTION_OBLIGATION AS DOUBLE))/1e9,2) tot_b FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_USASPENDING_CONTRACTS WHERE FOREIGN_OWNED='t' GROUP BY 1;
kept.evidence: FOREIGN_OWNED='t': 125,878 transactions, $29.81B total. Split: US-address recipients 94,559 rows / $24.87B; non-US-address recipients 31,319 rows / $4.95B. Top foreign-owned recipients include ROLLS-ROYCE CORPORATION ($1.35B, US addr), KONGSBERG DEFENCE & AEROSPACE AS (Norway, $847M), STONEX FINANCIAL LTD (UK, $1.32B), BAE SYSTEMS LAND & ARMAMENTS ($744M).
kept.why_matters: The foreign-ownership of US defense/contract dollars is mostly invisible if you filter by recipient country (84% of the foreign-owned money sits on a US address - foreign-owned US subsidiaries). The FOREIGN_OWNED flag is the only reliable lens, and it surfaces ~$30B in FY2025. Clean accountability angle on who really owns the contractors.
kept.implies: new detector: FOREIGN_OWNED='t' vs RECIPIENT_COUNTRY_NAME mismatch to surface foreign-owned US subsidiaries; cross to OFAC/sanctions by parent name (LEAD)

verdict.corrected_title: ~$24B of foreign-OWNED federal contract obligations (FY2025) are ~85% hidden behind US recipient addresses; the $29.8B 'FY2025' figure actually mixes FY2024+FY2025
verdict.notes:
Re-ran the candidate SQL verbatim: numbers reproduce EXACTLY (foreign_addr 31,319 / $4.95B; US_addr 94,559 / $24.87B; total 125,878 / $29.81B). FOREIGN_OWNED has only two values f/t; 125,878 't' rows. Top recipients all confirm (Rolls-Royce $1.347B US-addr, StoneX $1.315B UK, Kongsberg $847M Norway, BAE Land & Armaments $744M US-addr). The FOREIGN_OWNED flag is a sensible derived signal — it lines up with foreign PARENT companies (RECIPIENT_PARENT_NAME shows Stellantis N.V., Asahi Kasei, Martin-Baker, etc.), so the 'foreign-owned US subsidiary' framing is legitimate, not a name-match. No casting artifact: all 125,878 rows TRY_CAST cleanly (cast_ok=125,878), min length 4.

THE DEFECT (why DOWNGRADED, not CONFIRMED): the title says 'FY2025' but the table spans TWO fiscal years by ACTION_DATE. Splitting by LEFT(ACTION_DATE,4): 2025 = 100,638 rows / $23.98B; 2024 = 25,240 rows / $5.84B. So the headline $29.81B and $24.87B-US-address figures are FY2024+FY2025 COMBINED, inflating the FY2025 number by ~24%. FY2025-only: total $23.98B, of which US-addr $20.45B (85.3%), foreign-addr $3.53B. The 'hidden behind US addresses' insight is genuine and holds in BOTH years (FY24 US-addr 4.42/5.84 = 76%; FY25 85%), but the dollar magnitudes and year label are overstated.

Second caveat (minor): the SUM is NET of de-obligations — 9,392 rows carry negative obligations totaling -$2.1B, so this is net contract value, not gross awarded. Worth stating but doesn't undermine the pattern.

Tier stays FACT on the mechanism (pure aggregate over a derived ownership flag + native country column; no fuzzy/cross-ID matching), but any published number must be year-scoped and labeled net. Investigator value is real: filtering by RECIPIENT_COUNTRY_NAME alone misses ~85% of foreign-owned dollars.

kept.title: 2024 set the all-time record for conflict EVENTS (28,816) -- a 11x rise since 1989 -- while deaths peaked in 2022
kept.sql: SELECT TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT) yr, COUNT(*) events, SUM(TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT)) best_deaths FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_UCDP_GED GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
kept.evidence: Event count climbs 2,624 (1989) -> 7,885 (2010) -> 28,816 (2024), the single highest event-year in the dataset (2023 = 26,486 is second). Deaths, however, peaked at 309,636 in 2022 (Ethiopia-TPLF 307,568 over 2020-22 + Russia-Ukraine 242,373 over 2014-24) and fell to 161,237 (2023) and 160,900 (2024). So recent years are the most ACTIVE on record by event volume, but NOT the deadliest -- 1994 (824k) and 2022 (310k) still lead. Deaths-per-event has fallen sharply (94k deaths/2,624 events in 1990 vs 161k/28,816 in 2024).
kept.why_matters: Corrects a tempting headline: 'conflict is at a record high' is true for the number of violent events but false for deaths. The divergence -- more, smaller, more-precisely-geolocated events -- partly reflects better real-time data collection (drones, social media, OSINT) as much as more war, which is itself a finding about the data-generating process.

verdict.corrected_title: 2024 set the all-time record for conflict EVENTS (28,816) — an ~11x rise since 1989 — while deaths peaked in 2022 (309,636) and the deadliest single year remains 1994 (824,156)
verdict.notes:
Re-ran the exact SQL; every headline number reproduces to the digit. 385,918 total rows, all with castable YEAR and BEST (0 nulls dropped — no silent TRY_CAST artifact). Year range is a clean 1989-2024 with no partial trailing 2025 year that could understate a tail, so the "2024 is the record" claim isn't a truncation artifact. 28,816/2,624 = 10.98x (rounds to 11x), correct. Deaths peak 2022=309,636, then 2023=161,237, 2024=160,900 — all confirmed. 1994=824,156 (Rwanda) is the all-time deadliest — confirmed. Deaths-per-event divergence is real and stark (1989: 25.8, 2010: 4.3, 2022: 14.9, 2024: 5.6).

This is a pure within-source GROUP BY on one table — no cross-ID, no name match, no fuzzy country join. FACT tier is correct.

ONE DEFECT (does not change verdict): the evidence text says "94k deaths/2,624 events in 1990" — that's garbled. 2,624 events is 1989 (67,711 deaths); 1990 has 3,202 events / 95,744 deaths. The candidate conflated the two years in one supporting sentence. The deaths-per-event point survives regardless. Also the "Ethiopia-TPLF 307,568 (2020-22)" and "Russia-Ukraine 242,373 (2014-24)" figures are multi-year CONFLICT totals (correctly labeled), used as color to explain the single-year 2022 peak; I verified 2022 itself is genuinely Ethiopia (162,453) + Russia-Ukraine (91,502) = 254k of 310k, so the causal story holds.

Caveat for any downstream use: BEST is UCDP's per-event best-estimate of fatalities; the post-2012 event-count surge partly reflects expanded UCDP coverage/methodology (the data-generating-process point the candidate raises is fair, not yet quantified here). Title nudged only to surface that 1994 — not 2022 — is the deadliest year, which the original buried.

kept.title: Eastern Kentucky is America's overdose+jail double-extreme epicenter: 20 of the 50 counties in the top decile of BOTH metrics, despite KY being ~4% of US counties
kept.sql: WITH od AS (SELECT GEOID, NAME, ST_NAME, TRY_CAST(RATE AS DOUBLE) od_rate FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CDC_INJURY_VIOLENCE_COUNTY WHERE PERIOD='2023' AND INTENT='Drug_OD' AND TRY_CAST(RATE AS DOUBLE) >= 0), inc AS (SELECT COUNTY_FIPS, TRY_CAST(TOTAL_JAIL_POP_RATE AS DOUBLE) jail_rate FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_VERA_INCARCERATION_TRENDS WHERE YEAR='2019' AND TRY_CAST(TOTAL_JAIL_POP_RATE AS DOUBLE) > 0), j AS (SELECT od.ST_NAME, od.od_rate, inc.jail_rate FROM od JOIN inc ON od.GEOID=inc.COUNTY_FIPS) SELECT ST_NAME, COUNT(*) double_extreme FROM j WHERE od_rate >= 48.3 AND jail_rate >= 991 GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC;
kept.evidence: Thresholds = national p90 (overdose 48.3/100k for 2023; jail 991/100k for 2019), measured over the 2,801 counties present in both sources. 50 counties clear both p90s. By state: Kentucky 20, Tennessee 9, Virginia 8, Louisiana 5, Georgia 2, Colorado 2, Indiana 1, Florida 1. Named KY double-extremes include Breathitt (OD 146.7, jail 1611), Powell (112.4 / 2911), Owsley (99.8 / 1611), Clay (81.4 / 2342). FIPS join is exact: all 3,049 Vera-2019 counties match a CDC GEOID.
kept.why_matters: Central Appalachia is where the opioid death crisis and aggressive county jailing physically co-locate. 17.8% of overdose-extreme counties are also jail-extreme vs ~10% under independence (1.8x enrichment) — the two crises cluster geographically, and Kentucky is the single sharpest node. A ready-made map story.
kept.implies: new detector: county_double_burden (overdose p90 AND incarceration p90) -- a reusable cross-axis extremes flag

verdict.corrected_title: Eastern Kentucky is the overdose+jail double-extreme epicenter: 20 of 50 counties in the top decile of BOTH metrics despite KY being 3.9% of the 2,801-county universe (~10x over-representation) — robust to year alignment
verdict.notes:
Reproduced exactly. The candidate SQL returns 50 double-extreme counties (KY 20, TN 9, VA 8, LA 5, CO 2, GA 2, IN 1, OK 1, NC 1, FL 1 = 50; candidate text named IN/FL but silently dropped OK and NC, cosmetic). Thresholds verified as TRUE p90 over the joined set: od_p90=48.3, jail_p90=990.69 (rounds to 991). od_extreme=281, jail_extreme=280, both=50 -> 50/281=17.8% vs ~10% independence = 1.78x enrichment, as claimed. KY = 109/2801 = 3.9% of joined counties yet 40% of double-extremes (~10x concentration). All named KY counties verified (Breathitt 146.7/1611, Powell 112.4/2911, Owsley 99.8/1611, Clay 81.4/2342).

JOIN IS FACT-GRADE: both sources use exact 5-digit county FIPS (GEOID = COUNTY_FIPS); 0 of 3,049 Vera-2019 counties fail to match a CDC GEOID. No name/ISO/fuzzy matching involved -- STEEL geographic join. So FACT tier stands.

ADVERSARIAL CHECKS THAT FAILED TO BREAK IT:
(1) Year mismatch (OD=2023, jail=2019) is NOT the driver. 2019 is Vera's most complete year (3,049 counties; coverage collapses to 1,633 by 2024), so the choice is defensible. When BOTH metrics are aligned to 2019, KY still dominates (15 of ~33 double-extremes, still ~45%), Virginia/TN/Appalachia follow. Pattern robust.
(2) Casting: TRY_CAST handled cleanly; RATE and TOTAL_JAIL_POP_RATE are well-formed numerics; no text-sort or blank-as-zero artifact.

THE ONE REAL CAVEAT (kept it from flawless, didn't refute it): 31 of the 50 double-extreme counties rest on FEWER THAN 20 overdose deaths -- 13 are binned/suppressed in COUNT_SUP ('1-9','10-50'), 18 numeric <20. Tiny Appalachian populations make individual per-county OD rates statistically volatile (e.g. Owsley 99.8/100k off a 1-9 death count). This does NOT undermine the cluster (which is robust and matches established Appalachian opioid epidemiology), but individual named-county rates should be published WITH the small-count caveat, not as precise figures. Recommend the map story aggregate the cluster rather than rank micro-counties by exact rate.

kept.title: The two Open Payments tables are NOT duplicates: main=2024 (15.39M rows), _2023=2023 (14.7M), ZERO RECORD_ID overlap
kept.sql: SELECT (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT RECORD_ID) FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS) AS main_ids, (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT RECORD_ID) FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS_2023) AS y2023_ids, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM (SELECT RECORD_ID FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS INTERSECT SELECT RECORD_ID FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS_2023)) AS shared_ids; -- plus: SELECT PROGRAM_YEAR, COUNT(*) FROM each table GROUP BY 1
kept.evidence: FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS is 100% PROGRAM_YEAR=2024 (15,385,047 rows). FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS_2023 is 100% PROGRAM_YEAR=2023 (14,700,786 rows). RECORD_ID is fully unique within each table (15,385,047 and 14,700,786 distinct), and the INTERSECT of RECORD_IDs across the two tables = 0. They are consecutive program years, not a dup/superset.
kept.why_matters: Resolves the duplication risk for anyone unioning these for a multi-year trend. They can be stacked safely with no de-dup needed. Naming is misleading though: the 'main' table is silently 2024-only, so any query assuming it holds all years is wrong. A staging model should rename to program-year-keyed tables.

verdict.corrected_title: Open Payments tables are consecutive program years, not dups: main=2024 (15.39M), _2023=2023 (14.7M), RECORD_ID globally unique with ZERO cross-year overlap (verified text + numeric)
verdict.notes:
Fully reproduced. Exact numbers all hold: main 15,385,047 rows = 15,385,047 distinct RECORD_IDs, 100% PROGRAM_YEAR=2024; _2023 14,700,786 rows = 14,700,786 distinct, 100% PROGRAM_YEAR=2023; INTERSECT=0. RECORD_ID has 0 nulls/blanks in both, so the distinct counts aren't inflated by missing-value collapse.

Adversarial checks that FAILED to break it (i.e. strengthen the claim): (1) Text vs numeric artifact ruled out — re-ran INTERSECT on TRY_CAST(RECORD_ID AS BIGINT) and still got 0, so it's not leading-zeros/whitespace masking matches. (2) The zero-overlap is NOT a trivial consequence of disjoint ID ranges: 2023 IDs span 965,827,691–1,154,967,612 and 2024 IDs span 1,063,488,454–1,155,056,528 — the numeric bands overlap heavily, yet there are zero exact collisions. So RECORD_ID is a genuine global unique key across years, which is a stronger result than 'each year got its own number block.' (3) PROGRAM_YEAR is a clean TEXT column read directly (single value per table), no casting needed.

Tier stays FACT: this is a structural data-quality fact (de-dup/stackability + silent year scope), not an entity/wrongdoing claim, so libel tiering doesn't apply. The 'main table is silently 2024-only' trap is the most valuable part for downstream — anyone unioning or assuming 'all years' in FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS will be wrong. Recommendation to rename to program-year-keyed staging is sound. Surprise capped at 6 because uniqueness of a sequential key is partly expected; importance 7 because the silent-year-scope trap is real and the multi-year stacking guidance is actionable.

kept.title: Saudi Arabia is the #1 foreign principal lobbying the US via FARA; 'Bosnia' is a mirage -- it's almost entirely Republika Srpska
kept.sql: SELECT FOREIGN_PRINCIPAL_COUNTRY, COUNT(*) n, COUNT(DISTINCT FOREIGN_PRINCIPAL_NAME) principals FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_FARA_BULK WHERE NULLIF(FOREIGN_PRINCIPAL_COUNTRY,'') IS NOT NULL GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC LIMIT 30
kept.evidence: Top FARA foreign-principal countries by filing count: Saudi Arabia 2,597 (162 principals), China 2,081 (150), Japan 1,825 (518), South Korea 1,796 (188), Bosnia & Herzegovina 1,629 (only 24 principals), Russia 1,620 (219). Bosnia's per-principal intensity (68 filings/principal) is 4x Saudi's. Drilling Bosnia: Republika Srpska + variants ('Republika Srpska' 1,357, 'Government of Republika Srpska' 222, others) account for ~1,600 of 1,629 -- one sub-national Serb-entity government drove nearly all of it.
kept.why_matters: Two payoffs. (1) A ranked map of which foreign governments buy the most US influence -- Saudi/China/Gulf states lead. (2) A country-aggregation trap: classifying by FOREIGN_PRINCIPAL_COUNTRY hides that 'Bosnia's' lobbying is a single separatist-leaning entity (Republika Srpska), not the national government -- a real attribution distinction for any FARA analysis, and a warning to never trust the country field without checking principal concentration.

verdict.corrected_title: FARA country ranking is dominated by 'Informational Materials' (propaganda dissemination logs), not lobbying engagements -- by actual foreign-principal registrations Japan/Canada/Korea lead, Saudi is #7; the real, durable finding is the country-aggregation trap: 'Bosnia' is ~99% Republika Srpska
verdict.notes:
SQL reproduces EXACTLY: Saudi 2,597/162, China 2,081/150, Japan 1,825/518, Korea 1,796/188, Bosnia 1,629/24, Russia 1,620/219. Two things break the headline:

(1) COUNTING ARTIFACT. The FOREIGN_PRINCIPAL_COUNTRY field is populated on only 2 of 11 document types: Exhibit AB (16,655 rows = foreign-principal registration exhibits) and Informational Materials (20,805 rows = copies of distributed propaganda/content). 184,440 of 221,900 rows (83%) have NULL country, so the entire ranking is built on a 17% slice. The candidate's 'filing count' metric is dominated by Informational Materials, which is dissemination volume, NOT lobbying engagements. Saudi: 2,214 of its 2,597 rows (85%) are Informational Materials; only 383 are Exhibit AB. China: 1,841 of 2,081 are Informational Materials. So 'Saudi/China buy the most US influence' is really 'Saudi/China agents file the most propaganda pieces.'

(2) RANKING FLIPS by metric. By Exhibit AB only (true foreign-principal registrations): Japan 1,668, Canada 780, Korea 551, Mexico 539, GB 465, Taiwan 388, SAUDI 383 (#7), Russia 349... CHINA 240 (#15). By distinct foreign principals: Japan 518, Canada 429, Mexico 311, GB 302, Russia 219... SAUDI 162 (#9). So the #1 claim is metric-dependent and the chosen metric (raw filing count over a NULL-heavy field) is the one that inflates Saudi/China.

(3) Bosnia/Republika Srpska half HOLDS and reproduces: Srpska variants = 1,615 of 1,629 Bosnia rows (99%), of which 1,567 are Informational Materials, 47 Exhibit AB. The country-aggregation-trap insight (never trust FOREIGN_PRINCIPAL_COUNTRY without checking principal concentration) is the genuinely valuable, defensible takeaway -- but even here 'one separatist entity drove 1,629 filings' is really 'one PR agent logged 1,567 dissemination pieces,' so the 'intensity 4x Saudi' framing is a content-volume artifact, not lobbying intensity.

Tier: LEAD not FACT. No hard ID involved (FARA has no key; this is name/country text). The country field is self-reported and sparsely populated. The headline 'Saudi is #1' is overstated -- it is an artifact of which document types carry the country tag. The Republika Srpska de-aggregation point is the part worth publishing, with the dissemination-vs-registration caveat stated.

kept.title: The table NAMED 'open_payments' is secretly just 2024; the '_2023' table is 2023 -- no overlap, but the base name is a trap
kept.sql: SELECT PROGRAM_YEAR, COUNT(*) AS n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1; -- returns ONLY 2024=15,385,047. And: SELECT PROGRAM_YEAR, COUNT(*) AS n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS_2023 GROUP BY 1 -- returns ONLY 2023=14,700,786. Date check: SELECT MIN(LEFT(DATE_OF_PAYMENT,10)), MAX(LEFT(DATE_OF_PAYMENT,10)) FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS TABLESAMPLE (50000 ROWS) -> 01/01/2024 .. 12/31/2024
kept.evidence: FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS is 100% PROGRAM_YEAR=2024 (15,385,047 rows, all payment dates inside calendar 2024). FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS_2023 is 100% PROGRAM_YEAR=2023 (14,700,786 rows). The two tables are disjoint by year -- so UNION'ing them is correct (30.1M total, no double-count), but the GOOD news is also the trap: the unsuffixed table is a single year, not an all-years master.
kept.why_matters: Resolves the 'same year twice?' worry: there is NO duplicate-year double counting. But the naming is a landmine -- an analyst who assumes FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS is the full historical table will silently report only 2024 figures and undercount industry-to-physician payments by ~half. Either rename to FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS_2024 or build a unioned view.

verdict.corrected_title: FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS is 2024-only (15.4M rows); the '_2023' suffixed table is 2023-only (14.7M) -- record-level disjoint by RECORD_ID, so the unsuffixed name is a half-the-data trap
verdict.notes:
Every claimed number reproduced exactly. FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS: PROGRAM_YEAR=2024 for all 15,385,047 rows (MIN=MAX=2024, nonblank=total, zero nulls/blanks). FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS_2023: PROGRAM_YEAR=2023 for all 14,700,786 rows (MIN=MAX=2023, zero blanks). No hidden years lurking behind blanks -- I checked MIN/MAX/nonblank-count, not just GROUP BY.

I went BEYOND the candidate's evidence and strengthened it: the two tables share ZERO RECORD_ID values (CMS's hard unique payment key) -- `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM ... a JOIN ... b ON a.RECORD_ID=b.RECORD_ID` = 0. That proves disjointness at the record level, not merely by the PROGRAM_YEAR label, so a UNION is genuinely safe (no double-count). Both tables are identical 94-column schemas, so they are UNION-compatible.

Two minor corrections to the candidate's wording, neither fatal:
(1) The date-range claim 'all payment dates inside calendar 2024' is slightly false. 73 rows carry the garbage date string '11/30/0002' (a source data-entry typo). They are still PROGRAM_YEAR=2024, so the finding's substance is unaffected -- but the candidate's '01/01/2024..12/31/2024' was derived from a 50k TABLESAMPLE that happened to miss the 73 bad rows. PROGRAM_YEAR is the reliable partition key, not DATE_OF_PAYMENT.
(2) The candidate's own SQL string says 'all payment dates inside calendar 2024' which overstates cleanliness; corrected.

Tier stays FACT: this is a structural/metadata fact about our own tables (column profiling on a hard CMS key), no entity-level libel exposure, no fuzzy match. Surprise is moderate (5) -- experienced analysts know CMS Open Payments ships per-program-year files, but the naming asymmetry in OUR catalog (one suffixed, one not) is a real silent-undercount landmine. Importance 8: anyone treating FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS as the historical master silently reports only 51% of the 2023-2024 payment volume. Fix = rename to _2024 or build a unioned view. The detector ecosystem note ('338 leads sit on LEIE x Open Payments') is directly exposed to this -- if those detectors hit only the unsuffixed table, they are missing all of 2023.

kept.title: 49% of all data sources (49 of 101 landed/modeled) sit in UNCLASSIFIED -- and 6 whole domains have ZERO landed data
kept.sql: SELECT DOMAIN_PRIMARY, COUNT(*) AS n_sources, COUNT(CASE WHEN LIFECYCLE IN ('landed','modeled') THEN 1 END) AS n_with_data FROM LIBRARY_META.REGISTRY.CATALOG GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY n_with_data DESC; -- and SELECT COUNT(*) FROM LIBRARY_META.REGISTRY.CATALOG WHERE DOMAIN_PRIMARY='UNCLASSIFIED' AND LIFECYCLE IN ('landed','modeled') = 49
kept.evidence: Catalog lifecycle totals: 76 landed + 25 modeled = 101 with data (matches inventory). Of those, 49 are DOMAIN_PRIMARY='UNCLASSIFIED' -- the single largest data-bearing 'domain', bigger than health_medicine (14) or justice_courts (7). Six domains have 0 landed/modeled sources, with max lifecycle only 'sampled' or 'scouted': education (53 sources, all sampled/scouted), crime_security (25), geo_demographics (22), procurement_intl (27), elections_voting (5), immigration_migration (2). A blank/NULL domain holds another 76 sources, 0 with data.
kept.why_matters: Two distinct holes.
