(1) REAL data holes: education, geo/demographics, intl procurement, elections, and immigration have nothing landed despite 100+ scouted sources -- whole investigative angles are unbuildable today. (2) A CLASSIFICATION hole that masks coverage: the domain map is unreliable because half the actual data is untagged.

verdict.corrected_title: 49% of data-bearing sources (49 of 101 landed/modeled) sit in UNCLASSIFIED with no secondary domain or themes either -- and 6 whole domains have zero landed data
verdict.notes:
Every number reproduces exactly. Lifecycle totals: 76 landed + 25 modeled = 101 data-bearing (matches the inventory). Of those, exactly 49 are DOMAIN_PRIMARY='UNCLASSIFIED' -- the largest data-bearing bucket, ahead of health_medicine (14) and justice_courts (7). The 6 zero-data domains confirmed: education(53), procurement_intl(27), crime_security(25), geo_demographics(22), elections_voting(5), immigration_migration(2) = 134 sources, all sampled/scouted, 0 landed/modeled. A separate NULL DOMAIN_PRIMARY bucket holds 76 sources, 0 with data (genuinely NULL, not empty-string).

This is a pure COUNT on a hard catalog text field -- no TRY_CAST, no fuzzy/name/cross-ID matching, no unit/definitional confusion. FACT grade is correct.

I tried to break the 'classification hole' framing two ways and it got STRONGER, not weaker: (1) I listed the 49 UNCLASSIFIED sources -- they are obviously classifiable, not genuinely uncategorizable: fed_cdc_suicide_rates/fed_cdc_overdose/fed_cms_open_payments -> health, xc_owid_co2/temp_anomaly/fossil_share -> energy_environment, intl_ucdp_ged/owid_terrorism_deaths/nti_dprk_missile -> conflict, xc_owid_gini/cpi/freedomhouse -> economy/governance. (2) I checked whether UNCLASSIFIED might be a deliberate bucket with fallback tags -- it is not: all 49 have ZERO DOMAIN_SECONDARY and ZERO THEMES. Completely untagged. So the domain map genuinely masks ~half the actual data.

Two minor wording defects, neither affecting substance: (a) the candidate's inline SQL ends with invalid pseudo-syntax 'LIFECYCLE IN (...) = 49' -- clearly shorthand for the count, which is exactly 49; (b) 'max lifecycle only sampled or scouted' is imprecise -- alphabetical MAX() is misleading, and procurement_intl/crime_security/geo_demographics/elections_voting/immigration_migration actually top out at 'scouted' (no 'sampled') -- but the real claim (0 landed/modeled) holds for all 6. Corrected title adds the 'no secondary/themes either' detail since it materially strengthens the classification-hole point. Caveat for the investigator: 'UNCLASSIFIED' vs NULL are two different untagged states (49 vs 76 sources) -- the 49 is the data-bearing one that matters.

kept.title: World Bank IDS carries real forward-looking debt projections out to 2032 -- ~12k populated values in future-year columns
kept.sql: SELECT SERIES_NAME, COUNT(*) n, COUNT(NULLIF(TRIM(C_2030),'')) y2030 FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_WB_IDS WHERE NULLIF(TRIM(C_2030),'') IS NOT NULL GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY n DESC LIMIT 10
kept.evidence: Year columns run C_1970..C_2032. Filled counts: C_2023=50,641, C_2025=12,855, C_2026=12,760, C_2030=11,967, C_2032=11,566 (of 62,983 rows). The future-year fill is concentrated in debt-service SCHEDULES: 'Debt service on external debt, total (TDS)', 'Principal repayments on external debt, long-term (AMT)', 'GG official creditors (INT)' -- each with 134 rows (=134 countries/aggregates) fully populated through 2030/2032.
kept.why_matters: These aren't data-entry errors -- they're contractual debt-repayment obligations already scheduled. It means the table answers 'how much does country X already owe in 2030?' This is a genuinely useful, non-obvious forward-looking asset; a naive 'drop future-year columns as garbage' cleaning step would destroy it.
kept.implies: new detector: countries with the steepest scheduled debt-service spikes in 2025-2032 (repayment-cliff finder)

verdict.corrected_title: World Bank IDS carries real forward-looking debt-service SCHEDULES out to 2032 -- ~12k numerically-valid future-year values, concentrated in TDS/AMT/INT debt-flow series (134 countries+aggregates each)
verdict.notes:
Every check reproduces and the finding holds at FACT grade (single-table structural property -- no cross-source/entity match, so no libel/tier risk).

RECEIPTS:
- Fill counts reproduce exactly: SELECT COUNT(*), COUNT(NULLIF(TRIM(C_2023..2032),'')) -> total 62,983; C_2023=50,641, C_2025=12,855, C_2026=12,760, C_2030=11,967, C_2032=11,566.
- NOT a casting artifact: of 11,967 nonblank C_2030 values, all 11,967 are TRY_CAST AS DOUBLE-castable (numeric_castable == nonblank). Real debt magnitudes, not text.
- NOT fill-forward: values vary year-over-year and even cross over (Pakistan TDS 2025=23.1B -> 2027=16.3B -> 2030=8.2B -> 2032=6.7B; China rises at 2030=203B on a bullet repayment). A flat forward-fill would not produce this.
- 134 = 134 DISTINCT COUNTRY_NAME (COUNT DISTINCT confirmed) for the TDS series at C_2030. Caveat: many of these are regional/income AGGREGATES (e.g. 'Low & middle income', 'IDA total'), not all sovereigns -- candidate's '134 countries/aggregates' wording is accurate; do not say '134 countries.'
- Series scope is clean: 145 distinct series carry C_2032 fill, every one a debt-flow schedule type (TDS=debt service, AMT=principal, INT=interest, DIS=disbursements; PPG/PRVG/PRVG/OPS categories). Smaller-fill series (7-18 rows) are narrower instrument types (bonds, commercial banks), still all schedules -- no stock/non-schedule series leaked in.
- Column range confirmed via INFORMATION_SCHEMA: earliest C_1970, latest C_2032.

Only refinement vs candidate: title sharpened to flag these are debt-SERVICE schedules (flow), and to note the 134 includes aggregates. Substance unchanged. The 'naive drop-future-columns would destroy a useful asset' framing is fair.

kept.title: fed_cfpb_complaints is a 2-week snapshot (May 15-29 2026) capped at 500 rows -- and TIMELY is 100% 'Yes'
kept.sql: SELECT MIN(DATE_RECEIVED) min_d, MAX(DATE_RECEIVED) max_d, COUNT(*) n, COUNT(DISTINCT LEFT(DATE_RECEIVED,7)) n_months FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CFPB_COMPLAINTS; -- and -- SELECT TIMELY, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CFPB_COMPLAINTS GROUP BY 1
kept.evidence: 500 rows, ALL in month 2026-05 (DATE_RECEIVED spans 2026-05-15 to 2026-05-29 -- a 14-day window). TIMELY column = 'Yes' for all 500 rows (single-valued -- the entire timeliness signal is dead). ZIP_CODE is partially masked: 20/500 rows contain 'XX' (CFPB privacy redaction), 266 distinct zips.
kept.why_matters: CFPB complaints is a multi-year, millions-of-rows feed in reality; here it is a top-500 most-recent pull from a single fortnight. It cannot support any trend, year-over-year, or company-history analysis, and the TIMELY flag is unusable. Treat as a sample stub, not a panel.
kept.implies: new source to load: backfill full CFPB complaint history if trend analysis is wanted

verdict.corrected_title: fed_cfpb_complaints is two 250-row 'most-recent' API pulls (May 15 + May 29 2026), 500 rows total, TIMELY 100% 'Yes' -- and DATE_RECEIVED/DATE_SENT are INGESTION timestamps, not real complaint dates
verdict.notes:
Re-ran both SQLs: 500 rows, 1 month, TIMELY='Yes' x500 -- all reproduce exactly. ZIP claim reproduces (20 rows contain 'XX', 266 distinct zips; +2 blank). 

BUT the candidate misread the date column, which is the most interesting part. DATE_RECEIVED is NOT a 14-day spread of complaint-received dates. It is TWO discrete batches of EXACTLY 250 rows each, on EXACTLY TWO days (2026-05-15 and 2026-05-29), each compressed into an ~11-minute window (23:48:44 -> 23:59:55 on the 15th; 23:48:01 -> 23:59:56 on the 29th). Timestamps are second-by-second sequential. These are ingest/scrape timestamps, not complaint dates. Proof: DATE_SENT_TO_COMPANY for the first row is 23:55:46 -- 7 minutes after its 'received' time, same night (real CFPB routing takes days); and MAX(DATE_SENT_TO_COMPANY)=2026-06-15, AFTER the last 'received' batch. The loader overwrote the real date fields with processing timestamps, destroying the actual complaint dates. So 'a 14-day window' is doubly wrong: not a window (two point-in-time pulls), and not complaint dates at all.

COMPLAINT_IDs (500 distinct, 22264841 -> 22725288, a ~460k ID range over the two weeks) confirm these are two genuine 'top-250 most-recent' snapshots taken a fortnight apart -- so the structural read (500-row cap, sample stub, no trend/YoY/history possible) is CORRECT and arguably stronger than stated: even the per-pull date axis is unusable. TIMELY single-valued is confirmed.

Tier stays FACT (single-table structural facts on a hard ID/row count, no entity claim). Downgraded only because the headline's date interpretation is materially wrong and would mislead an analyst into thinking DATE_RECEIVED is queryable as a complaint date.

kept.title: Excluded oncologist Eduardo Miranda (NPI 1285673012) is billing ACTIVELY across THREE independent CMS datasets ~8 years after a conviction-based exclusion
kept.sql: WITH leie AS (SELECT NPI, EXCLDATE, EXCLTYPE FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_HHS_OIG_LEIE WHERE LEN(NPI)=10 AND NPI<>'0000000000' AND TRY_CAST(LEFT(EXCLDATE,4) AS INT) <= 2022) SELECT p.NPI, l.EXCLDATE, l.EXCLTYPE, p.PRSCRBR_STATE_ABRVTN, p.PRSCRBR_TYPE, p.TOT_CLMS, p.TOT_DRUG_CST, p.TOT_BENES, p.OPIOID_TOT_CLMS FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_PART_D_PRESCRIBERS p JOIN leie l ON p.NPI=l.NPI; -- Medicare: WITH leie AS (...) SELECT m.NPI, m.TOT_BENES, m.TOT_SRVCS, m.TOT_MDCR_PYMT_AMT FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_MEDICARE_PROVIDER m JOIN leie l ON m.NPI=l.NPI; -- Affiliation: SELECT DISTINCT f.NPI, f.FACILITY_TYPE, f.CCN FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_FACILITY_AFFILIATION f JOIN leie l ON f.NPI=l.NPI
kept.evidence: NPI 1285673012 (MIRANDA, EDUARDO, TX Hematology-Oncology) excluded 2015-06-18 under EXCLTYPE 1128a1 (mandatory exclusion for conviction of a program-related crime). Appears in: (1) Part D prescribers with TOT_DRUG_CST=$7,495,480.61, TOT_CLMS=1,382, TOT_BENES=279, OPIOID_TOT_CLMS=97; (2) Medicare physician/supplier with TOT_MDCR_PYMT_AMT=$1,229,994.33, TOT_SRVCS=62,599, TOT_BENES=312; (3) Facility Affiliation listing 4 Texas hospital CCNs (450643, 450092, 451387, 451390). Combined visible billing ~$8.7M. Hard-ID NPI match in all three = FACT.
kept.why_matters: This is exactly what the OIG exclusion list is supposed to prevent: an excluded provider still drawing Medicare/Part D dollars. The exclusion (2015) predates the data snapshot (CY2023) by 8 years, so this is not date-reconciliation noise. One excluded NPI corroborated across three independent CMS active datasets is publishable-grade. Worth a human pull of the actual claim years, but the cross-source agreement is striking.
kept.implies: new detector: excluded_but_actively_billing (LEIE NPI x Part D / Medicare provider / facility affiliation, date-gated on EXCLDATE < data year)

verdict.corrected_title: Excluded oncologist Eduardo Miranda (NPI 1285673012) appears in three CY2023 CMS billing datasets ~8 years post-exclusion — but his exact LEIE row carries an OIG WAIVER (WVRSTATE=TX), one of only 3 waivered records in the file; needs human review, not a clean 'exclusion failed' fact
verdict.notes:
REPRODUCED EXACTLY across all four tables. LEIE: NPI 1285673012, MIRANDA EDUARDO, EXCLDATE 20150618, EXCLTYPE 1128a1, TX, DOB 19580730. Part D: TOT_CLMS=1382, TOT_DRUG_CST=7,495,480.61, TOT_BENES=279, OPIOID_TOT_CLMS=97. Medicare: TOT_BENES=312, TOT_SRVCS=62,599, TOT_MDCR_PYMT_AMT=1,229,994.33. Facility Affiliation: CCNs 450092/450643/451387/451390. NPPES confirms same Type-1 individual, REPLACEMENT_NPI blank, not deactivated. Address Laredo TX consistent everywhere. Hard-ID NPI match = identity is genuinely FACT-grade, and he is the ONLY excluded NPI (EXCLDATE<=2022) appearing in Part D.

WHY DOWNGRADED — three problems the candidate ignored:
(1) WAIVER OMITTED. LEIE columns include REINDATE/WAIVERDATE/WVRSTATE. Miranda's row: REINDATE=00000000 (not reinstated) BUT WAIVERDATE=20150618, WVRSTATE=TX — a waiver dated the SAME DAY as the exclusion. He is 1 of only 3 NPI-bearing LEIE rows out of 8,684 that carry any waiver (the other two: FRASER, WAHIB). An OIG/state waiver (typically 1128(c)(3)(B), sole-community-provider) is a documented administrative carve-out. It does NOT clearly make Medicare/Part D billing proper (waivers are Medicaid- and state-specific), so a lead remains — but the headline 'still drawing Medicare dollars, exactly what the list should prevent' is materially incomplete when the same record is flagged with a waiver the finding never mentions.
(2) $8.7M IS A BAD SUM. TOT_MDCR_PYMT_AMT ($1.23M) = actual Medicare payment to him; TOT_DRUG_CST ($7.5M) = total drug cost (paid by Part D plans/patients, not money paid TO the provider, and a different unit). Adding them into one '$8.7M he drew' figure double-counts/conflates non-additive dollars and overstates flows to the provider.
(3) NO YEAR COLUMN in either CMS file — 'CY2023' is inferred from source naming, not provable from the row (it is the correct latest annual vintage, so minor).

NET: real, reproducible, single-standout cross-source hard-ID match — worth a human pull. But it is a LEAD for human review (the waiver flag demands it), not a clean publishable 'exclusion failed' FACT, and the dollar headline must be corrected.

kept.title: Rwanda 1994 alone = 772,463 deaths in one country-year, 94% of the deadliest year on record
kept.sql: SELECT TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT) yr, COUNT(*) events, SUM(TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT)) best_deaths FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_UCDP_GED GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1;  -- then:  SELECT COUNTRY, COUNT(*) events, SUM(TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT)) deaths FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_UCDP_GED WHERE TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT)=1994 GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 3 DESC LIMIT 8
kept.evidence: 1994 is the single deadliest year in the 1989-2024 GED: 824,156 best-estimate deaths (next worst is 2022 at 309,636). Rwanda contributes 772,463 of that 824k (93.7%) across 2,119 events; the dyad 'Government of Rwanda - Civilians' alone totals 794,321 deaths 1990-2024 -- the single most lethal dyad in the entire 385,918-event dataset.
kept.why_matters: The genocide dominates the global conflict-death record so heavily that any uncorrected year-over-year analysis must treat 1994 as a structural outlier, not a trend point. It is also the cleanest demonstration in the Library that one-sided violence against civilians (type 3) is the deadliest category per event.

verdict.corrected_title: Rwanda 1994 = 772,463 deaths in one country-year, 93.7% of the deadliest year on record (824,156) and ~3x the next-worst year
verdict.notes:
PRIMARY CLAIM HOLDS AS FACT — every figure reproduces exactly. Re-ran the year aggregation: 1994=824,156 best-deaths (deadliest year, next is 2022 at 309,636, a 2.66x gap). 1994 by country: Rwanda=772,463 across 2,119 events = 93.7% of the year (Afghanistan #2 at only 9,056). Dyad 'Government of Rwanda - Civilians' = 794,321 deaths / 2,425 events = the single most lethal dyad in the 385,918-event dataset (#2 is Govt of Ethiopia-TPLF at 307,568). Data integrity is clean: 385,918 total rows, ZERO TRY_CAST nulls on BEST or YEAR, so no silent cast loss and no blanks-as-zero artifact. Single-source intra-dataset aggregation on UCDP's own hard-coded COUNTRY/YEAR/BEST columns -> legitimately FACT-grade, no cross-ID/name-fuzzy match involved. The candidate's '2,119 events' refers to Rwanda-1994 specifically (correct), not the 7,569 total events in 1994 -- minor wording ambiguity, not an error.

WHY DOWNGRADED: the SECONDARY claim is REFUTED and must be struck. The candidate asserts 'type 3 one-sided violence is the deadliest category per event' citing 20.0 deaths/event for type 3 vs 8.7 (type 1) and 7.1 (type 2). That is ENTIRELY a Rwanda-1994 artifact. Excluding just that one country-year, type-3 avg collapses from 20.0 to 7.3 deaths/event and type 1 (state-based, 8.7) becomes the deadliest per event. Rwanda 1994 alone supplies 770,581 of the 1,193,015 type-3 deaths in the whole 35-year dataset (65%). The claim is circular: it uses the outlier to 'prove' a general category property that vanishes once the outlier is removed. Headline is FACT; the violence-type generalization is not. Also a framing caveat for any downstream user: this is exactly why the candidate's own 'why it matters' (treat 1994 as a structural outlier in YoY analysis) is correct -- but that same logic invalidates the per-category-per-event claim it pairs it with.

SQL (all re-run, numbers verified): year ranking -> SELECT TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT) yr, COUNT(*), SUM(TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT)) FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_UCDP_GED GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 3 DESC; country-in-1994 and dyad-overall as given in candidate (both reproduce); integrity -> SELECT COUNT(*), SUM(CASE WHEN TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT) IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) FROM ...INTL_UCDP_GED (385918 / 0); type test -> SELECT TYPE_OF_VIOLENCE, AVG(TRY_CAST(BEST AS DOUBLE)) FROM ...INTL_UCDP_GED [WHERE NOT (COUNTRY='Rwanda' AND TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT)=1994)] GROUP BY 1 (type3: 20.0 with Rwanda, 7.3 without).

kept.title: 92 events carry death tolls that are exact multiples of 1,000 -- 540,000 deaths -- and they cluster in 1994
kept.sql: SELECT CASE WHEN TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT)=0 THEN 'zero' WHEN TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT)%1000=0 THEN 'mult_1000' WHEN TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT)%500=0 THEN 'mult_500' WHEN TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT)%100=0 THEN 'mult_100' WHEN TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT)%10=0 THEN 'mult_10' ELSE 'other' END bucket, COUNT(*) events, SUM(TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT)) deaths FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_UCDP_GED WHERE TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT) IS NOT NULL GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 3 DESC;  -- then:  SELECT TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT) best, COUNT(*) n, MIN(TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT)) yr0, MAX(TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT)) yr1 FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_UCDP_GED WHERE TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT)%1000=0 AND TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT)>=1000 GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1 DESC
kept.evidence: Only 92 of 355,897 non-null events have BEST as an exact multiple of 1,000, but they sum to 540,000 deaths (13.6% of all 3.96M deaths). The round values are 40000 (x3, all 1994), 37000 (1994), 25000 (1994), 10000 (x4, all 1994). Of the 18 distinct round-thousand magnitudes, 15 first appear in 1994. By contrast 344,890 events have 'other' (non-round) tolls. This is the fingerprint of coder estimation: during the genocide, exact body counts were impossible so analysts entered round magnitudes.
kept.why_matters: A handful of round-number mega-estimates carry an outsized share of the total death count. Any precision claim about cumulative conflict mortality should flag that the largest single contributions are deliberately rounded estimates, not counted bodies.

verdict.corrected_title: 92 UCDP events carry death tolls that are exact multiples of 1,000 (540,000 deaths = 13.6% of the 3.96M total); the round-number estimates concentrate in older, uncountable conflicts -- above all 1994 Rwanda
verdict.notes:
Core numbers reproduce EXACTLY: bucket query returns mult_1000 = 92 events / 540,000 deaths; total non-null deaths = 3,957,143 so 540,000/3,957,143 = 13.65%. No casting artifact: COUNT(BEST)=385,918 and TRY_CAST nulls = 0, so every row casts cleanly (blanks/zeros are a separate 'zero' bucket of 30,021, not contaminating the round bucket). The 18 distinct round magnitudes reproduce verbatim.

THREE overclaims warrant the downgrade (real signal, sloppy specifics):
1. "Of 18 round magnitudes, 15 first appear in 1994" is FALSE -- measured first-appearance years: 1994=12, 1989=3, 1990=1, 1992=1, 1999=1. So 12 of 18, not 15.
2. Prose says "355,897 non-null events" but actual non-null = 385,918 (the 'other' bucket alone is 344,890). Minor number-slip but it's in the headline evidence.
3. "They cluster in 1994" is only loosely true. 1994 has the most round events (56) but its roundness RATE among large (>=1000) events is 49.6%, comparable to 1992 (50%), 1999 (60%), 1998 (43%). The real contrast is OLD conflicts (1989-1999, 25-60% round) vs RECENT (2021-2024, 0% round). And the 1994 cluster is almost entirely ONE conflict: CONFLICT_NEW_ID=472 (Rwanda genocide). So it's "old/uncountable conflicts, dominated by Rwanda," not a clean 1994 fingerprint.

Mechanism is well-supported: pulling LOW/HIGH on the big round events shows wide uncertainty bands (BEST=40000 with LOW=0/HIGH=50000; BEST=11000 with LOW=10400/HIGH=22378) -- these are rounded estimates, not counted bodies. Tier stays FACT (single table, hard numeric facts, no cross-source or name match involved). Verdict DOWNGRADED purely for the overstated "15 of 18" and "cluster in 1994" wording; corrected title reflects what the data supports.

kept.title: Civilians are 37% of all conflict deaths; one-sided violence is 15% of events but 30% of deaths
kept.sql: SELECT TYPE_OF_VIOLENCE, COUNT(*) events, SUM(TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT)) best_deaths, ROUND(100.0*COUNT(*)/SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER(),1) pct_events, ROUND(100.0*SUM(TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT))/SUM(SUM(TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT))) OVER(),1) pct_deaths FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_UCDP_GED GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1;  -- then:  SELECT SUM(TRY_CAST(DEATHS_CIVILIANS AS INT)) civ, SUM(TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT)) all_d, ROUND(100.0*SUM(TRY_CAST(DEATHS_CIVILIANS AS INT))/SUM(TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT)),1) pct FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_UCDP_GED
kept.evidence: Type-of-violence breakdown of 385,918 events / 3,957,143 deaths: state-based (1) = 70.3% of events, 60.0% of deaths; non-state (2) = 14.2% events, 9.9% deaths; one-sided/civilian-targeting (3) = 15.4% events but 30.1% of deaths. One-sided violence is ~2x as lethal per event as its event share. Separately, the explicit DEATHS_CIVILIANS column sums to 1,474,095 = 37.3% of all 3.96M deaths.
kept.why_matters: A clean, publishable structural fact: more than a third of everyone killed in armed conflict since 1989 was a civilian, and the deadliest-per-event category is deliberate violence against civilians rather than two-sided battle. This is the kind of crisp ratio that anchors a data-viz story.

verdict.corrected_title: Civilians are at least 37% of all conflict deaths (1989-2024); one-sided/civilian-targeting violence is 15% of events but 30% of deaths and ~2.3x as lethal per event
verdict.notes:
Every number reproduces exactly. Stress-tested hard and it survives:

CASTING: No artifact. All 385,918 rows cast cleanly for both BEST and DEATHS_CIVILIANS (385,918 castable, 0 blank for each). No silent TRY_CAST nulling. TYPE_OF_VIOLENCE is a clean 3-value categorical (1/2/3), so no text-sort-vs-numeric issue.

INTERNAL CONSISTENCY: Decisive proof DEATHS_CIVILIANS is a real native UCDP component, not derived/guessed: DEATHS_A(953,472)+DEATHS_B(866,610)+DEATHS_CIVILIANS(1,474,095)+DEATHS_UNKNOWN(662,966) = 3,957,143 = SUM(BEST) exactly. The four death components partition BEST with zero gap/overlap. This is the standard UCDP GED schema.

SCOPE: Year range 1989-2024 (36 distinct years) matches the 'since 1989' claim. Single source, single definition -> no cross-source/cross-ID join, so the FACT tier is legitimate (not a fuzzy/name match passed off as fact).

ONE-SIDED FRAMING: Deaths-per-event = type1 8.7, type2 7.1, type3 20.0. One-sided is ~2.3x state-based and ~2.8x non-state per event, supporting 'deadliest per event is deliberate violence against civilians.' Of type-3's 1,193,015 deaths, 1,181,150 (99%) are civilians, as expected by definition.

CAVEAT (already hedged in claim): 37.3% is a LOWER BOUND. DEATHS_UNKNOWN is 662,966 (~17% of all deaths) and is uncategorized -- it surely contains additional civilians not counted in DEATHS_CIVILIANS. So 'more than a third' is correct and conservative; the claim does NOT overstate. I added 'at least' to the corrected title to make the lower-bound nature explicit for a publishing context.

Surprise is moderate (5): that civilians are ~37% and one-sided violence is disproportionately lethal is a known feature of UCDP data to conflict researchers, but it's a crisp, clean, publishable structural fact for a general audience. Importance 7: solid anchor stat, fully receipted, libel-safe (no named entities).

kept.title: Menominee County WI (Menominee Indian Reservation) is the single worst-overdose county in America: 284/100k, ~14x the national median of 20.5
kept.sql: SELECT NAME, ST_NAME, ROUND(TRY_CAST(RATE AS DOUBLE),1) od_rate FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CDC_INJURY_VIOLENCE_COUNTY WHERE PERIOD='2023' AND INTENT='Drug_OD' AND TRY_CAST(RATE AS DOUBLE) >= 0 ORDER BY od_rate DESC LIMIT 12;
kept.evidence: 2023 Drug_OD national median 20.5, p90 47.5, max 284.0. Top counties: Menominee WI 284.0, McDowell WV 212.2, Estill KY 193.7, Wyoming WV 162.7, Mercer WV 148.1, Baltimore city MD 147.2, Breathitt KY 146.7. Menominee is ~13.9x the median and the only county above 215. (Caution: Menominee's Vera 'White' jail rate 3,706 vs 'Native' 298 is a tiny-White-denominator artifact -- county is ~87% Native -- so do NOT read it as White over-jailing.)
kept.why_matters: A lone Native American reservation county sits atop the national overdose table, far above the Appalachian cluster everyone writes about. A sharp single-axis outlier and an under-told angle.
kept.implies: new source to load: tribal-area / IHS overdose + AIAN demographic data to contextualize reservation counties

verdict.corrected_title: In CDC's 2023 county overdose panel, Menominee County WI (Menominee Reservation) shows the highest drug-OD rate at 284/100k -- but it rests on just 12 deaths, a tiny-denominator small-N estimate, not a robust #1
verdict.notes:
SQL reproduces EXACTLY: Menominee WI 284.0, McDowell WV 212.2, Estill KY 193.7, etc. -- numbers all hold. No duplicate GEOIDs (3143 distinct = 3143 rows), so no double-counting. The candidate correctly disambiguated the trap here: there are TWO Menominee Counties in this table -- Michigan (GEOID 26109, rate 11.6, COUNT_SUP 1-9) and Wisconsin (GEOID 55078, rate 284.0). The ORDER BY DESC correctly surfaces the WI one. Good catch, not an artifact.

WHY DOWNGRADED, two issues:

(1) FACTUAL ERROR in claimed evidence. Claimed national median 20.5; actual MEDIAN() and PERCENTILE_CONT(0.5) both return 20.0. Claimed p90 47.5; actual 46.9. So the multiple is 284/20.0 = 14.2x, not the claimed ~13.9x. Minor, but the headline cites a wrong median.

(2) TIER too high -- this is a small-N fragility problem the candidate flagged for OTHERS but not itself. Menominee WI's 284/100k is built on COUNT_SUP=12 (only 12 deaths). Compare: McDowell WV 37, Mercer WV 86, Baltimore city 832. Twelve deaths over ~4,200 pop means one or two deaths swings the rate by ~24/100k each -- this is exactly the tiny-denominator artifact class the candidate (rightly) warned about for the Vera 'White' jail rate, but then ignored for its own headline. The rate is a CDC NCHS county estimate, inherently unstable at N=12. Note RATE_M_CI is blank for Menominee -- though blank CI affects 1710 of 3143 rows so it's not Menominee-specific, the 12-death base is.

VERDICT: Real value, reproducible, genuinely under-told angle (a Native reservation county topping the table vs the Appalachian cluster). But 'single worst-overdose county in America' stated as FACT overclaims a fragile N=12 estimate. Belongs as a LEAD worth a human/journalist look, not a publishable FACT. Fix the median (20.0) and lead with the small-N caveat.

kept.title: Vera's incarceration panel has a hard coverage cliff: prison data flatlines completely after 2019, and 2020+ keeps only jail
kept.sql: SELECT YEAR, COUNT(NULLIF(TOTAL_JAIL_POP_RATE,'')) jail_rate, COUNT(NULLIF(TOTAL_PRISON_POP_RATE,'')) prison_rate, COUNT(NULLIF(TOTAL_INCARCERATION_RATE,'')) incarc_rate, COUNT(NULLIF(BLACK_PRISON_POP_RATE,'')) blk_prison FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_VERA_INCARCERATION_TRENDS WHERE YEAR >= '2018' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1 DESC;
kept.evidence: 2019 is the last fully-populated year: 2,902 jail-rate + 2,593 prison-rate + 2,479 total-incarceration-rate counties, plus full race breakdowns (Black/White prison rates ~1,400/1,900 counties). 2020 onward: prison_rate=0, total_incarceration_rate=0, blk_prison=0 for ALL years -- only jail survives (2020 jail_rate=2,228; 2024=1,440). Anything joining Vera prison or total-incarceration MUST use YEAR='2019' or it silently returns nothing.
kept.why_matters: Any cross-axis incarceration analysis past 2019 is jail-only by construction. Stating a 2023 'incarceration rate' from this table would be wrong -- the field is empty. Critical guardrail for every future query against this source.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: Vera incarceration panel: prison and total-incarceration rates flatline to 0 after 2019; only JAIL survives 2020-2024, and 2025-2026 are empty placeholder rows with no data at all
verdict.notes: Re-ran the exact candidate SQL via the helper -- numbers reproduce to the digit: 2019 jail=2902 / prison=2593 / incarc=2479 / blk_prison=1388; 2020+ prison=incarc=blk_prison=0. Checked the obvious casting trap and it is NOT an artifact: a CASE breakdown of TOTAL_PRISON_POP_RATE for YEAR='2023' shows all 2090 rows are genuinely BLANK empty-string (not '0', not NULL), so COUNT(NULLIF(col,'')) correctly returns 0 -- the zeros are real absence, not blanks-counted-as-zero or TRY_CAST silently nulling. Confirmed it is a true cliff, not a chronically sparse field: prison_rate is populated robustly through history (1970:146, 1990:2324, 2000:2340, 2010:2526, 2019:2593) then drops to exactly 0 starting 2020. Full range MIN(YEAR)=1970, MAX=2026, 57 distinct years, 128,507 rows; 90,497 non-blank prison values overall, all pre-2020. One overstatement in the candidate: it frames 2020+ as 'only jail survives,' but that is true only for 2020-2024 (jail 2020:2228, 2024:1440). 2025 (957 rows) and 2026 (597 rows) are empty placeholder rows where jail=0 and everything else=0 -- past 2024 even jail is gone. So the practical guardrail is sharper than stated: prison/total-incarceration require YEAR='2019' (or earlier); jail is usable through 2024 only; 2025-2026 carry no usable data. Tier stays FACT -- this is a structural coverage property of a single table, no entity claims, no cross-source/ID matching involved. Importance is high as a query guardrail (any post-2019 'incarceration rate' from this table is empty by construction); surprise is moderate because coverage tails truncating at the data vendor's last release year is a common and not especially shocking pattern.

kept.title: Two data-quality landmines in the county panel: CDC injury uses -999 as a suppression sentinel (822 Drug_OD rows), and Vera's 2025/2026 rows are population-only stubs with zero rates
kept.sql: SELECT COUNT(*) total, SUM(CASE WHEN TRY_CAST(RATE AS DOUBLE)=-999 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) neg999 FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CDC_INJURY_VIOLENCE_COUNTY WHERE INTENT='Drug_OD' AND TRY_CAST(RATE AS DOUBLE) IS NOT NULL; -- and: SELECT YEAR, COUNT(*) n_rows, COUNT(NULLIF(TOTAL_JAIL_POP,'')) has_jail_pop, COUNT(NULLIF(TOTAL_JAIL_POP_RATE,'')) has_jail_rate FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_VERA_INCARCERATION_TRENDS WHERE YEAR IN ('2025','2026') GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1;
kept.evidence: FED_CDC_INJURY_VIOLENCE_COUNTY: Drug_OD has 822 rows where RATE = -999 (suppressed/unreliable sentinel, smallest real positive value is 0.0). Filtering on 'TRY_CAST(RATE AS DOUBLE) IS NOT NULL' alone leaks these as -999 and deflates medians/p90 -- must use RATE >= 0. Vera 2025: 957 rows, 860 have jail population counts but 0 have computed jail rates; 2026: 597 rows, 597 jail-pop, 0 rates. These years cannot be used for rate-based extremes.
kept.why_matters: Both traps silently corrupt distribution math: -999 drags rate medians negative; the future-year stubs look like data but yield NULL rates. Anyone profiling this panel needs RATE >= 0 and YEAR <= 2024 to get honest numbers -- this protects every downstream finding.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: CDC injury panel uses RATE=-999 as a suppression sentinel (822 Drug_OD rows, panel-wide 6549; all tagged COUNT_SUP='1-9'), and Vera's 2025/2026 rows are population-only stubs with zero jail rates — both need filtering, though the leak's impact on summary stats is small
verdict.notes:
Both core numbers reproduce EXACTLY: Drug_OD has 822 rows with RATE=-999 (the only negative value; entire negative bucket is -999.0). Vera 2025=957 rows/860 jail-pop/0 rate, 2026=597/597/0 — reproduced to the row.

STRENGTHENED on one point: every -999 RATE row carries COUNT_SUP='1-9' (suppressed small count), which is definitive proof it's a privacy-suppression sentinel, not garbage. Also the sentinel is PANEL-WIDE, not Drug_OD-only: 6549 rows across all 6 INTENT types (All_Suicide 1713, FA_Suicide 1554, FA_Deaths 1327, Drug_OD 822, All_Homicide 583, FA_Homicide 550). Candidate undersold this by counting only Drug_OD. Vera 2025/2026 are uniquely rate-less — 2020-2024 all have rates (e.g. 2024: 1633/1441/1440).

TWO ERRORS in the candidate (reasons for DOWNGRADE):
(1) FACTUAL: 'smallest real positive value is 0.0' is WRONG. Smallest positive Drug_OD rate is 3.5; there are 3675 GENUINE zeros (real data, not sentinel) sitting separately. So RATE>=0 correctly keeps both the 3675 zeros and 17503 positives while dropping the 822 sentinels — the advice works, but the stated min is wrong.
(2) OVERSTATED MAGNITUDE: claim says -999 'drags rate medians negative' / 'deflates medians/p90'. Actual Drug_OD impact is tiny because suppressed rows are only ~822/22000 (~4%): MEDIAN 16.5 (leaky) vs 17.1 (clean); P90 40.94 vs 41.46. Median never goes negative. Directional point (leak deflates stats, use RATE>=0) is correct but the rhetoric implies a much bigger distortion than the data shows.

SCOPE MISMATCH in downstream advice: 'YEAR <= 2024' applies ONLY to Vera. The CDC table has NO YEAR column (DESCRIBE shows it's keyed on PERIOD/TTM_DATE_RANGE), so that guard doesn't transfer to the CDC trap. The unified prescription conflates two different tables' fixes.

Tier stays FACT (these are intrinsic data properties of named tables, verified by direct aggregation — no entity/name match involved). Surprise modest: -999 sentinels and future-year stubs are common, predictable data-quality patterns. Importance solid: filtering RATE>=0 and excluding rate-less Vera years does protect downstream rate math, just less dramatically than claimed.

kept.title: Largest single transfer-of-value to one physician in 2024: a $91.08M 'Acquisitions' payment to Dr. Charles Goodis from Edge Endo LLC, flagged physician-owned
kept.sql: SELECT RECORD_ID, NPI, COVERED_RECIPIENT_FIRST_NAME, COVERED_RECIPIENT_LAST_NAME, RECIPIENT_STATE, TRY_CAST(TOTAL_AMOUNT_OF_PAYMENT_USDOLLARS AS DOUBLE) AS amt, NATURE_OF_PAYMENT_OR_TRANSFER_OF_VALUE, PHYSICIAN_OWNERSHIP_INDICATOR FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS WHERE SUBMITTING_APPLICABLE_MANUFACTURER_OR_APPLICABLE_GPO_NAME = 'Edge Endo LLC' AND TRY_CAST(TOTAL_AMOUNT_OF_PAYMENT_USDOLLARS AS DOUBLE) > 1000000 ORDER BY amt DESC
kept.evidence: RECORD_ID 1150022143, NPI 1821157041, CHARLES GOODIS, FL. Single payment = $91,082,706.03, nature='Acquisitions', PHYSICIAN_OWNERSHIP_INDICATOR='Yes'. This single record IS essentially all of Edge Endo's reported spend (mfr total $91.14M across only 11 payments). Next-largest single payments: BioNTech 'Royalty/License' on COMIRNATY to a teaching hospital at $88.60M, $53.41M, $28.27M; a $25,000,000.00 round-number 'Acquisitions' payment from Zimmer Biomet to a physician GOYAL.
kept.why_matters: These mega-transfers are real buyouts of physician-owned device/IP companies, not bribes -- but they are the headline outliers a reader expects 'pharma paid a doctor $91M' to surface, and they are FACT-grade (hard NPI). The COMIRNATY royalties to a teaching hospital reflect the mRNA-vaccine patent stream (likely UPenn/BioNTech). Worth a 'biggest checks in Open Payments' story; the $25M round number warrants a glance.

verdict.corrected_title: Largest single payment in CMS Open Payments (2024): a $91.08M 'Acquisitions' transfer to endodontist Dr. Charles Goodis (NPI 1821157041, FL) from Edge Endo LLC, flagged physician-owned — 99.94% of that manufacturer's total reported spend
verdict.notes:
Every claim reproduces against LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS.

RECEIPTS:
1) Core record (RECORD_ID 1150022143): NPI 1821157041, CHARLES GOODIS, FL, amt=91082706.03, nature='Acquisitions', PHYSICIAN_OWNERSHIP_INDICATOR='Yes'. Reproduced exactly. COVERED_RECIPIENT_TYPE='Covered Recipient Physician', specialty='Dental Providers|Dentist|Endodontics'. Edge Endo is an endodontics device company -> physician-owned-company buyout interpretation is coherent, not a bribe.
2) It IS the single largest payment in the WHOLE table: SELECT ... WHERE amt > 20000000 ORDER BY amt DESC returns Goodis at top (91.08M), then BioNTech 88.60/53.41/28.27M, Henry Schein/Medoff 26.75M, Zimmer/Goyal 25.00M. Nothing above it.
3) Manufacturer total: Edge Endo LLC = 11 payments, SUM 91,139,126.87, MAX 91,082,706.03 -> the one record is 99.94% of the manufacturer's spend. Confirmed.
4) NO casting artifact: 0 rows where amount is non-blank but TRY_CAST AS DOUBLE is NULL, so no bigger value is being silently nulled. All amounts numeric.
5) BioNTech detail confirmed: RECORD_ID 1150660574 = COMIRNATY 'Royalty or License' to TEACHING_HOSPITAL 'Hospital Of The Univ Of Penna' (UPenn). Matches the mRNA-vaccine patent-stream claim exactly.
6) Zimmer $25,000,000.00 (RECORD_ID 1155025842) is an exact round-number 'Acquisitions' payment to physician GOYAL. Confirmed.

GOTCHA / minor downgrade-of-wording (not of tier): the title qualifier 'in 2024' is trivially true — the ENTIRE table is PROGRAM_YEAR=2024 (15,385,047 rows, single year). So 'largest in 2024' = 'largest, period' here; the year framing adds nothing and could mislead a reader into thinking other years were compared. Also note the 3 'Acquisitions' >$10M total $142.8M and 9 'Royalty or License' >$10M total $247.0M — these mega-transfers are buyouts/IP streams, correctly characterized as NOT bribes. Tier stays FACT (hard NPI match on the recipient). Corrected title folds in the 99.94% receipt and drops nothing material. Surprise moderate (mega-buyouts showing up in a 'pharma payments' table is somewhat expected once you know Acquisitions/Royalty natures exist); importance moderate — it's the clean headline outlier for a 'biggest checks in Open Payments' story, not a wrongdoing lead.

kept.title: US-Israel UN alignment hit an all-time high of 0.92 in 2024 -- Israel is the single most reliable US ally at the UN, while the US agrees with the average country only 46% of the time
kept.sql: SELECT CCODE2, ROUND(AVG(TRY_CAST(AGREE AS DOUBLE)),3) avg_agree_us, COUNT(*) n_years FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_VOETEN_UNGA_VOTES WHERE CCODE1='2' AND TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT) >= 2010 GROUP BY CCODE2 HAVING n_years >= 10 ORDER BY avg_agree_us DESC LIMIT 12;  -- and: SELECT TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT) yr, ROUND(AVG(TRY_CAST(AGREE AS DOUBLE)),3) FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_VOETEN_UNGA_VOTES WHERE CCODE1='2' AND CCODE2='666' AND TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT) IN (1950,1960,1970,1980,1990,2000,2010,2020,2024) GROUP BY yr ORDER BY yr
kept.evidence: CCODE1=2 (USA, COW code) vs CCODE2=666 (Israel) is the #1 partner since 2010 at mean AGREE=0.881, ahead of 987/Marshall Islands 0.839, 20/Canada 0.785, 200/UK 0.742. US-Israel agreement trajectory: 1950=0.667, 1970=0.659, 1990=0.732, 2000=0.861, 2010=0.881, 2020=0.853, 2024=0.921 (all-time peak). Contrast: US mean agreement with all 199 countries since 2010 is only 0.458. Bottom of the table: CCODE 731 (North Korea) 0.199, 652 (Syria) 0.208, 630 (Iran) 0.240.
kept.why_matters: A clean, publishable signal from 1.82M dyadic agreement scores: the US is increasingly isolated at the UN (agrees with the average member <50% of the time) yet its bond with Israel has tightened to a 78-year high. This is the quantitative backbone of every story about US-Israel UN votes and the famous lopsided ceasefire-resolution counts.

verdict.corrected_title: Israel is the single most reliable US ally at the UN (mean agreement 0.881 since 2010, #1 of 192 countries) while the US agrees with the average member only 46% of the time; US-Israel alignment was 0.92 in 2024 -- its second-highest year ever, just under the 0.934 all-time peak set in 2009
verdict.notes:
Re-ran every query; the numbers reproduce cleanly. No casting artifacts: AGREE has 0 nulls across 2,875 US rows since 2010, range [0.0, 0.958], so TRY_CAST is not silently dropping rows. NVOTES are healthy (~100/yr recently), so AGREE is well-supported, not a thin-sample fluke.

CONFIRMED parts (all FACT-grade -- CCODE1/CCODE2 are Correlates-of-War hard country IDs, AGREE is a precomputed dyadic agreement score native to the Voeten source):
- Israel (666) is #1 US partner since 2010 at mean 0.881, ahead of 987 Marshall Is 0.839, 20 Canada 0.785, 200 UK 0.742. Reproduces exactly.
- US mean agreement with all 192 countries since 2010 = 0.458 (matches claimed 0.458).
- Bottom: 731 N.Korea 0.199, 652 Syria 0.208, 630 Iran 0.240 -- all reproduce.
- Dyad is symmetric: reverse (CCODE1=666,CCODE2=2) for 2024 returns the identical 0.9206. No directionality problem.
- 2024 US-Israel = 0.9206 (rounds to 0.92 / 0.921), real.

THE ERROR (why downgraded): The headline 'all-time high of 0.92 in 2024' and 'a 78-year high' are FALSE. The candidate only sampled decade-boundary years (1950,1960,...,2010,2020,2024) and never looked at the full series. Sorting all 75 years DESC: 2009 = 0.934 is the TRUE peak, clearly above 2024's 0.921 (2013 = 0.904 is third). 2009 has 66 votes -- a legitimate, normal year, not a low-N artifact. So 2024 is the second-highest year on record, not the peak. The 'isolation tightening to a 78-year high' narrative is overstated; the bond was actually slightly tighter 15 years ago.

The core signal (Israel = most reliable ally, US near-isolation at 46%) holds at FACT grade and is publishable. Only the specific 'all-time peak / 78-year high' wording is wrong and must be corrected. Surprise is modest -- US-Israel UN alignment being high is widely known; the clean quantification (#1 of 192, 0.46 average) is the value-add.

kept.title: Edge/VPN/NAS appliance vendors are ransomware on-ramps: 82% of QNAP and 67% of SonicWall known-exploited vulns are ransomware-linked vs 27% for Microsoft and 6% for Cisco
kept.sql: SELECT VENDOR_PROJECT, COUNT(*) total, SUM(CASE WHEN KNOWN_RANSOMWARE_CAMPAIGN_USE='Known' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) ransomware, ROUND(100.0*SUM(CASE WHEN KNOWN_RANSOMWARE_CAMPAIGN_USE='Known' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/COUNT(*),0) pct FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CISA_KEV GROUP BY 1 HAVING total>=10 ORDER BY ransomware DESC LIMIT 12
kept.evidence: Of 1629 KEV entries, 327 are flagged 'Known' ransomware use. Ransomware share by vendor (>=10 vulns): QNAP 9/11=82%, SonicWall 10/15=67%, Atlassian 8/13=62%, Fortinet 13/26=50%, VMware 9/26=35%, Ivanti 12/35=34%, Citrix 7/22=32%, Oracle 13/44=30%, Microsoft 103/377=27%, Apache 7/39=18%, Adobe 10/79=13%, Cisco 6/93=6%. Microsoft has the most ransomware-linked vulns in absolute terms (103) but one of the lowest rates.
kept.why_matters: Despite Microsoft topping every raw count (377 of 1629 KEV entries), the ransomware *rate* points squarely at small perimeter-appliance vendors (NAS boxes, firewalls, VPN gateways). When those get a KEV listing it is far more likely to be actively used in a ransomware campaign -- a concrete patch-prioritization signal that contradicts the naive 'Microsoft is the problem' read.

verdict.corrected_title: Perimeter-appliance vendors (NAS/VPN/firewall) are disproportionate ransomware on-ramps in CISA KEV: aggregated they run 37% ransomware-linked (162 vulns) vs 16% for OS/browser/mainstream software (744) -- and individual vendors top out at QNAP ~75-82% (9/11-12) and SonicWall 67% (10/15), against Microsoft 27% (103/377) and Cisco 6% (6/93)
verdict.notes:
RE-RAN THE SQL VERBATIM: all 12 vendor rows reproduce to the digit (Microsoft 377/103/27, QNAP 11/9/82, SonicWall 15/10/67, Cisco 93/6/6, etc.). Overall KEV = 1629 rows, exactly 327 flagged 'Known' (20% baseline) -- matches claim exactly.

NO CASTING ARTIFACT: KNOWN_RANSOMWARE_CAMPAIGN_USE is a clean binary text field -- only 'Unknown' (1302) and 'Known' (327), zero blanks/nulls. No TRY_CAST, no numeric coercion, blanks-as-zero impossible. The flag is CISA's own curated field, not derived.

ROBUSTNESS CHECK (the important one): the headline thesis survives aggregation, which defeats the small-N-noise objection. Grouping all perimeter/appliance vendors (QNAP, SonicWall, Fortinet, Ivanti, Palo Alto, Citrix, Zyxel, D-Link; n=162) = 60 ransomware = 37%, vs OS/browser/mainstream (Apple, Google, Android, Mozilla, Microsoft, Adobe, Cisco; n=744) = 120 = 16%. A clean 2.3x gap not dependent on any single vendor. Apple (93) and Google (72) are literally 0% ransomware-linked; Android 0%, Mozilla 8% -- the bottom of the ranking is genuinely all browser/OS/mobile, the top genuinely all edge appliances. Not cherry-picked; that is the real shape of the full n>=10 ranking.

CAVEATS (why wording, not verdict, needs a tweak): (1) The exact 'QNAP 82%' depends on excluding a separate spelling 'QNAP Systems' (1 row, product=Helpdesk, Unknown). Merge the two spellings and QNAP is 9/12 = 75%. Still extreme, but 82% is fragile to vendor-name normalization. (2) Individual top-rate vendors are tiny-N: QNAP n=11, Atlassian n=13, SonicWall n=15 -- wide confidence intervals; these rates should always be shown WITH their denominator, never as a bare percentage. The aggregate 37% vs 16% is the publishable number.

TIER: This is a structural property of CVE/vendor counts in a public CISA dataset -- not an entity accusation about a named person/company doing wrong -- so libel tiering doesn't gate it; FACT-grade is appropriate. Surprise moderate: 'patch your firewalls' is somewhat known to security folks, but the inversion (Microsoft tops raw counts yet is below-average on rate) is a genuinely non-obvious, clean signal. CONFIRMED.

kept.title: Per-vintage data quirk in CISA KEV: ransomware-linked share of newly catalogued vulns FELL from 24% (2021) to 10% (2025) -- a coverage/labeling artifact, not a real decline in ransomware
kept.sql: SELECT KNOWN_RANSOMWARE_CAMPAIGN_USE, LEFT(DATE_ADDED,4) yr, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CISA_KEV WHERE LEFT(DATE_ADDED,4) IN ('2021','2025') GROUP BY 1,2 ORDER BY 2,1
kept.evidence: KEV vulns added in 2021: 76 'Known' ransomware / 235 'Unknown' = 24% ransomware-flagged. Vulns added in 2025: 25 'Known' / 220 'Unknown' = 10%. So CISA's own labeling says ransomware exploitation is becoming LESS common among new KEV entries -- yet ransomwarelive victim disclosures went the opposite way (1,816 in 2021 to 7,939 in 2025, same source family).
kept.why_matters: A direct contradiction between two sources in the Library: KEV ransomware-labeling trends down while actual ransomware victim counts surge ~4x over the same window. The likeliest explanation is labeling lag/coverage (CISA flags ransomware use retroactively, so recent vintages are under-counted) -- a data-quality caveat anyone analyzing KEV ransomware trends must respect before drawing conclusions.

verdict.corrected_title: CISA KEV ransomware-flag rate holds ~22-24% for 2021-2024 then drops to ~10-11% for 2025-2026 -- a labeling-lag artifact (CISA flags ransomware use retroactively, so recent vintages are under-flagged), NOT a real decline. It is a single-source KEV caveat, not a cross-source contradiction.
verdict.notes:
All numbers reproduce EXACTLY. Candidate SQL re-ran: 2021 = 76 Known / 235 Unknown (24.4%); 2025 = 25 Known / 220 Unknown (10.2%). ransomwarelive PUBLISHED-year counts also exact: 2021=1816, 2025=7939. No casting artifact -- KNOWN_RANSOMWARE_CAMPAIGN_USE is a clean 2-value enum ('Known' 327 / 'Unknown' 1302), no blanks/nulls, no numeric cast involved.

WHY DOWNGRADED (3 real problems with the framing):

(1) NOT a 2021->2025 gradient. Full year series: 2021=24.4%, 2022=22.5%, 2023=23.0%, 2024=22.6%, then 2025=10.2%, 2026=11.0%. It is FLAT then a CLIFF at 2025, the signature of retroactive labeling lag, not a steady 'becoming less common' trend. 'Fell from 24% to 10%' implies a slope that does not exist.

(2) Labeling-lag hypothesis CONFIRMED with receipts. Controlling for CVE age (SUBSTR(CVE_ID,5,4) vs LEFT(DATE_ADDED,4)): same-year-CVE flag rate 2021-2024 = 34/28/20/25%, drops to 11% in 2025-2026; older-CVE rate 2021-2024 = 18-29%, drops to 8-10% in 2025-2026. In the 2025 cohort, old CVEs (2007-2017, ~14 rows) are 0/14 flagged vs the 2021 cohort where 2017-2019 CVEs carry 25/78 flags. Recent vintages simply have not accumulated their retroactive flags yet. So the artifact claim is right and well-supported.

(3) The 'contradiction between two sources' framing is WRONG / decorative. The two metrics are different units: KEV = share of newly-CATALOGUED VULNERABILITIES flagged ransomware-linked (a labeling rate); ransomwarelive = count of VICTIM-ORG disclosure posts. A falling labeling rate and rising victim counts do not logically contradict -- more incidents can use fewer/older vulns. The candidate itself concedes the cause is lag, so the ransomwarelive comparison adds nothing load-bearing.

(4) FACTUAL ERROR: 'same source family' is false. CISA KEV is US government; ransomware.live is an independent community leak-site tracker. Unrelated publishers. Drop that claim entirely.

Net: the KEV labeling-lag caveat is real, reproducible, and FACT-grade for KEV itself -- useful to anyone trending KEV ransomware data. But retag from 'contradiction' to 'data-quality caveat / single-source quirk', fix the title to reflect flat-then-cliff and drop the cross-source contradiction and 'same source family' language.

kept.title: FDIC 'enforcement' source is 14 rows of FDIC.gov navigation chrome -- zero enforcement actions
kept.sql: SELECT LEFT(RAW_TEXT,90) txt, ORDER_URL FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_FDIC_ENFORCEMENT LIMIT 14;  -- and: SELECT COUNT(*) n, COUNT(DISTINCT ORDER_URL) nurl FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_FDIC_ENFORCEMENT
kept.evidence: 14 rows, only 7 distinct ORDER_URLs (each appears ~twice). Every URL is a generic FDIC.gov section: /about/, /resources/, /news/, /analysis/, /search/help.html, /household-survey. RAW_TEXT samples: 'About|About|The Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation...', 'Learn More About the FDIC', 'Search FDIC.gov|Advanced Search|How Can We Help You'. No enforcement order, no bank name, no date.
kept.why_matters: This is a failed scrape masquerading as a landed source -- it grabbed the homepage nav menu, not enforcement orders. Anyone joining FED_FDIC_ENFORCEMENT into a bank-misconduct analysis gets pure noise. Should be flagged empty/broken in the catalog, not counted among the 76 landed sources.
kept.implies: re-scout / re-onboard fed_fdic_enforcement against the real FDIC enforcement-decisions endpoint; mark current landing as broken in CATALOG lifecycle

verdict.corrected_title: FED_FDIC_ENFORCEMENT is 14 rows of FDIC.gov navigation chrome (7 generic section URLs) -- zero enforcement actions, yet cataloged 'landed' as "FDIC Enforcement Decisions and Orders"
verdict.notes:
Fully reproduced and hardened. Re-ran the counts: 14 rows, 7 distinct ORDER_URLs (confirmed). Schema is only RAW_TEXT + ORDER_URL plus provenance stamps -- no structured enforcement fields at all. The 7 URLs are all generic FDIC.gov sections: /about/, /analysis/, /news/, /resources/, /search/help.html, /household-survey, /doing-business-fdic.

I went beyond the candidate's LEFT-90 preview and searched the FULL RAW_TEXT (lengths up to 732 chars). RLIKE for enforcement|cease and desist|civil money penalty|consent order|prohibition|removal|restitution|respondent|docket = 0 hits. RLIKE for any year (19xx/20xx) or month name = 0 hits -- so there is no date or order content buried deeper. Mid-text segments parse to nav items ('What We Do', 'Leadership', 'Learn More About the FDIC', 'Additional FDIC Resources'). This is conclusively a failed scrape of the homepage nav menu.

Tier stays FACT: this is a data-quality claim about the source's own contents, verified by directly reading every row -- no fuzzy or cross-ID matching, so no libel/tier risk.

Two minor wording fixes to the candidate (do not affect verdict): (1) it said each URL 'appears ~twice' -- distribution is actually uneven (/resources/ 3x, /about/ 3x, /news/ 2x, /analysis/ 2x, /search/help.html 2x, /household-survey 1x, /doing-business-fdic 1x). (2) Candidate's URL list omitted /doing-business-fdic. Corrected title names the cataloged source name to sharpen the 'masquerading' point -- the catalog does list it as lifecycle='landed', name='FDIC Enforcement Decisions and Orders', so the masquerade is literal. Note the separate FED_FDIC_FAILED_BANKS (4115 rows) and FED_FDIC_ENFORCEMENT (14 rows) are distinct; only this enforcement table is broken. Recommendation stands: flag empty/broken, exclude from the 76-landed count.

kept.title: intl_ie_cro is not Irish company data -- it's 3 rows of scraped cookie-consent widget config
kept.sql: SELECT COMPANY_ID, COMPANY_NAME, COMPANY_TYPE, COMPANY_STATUS, REGISTERED_ADDRESS, DATASET_NAME FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_IE_CRO
kept.evidence: All 3 rows: COMPANY_ID='CRO' (constant), COMPANY_NAME in {auth_tkt, ckan, accept-cookie-policy} (these are cookie names, not companies), COMPANY_TYPE='functional', COMPANY_STATUS / REGISTERED_ADDRESS / INCORPORATION_DATE all blank, DATASET_NAME='Cookie & Privacy Policy' on every row. The scraper grabbed the site's cookie-consent table instead of the company register.
kept.why_matters: A source cataloged as Ireland's Companies Registration Office carries ZERO actual company records -- it's a failed scrape that landed the cookie banner. Any join keyed on it is empty; the registry row is misleading. Should be flagged 'empty'/failed-scrape, not counted as a landed source.
kept.implies: new source to load (re-scrape intl_ie_cro from the real CRO endpoint; quarantine the current landing as a scrape failure)

verdict.corrected_title: intl_ie_cro ("Ireland CRO Open Data Portal") holds 0 company records -- its 3 rows are the opendata.cro.ie cookie-consent table (cookie names, not companies)
verdict.notes:
Fully reproduces. 3 rows, COMPANY_ID='CRO' constant (1 distinct). COMPANY_NAME = {auth_tkt, ckan, accept-cookie-policy} -- these are real web cookie identifiers (mod_auth_tkt session cookie, CKAN portal session cookie, consent flag), not company names. COMPANY_TYPE='functional' is the standard cookie classification ("functional cookies"). DATASET_NAME='Cookie & Privacy Policy' on all 3. COMPANY_STATUS, REGISTERED_ADDRESS, INCORPORATION_DATE, FINANCIAL_YEAR_END all 0 non-blank (verified with NULLIF, not a casting artifact -- all cols are TEXT). Registry confirms source is cataloged as "Ireland CRO Open Data Portal" / Companies Registration Office, jurisdiction=international, category=Economy -- so the mismatch (cataloged as Irish company register, actually a scraped cookie banner from opendata.cro.ie) is genuine.

One correction to claimed evidence: COUNTRY is NOT blank -- it = 'Ireland' on all 3 rows. But it's a loader-stamped constant, not a register field, so it doesn't rescue the source; the conclusion (zero real company records, failed/wrong scrape) is unchanged. FACT grade -- this is a structural/data-quality fact, no entity is being accused, no fuzzy match involved. Should be flagged empty/failed-scrape, not counted as a landed source carrying Irish company data. Importance modest: it's a tiny 3-row dead source, but it does pollute the "landed" count and any join keyed on it returns nothing.

kept.title: Open Payments 2023: payments cluster hard on $125.00 AND on $124.99 sitting one cent below it
kept.sql: SELECT TOTAL_AMOUNT_OF_PAYMENT_USDOLLARS amt, COUNT(*) c FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS_2023 GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY c DESC LIMIT 15
kept.evidence: Top single payment amounts (of 14.7M rows): $125.00 x46,060, $25 x35,295, $10 x29,389, $20 x24,038, $15 x20,067, then $124.99 x19,883 -- a penny under the $125 mode and the 6th most common value in the entire dataset. Round-dollar 'meal' amounts ($10/$15/$20/$25/$125) dominate, consistent with templated per-meal valuations, and the $124.99 spike sits suspiciously just below the $125 round number.
kept.why_matters: The $124.99-just-below-$125 cluster is the kind of just-under-a-round-number fingerprint worth a closer look -- could be a specific manufacturer's standard meal valuation engineered to read as 'under $125'. The dominance of identical round amounts also means payment-size is near-categorical, not continuous, which matters for any spend-distribution analysis.
kept.implies: new detector (group $124.99 payments by SUBMITTING_APPLICABLE_MANUFACTURER to see if one payer drives the just-under-$125 spike)

verdict.corrected_title: Open Payments 2023 shows a sharp, asymmetric pile-up of meal payments JUST BELOW $125 (124.99 x19,883 + 124.98/.97/.95...), 6.4x the just-above side -- an industry-wide threshold fingerprint, not one manufacturer
verdict.notes:
RAW NUMBERS REPRODUCE EXACTLY. Re-ran the candidate SQL verbatim: 125.00 x46,060, 25 x35,295, 10 x29,389, 20 x24,038, 15 x20,067, 124.99 x19,883 -- identical. Confirmed NOT a text-form artifact: each amount has exactly RAWFORMS=1 (no "125"/"125.0"/"125.00" splitting), and all 14,700,786 rows are castable to DOUBLE with 0 blanks, so no hidden null bucket distorts the ranking. $124.99 really is the 6th most common value and the single largest .99 value in the dataset (next is 16.99 x9,531 -- 124.99 is 2x that). The $124.99 cluster is 99.6% Food and Beverage (19,808/19,883).

WHY DOWNGRADED (two corrections to the candidate's framing, not its numbers):
(1) The candidate's headline hypothesis -- "could be a SPECIFIC manufacturer's standard meal valuation engineered to read as under $125" -- is REFUTED. The $124.99 rows are spread across many major pharma payers: AbbVie 2,321+812, AstraZeneca 2,044, GSK 969, Horizon 781, Otsuka 779, Amgen 739, Novo Nordisk 664, etc. No single dominant source. It is industry-wide behavior, which makes the "engineered fingerprint" story weaker as stated but the underlying anomaly arguably MORE interesting.
(2) The candidate buried the actual signal. The real story isn't "$124.99 sits one cent below $125" in isolation -- it's a broad ASYMMETRIC threshold effect at $125: bucketing $124.50-124.99 = 49,522 vs $125.01-125.50 = only 7,791 (6.4x more mass just below than just above), plus 124.98 x4,424, 124.97 x2,510, 124.95 x1,405 all stacked below while 125.01 is only 1,511 and 126.00 only 761. That cliff-below-a-round-number is the genuine fingerprint and it points at a regulatory/behavioral $125 boundary in Open Payments meal reporting, not a single-cent quirk.

TIER stays FACT: this is a distributional/aggregate claim on clean numeric data, no entity is named as guilty, no fuzzy/cross-ID match involved -- nothing to libel. Verdict DOWNGRADED because the numbers hold but the candidate's causal framing ("specific manufacturer") is wrong and it understates the real, sharper finding (the asymmetric just-below-$125 mass). An investigator should restate it as the threshold pattern, which is what I put in corrected_title.

kept.title: intl_ember_elec VALUE column is polluted with 8,418 literal 'nan' strings and 244 percentages over 100%
kept.sql: SELECT COUNT(*) total, SUM(CASE WHEN LOWER(VALUE)='nan' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) literal_nan, SUM(CASE WHEN NULLIF(VALUE,'') IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) blank, SUM(CASE WHEN UNIT='%' AND TRY_CAST(VALUE AS DOUBLE) > 100 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) pct_over_100, MAX(CASE WHEN UNIT='%' THEN TRY_CAST(VALUE AS DOUBLE) END) max_pct FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_EMBER_ELEC;
kept.evidence: 369,264 rows. 8,418 (2.3%) have VALUE = literal string 'nan' (not blank, not NULL -- blank count = 0). 244 rows where UNIT='%' have VALUE > 100 (Clean/Fossil/Gas share maxing at 100.01). The 'nan' strings poison naive aggregates: MAX of % rows returns nan because TRY_CAST chokes on the mixed text.
kept.why_matters: A clean-looking 369k-row energy panel silently carries text 'nan' where numbers should be -- any AVG/SUM that forgets to filter NULLIF/lower(value)!='nan' inflates row counts and breaks. The >100% shares (100.01) are mild rounding but technically out of range. Staging must coerce 'nan'->NULL before casting.
kept.implies: new staging rule (NULLIF on literal 'nan'; clamp % to <=100)

verdict.corrected_title: intl_ember_elec VALUE has 8,418 literal 'nan' strings that TRY_CAST converts to IEEE NaN (not NULL); only 10 genuine rows exceed 100% (all 100.01)
verdict.notes:
Core counts reproduce exactly: 369,264 total; 8,418 literal 'nan' (2.3%); 0 blanks; 0 NULLs; all 8,418 are the exact string 'nan'. But TWO claims are wrong/overstated:

1) MECHANISM IS BACKWARDS. The finding says TRY_CAST 'chokes on' 'nan' and that's why MAX returns nan. False. TRY_CAST('nan' AS DOUBLE) SUCCEEDS and returns IEEE NaN (verified: result='nan', IS NULL=False), whereas genuine junk like TRY_CAST('abc' AS DOUBLE) returns NULL. So MAX returns nan because NaN PROPAGATES through the aggregate, not because the cast failed. This matters for the staging fix: a NULLIF/IS-NULL filter on the cast will NOT trap 'nan' — you must explicitly do lower(value)!='nan' BEFORE casting. The candidate's own remediation note happens to be right, but its stated reason is wrong.

2) pct_over_100=244 IS 96% ARTIFACT. Of the 244 rows the SQL flags as %>100, 234 are the literal 'nan' rows (Snowflake evaluates NaN>100 as TRUE). Verified: nan_counted_as_over100=234. Only 10 rows are GENUINELY over 100%, and every one is exactly 100.01 (variables: Renewables, Clean, Fossil, Gas and Other Fossil). So the real out-of-range story is trivial rounding on 10 rows, not 244.

Net: real data-quality issue (literal 'nan' contamination is genuine and the IEEE-NaN-not-NULL gotcha is a legit trap), but the headline >100% count is inflated ~24x by the same nan artifact it's warning about, and the causal explanation is inverted. FACT-grade as data-quality (no entity claims, no joins), but wording must be corrected. Surprise moderate (the TRY_CAST-nan-isn't-NULL behavior is genuinely non-obvious); importance modest (it's a staging-hygiene note on one panel, easily fixed with one filter).

kept.title: SEC EDGAR financials are 99% a single quarter (FY2024 Q3 = 4,645 of 6,491) - it's a snapshot, not a panel
kept.sql: SELECT FY, FP, COUNT(*) n, MIN(PERIOD) minp, MAX(PERIOD) maxp FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_SEC_EDGAR_FINANCIALS GROUP BY 1,2 ORDER BY n DESC LIMIT 15
kept.evidence: Of 6,491 filings, 4,645 (72%) are FY2024 FP=Q3; the next groups are FY2024 FY annual (433) and FY2025 Q1 (318). All meaningful volume sits in FY2024-2025; older fiscal years are single-digit (FY2022 FY=7, FY2020 FY=2). So this 'financials' source is effectively one SEC Financial Statement Data Set quarter (2024Q3), not a multi-year time series.
kept.why_matters: The table reads like company financial history but is a one-quarter snapshot - cross-period trend analysis (revenue growth, YoY) is impossible from this load alone. It is fine as a point-in-time roster of ~4.6k filers for that quarter, but the registry should say SNAPSHOT, not panel, so no one builds a time-trend chart on it. To get history you must land more quarterly SEC datasets.
kept.implies: new source to load: additional SEC Financial Statement Data Set quarters for a real time series

verdict.corrected_title: SEC EDGAR financials is a single quarterly drop: 100% of 6,491 filings were FILED in 2024-Q4 (Oct 1-Dec 31 2024); it's a one-quarter snapshot, not a multi-year panel
verdict.notes:
CORE CLAIM HOLDS, TITLE OVERSTATED. Re-ran the exact SQL: numbers reproduce perfectly (FY2024/FP=Q3 = 4,645; FY2024/FP=FY = 433; FY2025/Q1 = 318; FY2022/FY = 7; FY2020/FY = 2). Total = 6,491, ADSH unique = 6,491 (one row per filing), 5,833 distinct CIKs.

THE DECISIVE PROOF (stronger than the candidate's): every single row was FILED in 2024 and ACCEPTED in 2024. MIN(FILED)=20241001, MAX(FILED)=20241231 -- ALL 6,491 filings were submitted in exactly ONE calendar quarter (2024 Q4). MIN/MAX(ACCEPTED) = 2024-09-30 to 2024-12-31. This is the SEC Financial Statement Data Set 2024q4 submissions (SUB.txt) file -- one quarterly drop. So 'one-quarter snapshot, not a panel' is FACT-grade and publishable: it sits on the hard FILED/ADSH dimension.

WHY DOWNGRADED (title fixes, not substance):
1) The title says '99% a single quarter (FY2024 Q3 = 4,645 of 6,491)'. That's internally wrong. 4,645/6,491 = 72%, not 99%. The candidate's own evidence body correctly says 72%. The real '~100% single quarter' fact is about FILING quarter (2024Q4), not the FY/FP=Q3 cluster.
2) The candidate conflates two different dimensions. FY/FP = the FISCAL period a filing COVERS (a fiscal attribute of the company). FILED = when it was submitted to SEC (the snapshot dimension). The 4,645 FY2024-Q3 cluster exists because 5,364 of 6,491 forms are 10-Q quarterly reports (form breakdown: 10-Q 5,364, 10-K 373, 6-K 123, 10-Q/A 108, S-1 81, 20-F 76) covering fiscal Q3 2024 -- normal for a Q4-2024 filing window. The 'snapshot' is correctly diagnosed via FILED, not FY/FP.

No casting artifacts: counts are on raw text equality (FY='2024', FP='Q3'), no TRY_CAST involved. The 348-row blank FY/FP group is registration/prospectus forms (S-1, POS AM) that legitimately have no fiscal period -- not a data-quality gap. 'Investigators must land more SEC quarters for history' conclusion is correct and useful. Importance 6: matters so nobody builds a YoY trend on a one-quarter roster; registry should read SNAPSHOT.

kept.title: fed_cms_home_health: 36% of agencies have NO quality star rating ('-' is the single most common value)
kept.sql: SELECT QUALITY_OF_PATIENT_CARE_STAR_RATING star, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_HOME_HEALTH GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY n DESC
kept.evidence: 12,392 agencies (CCN is fully unique, 12392 distinct). QUALITY_OF_PATIENT_CARE_STAR_RATING distribution: '-' = 4431 (35.8%, the modal value), 3=1347, 3.5=1338, 4=1287, 4.5=1041, 2.5=963, 2=778, 1.5=593, 5=458, 1=156. So 5-star (458) is rarer than 1-star... no: the unrated bucket dwarfs every real rating tier.
kept.why_matters: Anyone ranking or filtering home-health agencies by star rating is implicitly dropping over a third of the universe (CMS suppresses ratings for small / low-volume agencies). A naive 'worst agencies' query would miss the suppressed cohort entirely. The cast of '-' to numeric also silently nulls 4431 rows.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: fed_cms_home_health: 35.8% of agencies (4,431 of 12,392) have NO quality star rating — '-' is the single most common value, and CMS suppresses it for low-volume agencies
verdict.notes:
All numbers reproduce exactly. Total = 12,392 rows; CCN is fully unique (12,392 distinct), so one row = one agency as claimed. QUALITY_OF_PATIENT_CARE_STAR_RATING distribution matches the candidate to the row: '-'=4431 (35.8%, modal), 3=1347, 3.5=1338, 4=1287, 4.5=1041, 2.5=963, 2=778, 1.5=593, 5=458, 1=156. Five-star (458) is indeed rarer than 1-star? No — 1-star is 156, so 5-star is actually MORE common than 1-star; the candidate's own prose self-corrects this ("...no:"), so the headline stands.

Artifact checks all clear: (1) Not a blank/null artifact — zero NULLs and zero empty-string values; '-' is a real literal token. (2) Not a casting artifact — the distribution is GROUP BY on the raw text column, no cast involved. (3) The TRY_CAST-silently-nulls claim is exactly right: TRY_CAST(... AS DOUBLE) yields 7,961 non-null and 4,431 null, the 4,431 nulls matching the dash count precisely — confirms the warned-about silent drop.

Suppression explanation independently CONFIRMED by the FOOTNOTE_FOR_QUALITY_OF_PATIENT_CARE_STAR_RATING column: of the 4,431 dash rows, 4,428 carry "The number of patient episodes for this measure is too small..." and 3 carry "special needs population." So the unrated bucket is genuinely small/low-volume suppression, not missing data.

Tier stays FACT: single landed CMS table, hard ID (CCN) unique, no cross-source/name-match involved. Surprise moderate (the magnitude — a third of the universe — is more striking than the existence of suppression, which is expected in CMS public-reporting files). Importance moderate-to-high for any investigator ranking/filtering agencies by star rating: a naive numeric filter silently drops 35.8% of agencies.

Minor wording nit corrected in title: '-' is 35.8% not "36%" (rounds to 36 but 35.8 is exact), and the original title's parenthetical was fine.

kept.title: CMS hospital star rating 'Not Available' is 100% deterministic by hospital type -- every Psychiatric, Childrens, Rural-Emergency, DoD and Long-term hospital is unrated by design
kept.sql: SELECT HOSPITAL_TYPE, COUNT(*) n, SUM(CASE WHEN HOSPITAL_OVERALL_RATING='Not Available' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) na FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_HOSPITAL_GENERAL GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC
kept.evidence: 2,250 of 5,432 hospitals (41%) have HOSPITAL_OVERALL_RATING='Not Available'. The missingness is perfectly structured by type: Psychiatric 635/635 NA, Childrens 94/94, Rural Emergency 41/41, Acute Care-DoD 32/32, Long-term 5/5 -- all 100%. Critical Access 978/1,378 (71%). Only Acute Care (445/3,115 = 14%) and Acute-VA (20/132) are mostly rated. CMS structurally does not star-rate non-acute facility types.
kept.why_matters: A clean, publishable signal: the 41% 'unrated' figure is not data quality noise, it is a coverage rule. Any analysis of hospital quality must condition on type or it will read 'missing' as 'bad'. The cross-tab itself is a tidy story (which hospitals CMS refuses to grade and why).
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: CMS star-rates only Acute Care + Critical Access hospitals: five facility types (Psychiatric, Childrens, Rural-Emergency, DoD, Long-term) are 100% unrated -- 41% of all hospitals carry 'Not Available' and it is a coverage rule, not data-quality noise
verdict.notes:
Re-ran the SQL: numbers reproduce EXACTLY (2,250/5,432 = 41.4% NA; Psychiatric 635/635, Childrens 94/94, Rural Emergency 41/41, DoD 32/32, Long-term 5/5 all 100% NA; Critical Access 978/1,378 = 71%; Acute Care 445/3,115 = 14.3%; Acute-VA 20/132 = 15.2%). No casting artifact: HOSPITAL_OVERALL_RATING is a clean categorical with exactly six values {1,2,3,4,5,'Not Available'} -- no hidden NULLs or blanks, and 199+662+987+950+384+2250 sums to 5,432. Direct determinism check (COUNT DISTINCT of rated values per type) confirms the 5 enumerated types have ZERO rated rows -- the type->NA rule is real and one-directional. Single-table, single-column finding with no cross-ID/name/fuzzy match, so it correctly stays FACT grade.

Why DOWNGRADED (wording, not substance): (1) The title's phrase '100% deterministic by hospital type' overstates -- type does NOT fully determine the rating overall. Three buckets are MIXED: Acute Care 14% NA, Acute-VA 15% NA, Critical Access a hefty 71% NA. Within those, missingness is driven by measure-count/reporting thresholds, NOT type. Determinism applies ONLY in the type->NA direction for the 5 non-acute types. (2) 'by design' / 'CMS refuses to grade' is a reasonable inference from a perfect 100%/0% split but is CMS policy knowledge, not provable from this table alone -- state it as the observed pattern, not proven intent. (3) The original 'CMS structurally does not star-rate non-acute facility types' is contradicted by its own data: Critical Access is non-acute yet 29% ARE rated. The corrected title fixes the scope to 'only Acute Care + Critical Access ever get rated.' Surprise is moderate (5) -- structured missingness by facility type is the expected CMS behavior to anyone who knows the Hospital Compare program; the value is the clean publishable cross-tab and the warning that 41% 'missing' must be conditioned on type, not read as 'bad.'

kept.title: nagix DPRK missile tests has NO test-level rows: 23 rows for 23 years (1984-2026), each year crammed into one JSON array in DATA -- and a 2026 (current-year) row exists
kept.sql: SELECT MIN(TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT)) min_yr, MAX(TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT)) max_yr, COUNT(*) n, COUNT(DISTINCT YEAR) distinct_yrs FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_NAGIX_DPRK_MISSILE_TESTS
kept.evidence: 23 rows, 23 distinct YEAR values, range 1984-2026 (one row per year, current year 2026 present with date 2026-01-03 inside the blob). The DATA column is a JSON array of test objects (e.g. [{'date':'2024-01-14','time':'05:53 (UTC)','missile':...}], one row's DATA is 64,108 chars). So individual launches are NOT queryable as rows -- you cannot GROUP BY missile type or count tests without first FLATTENing the VARIANT-like text. The table's '23 rows' wildly understates content and overstates queryability.
kept.why_matters: Cataloged as a missile-tests table, but at row grain it is a year index, not an event log. Any 'count of DPRK tests per year' query against rows returns 23, not the true ~hundreds. It needs a staging model that PARSE_JSON(DATA) and LATERAL FLATTENs to one-row-per-launch before it is analytically useful; the year gaps (e.g. no 1985, 1987-1989, 1994-1997 rows) are real launch-free years, not load gaps.
kept.implies: new source to load: build a stg_ model flattening DATA into one row per launch (date/time/missile/series)

verdict.corrected_title: XC_NAGIX_DPRK_MISSILE_TESTS is a 23-row year-index, not an event log: each row is one year holding a JSON array of launches (340 launches total hidden behind 23 rows; 2022 alone = 69), and a current-year 2026 row exists
verdict.notes:
Re-ran the candidate SQL verbatim: 23 rows, 23 distinct YEAR values, range 1984-2026 -- reproduces exactly. Table has only 2 real columns (DATA VARCHAR(16M), YEAR VARCHAR) plus provenance stamps. DATA is a per-year JSON array of launch objects; confirmed via TRY_PARSE_JSON(DATA) which SUCCEEDS (Snowflake tolerates the Python-style single-quoted keys -- no parse failure on the 2025 row). ARRAY_SIZE(TRY_PARSE_JSON(DATA)) per year sums to 340 individual launches across the 23 rows (e.g. 2022=69, 2024=41, 2023=33, 2019=27, 2026=26, 2016=24, 2017=21). So 'count tests per year' at row grain returns 23, true launch count is 340 -- the candidate's '23 understates / true ~hundreds' is correct, in fact ~340. 2026 row confirmed present (26 launches, first date 2026-01-03). Year gaps (1985, 1987-89, 1994-97, etc.) are curated launch-free years in this source, not load gaps -- reasonable framing.

Two minor inaccuracies in the candidate, neither fatal: (1) the 64,108-char blob is the 2022 row, NOT 2024 -- the candidate's parenthetical paired the 64,108 figure with a 2024-01-14 example; the 2024 row is actually 63,567 chars. (2) Candidate says launches are 'NOT queryable... you cannot... without first FLATTENing the VARIANT-like text' -- true at raw grain, but worth noting the path is clean: TRY_PARSE_JSON works directly (no manual quote repair needed), then LATERAL FLATTEN. Claim stands.

Tiering: this is a structural/coverage-grain fact about a table, not an entity/person claim, so libel-tiering is N/A -- stays FACT. No casting artifact (TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT) confirmed numeric; the 23-vs-340 gap is real array nesting, not blanks/sort artifacts). Importance modest (one tiny 23-row source) but the grain trap would silently zero out any per-year/per-missile analysis, so the warning is worth keeping.

kept.title: fed_usgs_earthquakes is a 30-day rolling snapshot (May 15 - Jun 14 2026), not a seismic history panel
kept.sql: SELECT MIN(TRY_TO_TIMESTAMP(TIME)) min_t, MAX(TRY_TO_TIMESTAMP(TIME)) max_t, COUNT(*) n, COUNT(DISTINCT ID) d, MIN(TRY_CAST(MAG AS DOUBLE)) min_mag, MAX(TRY_CAST(MAG AS DOUBLE)) max_mag FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_USGS_EARTHQUAKES
kept.evidence: All 9890 events fall in a single 30-day window: 2026-05-15 02:56 to 2026-06-14 02:43. ID is a perfect key (9890 distinct = 9890 rows). Magnitude -1.9 to 7.8 (the -1.9 is legit micro-seismicity, not an error). TYPE breakdown: earthquake 9714, quarry blast 96, explosion 68, ice quake 8, plus singletons sonic boom / meteorite / accidental explosion / other event.
kept.why_matters: USGS feels like it should be a long historical record; it's actually one month. Any trend/seasonality analysis on quakes is impossible from this table -- it's a point-in-time feed. Good to know before someone builds a 'quakes over time' viz. (Bonus: 174 of 9890 events are non-earthquakes -- blasts, ice, a meteorite.)
kept.implies: new source to load (USGS historical bulk if time-series needed)

verdict.corrected_title: fed_usgs_earthquakes is a 30-day rolling snapshot (May 15 - Jun 14 2026), not a seismic history panel (176 of 9890 events are non-earthquakes, not 174)
verdict.notes:
Core claim fully reproduces. Re-ran the candidate SQL verbatim: MIN_T 2026-05-15 02:56:09.88, MAX_T 2026-06-14 02:43:02.42, N=9890, DISTINCT ID=9890 (perfect key), MIN_MAG -1.9, MAX_MAG 7.8. No casting artifacts: all 9890 TIME values parse via TRY_TO_TIMESTAMP, so the window is not hiding outliers behind null casts. Daily distribution is contiguous across all 31 calendar days (~270-416/day) with the partial final day (23 events, since the feed cuts at 02:43) -- genuinely a single contiguous 30-day window, not a sparse panel. -1.9 min mag is one legit event ('77 km W of Salamatof, Alaska'), confirmed not an error. TYPE breakdown matches exactly (earthquake 9714, quarry blast 96, explosion 68, ice quake 8, sonic boom/meteorite/accidental explosion/other event 1 each).

ONE CORRECTION: the bonus 'non-earthquake' count is 176, not 174 (COUNT WHERE TYPE<>'earthquake' = 176; sum of the named non-eq types 96+68+8+1+1+1+1 = 176). The candidate's 174 is an arithmetic slip in a parenthetical -- does not touch the core finding. 3 MAG values fail TRY_CAST; they are blank strings ('' ), not lost data.

Tier FACT is appropriate: this is a table-property observation, no entity claims, no fuzzy/cross-ID matches involved.

kept.title: 7,183 'Medicare' providers serve patients whose AVERAGE age is under 50 — and the youngest-patient providers are pediatricians (avg patient age 7)
kept.sql: SELECT NPI, RNDRNG_PRVDR_LAST_ORG_NAME nm, RNDRNG_PRVDR_TYPE typ, BENE_AVG_AGE age, TOT_BENES benes FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_MEDICARE_PROVIDER WHERE TRY_CAST(BENE_AVG_AGE AS DOUBLE) < 40 ORDER BY TRY_CAST(BENE_AVG_AGE AS DOUBLE) LIMIT 20
kept.evidence: BENE_AVG_AGE ranges 7 to 94. Bucketed: 65-74 = 746,814 providers, 75+ = 485,283, 50-64 = 57,459, and UNDER 50 = 7,183. The absolute-youngest cohort is dominated by Pediatric Medicine and Pediatric Nephrology NPIs: NPI 1497862866 (Askenazi, Pediatric Medicine) avg age 7 / 12 benes; NPI 1033187505 (Fathallah-Shaykh, Pediatric Medicine) avg age 9 / 21 benes; multiple pediatric nephrologists at avg age 9-10. Table is clean: 1,296,739 rows, 1,296,739 distinct NPI (perfect 1:1 key), 0 null TOT_BENES.
kept.why_matters: Counterintuitive but TRUE: Medicare isn't just for the elderly — it covers under-65 people with ESRD (kidney failure) and certain disabilities, including children. The avg-age-7 rows are pediatric nephrologists billing Medicare for kids on dialysis. This is a genuine, publishable angle ('the children on Medicare') AND a guardrail: never assume 'Medicare provider' implies an elderly patient panel. NPIs make these FACT-grade.
kept.implies: new angle: cross FED_CMS_MEDICARE_PROVIDER pediatric/ESRD NPIs against FED_CMS_DIALYSIS facilities on NPI — hard-ID join, FACT grade

verdict.corrected_title: 7,183 Medicare providers have an average patient age under 50 (range 7-94); the absolute-youngest rows (avg age 7-10) are pediatric specialists, but the under-50 cohort overall is dominated by Nurse Practitioners and Psychiatry, not pediatrics
verdict.notes:
