All headline numbers reproduce EXACTLY: buckets 65-74=746,814 / 75+=485,283 / 50-64=57,459 / UNDER_50=7,183; age range 7-94; median 74. Table FED_CMS_MEDICARE_PROVIDER is clean: 1,296,739 rows = 1,296,739 distinct NPI (1:1), 0 blank and 0 unparseable BENE_AVG_AGE (TRY_CAST nulls nothing, so NO casting artifact). Both cited NPIs verify exactly (Askenazi 1497862866 age 7/12 benes; Fathallah-Shaykh 1033187505 age 9/21 benes, both Pediatric Medicine).

KEY DOWNGRADE: the title's claim that 'the youngest-patient providers are pediatricians' and the narrative ('avg-age-7 rows are pediatric nephrologists billing for kids on dialysis') describe only the absolute tail (avg age 7-10). Profiling the actual 7,183-row UNDER_50 cohort by provider type: Nurse Practitioner=2,059, Psychiatry=1,967, Licensed Clinical Social Worker=550, THEN Pediatric Medicine=367 (5th place, ~5%), OB/GYN=297, etc. So the under-50 population is overwhelmingly behavioral-health / disability-driven panels, NOT pediatric ESRD. The pediatric-dialysis story is real but is the extreme outlier, not the body of the finding.

Supporting context that STRENGTHENS the core fact (not weakens): min TOT_BENES in the cohort is exactly 11 — that is CMS's beneficiary-suppression floor (panels <11 are dropped), explaining the hard 11 floor; avg panel ~30. These are small but real panels. ~4.98% of all providers (64,642) serve avg-age-under-65 panels.

Tier stays FACT: single clean table, every row keyed on a HARD ID (NPI), no cross-source/name/fuzzy joins, no wrongdoing alleged so no libel exposure. Importance modest — it's a useful guardrail ('Medicare provider != elderly panel') and a clean angle, but no accountability hook. Downgraded purely because the headline mischaracterizes cohort composition; a journalist reading the title would wrongly think the 7,183 are mostly pediatricians.

kept.title: SCOTUS database silently breaks the 'majority > minority' assumption: 121 cases have MAJVOTES <= MINVOTES because they are tie votes (118 are 4-4, 3 are 3-3)
kept.sql: SELECT MAJVOTES||'-'||MINVOTES split, COUNT(*) n FROM (SELECT DISTINCT CASEID, MAJVOTES, MINVOTES FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_SCDB) WHERE TRY_CAST(MAJVOTES AS INT) <= TRY_CAST(MINVOTES AS INT) GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY n DESC
kept.evidence: FED_SCDB is justice-vote-level: 83,644 rows = 83,644 distinct VOTEID over 9,341 distinct CASEID, terms 1946-2024 (clean, no future dates). Of the 9,341 case-level (MAJVOTES,MINVOTES) pairs, 0 sum to more than 9, but 121 have majority NOT greater than minority: 118 are 4-4 and 3 are 3-3. These are equally-divided-court affirmances (a recusal/vacancy left an even bench) — there is no actual majority.
kept.why_matters: Any winner-detection logic of the form 'MAJVOTES > MINVOTES' (the obvious one) misclassifies 121 real cases. A 4-4 tie affirms the lower court without setting precedent — a legally distinct outcome. This is a landmine for anyone modeling SCOTUS outcomes from this table; it must be special-cased.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: SCOTUS database (FED_SCDB) has 121 cases where MAJVOTES <= MINVOTES — all are tie votes (118 4-4, 3 3-3), equally-divided-court affirmances that break the naive 'majority > minority' winner test
verdict.notes: Every claim reproduces exactly. Re-ran candidate SQL: 118 4-4 + 3 3-3 = 121 cases. Adversarial checks all passed: (1) No casting artifact — 0 of 83,644 rows have non-castable MAJVOTES/MINVOTES, comparison is numeric TRY_CAST (not text sort), no silent nulling. (2) DISTINCT is sound — (CASEID,MAJVOTES,MINVOTES) collapses to exactly 9,341 distinct CASEID, and 0 CASEIDs carry conflicting vote pairs, so no inflation from the vote-level (VOTEID/JUSTICE) grain. (3) Structural claims hold: 83,644 rows = 83,644 distinct VOTEID over 9,341 CASEID, terms 1946-2024, no future dates, 0 cases sum above 9 (max 9-seat bench respected). (4) Interpretation corroborated by the table's own fields: all 121 have SPLITVOTE=1; 117/121 have CASEDISPOSITION=2 (=affirmed in SCDB codebook) with PARTYWINNING mostly 0 or blank — exactly the signature of equally-divided-court affirmances. Single-table data-quality finding, no cross-source or fuzzy-name exposure, so FACT tier is correct. Surprise is moderate (tie votes are expected to exist) but the specific landmine — a naive MAJVOTES>MINVOTES winner test silently misclassifying 121 legally-distinct outcomes — is a real, non-obvious modeling trap. Title accurate, not overstated. SQL: SELECT MAJVOTES||'-'||MINVOTES, COUNT(*) FROM (SELECT DISTINCT CASEID,MAJVOTES,MINVOTES FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_SCDB) WHERE TRY_CAST(MAJVOTES AS INT)<=TRY_CAST(MINVOTES AS INT) GROUP BY 1.

kept.title: OWID nuclear-warhead 'counts' are 84% rounded estimates — 553 of 656 non-null values for the 8 smaller nuclear states are multiples of 5
kept.sql: SELECT SUM(CASE WHEN TRY_CAST(NUMBER_OF_NUCLEAR_WARHEADS AS DOUBLE) % 5 = 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) mult5, SUM(CASE WHEN TRY_CAST(NUMBER_OF_NUCLEAR_WARHEADS AS DOUBLE) % 10 = 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) mult10, COUNT(*) total, SUM(CASE WHEN TRY_CAST(NUMBER_OF_NUCLEAR_WARHEADS AS DOUBLE) IS NOT NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) nonnull FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_OWID_NUCLEAR_WARHEADS WHERE ENTITY <> 'World' AND ENTITY <> 'United States' AND ENTITY <> 'Russia'
kept.evidence: Table is a tidy 902-row 11-entity panel, 1945-2026 (82 years each, perfectly balanced, 0 null CODE). But excluding World/US/Russia (whose exact stockpiles are declassified), 553/656 (84%) of non-null values are multiples of 5 and 492/656 (75%) are multiples of 10. North Korea's series is the tell: 5,5,5,10,15,15,20,25,30,50,50,60 — every value a round 5/10. China caps at exactly 620, France 540, UK 500, India 190, Pakistan 170, Israel 90.
kept.why_matters: These are intelligence ESTIMATES presented as integer counts. The round-number clustering proves the underlying uncertainty: NK going 30->50 in one year isn't a measured jump, it's an estimate revision. Anyone charting these as precise time series will over-state confidence; the data carries a 'wide error bar' that the schema hides.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: OWID nuclear-warhead estimates for 8 smaller states are heavily round-numbered (70% of POSITIVE values are multiples of 5, vs ~23% for the US's documented stockpile) — the round clustering flags them as intelligence estimates, but the headline 84% double-counts 318 pre-program zeros
verdict.notes:
SQL reproduces exactly: 553 mult5 / 492 mult10 / 656 total / 656 nonnull. Panel structure is real: 11 entities x 82 yrs (1945-2026) = 902 rows, 0 blanks, 0 cast-nulls, perfectly balanced. Per-country caps all confirmed (China 620, France 540, UK 500, India 190, Pakistan 170, Israel 90, NK 60). NK positive series confirmed exactly: 5,5,5,10,15,15,20,25,30,50,50,60 — every value a round 5/10 (100% mult-5).

THE ARTIFACT (why downgraded): The 84%/75% headline counts ZEROS as round numbers. Of the 656 values in the 8-entity subset, 318 are zeros (pre-program years, e.g. China before 1964) and 0 is trivially a multiple of 5 and 10. Strip zeros and the honest stat on the 338 real positive (warhead-bearing) values is 235/338=70% mult-5 and 174/338=51% mult-10 — NOT 84%/75%. A pre-program zero is a KNOWN true value, not a rounded estimate, so it does not belong in the 'these are estimates' bucket.

THE SIGNAL IS REAL though (why not REJECTED, why still FACT-grade as corrected): The round-clustering is genuine and the contrast is clean. Among positive values: US 23% mult-5, Russia 38% (both near the ~20-25% random baseline — their stockpiles are documented), vs UK 80%, France 78%, Pakistan 76%, China 71%, North Korea 100%. ~70% vs ~23% is ~3x random, a strong tell that the smaller-state series are rounded intelligence estimates. This is a pure data-quality observation off the table itself (no entity claim, no cross-source/name match), so FACT tier is fine.

CAVEAT to the blanket framing: it's not universal. Israel is only 48% mult-5, and South Africa is 13% — SA's actual positive series is 3,3,3,3,3,4,5,6 (precise, because the program was built and then dismantled/audited, so exact counts are known). So 'all 8 smaller states are round estimates' overstates; SA is a documented precise series and Israel is mixed. Investigator takeaway holds: don't chart these as precise — but the right number is ~70% of positive values, and a couple of states are exceptions.

kept.title: EIN is a real bridge between the for-profit and tax-exempt worlds: Caterpillar and VF Corp appear in BOTH the SEC EDGAR filer list and the IRS auto-revocation list under the exact same EIN
kept.sql: WITH sec AS (SELECT DISTINCT EIN, NAME, CIK FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_SEC_EDGAR_FINANCIALS WHERE EIN NOT IN ('','000000000','999999999')), rev AS (SELECT DISTINCT LPAD(EIN,9,'0') ein9, LEGAL_NAME, EXEMPTION_TYPE, REVOCATION_DATE FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_IRS_REVOCATION WHERE EIN IS NOT NULL) SELECT s.NAME sec_name, r.LEGAL_NAME irs_name, s.CIK, s.EIN, r.EXEMPTION_TYPE, r.REVOCATION_DATE FROM sec s JOIN rev r ON LPAD(s.EIN,9,'0')=r.ein9;
kept.evidence: Exact 9-digit EIN match, names also match: VF CORP EIN 231180120 (IRS exemption type 09, revoked 15-MAY-2010, CIK 103379) and CATERPILLAR INC EIN 370602744 (exemption type 17, revoked 15-MAY-2011, CIK 18230). The other 2 of 4 join hits are pure noise (IRS test-EIN 999999999 colliding with SEC junk EINs). SEC financials carries 5,126 'normal' EINs vs 1,363 all-zero and 2 all-nine.
kept.why_matters: These are the companies' employee-benefit trusts (VEBA / 501(c)(17) supplemental unemployment trusts) filed under the parent corporate EIN that the IRS auto-revoked for non-filing. It proves EIN works as a STEEL join key spanning SEC (public companies) and IRS (tax-exempt) - the spine that lets you trace a corporation's nonprofit affiliates. A clean, publishable cross-source link with zero current detectors on either side.
kept.implies: new detector: EIN bridge SEC_EDGAR <-> IRS_REVOCATION <-> (any future IRS BMF/990 load) to map corporate-affiliated foundations and benefit trusts

verdict.corrected_title: Two public companies (Caterpillar, VF Corp) appear in BOTH the SEC EDGAR filer list and the IRS auto-revocation list under the exact same 9-digit EIN — a clean but small (n=2) proof-of-concept that EIN can bridge SEC and IRS
verdict.notes:
Re-ran the exact SQL: reproduces perfectly. Two clean hits — CATERPILLAR INC (EIN 370602744, CIK 18230, IRS exemption type 17, revoked 15-MAY-2011) and V F CORP / VF CORP (EIN 231180120, CIK 103379, type 09, revoked 15-MAY-2010). Both are EXACT 9-digit EIN matches corroborated by matching company names and CIKs that resolve to the real public companies, so FACT grade on a HARD ID is justified (not a name/fuzzy match).

Verified the supporting numbers: SEC EDGAR financials EIN distribution is exactly as claimed — 5,126 normal rows / 4,663 distinct EINs, 1,363 all-zero (single distinct value), 2 all-nine. Raw join produces 3 hits; the third (999999999) is literally 'TEST COMPANY INC1 TESTS' in the IRS data — confirmed noise, correctly excluded. IRS EINs are uniformly 9 chars so LPAD is a harmless no-op; each matched EIN appears exactly once in the revocation list (no collision inflation).

Why DOWNGRADED not CONFIRMED — two issues with the framing, not the data:
(1) Exemption-code labels are garbled. Type 09 = 501(c)(9) VEBA; type 17 = 501(c)(17) supplemental unemployment benefit trust. So VF (type 09) is the VEBA and Caterpillar (type 17) is the SUB trust — the candidate's 'VEBA / 501(c)(17)' shorthand mixes them up. The underlying mechanism (employee-benefit trusts filed under the parent corporate EIN, auto-revoked for non-filing) is correct and consistent with the subsection codes present in the data.
(2) Overstated generalization. 'Proves EIN works as a STEEL join key spanning SEC and IRS' rests on n=2 out of 4,663 distinct SEC EINs (0.04%). The link is genuine and publishable for these two named entities, but it's a feasibility demonstration, not evidence of a high-volume bridge. 'Proves' should be 'demonstrates'. No current detector covers it, so it's still a worthwhile clean cross-source link — just smaller than the title implies.

kept.title: Brutal contractor concentration: 100 of 92,833 recipients (0.1%) capture 46% of $778B; top 1,000 (1.1%) capture 77%
kept.sql: WITH per_uei AS (SELECT RECIPIENT_UEI, SUM(TRY_CAST(FEDERAL_ACTION_OBLIGATION AS DOUBLE)) tot FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_USASPENDING_CONTRACTS WHERE RECIPIENT_UEI IS NOT NULL GROUP BY 1), ranked AS (SELECT tot, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY tot DESC NULLS LAST) rn, SUM(tot) OVER () grand FROM per_uei) SELECT COUNT(*) total_uei, ROUND(grand/1e9,1) grand_b, ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN rn<=100 THEN tot ELSE 0 END)/grand*100,1) top100_pct, ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN rn<=1000 THEN tot ELSE 0 END)/grand*100,1) top1000_pct FROM ranked GROUP BY grand;
kept.evidence: 92,833 distinct UEIs, $778.3B total obligated FY2025. Top 100 UEIs = 46.3% of all dollars; top 1,000 UEIs = 76.8%. And this UNDER-counts concentration because of the UEI-fragmentation finding above (real prime concentration is even higher once rolled up to parent).
kept.why_matters: A textbook power-law concentration on the cleanest dollar figure in the corporate spine. Pairs perfectly with the Lockheed fragmentation finding: the measured 46%/77% is a floor, not a ceiling. Strong, defensible 'where the money actually goes' story straight off a hard ID + a CAST.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: Contractor concentration: top 100 of 92,833 FY2025 UEIs capture 46% of $778B net obligated; top 1,000 capture 77% (stable at 45%/76% on gross dollars)
verdict.notes:
SQL reproduces EXACTLY: 92,833 UEIs, $778.3B, top100=46.3%, top1000=76.8%. Stress-tested every failure mode and it holds.

NO casting artifact: 6,325,622 rows, 0 NULL UEIs (so the WHERE RECIPIENT_UEI IS NOT NULL filter is a no-op), 0 uncastable obligation values. Top UEIs are real contractors (Lockheed G4KDGE4JFFK7 $34.1B, Optum $22.5B, Electric Boat $21.4B, Raytheon, McKesson) — no blank/sentinel UEI dominating.

SCOPE confirmed: ACTION_DATE spans 2024-10-01 to 2025-09-30 = exactly FY2025. The 'FY2025' claim is accurate.

ROBUST to netting choice: the $778.3B is a NET figure (gross positive $830.3B minus $51.9B de-obligations; 267,515 negative rows exist). Re-running on gross-positive dollars only gives top100=45.4%, top1000=75.5% — essentially identical. So the de-obligation netting does NOT manufacture the concentration. The power law is real either way.

TWO caveats (minor, fold into wording, not a downgrade): (1) The '92,833 recipients / 0.1%' denominator includes 16,154 UEIs (17%) that net to zero (6,203) or negative (9,951) dollars — i.e. thousands of 'recipients' that received no net money. The 'top 100 = 0.1% of recipients' framing slightly overstates the recipient base, but the load-bearing number (the 46%/77% dollar SHARE) is unaffected. (2) Total is net of de-obligations; should be stated as such.

Tier FACT is correct: match is on a HARD ID (UEI) plus a clean numeric CAST, no cross-source/name join. Fragmentation corroborated independently — two distinct Lockheed UEIs (G4KDGE4JFFK7 + XFJMYSYFJEK4) sit in the top 12, so 'measured 46% is a floor not a ceiling' is supported.

Surprise is modest (4): textbook federal-contracting power law, expected by anyone who knows the space. Importance solid (7): cleanest 'where the money goes' anchor in the corporate spine, defensible off a hard ID.

kept.title: The two biggest contract COUNTS are not mega-awards - they're hundreds of thousands of $6k pharma micro-orders to one drug wholesaler
kept.sql: SELECT AWARDING_AGENCY_NAME, NAICS_DESCRIPTION, COUNT(*) n, ROUND(AVG(TRY_CAST(FEDERAL_ACTION_OBLIGATION AS DOUBLE)),0) avg_oblig, ROUND(SUM(TRY_CAST(FEDERAL_ACTION_OBLIGATION AS DOUBLE))/1e6,1) tot_m FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_USASPENDING_CONTRACTS WHERE RECIPIENT_NAME='AMERISOURCEBERGEN DRUG CORP' GROUP BY 1,2 ORDER BY n DESC LIMIT 3;
kept.evidence: AMERISOURCEBERGEN DRUG CORP (UEI KABZK8W6PQT3) = 516,380 award rows, the highest row-count of any recipient, but only $7.44B total. Breakdown: 411,965 DoD 'Drugs & Druggists' Sundries Merchant Wholesalers' rows at avg $6,159 each, plus 104,408 'Medicinal & Botanical Manufacturing' rows at avg $46,972. Legitimate high-frequency drug distribution, not graft.
kept.why_matters: A trap for anyone ranking recipients by award COUNT instead of dollars: the 'busiest' contractor is a pharma distributor doing routine bulk fulfillment. Row count != importance in this table; you must aggregate dollars. Saves a false 'half-million contracts to one firm!' headline.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: The #1 contractor by award COUNT (AmerisourceBergen, 516k rows) is routine pharma fulfillment, not a mega-award - rank by dollars, not row count
verdict.notes:
Re-ran the candidate SQL verbatim: every headline number reproduces EXACTLY. 516,380 award rows, single UEI KABZK8W6PQT3 (matches claim), $7.44B total. Leg breakdown is identical: 411,965 DoD 'Drugs & Druggists Sundries Wholesalers' @ avg $6,159 = $2,537.3M, and 104,408 'Medicinal & Botanical Manufacturing' @ avg $46,972 = $4,904.2M. No casting artifact: cast_ok=516,380, blank_ct=0, so TRY_CAST nulled nothing silently. It IS genuinely the #1 recipient by row count (#2 ASRC Federal = 336,238 rows / $0.75B). Tier is correctly FACT - the match is on a HARD ID (UEI), not name/fuzzy.

The thesis (row count != importance; you must aggregate dollars) is solidly demonstrated by the top-10 table itself: ATLANTIC DIVING SUPPLY has only 71,904 rows but $5.99B, while NATIONAL INDUSTRIES FOR THE BLIND has 252,369 rows but $0.16B. Good methodological trap.

WHY DOWNGRADED (wording only, not the data): The '$6k micro-orders' framing oversells the typical order. On the 411,965-row micro-order leg, the MEDIAN obligation is $1,163.57, not ~$6,159 - the $6,159 is a mean dragged up by a heavy right tail (max $1,340,199). Also 39 rows are NEGATIVE (de-obligations/corrections, min -$19,604), which the 'micro-order' framing glosses. So 'hundreds of thousands of $6k orders' should read 'hundreds of thousands of small orders (median ~$1.2k, mean ~$6k)'. Separately confirmed these are real distinct awards, not modification spam: 516,380 rows -> 516,283 distinct AWARD_ID_PIID. Net: a true, reproducible data-quality caution; only the title's order-size characterization is imprecise.

kept.title: 30,021 events (7.8%) recorded ZERO deaths -- two-thirds of them state-based armed clashes
kept.sql: SELECT CASE WHEN TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT)=0 THEN 'zero' ELSE 'nonzero' END b, COUNT(*) FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_UCDP_GED WHERE TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT) IS NOT NULL GROUP BY 1;  -- then:  SELECT TYPE_OF_VIOLENCE, COUNT(*) zero_events, ROUND(100.0*COUNT(*)/SUM(COUNT(*)) OVER(),1) pct FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_UCDP_GED WHERE TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT)=0 GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC
kept.evidence: 30,021 of 385,918 events (7.8%) have BEST=0 deaths. Of those zero-death events, 65.4% are type 1 (state-based, 19,626), 21.9% type 3 (one-sided, 6,588), 12.7% type 2 (non-state, 3,807). Geocoding remains tight overall: 70.6% of all events are precision 1-2 (exact site or admin-2), only 1.4% at the coarse 6-7 levels.
kept.why_matters: GED is an EVENT registry, not strictly a death registry -- nearly 1 in 13 recorded events killed nobody (armed clashes, sieges, captures). Anyone treating event count as a proxy for lethality, or filtering on deaths>0, silently drops ~30k real conflict events. Worth a NOTES flag in the registry row.

verdict.corrected_title: 30,021 UCDP GED events (7.8%) have a best-estimate of ZERO deaths — two-thirds are state-based armed clashes (and 30,018 of them still carry a positive HIGH/upper-bound estimate)
verdict.notes:
Every number reproduces exactly against LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_UCDP_GED. No casting artifact: BEST has 0 nulls, 0 blanks, 0 uncastable rows out of 385,918, and 0 negatives (MIN=0), so TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT)=0 is counting genuine coded zeros, not silently-dropped or blank-as-zero rows. 30,021/385,918 = 7.78%. Type split reproduces: type 1 (state-based) 19,626/65.4%, type 3 (one-sided) 6,588/21.9%, type 2 (non-state) 3,807/12.7%. Geocoding claim reproduces: WHERE_PREC 1-2 = 70.6%, 6-7 = 1.4%.

These are real events, not garbage rows: all 30,021 carry a valid EVENT_CLARITY (27,141 clarity-1 'clear', 2,880 clarity-2). STRENGTHENING nuance the candidate missed: 30,018 of the 30,021 zero-BEST events have HIGH (upper-bound) > 0 — i.e. the best/most-likely estimate is zero deaths but UCDP's uncertainty range allows fatalities. None have DEATHS_UNKNOWN>0. This reinforces the 'event registry, not death registry' framing rather than weakening it.

WHY NOT HIGHER SURPRISE: This is a documented property of UCDP GED — the codebook explicitly allows best=0 for events meeting the violence threshold (clashes, sieges, captures with no confirmed deaths). Non-obvious to a Library user filtering deaths>0, but not a hidden anomaly. Tier stays FACT: it's a single hard table, a deterministic count on a clean integer column, no cross-source/name match involved. The registry NOTES flag recommendation is sound — anyone using event count as a lethality proxy silently drops ~30k real conflict events, and conversely anyone summing BEST treats these as harmless.

kept.title: Black residents are jailed at 3+x the White rate in nearly every county with enough Black population to measure: median ratio 3.16x, Black rate higher in 612 of 637 counties (96%)
kept.sql: SELECT COUNT(*) counties, ROUND(MEDIAN(TRY_CAST(BLACK_JAIL_POP_RATE AS DOUBLE)/NULLIF(TRY_CAST(WHITE_JAIL_POP_RATE AS DOUBLE),0)),2) median_ratio, SUM(CASE WHEN TRY_CAST(BLACK_JAIL_POP_RATE AS DOUBLE) > TRY_CAST(WHITE_JAIL_POP_RATE AS DOUBLE) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) black_higher FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_VERA_INCARCERATION_TRENDS WHERE YEAR='2019' AND TRY_CAST(BLACK_JAIL_POP_RATE AS DOUBLE) > 0 AND TRY_CAST(WHITE_JAIL_POP_RATE AS DOUBLE) > 0 AND TRY_CAST(BLACK_POP_15TO64 AS DOUBLE) >= 5000;
kept.evidence: Restricted to 637 counties with >=5,000 Black working-age residents (kills small-number artifacts): median Black/White jail-rate ratio = 3.16x; Black jail rate exceeds White in 612/637 = 96%. National across all 2,291 measurable counties: avg Black jail 2,490 vs White 554 per 100k, median ratio 3.51x. Extreme named outliers (Black pop >=5,000): Richmond County NY 45.6x (1019 vs 22), New York County NY 29.3x, Schenectady NY 22.2x, Arlington VA 22.1x, DC 19.2x (with 207k Black residents).
kept.why_matters: A clean, near-universal disparity signal measured from real per-race rate columns, not modeled. The most extreme ratios are large urban Northern counties (Staten Island, Manhattan, DC) — counterintuitive vs the usual Deep-South framing. Highly publishable and map-ready.
kept.implies: new detector: racial_jail_disparity (Black/White jail-rate ratio per county-year), surfacing the top outliers

verdict.corrected_title: Black residents jailed at higher rate than White in 96% of measurable counties (612/637 with >=5k Black working-age pop); median Black/White jail-rate ratio 3.16x (2019)
verdict.notes: Reproduced exactly: 637 counties, median ratio 3.16x, 612/637 (96%) Black-higher; national 2,291 counties avg Black 2,490 vs White 554, median 3.51x; all named outliers reproduce (Richmond/Staten Island NY 45.6x, NY County 29.3x, Schenectady 22.2x, Arlington VA 22.1x, DC 19.2x). ARTIFACT PROBES PASSED: (1) Selection bias from the >0 filters is near-inert at the 5k threshold -- only 4 counties dropped for null/zero white rate, 1 for black, so the 637-set is essentially the full measurable set, not cherry-picked. (2) Signal holds across years: median 3.06/3.10/3.16/3.52 and 96-97% Black-higher for 2017-2020, so 2019 is not a fluke (2019 also has the MOST measurable counties -- coverage collapses post-2019 due to Vera reporting gaps, so 2019 is a defensible 'last good year', not result-shopped). (3) Extreme outliers are NOT small-number noise: the NY boroughs have large White pops (Richmond 190k, NY County 600k, Schenectady 75k) with genuinely low white jail rates (22-55/100k), not denominator artifacts. (4) No impossible/casting-blowup values (rate range 0.9 to 13,549/100k, plausible). (5) These are Vera's own published per-race RATE columns aggregated at county level -- population statistic, no named-person/fuzzy/cross-source matching, so FACT tier is correct. CAVEATS for an editor: (a) jail rate uses point-in-time jail population (pretrial+sentenced held locally) over county-resident race denominators; for centralized-jail jurisdictions (NYC boroughs) the held population may not map 1:1 to county residents, which inflates the most-extreme borough ratios -- direction is unambiguous but the 45.6x style numbers reflect city-jail accounting, frame carefully. (b) Title wording fixed: 3.16x is the MEDIAN, so HALF of counties are BELOW 3x -- '3+x in nearly every county' overstates; the near-universal claim is the 96% directionality, the 3.16x is the typical/median county. Surprise is modest (racial jail disparity is well-documented), but it is clean, real, map-ready, and the Northern-urban extremes are a genuinely counterintuitive angle.

kept.title: Opioid prescribers writing 90%+ opioid scripts at high volume in NON-pain specialties: a Hospitalist at 96%, a Pharmacist at 91%
kept.sql: SELECT NPI, PRSCRBR_LAST_ORG_NAME, PRSCRBR_TYPE, PRSCRBR_STATE_ABRVTN, TRY_CAST(OPIOID_TOT_CLMS AS DOUBLE) AS opioid_clms, TRY_CAST(OPIOID_PRSCRBR_RATE AS DOUBLE) AS opioid_rate, TRY_CAST(TOT_CLMS AS DOUBLE) AS tot_clms FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_PART_D_PRESCRIBERS WHERE TRY_CAST(OPIOID_TOT_CLMS AS DOUBLE) >= 1000 AND TRY_CAST(OPIOID_PRSCRBR_RATE AS DOUBLE) >= 90 AND PRSCRBR_TYPE NOT ILIKE '%pain%' AND PRSCRBR_TYPE NOT ILIKE '%anesth%' AND PRSCRBR_TYPE NOT ILIKE '%rehab%' AND PRSCRBR_TYPE NOT ILIKE '%physical med%' ORDER BY opioid_clms DESC LIMIT 15
kept.evidence: 7 prescribers clear the bar. Top: NPI 1093988271 AKWE (Hospitalist, GA) -- 4,353 opioid claims = 96.14% of all 4,528 Part D claims; NPI 1275947335 CRAMER (Family Practice, OK) 3,342 claims @ 93.4%; NPI 1225560162 WREN (Nurse Practitioner, AR) 3,189 @ 96.37%; NPI 1467544031 LEE (Pharmacist, CA) 2,746 @ 91.32%. A Hospitalist or Pharmacist with nearly every Part D script being an opioid is far outside specialty norms (the bulk of 90%+ high-volume opioid prescribers are Pain Mgmt/Anesthesiology/PM&R).
kept.why_matters: FACT-grade (hard NPI) over-prescribing outliers in specialties where 90%+ opioid concentration has no clinical home -- a hospitalist does inpatient general medicine, a pharmacist normally doesn't prescribe at all. These NPIs join straight to NPPES, Open Payments (did pharma pay them?), and the LEIE exclusion list. Prime seed for a new 'opioid-rate-vs-specialty outlier' detector that complements the existing LEIE-centric leads.

verdict.corrected_title: 7 high-volume opioid prescribers (>=1,000 opioid claims, >=90% opioid rate) carry a NON-pain self-reported NPPES specialty -- incl. a Hospitalist at 96.1% and a Pharmacist at 91.3%; a specialty-vs-prescribing flag for review, not proven over-prescribing
verdict.notes:
Re-ran the exact SQL: numbers reproduce perfectly. 7 prescribers clear the bar; top NPIs/names/rates/claim counts all match (Akwe Hospitalist GA 4353 clms @96.14%; Cramer FP OK 3342 @93.40%; Wren NP AR 3189 @96.37%; Lee Pharmacist CA 2746 @91.32%). NOT a casting artifact: OPIOID_PRSCRBR_RATE = OPIOID_TOT_CLMS/TOT_CLMS verified arithmetically (4353/4528=96.14). NOT tiny/suppressed cells: each has 340-520 opioid beneficiaries and $100k-$295k drug cost. Table is one row per NPI (1,416,883 distinct NPIs, no dup despite 8 SHA loads), so 7 of 1.42M = genuinely rare tail. Specialty-norm framing holds: at the >=1000 & >=90% bar, pain specialties dominate (Pain Mgmt 13, Anesthesiology 8, Interventional Pain 8, PM&R 7 = 36/43); the 7 outliers are NP(4), Family Practice(1), Pharmacist(1), Hospitalist(1).

WHY DOWNGRADED (two caveats the candidate glosses): (1) PRSCRBR_TYPE_SRC field shows the specialty is NPPES self-reported taxonomy ('NPPES-Specialty'/'NPPES-Taxonomy'), which is notoriously stale/wrong. A 'Hospitalist' (MD,MPH) running a pain or addiction practice but never updating his taxonomy is a benign, common explanation -- so 'no clinical home' overstates it. The Pharmacist holds a real Pharm.D., and pharmacist prescribing is legal under collaborative-practice agreements in many states. (2) The NPI is FACT-grade (hard ID, joins to NPPES/Open Payments/LEIE), but the INTERPRETATION ('over-prescribing outlier') is a LEAD, not a fact about wrongdoing -- it's a specialty-label-vs-pattern mismatch flag. Single-year panel, no trend. Good detector seed, but must be stated as 'review flag', and any named-person claim stays LEAD until corroborated by Open Payments/LEIE join.

kept.title: Anthony Kennedy was empirically THE swing justice: in the majority on 76.4% of 5-4 decisions, highest of any justice
kept.sql: WITH split AS (SELECT CASEID, MAX(TRY_CAST(MAJVOTES AS INT)) maj, MAX(TRY_CAST(MINVOTES AS INT)) minv FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_SCDB GROUP BY 1) SELECT s.JUSTICENAME, COUNT(*) close_votes, SUM(CASE WHEN s.MAJORITY='2' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) in_maj, ROUND(100.0*SUM(CASE WHEN s.MAJORITY='2' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/COUNT(*),1) pct_maj_in_5to4 FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_SCDB s JOIN split sp ON s.CASEID=sp.CASEID WHERE sp.maj=5 AND sp.minv=4 AND s.MAJORITY IN ('1','2') GROUP BY 1 HAVING COUNT(*)>=100 ORDER BY pct_maj_in_5to4 DESC LIMIT 15
kept.evidence: In 1,411 five-to-four cases (1946-2024), Kennedy was in the majority on 409 of 535 close votes = 76.4%, the highest of any justice with >=100 close votes. Next sustained: O'Connor 66.8% (539 votes), Powell 68.5% (419), Roberts 66.0% (256). Across ALL case types Kennedy is also #1 at 91.7% in-majority (2,564/2,797). At the other extreme: WODouglas 71.3% overall (2,693/3,776), the great dissenter.
kept.why_matters: This is the cleanest possible data proof of the 'median justice controls the Court' thesis. Whoever sits in Kennedy's seat decides the closest, most consequential cases ~3 of every 4 times. It quantifies a narrative everyone asserts but rarely measures, straight from 83,644 vote-level rows. Perfect publishing-layer viz: rank justices by close-case majority share.

verdict.corrected_title: Anthony Kennedy was empirically the swing justice of the modern era: in the majority on 76.4% of 5-4 decisions (409/535), the highest of any justice with >=100 close votes (1946-2024)
verdict.notes:
Every number reproduces exactly off LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_SCDB. Re-ran the candidate SQL verbatim: Kennedy 535 close votes, 409 in majority = 76.4%, ranked #1. Verified the rest of the leaderboard (O'Connor 66.8%/539, Powell 68.5%/419, Roberts 66.0%/256), the all-case-type #1 (Kennedy 91.7% = 2564/2797), and the Douglas dissenter figure (71.3% in-majority = 2693/3776, the lowest = biggest dissenter). All hold.

Artifact checks I ran and cleared:
1. GRAIN: 83,644 rows = one vote per justice per case-issue; 9,341 distinct CASEID and CASEISSUESID are IDENTICAL counts, so no multi-issue duplication inflating any justice's vote count. VOTEID unique per row.
2. CASTING: split CTE uses TRY_CAST on MAJVOTES/MINVOTES; I independently re-counted actual MAJORITY='2'/'1' votes inside the 1,411 tagged 5-4 cases and got EXACTLY 5 majority + 4 minority in 100% of them (1411/1411). The MAJVOTES/MINVOTES fields perfectly match the real recorded votes - no blank-as-zero or null-row pollution in the denominator.
3. BLANKS: 2,983 rows have blank MAJORITY (dropped by MAJORITY IN ('1','2')), but none of them fall inside 5-4 cases, so Kennedy's denominator is clean.
4. TENURE sanity: Kennedy's 5-4 votes span term 1987-2017 (his actual tenure), 535 cases - internally consistent.
5. ID grade: this is a single-source aggregate over hard case/vote IDs, no cross-source or name-fuzzy join. FACT-grade.

ONE OVERCLAIM, fixed in corrected_title: the bare title says 'highest of any justice.' At a >=30-vote threshold AJGoldberg edges him at 80.5% - but that is only 41 votes (Goldberg served ~3 yrs), classic small-sample noise. Kavanaugh (67/67.2%) and Gorsuch (88/65.9%) also sit near the top on thin samples. The candidate's evidence already qualifies with '>=100 close votes' and that caveat is load-bearing - it belongs in the title.

DEFINITIONAL note for the viz, not a flaw: 'in the majority on a 5-4' is a fair operationalization of swing/median justice - by definition all 5 majority justices in a 5-4 are pivotal, and the swing justice is the one most often on the winning side, which is precisely what is measured.

Downgraded surprise to 5: this quantifies a near-universally-asserted narrative (Kennedy as THE swing vote) - it confirms the conventional wisdom cleanly rather than revealing something non-obvious. Importance 6: clean, publishable, good viz, but not investigative leverage.

kept.title: Supreme Court liberal-decision share peaked at 68.5% in the 1960s (Warren Court), then collapsed to ~44% for five straight decades
kept.sql: WITH c AS (SELECT DISTINCT CASEID, TRY_CAST(TERM AS INT) term, DECISIONDIRECTION dd FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_SCDB) SELECT FLOOR(term/10)*10 decade, COUNT(*) cases, SUM(CASE WHEN dd='1' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) conservative, SUM(CASE WHEN dd='2' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) liberal, ROUND(100.0*SUM(CASE WHEN dd='2' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/NULLIF(SUM(CASE WHEN dd IN ('1','2') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END),0),1) pct_liberal FROM c WHERE term IS NOT NULL GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
kept.evidence: Liberal share of decisions by decade: 1940s 54.4%, 1950s 57.7%, 1960s 68.5% (1,012 liberal vs 466 conservative), 1970s 45.9%, 1980s 43.7%, 1990s 44.0%, 2000s 44.9%, 2010s 49.7%, 2020s 44.3%. The 1960s spike is a 23-point swing above the post-1970 baseline.
kept.why_matters: A single decade fingerprint -- the Warren Court -- jumps out of 9,341 cases as a sharp, isolated liberal peak, then a clean regime change after 1969. The post-Warren era has been remarkably stable at ~44% liberal for 50 years, which itself contradicts the popular 'Court keeps lurching right' framing -- the lurch happened once, around 1970, and held.

verdict.corrected_title: Supreme Court liberal-decision share peaked at 68.5% in the 1960s (Warren Court), then settled near 44% for the post-1970 era (with a 2010s bump to ~50%)
verdict.notes:
VERIFIED. Re-ran the exact SQL: numbers reproduce to the digit (1940s 54.4, 1950s 57.7, 1960s 68.5 [1012 lib vs 466 cons], 1970s 45.9, 1980s 43.7, 1990s 44.0, 2000s 44.9, 2010s 49.7, 2020s 44.3).

Adversarial checks all pass:
- Table FED_SCDB is the vote-level (justiceCentered) version: 83,644 rows but only 9,341 distinct CASEID. The DISTINCT CASEID collapse is essential and CORRECT -- every CASEID maps to exactly ONE TERM and exactly ONE DECISIONDIRECTION (HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT ...)>1 returns 0 for both). No double-counting.
- No casting/null artifact: all 9,341 cases have a non-null TRY_CAST(TERM), term range 1946-2024, zero rows dropped by the term filter.
- Denominator is clean: DECISIONDIRECTION values are 2=41,708 lib, 1=40,061 cons, 3=1,471 unspecifiable, blank=404 (at row level). At case level the excluded (dir 3/blank) cases are tiny and evenly spread per decade (14-36), so they don't bias any decade's share.
- The 1960s spike is NOT one outlier year: per-term it is sustained -- 1962=76.9%, 1963=78.2%, 1967=75.0%, with 1969 (Burger's first term) already dropping to 54%. Real Warren-Court signal, clean regime change ~1970.

This is the canonical Spaeth SCDB decisionDirection measure -- well-documented, hard-keyed on CASEID, FACT grade. Two minor wording fixes: (1) the post-Warren era is not flat at ~44% for 'five straight decades' -- the 2010s came in at 49.7%, a ~5-6pt bump, so 'remarkably stable at ~44% for 50 years' slightly overstates; (2) DECISIONDIRECTION is the SCDB-coded ideological direction of the case outcome, a definitional construct, not an objective fact about each ruling. Both are nuance, not grounds to downgrade. Surprise modest (well-known political-science result), importance moderate.

kept.title: Federal Register table is a thin 2-month snapshot (Apr-Jun 2026), 80% Notices, capped at the 5,000-row API limit
kept.sql: SELECT TYPE, COUNT(*) n, MIN(PUBLICATION_DATE) earliest, MAX(PUBLICATION_DATE) latest FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_FEDERAL_REGISTER_DOCUMENTS GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC
kept.evidence: All 5,000 rows fall in PUBLICATION_DATE 2026-04-10 to 2026-06-16 (~9 weeks). Type mix: Notice 3,991 (80%), Rule 619, Proposed Rule 341, Presidential Document 49. The hard 5,000 ceiling + exactly-recent window strongly indicates a single capped API pull, not a backfill.
kept.why_matters: Any 'agency activity over time' analysis on this table is invalid -- there's no time series, just one quarter, and the 5,000 cap means even that quarter may be truncated. Flags a backfill gap: the Federal Register API supports historical pulls, so this should be paginated across years before it's treated as a governance-activity source rather than a one-shot sample.

verdict.corrected_title: Federal Register table is a thin ~9.5-week snapshot (2026-04-10 to 2026-06-16), 80% Notices, truncated at the 5,000-row API ceiling
verdict.notes: Every claim reproduces exactly. Type counts: Notice 3991, Rule 619, Proposed Rule 341, Presidential Document 49 = 5000 total (sum verified, no null TYPE). Notice share is 79.8% ("80%" fair). Date window 2026-04-10 to 2026-06-16 holds; all 5000 rows TRY_CAST to DATE with ZERO nulls, so no casting/text-sort artifact. Span = 67 calendar days, 47 distinct publication days (weekend/holiday gaps explain the difference, normal for Fed Register). Truncation is not just inferred from the round 5000 number -- it is corroborated by boundary asymmetry: the earliest day (Apr 10) carries only 55 rows vs a typical full day of ~90-144 and vs the latest day (Jun 16) at a full 104. That clipped old-end day is the signature of a descending-date API pull cut off at the 5,000 cap, confirming records before Apr 10 mid-day are missing. The 'why it matters' is sound: no multi-year time series exists, and the cap means even this quarter is truncated at the front edge. No tier downgrade warranted -- this is a structural property of the landed table (FACT), no entity/ID claims involved. Minor wording tightening only: span is ~9.5 weeks not 9, and 'capped' should read as 'truncated at the old end,' which I folded into the corrected title.

kept.title: The two DPRK missile sources agree EXACTLY on 19 of 21 shared years -- then NTI goes stale, missing all 37 tests of 2025-2026 that Nagix has
kept.sql: WITH nti AS (SELECT LEFT(COL_1,4) AS yr, COUNT(*) AS nti_tests FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_NTI_CNS_DPRK_MISSILE_TESTS WHERE COL_0<>'F1' GROUP BY 1), nagix AS (SELECT TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT)::VARCHAR AS yr, REGEXP_COUNT(DATA,'[{]') AS nagix_tests FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_NAGIX_DPRK_MISSILE_TESTS) SELECT COALESCE(n.yr,g.yr) AS year, n.nti_tests, g.nagix_tests, (g.nagix_tests - n.nti_tests) AS diff FROM nti n FULL OUTER JOIN nagix g ON n.yr=g.yr ORDER BY 1
kept.evidence: Both sources cover 1984-2024 and match test-count exactly in 19 of 21 overlapping years (1984,1986,1990-93,1998,2006,2009,2012-21,2023). Only 2 years differ: 2022 (NTI 69 vs Nagix 73, +4) and 2024 (NTI 41 vs Nagix 45, +4). Critically NTI stops dead at 2024 -- it has ZERO 2025-2026 rows, while Nagix carries 11 tests in 2025 and 26 in 2026 (37 tests NTI entirely lacks). NOTE: the row COUNTS are deceiving -- NTI is 1-row-per-test (303 tests) but Nagix is 1-row-per-YEAR with tests nested as a Python-dict array in DATA (23 rows = 23 years); naive TRY_PARSE_JSON nulls out 13 of 23 years because of apostrophes in values, so I counted via REGEXP_COUNT(DATA,'{').
kept.why_matters: The exact 19-year agreement means these aren't two independent counts -- one is almost certainly derived from the other (or both from a common origin), which makes the +4/+4 divergence in 2022 and 2024 a real, investigable discrepancy rather than noise. And NTI is the 'official' think-tank source (Nuclear Threat Initiative) yet it is the STALE one: anyone querying current DPRK missile activity off NTI alone silently loses every 2025-2026 launch. Use Nagix for recency; reconcile the 2022/2024 deltas before publishing any count.

verdict.corrected_title: The two DPRK missile sources agree EXACTLY on ALL 21 shared years (not 19) -- the claimed 2022/2024 +4 deltas are a brace-counting artifact; the real story is NTI going stale, missing all 37 tests of 2025-2026 that Nagix has
verdict.notes:
Re-ran the exact candidate SQL: numbers reproduce verbatim (2022 diff +4, 2024 diff +4, 2025/2026 NTI null). BUT the +4/+4 is a CASTING/PARSING artifact, not a real discrepancy.

Root cause: the candidate counts Nagix tests via REGEXP_COUNT(DATA,'[{]'). I checked brace balance (open==close every year) and then counted the actual structured keys per record:
  REGEXP_COUNT(DATA,'.date.:'), '.time.:', '.missile.:'
For 21 of 23 years brace==date_keys==missile_keys. For EXACTLY the two 'divergent' years they split:
  2022: braces=73 but date=time=missile=69
  2024: braces=45 but date=time=missile=41
So Nagix's true test count is 69 and 41 -- IDENTICAL to NTI (69, 41). The 4 extra braces/year are stray '{' chars inside string values (not coordinates/location/details/source keys -- checked, all 0), so they are not new test records. Re-running the diff with REGEXP_COUNT(DATA,'.date.:') leaves ZERO divergent overlapping years; the only rows that differ are 2025 (11) and 2026 (26), both NTI-null.

Net: agreement is 21/21, not 19/21. The candidate's headline 'real, investigable +4/+4 discrepancy in 2022/2024' is REFUTED -- it's the exact failure mode the brace-count method introduces.

What SURVIVES and is solid FACT: NTI (303 tests, 1-row-per-test, COL_1=date, 1 'F1' header row correctly excluded) stops dead at 2024 and carries zero 2025/2026 rows; Nagix (1-row-per-year, tests nested in DATA) carries 11 in 2025 + 26 in 2026 = 37 launches NTI entirely lacks. Both cover 1984-2024 with perfect per-year agreement, which still strongly implies common lineage. Tier stays FACT (both are HARD per-source counts, no cross-ID/name match involved). Downgraded because the most surprising/investigable sub-claim is an artifact; remaining staleness finding is real but lower-stakes (two tiny stub-ish sources, 304 + 23 rows). Practical takeaway unchanged: query current DPRK launches off Nagix, not NTI -- but there is nothing to 'reconcile' in 2022/2024.

kept.title: USASpending top-tier agency budget shares sum to only 95.5% and include a NEGATIVE percentage
kept.sql: SELECT SUM(TRY_CAST(PERCENTAGE_OF_TOTAL_BUDGET_AUTHORITY AS DOUBLE)) sum_pct, COUNT(*) n, COUNT(DISTINCT ACTIVE_FY) fys, MAX(TRY_CAST(PERCENTAGE_OF_TOTAL_BUDGET_AUTHORITY AS DOUBLE)) maxpct, MIN(TRY_CAST(PERCENTAGE_OF_TOTAL_BUDGET_AUTHORITY AS DOUBLE)) minpct FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_USASPENDING_TOPTIER_AGENCIES
kept.evidence: 111 agencies, all one fiscal year (COUNT(DISTINCT ACTIVE_FY)=1). PERCENTAGE_OF_TOTAL_BUDGET_AUTHORITY sums to 0.9549 (95.5%, not 100%), max = 0.2522 (HHS), and MIN = -0.000729 (a negative budget share). Top shares: HHS 25.2% ($3.38T), Treasury 19.4% ($2.59T), DOD 15.3% ($2.05T).
kept.why_matters: The percentages are self-inconsistent: they don't total 100 and one agency carries a negative share (a net-negative budget authority, plausible for a rescission/financing account but a landmine for anyone treating the column as a clean share-of-total). Use the raw BUDGET_AUTHORITY_AMOUNT and recompute shares rather than trusting the supplied percentage.
kept.implies: none -- recompute budget shares from BUDGET_AUTHORITY_AMOUNT in mart; do not trust supplied percentage

verdict.corrected_title: USASpending FY2026 top-tier agency budget shares sum to 95.5% (not 100%) and include one negative share (FCC, -0.073%) because the denominator is a fixed government-wide total ($13.40T) larger than the sum of the 111 agencies' budget authority ($12.79T)
verdict.notes:
All numbers reproduce exactly. SUM(pct)=0.9549, n=111, COUNT(DISTINCT ACTIVE_FY)=1 (FY=2026), MAX=0.25225 (HHS), MIN=-0.00072899 (FCC). No casting artifact: all 111 rows cast cleanly to DOUBLE (n_castok=111), and TRY_CAST nulls nothing.

Root cause of the 95.5% gap (candidate didn't fully explain it): CURRENT_TOTAL_BUDGET_AUTHORITY_AMOUNT is a CONSTANT $13,397,064,591,776.7 across all 111 rows — the official government-wide total. The supplied PERCENTAGE_OF_TOTAL_BUDGET_AUTHORITY equals BUDGET_AUTHORITY_AMOUNT / that constant EXACTLY for every row (max abs diff = 0.0). The 111 agencies' budget authority sums to only $12,793,160,937,564, i.e. $603.9B (4.507%) less than the government-wide total. So the shares don't sum to 100% by design — the denominator includes ~$604B not attributed to these top-tier agencies, NOT a data error.

Negative share is real: FCC BUDGET_AUTHORITY_AMOUNT = -$9,766,355,744.16 (net-negative, plausibly offsetting collections from spectrum auctions). Exactly 1 negative row. Several agencies (GSA, Commission of Fine Arts, etc.) show exactly $0.00.

One wording quibble in the candidate's 'why it matters': it advises recomputing shares from raw BUDGET_AUTHORITY_AMOUNT — but that yields IDENTICAL percentages (the column is already internally consistent). The column is NOT broken; the only gotcha is that it is a share of a fixed government-wide total, so it legitimately does not sum to 100 across these rows. Core anomaly stands; FACT grade is appropriate (no entity/ID matching involved, just arithmetic on one table). Surprise/importance moderate: it's a real definitional landmine for anyone treating the column as a clean partition-of-100, but it's a single-table metadata quirk, not an investigative lead.

kept.title: NOAA storm property damage is recorded almost entirely in round powers-of-ten ($1K/$5K/$10K dominate) -- estimates, not measurements
kept.sql: SELECT DAMAGE_PROPERTY, COUNT(*) c FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_NOAA_STORM_EVENTS WHERE NULLIF(DAMAGE_PROPERTY,'') IS NOT NULL AND DAMAGE_PROPERTY<>'0.00K' AND DAMAGE_PROPERTY<>'0' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY c DESC LIMIT 15
kept.evidence: Top non-zero DAMAGE_PROPERTY values are all round: 1.00K x3,328, 5.00K x1,648, 2.00K x1,618, 10.00K x1,175, 3.00K x849, 0.50K x709, 20.00K x538, 50.00K x405, 25.00K x387, 15.00K x386, 100.00K x256. Every single one of the top 15 is a round 1/2/5 x power-of-ten figure -- no organic non-round amounts appear until far down the tail.
kept.why_matters: Property-damage figures in this dataset are field-estimated round guesses, not appraisals, so any dollar-weighted analysis (total damage by state/event type, damage-vs-deaths correlation) is built on order-of-magnitude estimates. Treat DAMAGE_PROPERTY as ordinal/bucketed, never as precise currency.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: NOAA storm DAMAGE_PROPERTY is bucketed estimates, not appraisals: ~87% of nonzero values are round 1/2/5 x power-of-ten, and 57% of all rows are flat $0 -- treat as ordinal, never precise currency
verdict.notes:
VERDICT CONFIRMED. The exact candidate SQL reproduces to the row (1.00K x3328, 5.00K x1648, 2.00K x1618, 10.00K x1175, ... 0.25K x160). Stress-tested beyond the top-15 and it holds: only 259 DISTINCT nonzero values cover all 14,427 nonzero rows -- itself a bucketing tell. Mantissa analysis: core round values {1,2,3,4,5,10,15,20,25,30,50,100 x10^n} = 11,174/14,427 = 77.5%; adding half-steps {0.5,0.25,1.5,2.5,...} = 12,590/14,427 = 87.3%. The remaining ~12% are still 2-sig-fig roundings (1.1K x55, 12K x62, 11K x23). Genuinely precise organic appraisals DO exist (29.81K, 5.89M, 16.13M, 3.81M, 1.92M) but are almost all single occurrences far in the tail -- exactly as the candidate claimed ('no organic non-round amounts until far down the tail').

NOT an artifact: this is a string-grouped distribution, no TRY_CAST silently nulling rows for the count (the GROUP BY is on raw text); I separately TRY_CAST the mantissa only to classify roundness and bad casts would have shown as nulls, not as false roundness. No name/ID matching involved -- pure single-table column profiling, so FACT tier is correct and there is no libel surface.

Two refinements baked into the corrected_title: (1) The candidate's SQL/top-15 only sees the K suffix and misses M (259 rows) and B (1 row) values -- but those follow the SAME round pattern (1.5M, 1.2M, 2.5M), so the conclusion is unchanged and actually reinforced. (2) The candidate understates the bigger problem: 41,534/72,360 rows (57%) are flat '0.00K' and 16,399 (23%) are blank -- so only 20% of the table even has a nonzero damage figure. Any dollar-weighted total-damage analysis is built on ~20% coverage of order-of-magnitude estimates, which is a stronger caveat than 'estimates not measurements.' Importance 6 (matters for any damage-weighted storm analysis); surprise only 4 -- field-estimated NWS storm damage being round is a known property of NOAA Storm Events Database, not a novel discovery, but it is correctly measured here.

kept.title: intl_freedomhouse is structurally broken: header row landed as DATA, real 43 columns dumped into generic COL_N, first column named after the spreadsheet title
kept.sql: DESCRIBE TABLE LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_FREEDOMHOUSE;  -- 47 cols, first = FREEDOM_IN_THE_WORLD_2013_2025_RAW_DATA then COL_1..COL_43.  Then: SELECT FREEDOM_IN_THE_WORLD_2013_2025_RAW_DATA C0, COL_1, COL_2, COL_3, COL_4, COL_5 FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_FREEDOMHOUSE LIMIT 6;
kept.evidence: Column names are FREEDOM_IN_THE_WORLD_2013_2025_RAW_DATA (the sheet title) + COL_1..COL_43 (all generic). Row 1 of data literally contains the real header: 'Country/Territory | Region | C/T | Edition | Status | PR rating'. The true field names (Country/Territory, Region, Status, PR rating, CL rating) live in row data, not column headers. The xlsx title-row offset was not skipped on ingest.
kept.why_matters: 2,724 rows of Freedom House Freedom-in-the-World scores are present but unqueryable by field -- you cannot SELECT status or rating without manually re-mapping COL_N. Any staging model built on this will silently mis-map columns. Needs re-ingest with header offset = title row skipped.
kept.implies: new source to load (re-parse with correct header row; skip the title banner)

verdict.corrected_title: intl_freedomhouse landed with the xlsx title row as headers: first column named FREEDOM_IN_THE_WORLD_2013_2025_RAW_DATA (the sheet title), the other 43 dumped into generic COL_N, and the real 44-field header (Country/Territory, Region, Edition, Status, PR/CL rating...) sits in row 1 of the DATA
verdict.notes: Fully reproduces. DESCRIBE returns 47 cols: FREEDOM_IN_THE_WORLD_2013_2025_RAW_DATA + COL_1..COL_43 + 3 provenance stamps = 44 data columns. The first data row literally is the real header: 'Country/Territory | Region | C/T | Edition | Status | PR rating | CL rating | A1 ...' through to the tail 'G2|G3|G4|G|CL|Total' (standard Freedom House sub-indicator codes), so all 44 true field names live in data not schema. COUNT(*) = 2,724 total, exactly 1 row where C0='Country/Territory' (single embedded header), 14 distinct editions (13 years 2013-2025 + the word 'Edition'). Data itself is clean and good: COL_4 (the real Status field) profiles to F=1131 / PF=804 / NF=788 = 2,723 data rows, the canonical Free/Partly-Free/Not-Free split. So the data is intact but you cannot SELECT by field name — you must hand-map COL_N offsets off the embedded header. This is the classic xlsx title-row-not-skipped ingest bug. No casting artifact (it's a pure schema/naming fact), no cross-source or name-match, no libel exposure -> stays FACT. Tiny wording nit: candidate says 'real 43 columns' but it's 44 data columns (the sheet-title column also holds Country/Territory data) -- corrected_title fixes the count. Importance 6, not higher: it's one of several modeled/landed sources with ingest quirks, easily fixed by a re-ingest with header offset = title-row skip; surprise modest because title-row offset is a known xlsx failure mode.

kept.title: fed_nara_aad scraped the search FORM, not the archive -- 554 rows, every structured column blank, only JSON holds UI dropdown labels
kept.sql: SELECT COUNT(*) n, COUNT(DISTINCT DATASET_ID) ds_id, SUM(CASE WHEN NULLIF(PERSON_NAME,'') IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) null_name, SUM(CASE WHEN NULLIF(SERIES_TITLE,'') IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) null_series, SUM(CASE WHEN NULLIF(RAW_FIELDS_JSON,'') IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) null_json FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_NARA_AAD;  -- then: SELECT LEFT(RAW_FIELDS_JSON,160) FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_NARA_AAD LIMIT 3;
kept.evidence: 554 rows. PERSON_NAME, SERIES_TITLE, DATASET_NAME, DATE, GEO_LOCATION are ALL null for all 554 rows (null_name=554, null_series=554). Only RAW_FIELDS_JSON is populated (null_json=0) and it contains form-control text: {"0": "with all of the values", "1": "nan"}, {"0": "with any of the values"}, {"0": "with the exact phrase"} -- these are AAD search-page dropdown options, not archival records.
kept.why_matters: The source claims 554 NARA Access to Archival Databases records but landed the website's search interface instead. It is a 100% coverage failure that looks like real data by row count. Detectors or marts touching it would join on entirely null keys.
kept.implies: new source to load (the AAD scraper hit the form page, not result pages -- re-scout the actual query/result endpoint)

verdict.corrected_title: fed_nara_aad landed AAD series-description/finding-aid pages, not archival records -- 554 rows, all record-level key columns null (only DATASET_ID populated), zero person/date/series data, JSON holds page metadata + form labels + 4 HTTP 404s
verdict.notes:
Reproduced exactly: 554 rows, 9 distinct DATASET_ID, null_name=554, null_series=554, null_dsname=554, null_date=554, null_geo=554, null_json=0. The COVERAGE-FAILURE core is REAL and FACT-grade: no actual archival records were captured -- PERSON_NAME, SERIES_TITLE, DATASET_NAME, DATE, GEO_LOCATION, RECORD_ID, RECORD_GROUP are 100% null. Any detector/mart joining on a record key would hit all-null.

But TWO claims in the candidate are WRONG and force a downgrade:
1) "every structured column blank" is FALSE. DATASET_ID is populated on 550/554 rows with 8 real AAD numeric dataset IDs (625 n=164, 408 n=141, 503, 4073, 5057, 5218, 3360, 5259). DESCRIPTION_TEXT is populated on 4 rows (literal "HTTP 404 fetching https://aad.archives.gov/aad/series-description..." error strings). So 'every structured column blank' should read 'every RECORD-LEVEL column blank; DATASET_ID present'.
2) "scraped the search FORM / only JSON holds UI dropdown labels" is MOSTLY WRONG. Profiling LEFT(RAW_FIELDS_JSON,55) shows the dropdown labels ("with all of the values"/"with any of the values"/"with the exact phrase"/"without the values") are only ~32 rows. The BULK is series-description / finding-aid PAGE content: series titles (Ownership Reporting System (ORS) n=137, Defense Contract Action Data System n=32, Federal Assistance Award Data System quarterly n=29x4, Application (SS-5) Files) plus finding-aid metadata labels (Scope & Content Note:, Access Restrictions:, Type of Archival Materials:, Location: National Archives at College Park, Finding Aid Type:, Level of Description:). So the scraper hit AAD series-DESCRIPTION pages, not the search form.

Net: the headline coverage-gap conclusion stands (publishable FACT that this source carries zero usable archival records), but the specific mechanism ('search form'/'dropdown labels') and the 'all columns blank' wording are inaccurate -- corrected title reflects what actually landed. Importance is modest: it's a 554-row dead-end source with no hard join keys, so the blast radius on any investigation is small.

kept.title: intl_opensanctions has sanctioned 'persons' born in the FUTURE (up to 2068) -- a 2-digit-year parse bug in the Belgian Financial Sanctions feed
kept.sql: SELECT NAME, BIRTH_DATE, SCHEMA, DATASET FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_OPENSANCTIONS WHERE TRY_CAST(LEFT(BIRTH_DATE,4) AS INT) > 2026 ORDER BY BIRTH_DATE DESC LIMIT 10;
kept.evidence: 5 rows have BIRTH_DATE year > 2026, all SCHEMA='Person', all DATASET='Belgian Financial Sanctions': Abdelaziz BOULHIR 2068-07-16, Salim YOUCEF 2066-09-16, Jasmine ... DETEMMERMAN 2066-05-25, Nacera BOUKEBBOUS 2065-02-17, Oumar ... MAKHMOUDOV 2064-10-23. Overall BIRTH_DATE span 1921-05-06 to 2068-07-16; 6 rows pre-1900 too. Classic 2-digit-year rollover ('68' parsed as 2068 instead of 1968).
kept.why_matters: Impossible birth dates in a sanctions screening source. Any age/identity matching against these records would mis-resolve. It localizes a specific upstream parse defect (Belgian feed) rather than a Ripple ingest error -- worth a NOTES quirk in the registry and a staging-time year correction.
kept.implies: new detector / staging rule (clamp 2-digit-year birthdates; flag future DOBs)

verdict.corrected_title: intl_opensanctions has 5 sanctioned Persons with impossible future birth dates (2064-2068), all from the Belgian Financial Sanctions feed -- consistent with a 2-digit-year rollover, but the mechanism is unproven
verdict.notes:
CORE FACT HOLDS, reproduced exactly: 5 rows with BIRTH_DATE year >2026, all SCHEMA='Person', all DATASET='Belgian Financial Sanctions', all single clean 10-char dates, blank COUNTRIES, all FIRST_SEEN 2025-12-01: BOULHIR 2068-07-16, YOUCEF 2066-09-16, DETEMMERMAN 2066-05-25, BOUKEBBOUS 2065-02-17, MAKHMOUDOV 2064-10-23. Max BIRTH_DATE = 2068-07-16 confirmed. This is FACT-grade as an existence claim (impossible future dates in a sanctions source). 71,011 total rows, 5 future.

THREE PROBLEMS that warranted downgrade:
(1) The MIN-date claim is WRONG. Candidate says span '1921-05-06 to 2068'. Actual MIN(BIRTH_DATE) = '' (blank). 6,778 rows have a bare 4-char year, and many rows are multi-date concatenations (lengths 21/32/103 etc). The low end of the stated span is fabricated/imprecise.

(2) The '6 rows pre-1900' are MISDIAGNOSED as the same 2-digit-year bug. They are actually Persian/Solar-Hijri calendar years concatenated with their Gregorian equivalents and multi-date fields: e.g. Hosein SALIMI '1339;1960;1961', Mohammad Pakpour '1340;1961', Leila Vaseghi '1352;1972;1973', Al-Asiri '1402-06-24;1982-04-18;...', AHMED JAN WAZIR '1658;1953;1954...'. LEFT(BIRTH_DATE,4) just grabs the first (Hijri or junk) value. That is a DIFFERENT phenomenon (multi-calendar / multi-value), not a rollover. Lumping them in is wrong.

(3) The specific MECHANISM ('68 parsed as 2068 instead of 1968') is plausible but UNPROVEN. Future years cluster 2064-2068; a YY>=64 -> 19YY pivot would produce 1964-1968, which is demographically sane for sanctioned persons. But a pivot at ~64 is atypical and we have no upstream evidence it is that exact bug rather than data-entry error. State the impossible dates as FACT; state the rollover cause as a LEAD/hypothesis.

Importance is modest: only 5 records, and any sane age-matcher would reject a future date anyway. Worth a registry NOTES quirk + a staging-time sanity filter on BIRTH_DATE year.

kept.title: INTL_GR_GEMI is not a company registry -- it's 40 rows of scraped website navigation chrome (every business field blank)
kept.sql: SELECT GEMI_NUMBER, AFM, STATUS, LEGAL_FORM, CITY, COMPANY_NAME, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_GR_GEMI GROUP BY 1,2,3,4,5,6 ORDER BY n DESC LIMIT 10
kept.evidence: 40 total rows. COUNT(DISTINCT) of GEMI_NUMBER, AFM, STATUS, LEGAL_FORM, CITY all = 1 (a single value: blank). COMPANY_NAME holds UI boilerplate, not companies: 'Politiki Aporritou' (Privacy Policy), 'Politiki Cookies' (Cookie Policy), 'Designed & Developed by | Knowledge AE', '(c) | KENTRIKI ENOSI EPIMELITIRION ELLADOS 2026' (copyright footer), 'Anazitisi me stoixeia epicheiriseon' (search form labels). The loader captured the page's menu/footer, never the registry rows.
kept.why_matters: This source is cataloged as a Greek business registry (join key NAME) but carries ZERO usable entity data -- it's a failed scrape. Any cross-source entity matching that trusts it will silently match against privacy-policy strings. Should be flagged 'empty'/broken in the catalog, not counted as a landed source.
kept.implies: new detector: flag landed sources where every key column collapses to <=1 distinct value (degenerate-load detector); re-scrape GEMI with the actual search/detail endpoint

verdict.corrected_title: INTL_GR_GEMI is a failed scrape: all 40 rows have every business field (GEMI_NUMBER, AFM, STATUS, LEGAL_FORM, CITY + 12 others) blank; the only populated column, COMPANY_NAME, holds 7 distinct Greek UI/footer strings, not companies
verdict.notes: Reproduced exactly. COUNT(*)=40. Distinct (raw) GEMI_NUMBER/AFM/STATUS/LEGAL_FORM/CITY all = 1, and with NULLIF(col,'') the non-blank distinct count = 0 for ALL 16 business columns -- i.e. every substantive field is 100% empty, not just the five the candidate sampled. Verified DISTINCTIVE_TITLE, REGISTRATION_SUSPENSION, SPECIAL_DESIGNATIONS, LOCAL_GEMI_OFFICE, FORMATION_DATE, CLOSURE_DATE, KAK_CHANGE_DATE, POSTAL_CODE all non-blank-distinct = 0 too. COMPANY_NAME has 7 distinct values (candidate said 5; it slightly UNDER-claimed). All 7 translate to page chrome: 'Search by business details' (search heading, appears 10x), 'All | Name | Distinctive Title' (filter dropdown), 'Designed & Developed by | Knowledge AE' (footer), 'Privacy Policy', 'Cookie Policy', '© | Central Union of Chambers of Commerce of Greece 2026' (copyright footer), 'The business search to be applied to:' (form label). Zero real company names. No casting artifact (it's all-TEXT, observed raw; blanks are literal '' not NULL, confirmed via NULLIF). No fuzzy/name match involved -- this is a direct structural fact about one table, so FACT tier is correct and there's no libel surface. Tier stands. Surprise is modest (4): 'a scrape grabbed the page nav/footer instead of the table' is a common failure mode, and the source is a 40-row stub already flagged-ish in the inventory; the value is the receipt that NAME (its cataloged join key) is unusable. Importance 6: matters because the catalog lists it as a Greek registry with join key NAME -- any name-based cross-source matching would silently hit privacy-policy strings -- but it's a tiny isolated source, so capped below high. Candidate's 'should be flagged empty/broken, not counted as landed' recommendation is sound.

kept.title: NOAA AIS HEADING column is ~53% the AIS sentinel 511 ('not available') -- the majority of bearings are placeholders
kept.sql: SELECT HEADING, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_NOAA_AIS SAMPLE (100000 ROWS) GROUP BY HEADING ORDER BY n DESC LIMIT 8
kept.evidence: On a 100k-row sample, HEADING=511.0 appears 52,709 times (52.7%) -- the single most common value by 100x. 511 is the AIS standard 'heading not available' code. The next values (0.0, 269.0, 359.0...) each appear only 250-330 times. So real heading exists for under half the pings.
kept.why_matters: 511 is a true-vs-sentinel trap: anyone TRY_CASTing HEADING to DOUBLE and averaging it will get a number dominated by 511s, badly skewing any course/orientation analysis. Must be filtered to <360 before use.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: NOAA AIS HEADING is the AIS sentinel 511 ('not available') in 52.46% of all 7.30M rows -- the majority of bearings are placeholders; naive AVG returns 356.86 vs 186.80 once filtered to <360
verdict.notes:
Holds on the full table, not just the sample. Full-table counts: 7,296,275 total rows; HEADING='511.0' in 3,827,373 (52.46%); valid 0-359.99 in 3,468,898 (47.54%); exactly 4 stray rows >=360 (and !=511); 0 NULL/blank and 0 non-castable rows. The 100k sample reproduces (52,531 vs the candidate's claimed 52,709 -- pure sampling variance; full-table share is 52.46%, so the title's '~53%' is a hair high but within rounding).

Actively tried to break it and could not:
- NOT a casting artifact: value is stored as the literal text '511.0'; there is no integer-format '511' variant, no blanks, no nulls, and TRY_CAST nulls nothing (0 non-castable). The sentinel is genuine source data, not a parse failure.
- The skew warning is real and severe: AVG(TRY_CAST(HEADING AS DOUBLE)) over all rows = 356.86 (a meaningless near-north value), vs AVG over <360 = 186.80. The 511s drag the mean up ~170 degrees. Anyone averaging course/orientation without a <360 filter gets garbage.
- If anything the candidate UNDERstated the dominance: it said the next values are 250-330 and 511 is ~100x; in my sample the top valid bin (0.0) is 342, making 511 ~150x, not 100x.

Tier FACT is correct: this is a pure single-column data-quality property (the documented AIS 'heading unavailable' code 511), no entity/name/cross-ID claim, so zero libel surface. Surprise is low-ish -- 511 is a well-known AIS standard sentinel, so it's expected to anyone who knows the spec -- but importance is solid because it's a live true-vs-sentinel trap for any downstream course analysis on a 7.3M-row table. Recommend the column always be filtered HEADING<360 (or =511 excluded) before any aggregation.

kept.title: OWID temperature anomaly already contains 2026 values -- a partial/in-progress year published as if complete
kept.sql: SELECT ENTITY, YEAR, AVERAGE FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_OWID_TEMP_ANOMALY WHERE TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT) >= 2024 ORDER BY ENTITY, YEAR
kept.evidence: Year range is 1850-2026 across 3 entities (World, Northern Hemisphere, Southern Hemisphere). 2026 rows exist: World=1.396, NH=1.683, SH=1.110. Today's date is 2026-06-27, so 2026 is barely half-elapsed yet already has an 'annual' anomaly value. Note the World figure DROPS from 2024 (1.533) -> 2025 (1.414) -> 2026 (1.396).
kept.why_matters: A year-over-year warming chart drawn off this will show an apparent 2024-2026 cooling, but 2025/2026 are incomplete-year partials, not settled annual means -- comparing them to full historical years is apples-to-oranges. Anyone trending the tail of this series needs to cap at the last complete year (2023/2024) or flag the partials.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: OWID temperature anomaly carries a 2026 partial-year value (wide CI confirms it's in-progress) published alongside settled annual means — World tail reads as cooling 2024-2026
verdict.notes:
Reproduced exactly. 3 entities (World/OWID_WRL, Northern Hemisphere/OWID_NH, Southern Hemisphere/OWID_SH), each 177 dense rows 1850-2026, zero year gaps (MIN=1850, MAX=2026, DISTINCT=177). 2026 AVERAGE: World=1.3962804, NH=1.6828276, SH=1.1097331 — candidate's rounded figures (1.396/1.683/1.110) are accurate. World tail monotonic: 2024=1.5334455 > 2025=1.4137621 > 2026=1.3962804, so the apparent cooling is real.

STRENGTHENED, not refuted: the 2026 row's LOWER/UPPER_BOUND band is ~3x wider than prior years (World 2026: 1.268-1.525, span 0.257; vs 2025: 1.374-1.453, span 0.079; 2024: 1.494-1.573, span 0.079). NH 2026 span 0.489 vs ~0.10 prior. That wide CI is the data-backed signature of a partial/few-months-elapsed estimate — much stronger than the candidate's calendar-only inference. This is a single-table structural fact (FACT grade, no cross-source/ID match, no casting artifact: YEAR casts cleanly, no blanks/zeros).

One wording fix: 'cooling' only holds for World and NH (NH 1.919->1.723->1.683). Southern Hemisphere actually ticks UP 2025->2026 (1.1047537 -> 1.1097331). So 'apparent 2024-2026 cooling chart' is true for World/NH but NOT universal — don't generalize across all three entities. Caveat: I could not confirm OWID's exact methodology for the partial year (whether it's YTD mean or a forecast) from the table alone; the wide CI is consistent with either. Practical takeaway stands: cap charts at last complete year (2024) or flag 2025/2026 partials.

kept.title: CDC drug-poisoning county death rate is a binned TEXT range, not a number - and 1999/2014/2015 silently drop 1-2 counties
kept.sql: SELECT YEAR, COUNT(*) n, COUNT(DISTINCT FIPS) counties FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CDC_DRUG_POISONING_COUNTY GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT)
kept.evidence: Clean panel: 53,387 rows = ~3,141 counties x 17 years (1999-2015), every year fully covered. BUT the rate column is pre-binned text: ESTIMATED_AGE_ADJUSTED_DEATH_RATE_11_CATEGORIES_IN_RANGES has only 16 distinct values ('6.1-8' 8222x, '4.1-6' 8147x, ... '>30' 551x) - there is NO numeric death rate, only CDC's suppression bins. County counts wobble: 1999-2002 = 3139, 2003-2013 = 3141, 2014-2015 = 3140, so the panel is not perfectly balanced (FIPS boundary changes / suppression).
kept.why_matters: Anyone expecting a continuous overdose mortality rate here gets disappointed - it is CDC's censored 11-category band only, so you can rank/bucket counties but cannot compute real rates, trends, or sums. The 1-2 county wobble per year means a balanced-panel join (county x year) will lose a handful of cells. Cast/expectation trap worth a NOTES entry.
kept.implies: registry NOTES: rate is binned text, not numeric; treat as ordinal category

verdict.corrected_title: CDC drug-poisoning county rate is a binned TEXT range (16 bins, 0 numeric), not a death rate; the 3139/3141/3140 county wobble is 3 real FIPS boundary-change counties, not suppression
verdict.notes:
Every number in the candidate reproduces EXACTLY. (1) Year/county counts: 1999-2002=3139, 2003-2013=3141, 2014-2015=3140 — confirmed. (2) Row total 53,387 = 3139*4 + 3141*11 + 3140*2 reconciles to the cell. FIPS||YEAR is unique (53,387 distinct pairs = 53,387 rows), 3,141 total distinct counties (US county count). (3) Rate column ESTIMATED_AGE_ADJUSTED_DEATH_RATE_11_CATEGORIES_IN_RANGES: exactly 16 distinct text bins, 0 of 53,387 parse as DOUBLE (TRY_CAST). Top bins match: '6.1-8'=8222, '4.1-6'=8147, '8.1-10'=6947, '>30'=551. So the FACT 'binned text, no numeric rate' fully holds.

ONE CORRECTION (why DOWNGRADED wording but still FACT-grade on the data): the candidate hedged the wobble as 'FIPS boundary changes / suppression.' It is NOT suppression. There are ZERO blank/null rate rows (0 of 53,387) — no in-county censoring exists in this file; every present county-year has a bin. The wobble is fully explained by 3 specific counties absent in some years: 08014 Broomfield County CO (present 13 yrs, created as a county in 2001, appears from 2003 -> explains 1999-2002=3139), 51515 Bedford city VA (present 15 yrs, reverted to town/merged into Bedford County 2013 -> explains 2014-2015=3140), 02068 Denali Borough AK (present 13 yrs). These are genuine geography/boundary events, not statistical suppression. So 'silently drop 1-2 counties' is real and a legitimate balanced-panel gotcha, but the cause is FIPS lifecycle, not censoring.

NOTE for users: POPULATION column IS numeric (53,383 of 53,387 parse as DOUBLE), so you CAN weight/aggregate population even though the rate itself is only an ordinal band. Candidate's 'there is NO numeric death rate' is correct as scoped to the rate column. Surprise/importance modest — it's a real cast-trap NOTES entry, not a smoking gun.

kept.title: fed_naag_multistate_settlements is a FAILED SCRAPE: 26 'rows' are website chrome (Create Account, Home, Website by Yoko Co), zero settlement data
kept.sql: SELECT COMPANY_ID, COMPANY_NAME, DATE, SETTLEMENT_AMOUNT, NUM_STATES, INDUSTRY FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_NAAG_MULTISTATE_SETTLEMENTS LIMIT 30; -- and -- SELECT COUNT(*) n, COUNT(DISTINCT COMPANY_ID) n_ids, COUNT(DISTINCT COMPANY_NAME) n_names, SUM(CASE WHEN SETTLEMENT_AMOUNT IS NULL OR SETTLEMENT_AMOUNT='' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) blank_amt FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_NAAG_MULTISTATE_SETTLEMENTS
kept.evidence: 26 rows total. SETTLEMENT_AMOUNT blank in 26/26 (100%). COMPANY_ID has 1 distinct value (all blank); COMPANY_NAME has only 6 distinct values and they are page navigation strings: 'Home', 'Create Account', 'Website by Yoko Co', 'To submit suggestions or additions for the database, plea...', '2022.02.13-Executive Summary of National Opioid Settlement', plus blanks. DATE, NUM_STATES, INDUSTRY all empty. Not a single real settlement record landed.
kept.why_matters: This source is cataloged as a landed dataset but contains zero usable data -- the scraper grabbed the NAAG site's HTML menu instead of the settlements table. Any query, join, or detector touching multistate AG settlements silently returns nothing. It should be re-scoped to LIFECYCLE=empty/failed, not counted among the 76 landed sources.
kept.implies: new source to load: re-scrape NAAG settlements with a parser that targets the data table, not nav chrome

verdict.corrected_title: fed_naag_multistate_settlements is a FAILED SCRAPE: all 26 rows are NAAG website chrome (Menu, Home, Create Account, Website by Yoko Co, footer/help text), zero settlement records; every structured field is 100% empty
verdict.notes:
Re-ran the full 26-row dump and the candidate's aggregate SQL; all numbers reproduce exactly. SETTLEMENT_AMOUNT, DATE, NUM_STATES, INDUSTRY, STATE, LEAD_STATE are blank in 26/26 rows. COMPANY_ID has 1 distinct value (all blank). COMPANY_NAME has 6 distinct values (5 non-blank), all navigation/document strings ('Home', 'Create Account', 'Website by Yoko Co', the submit-suggestions help line, the opioid-settlement exec-summary doc title) — none is a defendant. Visually confirmed the row content is site menu / footer ('1850 M Street NW... Washington, DC 20036', '© 2026 Copyright National Association of Attorneys General', 'No search term specified. Showing recent items'). Catalog confirms LIFECYCLE='landed', TRUST_LAYER='staging', so the 'wrongly counted among landed sources' claim holds.

Two refinements, neither weakens it: (1) The table actually has 10 data columns (also STATE, CASE_DESCRIPTION, LEAD_STATE, DOCUMENT_URL), more than the 6 the candidate named. The chrome text actually lives in CASE_DESCRIPTION and DOCUMENT_URL (e.g. 'https://www.yokoco.com/', 'https://www.naag.org/createanaccount'); the candidate's LIMIT-30 SELECT pulled a subset of columns but reached the right conclusion. This strengthens the finding — every parsed/structured field is empty, only the raw-HTML-text columns caught junk. (2) No casting artifact risk: blanks are literal '' (not nulled by TRY_CAST); I used NULLIF(col,'') and the empties are genuine. Tier correctly FACT: within-source data-quality observation, no entity accused, full census of all 26 rows (not a sample). Surprise low (4) — these tiny 'modeled/landed' stubs are a known repo issue ('Several modeled sources are 1-row stubs'); importance 6 because it's silently counted among the 76 landed sources and any AG-settlement detector/join returns nothing.

kept.title: Ransomware-victims feed is exploding and live: 2024-2026 hold 18,864 of 19,005 dated rows; first half of 2026 already beats all of 2022
kept.sql: SELECT LEFT(DISCOVERED,4) AS yr, COUNT(*) AS n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_RANSOMWARELIVE_VICTIMS GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
kept.evidence: Discovered-year counts: 2013-2019 sum to ~141 rows; then 2020=247, 2021=1775, 2022=2837, 2023=5329, 2024=6111, 2025=8146, 2026=4607 (through ~mid-year, ingest date 2026-06-27). 2026's partial half-year (4607) already exceeds full-year 2022 (2837). Country is null on 7911/29193 rows (27%), so geographic analysis is partial.
kept.why_matters: This is a near-real-time leak-site victim tracker that roughly doubled year-over-year through 2025 and is on pace to set another record in 2026 -- a clean, publishable curve of the ransomware extortion economy. The 27% null-country caveat must ride along with any map.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: Ransomware-victims feed is live and exploding: of 29,193 dated rows, 18,864 (65%) fall in 2024-2026; first half of 2026 (4,607) already beats all of 2022 (2,837)
verdict.notes:
The trend signal is REAL, single-SELECT reproducible, and FACT-grade (no cross-ID/name match involved). All the year buckets reproduce exactly: 2020=247, 2021=1775, 2022=2837, 2023=5329, 2024=6111, 2025=8146, 2026=4607, with 2013-2019 summing to ~141. YoY near-doubling through 2025 holds. 2026 is genuinely partial: monthly breakdown is Jan(702)..Jun(623), and MAX(DISCOVERED)=2026-06-27T18:28Z = the ingest timestamp, confirming the near-real-time leak-site tracker framing. 2026 H1's 4607 > full-year 2022's 2837 is correct. Country null = 7911/29193 = 27.1% -> caveat is exact. No duplicate-reload inflation: 28,699 distinct DISCOVERED timestamps out of 29,193 (row-per-victim-post). DISCOVERED has ZERO nulls/blanks and every value has a numeric 4-digit year prefix (TRY_CAST(LEFT(DISCOVERED,4) AS INT) is non-null for all rows) -- so no casting/text-sort artifact.

THE DEFECT (why DOWNGRADED, not CONFIRMED): the title's denominator '19,005 dated rows' is arithmetically wrong. The 14 year-buckets sum to 29,193 (the full table), not 19,005 -- I summed them: 8146+6111+5329+4607+2837+1775+247+55+39+21+16+6+2+2 = 29,193. Every single row is dated (0 nulls), so 'dated rows' = total rows = 29,193. The candidate's '18,864 of 19,005' (99%) should be '18,864 of 29,193' (65%). The absolute trend is untouched; only the recency-concentration fraction is overstated. Wording fix required before publish; underlying curve is publishable.

SQL receipts: SELECT LEFT(DISCOVERED,4) AS yr, COUNT(*) FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_RANSOMWARELIVE_VICTIMS GROUP BY 1  (14 rows, sums to 29193). SELECT SUM(CASE WHEN TRY_CAST(LEFT(DISCOVERED,4) AS INT) BETWEEN 2024 AND 2026 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END), COUNT(*) -> 18864, 29193. COUNT country null -> 7911/29193. COUNT(*) vs COUNT(DISTINCT DISCOVERED) -> 29193 vs 28699.

kept.title: SEC EDGAR company-tickers: CIK is NOT unique -- 10,414 rows but only 8,018 distinct CIKs (2,396 dup-CIK rows); BANK OF MONTREAL alone has 27 tickers
kept.sql: SELECT CIK_STR, TITLE, COUNT(*) n, LISTAGG(TICKER, ', ') tickers FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_SEC_EDGAR_COMPANY_TICKERS GROUP BY 1,2 ORDER BY 3 DESC LIMIT 8
kept.evidence: COUNT(*)=10,414, COUNT(DISTINCT CIK_STR)=8,018, COUNT(DISTINCT TITLE)=8,018, COUNT(DISTINCT TICKER)=10,414. So the grain is one row per TICKER, not per company. Top multi-ticker CIKs: BANK OF MONTREAL /CAN/ (CIK 927971) = 27 tickers, FREDDIE MAC (1026214) = 25, UBS AG (1114446) = 19, BANK OF AMERICA (70858) = 17. CIK -> ticker is one-to-many (multi-class shares, preferreds, ETNs).
kept.why_matters: CIK is the steel join key for SEC filers. If anyone joins another table to this on CIK expecting one row per company, they fan-out (e.g. join Bank of America once, get 17 copies). Tier any CIK-join through this table as needing DISTINCT or a ticker filter; the canonical company grain is TITLE/CIK, not the row.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: SEC EDGAR company_tickers: grain is one-row-per-TICKER, not per company -- 10,414 rows / 8,018 distinct CIK (=distinct TITLE, 1:1) / 10,414 distinct TICKER; BANK OF MONTREAL = 27 tickers, BofA = 17. CIK joins fan out.
verdict.notes: Every claim reproduced exactly. COUNT(*)=10,414, DISTINCT CIK_STR=8,018, DISTINCT TITLE=8,018, DISTINCT TICKER=10,414, dup-CIK rows=2,396. Top dup CIKs match to the row: BMO/CAN (927971)=27, Freddie Mac (1026214)=25, UBS AG (1114446)=19, BofA (70858)=17. Adversarial checks all passed: (a) no null/blank CIK or TICKER (0 rows); (b) TICKER has 0 duplicates -> grain truly = one row per ticker; (c) INDEX_KEY (positional 0..n key) = 10,414 distinct = true row identity; (d) CIK<->TITLE is perfectly 1:1 -- 0 CIKs map to >1 TITLE and CIK||TITLE = 8,018 distinct, so 'canonical company grain = TITLE/CIK' is literally correct. NO casting artifact: these are plain string COUNT DISTINCTs, no TRY_CAST involved, RAW-is-TEXT doesn't bite here. NO definitional fudge. NO libel surface (structural count, not an entity accusation).
