The multi-ticker rows are legit multi-class/preferred/ETN listings -- visible in LISTAGG (BAC-PB/BAC-PK preferreds, BMO's FNGU/FNGD/GDXD ETNs). Fan-out hazard is real: joining BofA on CIK yields 17 copies. Only downgrade pressure is surprise: this is the documented native structure of SEC's company_tickers.json (publicly one-row-per-ticker), so low surprise for anyone who knows the source -- but the join-hazard is a genuine, useful guardrail. FACT tier appropriate.

kept.title: fed_federal_register_documents is a 67-day window capped at exactly 5000 rows, not an archive -- and SIGNIFICANT + PRESIDENT columns are 100% blank
kept.sql: SELECT MIN(TRY_TO_DATE(PUBLICATION_DATE)) min_pub, MAX(TRY_TO_DATE(PUBLICATION_DATE)) max_pub, COUNT(*) n, COUNT(DISTINCT DOCUMENT_NUMBER) d FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_FEDERAL_REGISTER_DOCUMENTS;  -- then: SELECT SIGNIFICANT, COUNT(*) FROM ... GROUP BY 1;  SELECT PRESIDENT, COUNT(*) FROM ... GROUP BY 1
kept.evidence: 5000 rows, 5000 distinct DOCUMENT_NUMBER (clean key), but PUBLICATION_DATE spans only 2026-04-10 to 2026-06-16 -- a 67-day window. The round 5000 is a hard API page cap, not the true Federal Register volume. SIGNIFICANT column = blank in 5000/5000 rows; PRESIDENT column = blank in 5000/5000 rows, despite 49 'Presidential Document' type rows being present. Type mix: Notice 3991, Rule 619, Proposed Rule 341, Presidential Document 49.
kept.why_matters: Anyone treating this as 'the Federal Register' would get only ~2 months of 2026 and miss the entire historical corpus. Two metadata columns that should be populated (regulatory significance, signing president) are entirely empty, so they can't support any analysis.
kept.implies: new source to load (paginate Federal Register beyond the 5000 cap; backfill history)

verdict.corrected_title: fed_federal_register_documents is a "most-recent 5000" API-capped pull (2026-04-10 to 2026-06-16; 47 publication days), not an archive -- and SIGNIFICANT + PRESIDENT columns are 100% empty (blank), including all 49 Presidential Documents
verdict.notes:
Every sub-claim reproduces exactly. Counts: 5000 rows, 5000 distinct DOCUMENT_NUMBER (clean key, no dupes), 0 null PUBLICATION_DATEs, span 2026-04-10..2026-06-16. SIGNIFICANT and PRESIDENT both COUNT()=5000 non-null but 100% empty STRING (NULLIF(col,'') IS NOT NULL = 0 for both) -- so they are blank '' not NULL, but functionally unusable either way. Type mix exact: Notice 3991 / Rule 619 / Proposed Rule 341 / Presidential Document 49. All 49 Presidential Documents have blank PRESIDENT AND blank SUBTYPE -- those are fields the FR API does populate for that type, so the emptiness is a loader field-mapping gap, not a true source absence.

Refutation checks I ran and they did NOT break it: (1) Window is CONTIGUOUS and truncated at the OLD end -- earliest day 2026-04-10 has only 55 docs while the latest day 2026-06-16 has 104 (a full normal day). That is the signature of a "most recent N rows" pull hitting a row cap going backwards, NOT a deliberately chosen 67-day archive. Strong support for "5000 = hard cap, not the real corpus." (2) The "67-day window" is calendar days; it is actually 47 distinct publication (weekday) days, avg 106 docs/day, min 55 max 203 -- FR only publishes business days, so no weekend gap anomaly to explain away. (3) No casting artifact: TRY_TO_DATE nulled 0 rows; blanks counted explicitly, not as zero.

Tier stays FACT: this is a pure intra-table coverage/completeness fact (single landing table, no cross-source or name/ID match), fully reproducible. Minor wording fixes folded into corrected_title (47 publication days vs 67 calendar; "most-recent N" framing rather than chosen date range). Surprise is modest -- round 5000 + recent-only is the expected shape of an uncapped API loader, consistent with fed_clinicaltrials:500 and fed_cfpb_complaints:500 in the same inventory. Importance moderate: anyone treating this as "the Federal Register" gets ~2 months of 2026 and zero history, and the two regulatory-significance / signing-president columns can't support any analysis.

kept.title: OWID military-expenditure panel: 644 of 9,112 rows aren't countries — they're SIPRI regional aggregates (with no ISO code) that will double-count if summed; and the panel runs to 2025
kept.sql: SELECT ENTITY, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_OWID_MILSPEND WHERE NULLIF(CODE,'') IS NULL GROUP BY ENTITY ORDER BY n DESC LIMIT 25
kept.evidence: 9,112 rows, years 1949-2025, max expenditure 2.77e12 (USD 2.77 trillion, the World/US-scale aggregate). 644 rows have a blank CODE — every one is a SIPRI region, not a country: 'South Asia (SIPRI)' 38, 'Africa (SIPRI)' 38, 'Middle East (SIPRI)' 38, 'Europe (SIPRI)' 37, ... down to 'European Union (SIPRI)' 4. The blank CODE is the ONLY thing distinguishing an aggregate from a country row.
kept.why_matters: This is the classic OWID trap from the country-key reconciliation rule: rows with no ISO3 CODE are continent/region rollups. SUM(MILITARY_EXPENDITURE) over the whole table triple-counts (country + region + World). Any cross-country join MUST filter to CODE IS NOT NULL AND CODE not a region tag first. Same null-CODE aggregate pattern appears in the fertility table (387 null-CODE rows).
kept.implies: new detector: 'OWID aggregate row' filter (CODE IS NULL OR ENTITY RLIKE '.*SIPRI.*|.*World.*') reusable across all xc_owid_* / intl_owid_* tables before any sum or join

verdict.notes:
All headline numbers reproduce exactly: 9,112 total rows; 644 blank-CODE rows; years 1949-2025; max MILITARY_EXPENDITURE = 2,771,294,000,000 (2.77e12). The "every blank-CODE row is a SIPRI region" claim holds perfectly: 644/644 ENTITY values carry the '(SIPRI)' tag, 0 exceptions. Per-entity counts in the candidate SQL reproduce.

SQL re-run results: (1) candidate group-by reproduced the 18 SIPRI region rows. (2) COUNT(*), null_code, min/max year, max exp = 9112 / 644 / 1949 / 2025 / 2.77e12. (3) null-CODE rows tagged '(SIPRI)' = 644/644, not_sipri = 0. (4) ORDER BY exp DESC top row = ENTITY 'World', CODE 'OWID_WRL', YEAR 2025, exp 2.77e12. (5) CODE bucketing: iso3_like 8341 rows/162 entities; blank 644/18; OWID_pseudo 127/6.

WHY DOWNGRADED (not confirmed): the load-bearing claim 'The blank CODE is the ONLY thing distinguishing an aggregate from a country row' is FALSE. The single largest aggregate, World, has a NON-blank CODE = 'OWID_WRL' and is exactly the 2.77e12 max the finding quotes. The OWID_pseudo bucket (CODE LIKE 'OWID_%', 127 rows) also holds USSR (OWID_USS), Czechoslovakia (OWID_CZS), East Germany (OWID_GDR), Kosovo, Yemen Arab Republic — historical states that time-overlap successor countries (second double-count trap). So aggregates come in TWO flavors, not one.

CONSEQUENCE: the prescribed guard (filter blank CODE / region tags before SUM or cross-country join) is INSUFFICIENT — it leaves World + historical pseudo-states in, so SUM still triple-counts exactly as warned. Correct guard: keep only real 3-char ISO3 codes AND exclude CODE LIKE 'OWID_%' AND blank. No casting artifact (TRY_CAST clean; max sorts numerically). Tier stays FACT (structural property of hard CODE values; no entity-guilt or fuzzy match), but title/wording corrected. Surprise modest — OWID_ synthetic codes are a documented OWID convention and the exact trap the country-key reconciliation rule flags. Importance moderate: anyone charting global military spend off this panel will inflate it, and the candidate's own filter would NOT prevent that.

CORRECTED TITLE: OWID military-expenditure panel (9,112 rows, 1949-2025): non-country aggregates come in TWO flavors — 644 SIPRI region rows with blank CODE, AND 127 OWID_pseudo-code rows (CODE LIKE 'OWID_%') that include World (2.77e12, the table max) plus historical states; blank CODE alone does NOT isolate aggregates.

kept.title: Heavy round-number clustering in payment amounts: $125 appears 48,825 times; near-round $124.99 appears 20,733x (per-meal Food & Beverage caps)
kept.sql: SELECT TRY_CAST(TOTAL_AMOUNT_OF_PAYMENT_USDOLLARS AS DOUBLE) AS amt, COUNT(*) AS n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS WHERE TRY_CAST(TOTAL_AMOUNT_OF_PAYMENT_USDOLLARS AS DOUBLE) >= 100 GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY n DESC LIMIT 25
kept.evidence: Most-frequent amounts >=$100 (2024): $125.00 x48,825; $124.99 x20,733; $150.00 x16,388; $1,500.00 x14,071; $1,000.00 x13,187; $500.00 x10,051; $4,000.00 x9,399; $100.00 x9,138; $2,000.00 x8,478; $2,500.00 x8,168. Strong clustering on flat round figures ($1k/$1.5k/$2k/$4k = honoraria/consulting day-rates) plus the off-round $124.99 and $121.84 (x5,023) which are recurring per-attendee meal caps in Food & Beverage.
kept.why_matters: Confirms the payment economy is dominated by templated flat fees, not negotiated amounts -- useful for a 'how pharma actually pays doctors' explainer. The $124.99 / $121.84 non-round spikes are a fingerprint of compliance-capped catered meals; a detector could flag manufacturers whose Food & Beverage payments cluster suspiciously tight at the cap (gaming the de-minimis threshold).

verdict.corrected_title: Open Payments amounts cluster on caps and flat fees: $125 (48,825x) and $124.99 (20,733x) are both ~98%+ Food & Beverage meal-cap charges, not day-rates; round figures like $1k/$1.5k/$4k are the consulting/compensation/education fees
verdict.notes:
Numbers reproduce EXACTLY (re-ran helper: $125x48,825, $124.99x20,733, $150x16,388, $1,500x14,071, etc.). No casting artifact: all 15,385,047 rows cast cleanly to DOUBLE, 0 blanks/nulls. Table is 100% PROGRAM_YEAR=2024 (15.4M rows), so the "(2024)" label is accurate. The off-round cap fingerprint is REAL and strong: $124.99/$121.84 are 99.7% Food and Beverage, and there is a dense just-under-$125 cluster ($124.99, $124.98, $124.97, $124.74, $124.95 + the $121.xx family) — exactly the de-minimis-meal-cap pattern claimed. Detector idea is sound.

DOWNGRADE reasons (interpretation, not arithmetic): (1) The candidate's clean dichotomy "round = flat fees / off-round = meal caps" is FALSE. The single largest spike, $125.00 (48,825), is itself 97.5% Food and Beverage (47,607) — a round-number meal cap, NOT a consulting day-rate. (2) "Round figures = honoraria/consulting day-rates" is only partly right: $1,000=Consulting Fee (4,322), $1,500/$2,000=Compensation for services other than consulting, but $4,000 is mostly EDUCATION (5,493), and Honoraria proper is a tiny slice everywhere. (3) "Payment economy dominated by templated flat fees" is overstated: top-10 amounts = 158,438 of 1,844,713 payments >=$100 = only 8.6%, spread across 118,618 distinct amounts (long tail). Clustering is visible but a minority of volume. Tier stays FACT (hard aggregate on one table, no entity/ID claim) but title and framing need the corrections above.

kept.title: US police killings have been eerily flat at ~1,043 per year for a decade (959-1,175) -- a stable 'floor' that no policy moment moved, and only 17% of fatal encounters were body-camera recorded
kept.sql: SELECT ROUND(AVG(yr_n),0) avg_per_year, MIN(yr_n) min_yr, MAX(yr_n) max_yr FROM (SELECT LEFT(DATE,4) yr, COUNT(*) yr_n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_WAPO_FATAL_FORCE WHERE LEFT(DATE,4) BETWEEN '2015' AND '2024' GROUP BY 1);  -- and: SELECT ROUND(100.0*SUM(CASE WHEN BODY_CAMERA='True' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/COUNT(*),1) bodycam_pct, COUNT(*) total FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_WAPO_FATAL_FORCE WHERE DATE<>''
kept.evidence: Annual counts 2015-2024: 995, 959, 984, 992, 993, 1021, 1050, 1097, 1164, 1175. Mean 1,043/yr, min 959, max 1,175 -- a remarkably tight band that did NOT dip after 2020. Body-camera recorded only 17.2% of all 10,430 killings, and never exceeded ~30% in any year (2023 best: 353/1164=30%). Armed status: 57.9% gun, 17.0% knife, 5.4% explicitly unarmed.
kept.why_matters: The most striking thing in this dataset is its flatness: roughly 1,000 Americans killed by police every single year, a constant that survived the entire post-Ferguson/post-Floyd reform era. Combined with body cameras capturing fewer than 1 in 5 deaths, this is a powerful accountability-and-transparency-gap story straight from the numbers.

verdict.corrected_title: US police killings ROSE ~12% after 2020, not flat: 985/yr avg (2015-19) to 1,101/yr (2020-24), with 2021-2024 each setting a new record (peak 1,175 in 2024) -- and body cameras recorded only 17.2% of all 10,430 deaths
verdict.notes:
Every NUMBER reproduces exactly off LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_WAPO_FATAL_FORCE (10,430 rows, clean 2015-01-02 to 2024-12-31, zero blank dates, no casting tricks needed -- LEFT(DATE,4) is reliable since dates are ISO 'YYYY-MM-DD'). Annual: 995,959,984,992,993,1021,1050,1097,1164,1175. Mean 1043, min 959, max 1175 -- all confirmed. Bodycam: 1799 True / 8631 False = 17.25% confirmed; per-year peak 2023 = 353/1164 = 30.3% confirmed, never above ~30%. Armed: gun 57.9%, knife 17.0%, unarmed 5.4% confirmed.

WHY DOWNGRADED (interpretation, not arithmetic): the headline 'eerily flat ~1,043/yr, a stable floor no policy moment moved' is the OPPOSITE of what the series shows. Split at 2020: avg 985/yr (2015-19) vs 1,101/yr (2020-24) = +12%. The four years 2021,2022,2023,2024 each set a successive all-time record; 2024 (1,175) is the peak. Range 959->1175 is a 23% spread, not a 'tight band.' Killings RISE after the post-Floyd reform era, they do not hold flat. Citing the mean (1043) as if it were a constant masks a clear upward trend. The body-camera transparency-gap point (17%, never above ~30%) is solid and publishable as-is.

Tier stays FACT for the numbers (single-source aggregate, no cross-ID/name matching, no fuzzy join). Caveat for an investigator: WaPo confirms deaths over time so the most recent year can lag/undercount -- if anything that means the real rise is even steeper, it does not rescue the 'flat' claim. Surprise low (4): 'cop killings ~1000/yr' is widely reported; the genuinely non-obvious bit (post-Floyd RISE) is exactly what the original title got backwards.

kept.title: fed_noaa_storm_events is a one-year snapshot wearing a panel's clothes -- 72,360 rows, 100% year 2025
kept.sql: SELECT MIN(TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT)) miny, MAX(TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT)) maxy, COUNT(DISTINCT YEAR) yrs, COUNT(*) total FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_NOAA_STORM_EVENTS
kept.evidence: miny=2025, maxy=2025, distinct YEAR=1, total=72,360. NOAA Storm Events Database goes back to 1950; this landing holds only the 2025 file. Lat/lon are in-bounds (BEGIN_LAT -14.34..69.38, BEGIN_LON -170.83..171.31) and DEATHS_DIRECT has no negatives, so the rows themselves are clean -- it's purely a coverage gap.
kept.why_matters: Any time-series, trend, or climate-vs-event analysis against this table is silently capped at a single year. The '72k rows' headline implies depth that isn't there temporally. Decade-scale storm trends need the full 1950-2024 backfill before this source is usable for trend work.
kept.implies: new source to load (backfill prior-year NOAA storm event files, one per year)

verdict.corrected_title: fed_noaa_storm_events is a single-year snapshot, not a panel: all 72,360 rows are year 2025 (NOAA's DB goes back to 1950)
verdict.notes:
Re-ran the candidate SQL verbatim and it reproduces exactly: miny=2025, maxy=2025, distinct YEAR=1, total=72,360. Hunted for the obvious artifacts and found none:

(1) No casting artifact. YEAR has 0 nulls and 0 TRY_CAST-uncastable values (all 72,360 cast cleanly to INT 2025). The "single year" is not blanks-counted-as-zero or text-sort weirdness.

(2) Independently cross-validated the year using a DIFFERENT column, BEGIN_YEARMONTH: LEFT(BEGIN_YEARMONTH,4) is '2025' for 100% of rows. So the single-year result is not an artifact of a constant/defaulted YEAR column -- the underlying event dates agree.

(3) Lat/lon and deaths claims hold: BEGIN_LAT -14.338..69.378, BEGIN_LON -170.834..171.307 (candidate's rounded -14.34..69.38 / -170.83..171.31 are correct), DEATHS_DIRECT min = 0 (no negatives). Rows are clean; it is purely a temporal coverage gap.

Minor caveat (does not weaken the finding): within 2025 the monthly distribution thins in the fall -- June 9,936 / July 9,713 down to Oct 1,703 / Nov 2,417 / Dec 4,605. As of today (2026-06-27) a 2025 file should be complete, so this tail is most likely normal storm seasonality (spring/summer peak) possibly plus NOAA's preliminary-data lag, not a defect. All 12 months are present, so it is a full calendar year, just one of 75+.

Tier stays FACT: this is a self-describing property of one landing table (no cross-source or name match involved), so no libel/LEAD concern. Surprise is low -- single-file snapshots are common in this library and the inventory already flags several one-year sources -- but importance is solid: the '72k rows' headline genuinely implies temporal depth that is not there, and any trend/climate work against this table is silently capped at 2025 until the 1950-2024 backfill lands.

kept.title: fed_fdic_failed_banks stores the string 'nan' in CERT for 488 of 4,115 rows -- a pandas null-as-text bug
kept.sql: SELECT SUM(CASE WHEN CERT='nan' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) nan_cert, COUNT(*) total FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_FDIC_FAILED_BANKS
kept.evidence: CERT='nan' (literal 3-char string) on 488 rows / 4,115 total (11.9%); BLANK_COST=0, LITERAL_NULL=0 confirms the loader wrote pandas NaN as text 'nan' rather than empty/NULL. This collapses COUNT(DISTINCT CERT) to 3,584 and creates a fake 'most common CERT' bucket of 488. The string-nan is isolated to CERT (NAN_BANKNO=0, NAN_ACQ=0) -- so it's column-specific, not a whole-row issue.
kept.why_matters: CERT is the FDIC primary key used to join failed banks to other FDIC/bank datasets. 488 unjoinable rows look like one giant duplicate bank if you GROUP BY CERT, and any TRY_CAST(CERT) join silently drops them. NULLIF(CERT,'nan') is required on every query touching this table.
kept.implies: new detector / staging fix (NULLIF 'nan' in stg_fed_fdic_failed_banks; audit other landings for the same loader pattern)

verdict.corrected_title: fed_fdic_failed_banks: loader wrote pandas NaN as literal text 'nan' across multiple numeric columns (CERT 488, COST 638, QBFASSET 154 rows) -- not CERT-specific; the 488 CERT='nan' rows are pre-1977 bank failures (1934-1976) that have no FDIC cert, and ID is a clean alternate primary key
verdict.notes:
Core numbers REPRODUCE EXACTLY: CERT='nan' on 488/4115 (11.9%), BLANK=0, NULL=0, COUNT(DISTINCT CERT)=3584, single canonical lowercase 'nan' (no case/whitespace variants). The pandas-NaN-as-text mechanism is real and FACT-grade.

But the candidate is WRONG/overstated on three points, hence DOWNGRADE:

1) NOT CERT-specific. Candidate claimed 'string-nan is isolated to CERT'. False: COST has 638 'nan' rows and QBFASSET has 154. It's a table-wide loader behavior wherever the source has missing numeric values -- a more general (and slightly more interesting) bug than claimed.

2) NOT 'unjoinable / one giant duplicate bank'. The table has a clean alternate PK: ID column = 4115 distinct, zero 'nan', perfectly unique. FIN is also fully populated (no nan/blank/null). So GROUP BY ID or FIN is fine; only CERT collapses. The 488 'nan' CERT rows are simply OLD failures (FAILYR 1934-1976) that predate FDIC certificate numbering -- genuinely missing source data, not a corruption that strands records.

3) The cast-drop claim is half-right and depends on type. TRY_CAST(CERT AS INT) -> 3627 castable, so the 488 'nan' DO get nulled/dropped (candidate correct here). BUT TRY_TO_DOUBLE(CERT) -> 4115 castable, nan_nulled=0: 'nan' casts to IEEE float NaN, which does NOT drop -- it POISONS aggregates instead (SUM(TRY_TO_DOUBLE(COST)) over nan rows literally returned 'nan'). So 'silently drops them' is only true for integer casts; double casts silently corrupt math.

Practical guidance stands: NULLIF(col,'nan') (and ideally on COST/QBFASSET too) is required, especially before any TRY_TO_DOUBLE math. surprise/importance modest: it's a routine pandas-to-Snowflake null artifact on a 4k-row table, real and worth a fix but not a high-stakes signal.

kept.title: FARA registration dates are MM/DD/YYYY text - any year parse via SUBSTR(1,4) silently fails on 221,900 rows
kept.sql: SELECT REGISTRATION_DATE, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_FARA_BULK WHERE NULLIF(REGISTRATION_DATE,'') IS NOT NULL GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY n DESC LIMIT 10
kept.evidence: REGISTRATION_DATE is US-format text e.g. '11/10/1981' (1002x), '11/15/1984' (680x), '06/25/1963' (515x). TRY_CAST(SUBSTR(REGISTRATION_DATE,1,4) AS INT) returns NULL for all 221,900 rows (MIN=MAX=NULL) because the first 4 chars are 'MM/D'. Using the correct SUBSTR(REGISTRATION_DATE,7,4) yields a real span MINY=1942, MAXY=2026. Separately, FOREIGN_PRINCIPAL_COUNTRY is blank on 184,440 of 221,900 rows (83%) - most rows are registrant/admin records, not foreign-principal records.
kept.why_matters: Two traps for the staging model: (1) FARA dates need MM/DD/YYYY parsing (TO_DATE(col,'MM/DD/YYYY')), not naive substring/ISO cast - a default cast drops the entire 84-year time axis to NULL; (2) the table mixes registrant rows and foreign-principal rows, so foreign-principal analysis must filter to the 37k rows where FOREIGN_PRINCIPAL_COUNTRY is populated, not the full 222k.
kept.implies: staging rule: TO_DATE(REGISTRATION_DATE,'MM/DD/YYYY') and split FARA into registrant vs foreign-principal grains

verdict.corrected_title: FARA dates are MM/DD/YYYY text - SUBSTR(1,4) year parse fails, but TRY_CAST AS DATE works fine; only 51,575 of 221,900 rows even carry a REGISTRATION_DATE
verdict.notes:
Mostly reproduces, but two material overstatements force a downgrade.

WHAT HOLDS (verified): REGISTRATION_DATE is MM/DD/YYYY text - top values exact: '11/10/1981' 1002x, '11/15/1984' 680x, '06/25/1963' 515x. TRY_CAST(SUBSTR(REGISTRATION_DATE,1,4) AS INT) = NULL (grabs 'MM/D'); SUBSTR(...,7,4) gives real span MIN 1942-07-03, MAX 2026-06-08. FOREIGN_PRINCIPAL_COUNTRY blank on 184,440/221,900 (83%) - exact. Table mixes document types - confirmed via DOCUMENT_TYPE (152,660 nonblank) and split date cols.

WHAT IS WRONG / OVERSTATED (the downgrade):
1) ROW COUNT MISFRAMED ~4x. Claim says the parse 'silently fails on 221,900 rows.' Only 51,575 rows carry a non-blank REGISTRATION_DATE; the other 170,325 are legitimately blank (different doc types put their date in REGISTRANT_DATE 17,665, SHORT_FORM_DATE 44,485, FOREIGN_PRINCIPAL_REGISTRATION_DATE 17,665). MIN=MAX=NULL across all rows is technically true but the 'fails on 221,900' framing implies 221,900 broken dates when there are at most ~51,575.
2) CENTRAL TRAP (1) LARGELY REFUTED. Claim: 'a default cast drops the entire 84-year time axis to NULL.' FALSE for the obvious default cast: TRY_CAST(REGISTRATION_DATE AS DATE) parses ALL 51,575 (Snowflake AUTO DATE_INPUT_FORMAT handles MM/DD/YYYY). TRY_TO_DATE(col) default also = 51,575. Only the narrow anti-patterns fail: SUBSTR(1,4) and explicit TO_DATE(col,'YYYY-MM-DD') -> 0. So a naive CAST works; only a naive SUBSTRING/ISO-format-string fails. Same holds for REGISTRANT_DATE (17,664/17,665 cast OK) and SHORT_FORM_DATE (44,485/44,485).

NET: data-quality finding is real and FACT-grade (pure structural facts about one table, no entity/libel exposure), but headline overstates blast radius and the 'default cast breaks' selling point doesn't survive. Useful staging note: parse FARA dates with explicit MM/DD/YYYY (or rely on AUTO cast), never SUBSTR(1,4); and there are 4 separate date columns by doc type, not one. Low surprise (US-format dates in fed text dumps are expected); moderate importance (prevents a staging bug, modest).

kept.title: Guttmacher abortion estimates are 100% rounded to the nearest 10 -- every one of 2,040 medians is divisible by 10
kept.sql: SELECT SUM(CASE WHEN TRY_CAST(MEDIAN AS INT) % 10 = 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) div10, COUNT(*) tot, MIN(TRY_CAST(MEDIAN AS INT)) min_m, MAX(TRY_CAST(MEDIAN AS INT)) max_m FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_GUTTMACHER_MONTHLY_ABORTION
kept.evidence: div10=2040 of tot=2040 (100%); MEDIAN range 0 to 105,910; every value ends in digit '0' (COUNT(DISTINCT RIGHT(MEDIAN,1))=1). Same holds for the LOWERBOUND/UPPERBOUND confidence band. These are modeled #WeCount estimates, not raw clinic counts.
kept.why_matters: Anyone treating MEDIAN as an exact monthly abortion count is wrong -- it is a smoothed model output rounded to 10. Month-over-month deltas under ~10 are noise. The whole table is an estimate, and any mart that sums or rates these needs that caveat or it overstates precision.

verdict.corrected_title: All Guttmacher #WeCount abortion estimates are rounded to the nearest 10 -- MEDIAN, LOWERBOUND, and UPPERBOUND are each 100% divisible by 10 (2,040/2,040), and the table's own NOTES field says so
verdict.notes: Re-ran the exact SQL: div10=2040, tot=2040 (100%), MIN=0, MAX=105910 -- reproduces perfectly. Hunted for casting artifacts and found none: cast_ok=2040 (TRY_CAST nulled nothing), blank_or_null=0, has_decimal=0, has_nonnumeric_char=0, so raw text values are pure integers and the % 10 = 0 test is honest (no truncation masking). COUNT(DISTINCT RIGHT(MEDIAN,1))=1 and that single char is literally '0' across all 2040 rows. The claim is if anything UNDER-stated: LOWERBOUND and UPPERBOUND are ALSO 100% divisible by 10 (lb_div10=2040/2040, ub_div10=2040/2040), each with a single distinct last char. Strongest piece of evidence the candidate didn't fully lean on: the table's own NOTES column reads verbatim "All estimates rounded to nearest 10. Estimates include da..." and SOURCE = guttmacher.org/monthly-abortion-provision-study -- so this is publisher-disclosed, not a derived inference, which is why FACT tier is correct. No cross-source/cross-ID/name-match concerns; single-table data-quality property. Downgraded nothing. Scored surprise low (the NOTES field gives it away on a glance and rounded modeled estimates are routine) and importance moderate (real precision caveat for any mart summing/rating these, sub-10 MoM deltas are noise, but it's one small 2,040-row table the publisher already flags).

kept.title: FHFA 'all-transactions' flavor (89k rows, 48% of the table) has a structurally EMPTY seasonally-adjusted column
kept.sql: SELECT HPI_FLAVOR, COUNT(*) n_rows, COUNT(NULLIF(INDEX_SA,'')) n_sa, ROUND(100*COUNT(NULLIF(INDEX_SA,''))/COUNT(*),1) pct_sa FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_FHFA_HPI GROUP BY HPI_FLAVOR ORDER BY n_rows DESC
kept.evidence: all-transactions: 89,265 rows, INDEX_SA populated 0 (0.0%). expanded-data: 66,411 rows, 100% SA. purchase-only: 29,131 rows, 99.6% SA. Overall INDEX_SA is only 95,435/184,807 = 52% populated -- the missingness is not random, it is entirely the all-transactions slice (FHFA never publishes a seasonally-adjusted all-transactions series). INDEX_NSA is 184,805/184,807 ~ 100%.
kept.why_matters: Any join or chart on INDEX_SA silently drops nearly half the table (and the most granular all-transactions series). The 'SA is half-null' smell is benign once you know it is one flavor -- but a model picking INDEX_SA as the default index would mysteriously lose all-transactions data.

verdict.corrected_title: FHFA 'all-transactions' flavor (89,265 rows, 48% of the table) has a structurally EMPTY seasonally-adjusted column (INDEX_SA), so SA is only 52% populated table-wide
verdict.notes:
Numbers reproduce exactly. all-transactions: 89,265 rows, INDEX_SA populated 0 (0.0%); expanded-data: 66,411 rows, 66,409 SA; purchase-only: 29,131 rows, 29,026 SA (99.6%). Table total 184,807; SA populated 95,435 = 51.6% (matches claimed 52%); INDEX_NSA 184,805 (~100%). Decomposition is airtight: 66,409 + 29,026 = 95,435 exactly, so 100% of the SA-missingness lives in the all-transactions slice -- it is NOT random.

Ruled out a casting artifact: SELECT DISTINCT INDEX_SA WHERE HPI_FLAVOR='all-transactions' returns a single value, the empty string -- the column is uniformly blank, not holding non-numeric junk that NULLIF/COUNT would silently miss. TRIM gives the same count. No TRY_CAST involved.

This is a definitional/publishing property, not an anomaly: FHFA does not publish a seasonally-adjusted all-transactions series, so the blank is expected. Benign coverage gap, correctly flagged.

Tier FACT is correct -- this is a structural data-coverage statement, not an entity/person claim, so no libel exposure.

Two trivial caveats the candidate glossed: (1) expanded-data is 66,409/66,411, i.e. 2 SA rows blank, not a clean 100%; (2) the 105 missing purchase-only SA rows are all in the quarterly slice (24,796/24,901), monthly is 4,230/4,230 complete. Neither dents the headline. Surprise is modest (it's a known FHFA convention once you see it); importance is moderate -- a model defaulting to INDEX_SA would silently drop the entire all-transactions series, which is the most granular flavor.

kept.title: 11 long-term-care hospitals all share certification date 07/01/1966 -- the exact day Medicare launched
kept.sql: SELECT CERTIFICATION_DATE, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_LTCH GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY n DESC LIMIT 5
kept.evidence: 07/01/1966 -> 11 facilities (the single most common cert date), next dates are 01/01/2002 (5), 01/01/2008 (5). July 1, 1966 is the day Medicare went live. Table is small and clean: 311 rows, 311 distinct CCN (no dup keys), 47 states, beds 9-760, ownership skews 218/311 For-profit (avg 61 beds) vs 11 Government (avg 219 beds).
kept.why_matters: A clean historical fingerprint: the oldest LTCHs are pinned to Medicare's birthday because CMS backdated day-one enrollees to the program start. It is a true data property (not an error) and a nice 'institutional age' signal -- and it confirms CERTIFICATION_DATE is MM/DD/YYYY text, which a naive LEFT(...,4) year-parse silently fails on.

verdict.corrected_title: 11 long-term-care hospitals share certification date 07/01/1966 -- the day Medicare's hospital insurance launched -- and they are the oldest LTCHs in the table
verdict.notes:
All load-bearing numbers reproduce exactly. SQL re-ran clean: 07/01/1966 = 11 facilities (single most common date); next dates 01/01/2002=5, 01/01/2008=5. Table: 311 rows = 311 distinct CCN (no dup keys), 0 blank dates, 214 distinct dates, 47 states, beds 9-760. All 311 dates parse cleanly as MM/DD/YYYY (TRY_TO_DATE on 'MM/DD/YYYY' nulls nothing), so 1966-07-01 is the UNAMBIGUOUS earliest date -- next-earliest year is 1969 with just 1 facility. July 1, 1966 is genuinely the day Medicare HI went live, so this is a real institutional-age fingerprint (CMS backdated day-one enrollees to program start), not a casting artifact or text-sort illusion.

WHY IT'S A FACT (not a fuzzy/cross-ID lead): no cross-source join involved -- it's a within-table date frequency on a clean 311-row file keyed by hard CCN. No libel exposure.

ONE WORDING FLAW (does not change verdict): candidate says 'ownership skews 218/311 For-profit (avg 61 beds) vs 11 Government (avg 219 beds)' -- those are TABLE-WIDE stats (For profit 218 avg 61.1; Government 11 avg 218.7), and it conflates the table-wide 'Government=11' with the cohort 'count=11.' The 1966 COHORT itself is the inverse: 6 Government / 4 Non-profit / 1 For profit. That inversion actually strengthens the story (day-one Medicare hospitals were old public/charitable institutions, not the modern for-profit LTCH industry that dominates the table 218/311). Cohort also clusters geographically: 8 of 11 in MA/CT/MD/RI (New England + Mid-Atlantic).

DOWNGRADED IMPORTANCE: surprising and clean, but it's a 'beautiful data property' with low investigative payload -- it's a confirmation that CERTIFICATION_DATE is MM/DD/YYYY text and an age signal, not a wrongdoing/contradiction lead. Real and publishable, just not high-stakes.

kept.title: Oyez 'modeled' source is a 25-row hollow stub: DECISION, DISPOSITION and MAJORITY_AUTHOR are 100% blank
kept.sql: SELECT DISPOSITION, DECISION, MAJORITY_AUTHOR, COUNT(*) AS n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_OYEZ GROUP BY 1,2,3 ORDER BY 4 DESC
kept.evidence: All 25 rows share (DISPOSITION='', DECISION='', MAJORITY_AUTHOR=''). NULL_DECISION=25/25. TERM only spans 1966-1971 (5 terms) and is lopsided: TERM 1971 holds 19 of 25 rows, the other four terms have 1-2 each. JUSTICE_VOTES and DOCKET are populated (25 distinct dockets) but the actual case-outcome fields are empty.
kept.why_matters: Oyez is cataloged with DOCKET+NAME join keys as if it carries SCOTUS outcomes, but the outcome columns are entirely empty and coverage is 5 stale terms from the late 1960s. It cannot answer 'how did the Court decide X' for anything modern -- a coverage hole hiding behind a 'modeled' label.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: Oyez 'modeled' source is a 25-row hollow stub: DECISION, DISPOSITION, MAJORITY_AUTHOR and CITATION are 100% blank; coverage is 5 scattered terms (1966-1969, 1971)
verdict.notes:
Every claim reproduced exactly. Re-ran candidate SQL: all 25 rows collapse to (DISPOSITION='', DECISION='', MAJORITY_AUTHOR='') -> one group of 25. Profiled with NULLIF: DISP/DEC/MAJ all 0/25 non-blank; JUSTICE_VOTES 25/25 populated; 25 distinct DOCKETs. TERM: 1966=2,1967=2,1968=1,1969=1,1971=19 -> 1971 holds 19/25, confirmed.

Refinements (not refutations):
1. Casting check: outcome fields are EMPTY-STRING blanks ('') not true NULLs. SUM(DECISION IS NULL)=0, SUM(DECISION='')=25. The candidate's phrase 'NULL_DECISION=25/25' conflates blank with NULL -- harmless because the practical conclusion (no outcome data) is identical, and the candidate's headline word 'blank' is correct.
2. Candidate said 'TERM only spans 1966-1971 (5 terms)... the other four terms have 1-2 each.' Accurate, but worth noting 1970 is MISSING -- it's 5 scattered terms across a 6-year window, not 5 contiguous terms. Corrected title reflects this.
3. CITATION is also blank (sample shows empty), so the empty-column set is broader than the three named -- DECISION, DISPOSITION, MAJORITY_AUTHOR, CITATION all empty.

No artifact: not a text-vs-numeric sort issue, not a TRY_CAST silently nulling rows (no cast involved), not a definitional/units mismatch. data-quality tag carries no entity/libel claim so FACT grade is correct -- nothing to downgrade. Real but modest: it's a 25-row stub nobody would mistake for a serious dataset, and the catalog DOCKET+NAME keys are technically honest (those cols ARE populated) -- the overstatement risk is only if someone trusts it for case OUTCOMES, which the finding correctly flags.

kept.title: DOJ FCA settlements: the SETTLEMENT_AMOUNT column is unusable free-text — 14 of 19 blank, the rest are '$10', '$202', '$40', '$6.8', '$2.9 billion'
kept.sql: SELECT SETTLEMENT_AMOUNT, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_DOJ_FCA_SETTLEMENTS GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC
kept.evidence: 19 total rows. SETTLEMENT_AMOUNT values: blank=14, '$10'=1, '$202'=1, '$2.9 billion'=1, '$40'=1, '$6.8'=1. No consistent units (mixes a literal '$2.9 billion' string with bare '$10'/'$40'). DEFENDANT_PERSON and RELATOR_NAME are 0/19 filled. TRY_CAST(SETTLEMENT_AMOUNT AS DOUBLE) min/max both NULL because '$' prefix blocks every cast.
kept.why_matters: A False-Claims-Act SETTLEMENTS table where the settlement dollar amount cannot be summed or compared is functionally hollow for its headline purpose. The '$2.9 billion' vs '$10' mix means the column was scraped from prose, not a numeric field. Tier this table as a stub: 19 rows, no parseable money, no defendant persons.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: DOJ FCA settlements table is a failed scrape: all 19 rows are DOJ website nav/boilerplate + scraper error strings (not one real settlement); SETTLEMENT_AMOUNT is unparseable free-text ('$10','$2.9 billion'), defendant/relator 0/19 filled
verdict.notes:
Every claimed number reproduces EXACTLY: 19 rows; SETTLEMENT_AMOUNT blank=14, '$10'/'$202'/'$2.9 billion'/'$40'/'$6.8' =1 each (5 filled). TRY_CAST(SETTLEMENT_AMOUNT AS DOUBLE) = 0/19 cast_ok (the '$' prefix and ' billion' word both block casting — verified, not a silent-null artifact since the raw GROUP BY shows the literal strings). DEFENDANT_PERSON 0/19, RELATOR_NAME 0/19, DEFENDANT_COMPANY 0/19, CASE_NUMBER 0/19, DISTRICT 0/19, RELATOR_SHARE 0/19 all empty.

The candidate is CONFIRMED but UNDERSTATED — I upgraded the title. The real problem isn't just the amount column: the table is a broken scrape with ZERO genuine settlements. CASE_TITLE values are DOJ Civil Division site chrome ('About the Civil Division','Civil Division FOIA','Contact the Division','Documents and Forms'), scraper error strings ('Error fetching http://www.facebook.com/sharer...','Error fetching mailto:...'), and even 'LinkedIn Login, Sign in | LinkedIn'. The '$2.9 billion' is attached to a row titled 'The False Claims Act' (the FCA overview/landing page) — i.e. the annual program-wide recovery headline number scraped from prose, not any one settlement.

Gotcha worth flagging: FRAUD_TYPE (14/19), AGENCY_DEFRAUDED (15/19), QUI_TAM (16/19) DO have fills — but they are noise/likely-hallucinated, not real data: e.g. CASE_TITLE 'About the Civil Division' carries FRAUD_TYPE='Healthcare', AGENCY_DEFRAUDED='Medicare'; 'LinkedIn Login' carries AGENCY_DEFRAUDED='VA'. No nav page has a fraud type. Do not treat these columns as usable either.

Verdict: this source_id (fed_doj_fca_settlements, 19 rows) should be marked EMPTY/failed-scrape in the catalog, not 'landed'. data-quality finding, FACT grade (it's a single-table structural fact, no entity/libel exposure). Surprise modest (it's a known-stub-prone source per the brief); importance modest (one tiny dead table), but it matters because anyone trusting the column names would think they had 19 real FCA settlements with fraud types.

kept.title: Mapping Inequality (HOLC redlining) grade column is dirty: should be 4 grades (A/B/C/D), has 10 distinct values incl. spurious 'E'/'F', 814 blanks, and whitespace/case dup grades
kept.sql: SELECT HOLC_GRADE, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_MAPPING_INEQUALITY GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC
kept.evidence: 10,154 rows, GROUP BY HOLC_GRADE returns 10 buckets: C=3559, B=2481, D=2188, A=1094, ''(blank)=814, E=9, F=4, ''=3, plus a SECOND 'A'=1 and SECOND 'C'=1 (distinct due to trailing whitespace/case). HOLC only ever issued grades A-D, so E (9) and F (4) are invalid, and the duplicate A/C buckets mean the column isn't trimmed/normalized. Separately, YEAR_MAPPED is 100% NULL (MIN and MAX both blank across all 10,154 rows).
kept.why_matters: Redlining analysis hinges on grouping by HOLC grade. Grouping raw will split 'A' into two buckets, leak 13 invalid E/F rows, and drop the 817 blank-grade polygons — and there is NO usable year (YEAR_MAPPED entirely empty), so you can't even date the maps. Needs TRIM/UPPER + a grade whitelist before any equity join.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: Mapping Inequality (HOLC) grade column needs TRIM/UPPER + an A-D whitelist: 10 raw buckets collapse to 6 real grades (A/B/C/D + upstream E/F) plus 817 blanks; whitespace creates dup A/C buckets and YEAR_MAPPED is 100% empty
verdict.notes:
All headline numbers reproduce EXACTLY. GROUP BY HOLC_GRADE returns 10 buckets: C=3559, B=2481, D=2188, A=1094, ''=814, E=9, F=4, ' '(single space)=3, 'A '(trailing space)=1, 'C '(trailing space)=1. Byte-level check confirms the dup A/C are trailing-whitespace ([A ],[C ] len=2) and the two blank buckets are [] (empty, 814) + [ ] (single space, 3) = 817 total blank. YEAR_MAPPED genuinely 100% blank: 0 non-blank of 10,154, MIN/MAX both empty. So the data-quality core (raw GROUP BY splits A, leaks E/F, drops 817 blanks; no usable year) is TRUE and the column does need TRIM/UPPER before any equity join.

DOWNGRADED for two framing errors, not number errors:
(1) The claim 'should be 4 grades, E/F are spurious/invalid' overstates it. After TRIM/UPPER the column collapses to 6 distinct non-blank grades (A,B,C,D,E,F) + blank — NOT 4. E and F do NOT disappear with normalization; they are real distinct values faithfully carried from the upstream Mapping Inequality (Univ. of Richmond DSL) data, appearing in named cities (E: Savannah GA x3, Wichita Falls TX x2, Duluth, Fargo, Bronx, Grand Forks; F: Fargo x2, Wichita Falls, Grand Forks). A-D is the standard HOLC rubric, so filtering E/F for a clean A-D analysis is reasonable, but they are upstream data quirks, not dirt this loader introduced — calling them 'spurious' is wrong. Corrected the title to say 6 real grades and to frame the fix as a whitelist choice.
(2) HOLC_COLOR is entirely blank in this load, so it can't be used to disambiguate grade — minor, not claimed but worth noting.

Tier FACT is appropriate: pure single-table data-quality observation, no entity/person claim, nothing to libel. Surprise is low (dirty all-TEXT raw columns and whitespace dup buckets are the expected/known property of LANDING per the guardrails); the only mildly non-obvious bit is YEAR_MAPPED being 100% empty and E/F being genuine upstream grades rather than typos. Importance moderate: real and actionable for anyone modeling this source, but it's a staging-hygiene fix, not an investigative signal.

kept.title: Leiden Russian-ops-in-Europe shows a sharp 2024 surge: incidents nearly triple (24 -> 69) then half in 2025
kept.sql: SELECT INCIDENTYEAR, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_LEIDEN_RUSSIAN_OPS_EUROPE GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1;  -- plus EVENTCATEGORY breakdown and SELECT MIN(TRY_TO_DATE(INCIDENTDATESTART)), MAX(...) FROM ...
kept.evidence: By INCIDENTYEAR: 2022=13, 2023=24, 2024=69, 2025=40 (plus 7 undated). Dates span 2022-01-01 to 2025-10-01 across 39 distinct locations. 2024 is the spike. EVENTCATEGORY mix is dominated by 'Influence operation' (43), then Reconnaissance 17, Sabotage 15, Arson 14, Vandalism 12, Disruption 12, with a long tail down to Assassination (1) and Assassination plot (2). No duplicate key issues (all year+month+what combos distinct).
kept.why_matters: A clean, small (153-row) hand-curated dataset with a real, legible signal: documented Russian hybrid-warfare incidents in Europe roughly tripled in 2024. 2025 at 40 is a partial year (data ends Oct 2025), so the apparent drop may be undercount, not de-escalation. This is a publishable trend line, not just a data quirk.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: Leiden Russian-ops-in-Europe: documented incidents nearly triple in 2024 (24 -> 69), then 40 in a partial 2025 (data ends Oct)
verdict.notes:
Re-ran the SQL; core trend reproduces EXACTLY: INCIDENTYEAR 2022=13, 2023=24, 2024=69, 2025=40, +7 blank-year. 2024 spike is genuine and well-distributed across all 12 months (Jan 14, Jul/May/Jun 8 each, Mar 7, Apr/Nov 5...) -- not a single-month or duplicate artifact. Per-year distinct(month||what) combo count equals row count, so no dup inflation in the 2024 spike. Only 4 distinct years exist; no text-vs-numeric sort issue.

CASTING CHECK / one overstatement: the candidate's claim that dates 'span 2022-01-01 to 2025-10-01 across 39 locations' rests on only 45 of 153 rows parsing via TRY_TO_DATE(INCIDENTDATESTART). The other 108 don't parse -- 97 are literally the string 'DNA' (Date Not Available), 7 blank, 4 are bare month names ('June','March'). So the date column is sparse BY DESIGN; the trend does NOT depend on it because INCIDENTYEAR is fully populated for the 146 dated-year rows. The MIN/MAX date range is technically true but the candidate failed to flag that <30% of rows carry a parseable date.

Minor: candidate said 'all year+month+what combos distinct' -- true within each populated year, but the 7 undated rows have only 5 distinct month||what combos (2 dupes). Irrelevant to the trend.

TIER: This is an aggregate count trend on a single self-contained hand-curated 153-row dataset -- NOT an entity claim on a hard or fuzzy ID, so no cross-source/name-match libel exposure. FACT grade is appropriate. Candidate already correctly hedged that 2025=40 is a partial year (data ends Oct 2025), so the drop may be undercount not de-escalation -- that caveat is sound and should be retained. EVENTCATEGORY mix also reproduces (Influence operation 43, Reconnaissance 17, Sabotage 15, Arson 14, Vandalism 12, Disruption 12, Sabotage(preparation) 11, ... Assassination 1, plot 2).

kept.title: FED_VA_SUICIDE_APPENDIX is not a dataset — it's a flattened PDF with 6+ stacked tables, blank separators, and multi-line headers crammed into 144 rows
kept.sql: SELECT SUM(CASE WHEN TRY_CAST($1 AS INT) BETWEEN 2001 AND 2030 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) data_year_rows, SUM(CASE WHEN NULLIF(TRIM($1),'') IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) blank_rows, SUM(CASE WHEN $1 LIKE '%Year%' OR $1 LIKE '%Veteran%' OR $1 LIKE '%Note%' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) header_rows, COUNT(*) total FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_VA_SUICIDE_APPENDIX
kept.evidence:
144 total rows. Col_1 (a single VARCHAR) holds header labels with literal embedded newlines ('Year
of
Death', 'Suicide
Deaths', 'Population
Estimate'); the SAME header line repeats mid-table because at least two sub-tables (overall by-sex 2001-2023, then by age-group starting 2001 18-34) are stacked vertically in one flat file. Year-looking values appear 138 times but a single year like 2001 recurs across multiple sub-tables; 3 fully blank separator rows; 3 narrative/title rows ('Veteran Deaths by Suicide: Unadjusted Suicide Rates by Sex...'). Columns are COL_1..COL_12 (header row never became the schema), so EVERY numeric column is mislabeled.
kept.why_matters: This source is registered/landed but is unusable as-is: no clean primary key, no consistent column meaning, repeated headers, and the real column names live in data cells. Any staging model on it will silently produce garbage. It needs bespoke parsing (split by the blank-row delimiters into N sub-tables) before it can be modeled — it should not be treated as 'landed and ready'.
kept.implies: new detector / new source to load: flag PDF-flattened tables (multi-line header strings in column values) across the library as a data-quality class needing re-parse

verdict.corrected_title: FED_VA_SUICIDE_APPENDIX is a flattened PDF: 2 vertically-stacked sub-tables (by-sex 2001-2023, then by-age-group) with multi-line embedded-newline headers, blank separators, and a narrative title — 138 data rows + 6 structural rows = 144, columns are positional COL_1..COL_12 so the header never became the schema
verdict.notes:
Central thesis CONFIRMED and reproducible. Exact candidate SQL re-runs to identical numbers: data_year_rows=138, blank_rows=3, header_rows=3, total=144. Verified directly: 3 blank separator rows, 1 narrative title row ('Veteran Deaths by Suicide: Unadjusted Suicide Rates by Sex...'), and 2 distinct column-header rows with literal CHR(10) newlines ('Year
of
Death', 'Suicide
Deaths', 'Population
Estimate', 'Age
Group'). Each year 2001-2023 appears exactly 6 times = 1 (by-sex overall) + 5 (age groups 18-34/35-54/55-74/75+/All), so 23*6=138 — proves exactly 2 vertical sub-tables. Columns ARE positional COL_1..COL_12 and column 1 is named after the table TITLE; the finding is right that any naive staging model produces garbage. That part is FACT-grade.

TWO WORDING OVERSTATEMENTS (why downgraded, not confirmed): (1) '6+ stacked tables' is wrong as stated — there are 2 VERTICALLY stacked sub-tables. The wide columns COL_5..COL_12 (positions $6-$13, each ~140 non-blank) DO carry additional male/female rate sections and the age sub-table is column-shifted (the '18-34' label lands in COL_8 / $9, with literal '.' placeholder cells), so '6+ logical sections' is defensible but '6+ stacked tables' is not. (2) '3 narrative/title rows' is wrong — only 1 row matches '%Veteran%' (the title). The candidate's own header_rows=3 is 2 column-headers + 1 title; it got mis-narrated as 3 narrative rows. (3) Minor: prose says 'Col_1 holds header labels' but the helper '$1' is column POSITION 1 = the long-named first column, NOT the column literally named COL_1.

Tier stays FACT (it's a pure structural/data-quality claim about one table, no entity/libel exposure). Importance is moderate: it's one tiny 144-row stub, but the warning (do not treat as 'landed and ready', needs bespoke split-by-blank-row parsing) is correct and actionable. Surprise low — it's a known data-quirk class (flattened gov PDF), exactly the kind of thing expected from PDF-sourced appendices.

kept.title: Corruption tracks life expectancy hard (+0.73, stable 5 years) -- but Syria, Venezuela, Libya, North Korea & Lebanon are the rebels: bottom-decile-corrupt yet live like clean middle-income states
kept.sql: WITH cpi AS (SELECT CODE iso, ENTITY name, TRY_CAST(CORRUPTION_PERCEPTIONS_INDEX AS DOUBLE) cpi FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_OWID_CPI WHERE YEAR='2020' AND LENGTH(CODE)=3), le AS (SELECT CODE iso, TRY_CAST(LIFE_EXPECTANCY AS DOUBLE) le FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_OWID_LIFE_EXPECTANCY WHERE YEAR='2020' AND LENGTH(CODE)=3), j AS (SELECT cpi.iso,cpi.name,cpi.cpi,le.le FROM cpi JOIN le ON cpi.iso=le.iso WHERE cpi.cpi IS NOT NULL AND le.le IS NOT NULL), reg AS (SELECT REGR_SLOPE(le,cpi) m, REGR_INTERCEPT(le,cpi) b FROM j) SELECT j.name,j.iso,j.cpi,ROUND(j.le,1) life_exp,ROUND(j.le-(reg.m*j.cpi+reg.b),1) residual FROM j,reg ORDER BY residual DESC LIMIT 8;
kept.evidence: CORR(CPI, life_exp) on ISO3 = +0.734 in 2020 (n=179), stable: 0.684/0.670/0.734/0.713/0.626 for 2018-2022 (n=179 each). Linear-fit residuals: the 5 biggest positive outliers are all extreme-corruption states living far longer than predicted -- Lebanon CPI25/76.3yr (+9.9 vs predicted 66.4), Venezuela CPI15/72.4 (+8.8), Syria CPI14/71.9 (+8.7), North Korea CPI18/72.8 (+8.4), Libya CPI17/72.4 (+8.3). For context Denmark (cleanest, CPI90) and Singapore (CPI83) sit on-trend. The biggest NEGATIVE residuals are all Sub-Saharan: Central African Republic -16.1, Lesotho -16.0, Nigeria -13.4.
kept.why_matters: Corruption-perception is one of the strongest single predictors of national lifespan in the Library (r=0.73, durable). The named positive outliers are a clean editorial hook: pariah/post-conflict states (Syria, Venezuela, Libya, NK, Lebanon) inherited health systems and life expectancies that their governance scores can't explain -- a 'health legacy outlives the regime' story. The negative-residual cluster shows the relationship is really development-confounded, not causal.
kept.implies: new detector: flag countries whose health/development indicators sit >2 SD off their governance score (governance-outcome decoupling)

verdict.corrected_title: Corruption-perception correlates strongly with national life expectancy (Pearson +0.73, Spearman +0.74, n=179, stable 2018-2022) -- but the relationship saturates/fans out, so the low-CPI 'survivors' (Lebanon, Venezuela, Syria, NK, Libya) are the upper tail of a 26-year-wide corrupt-state band (Iran, Uzbekistan, Bangladesh sit right with them), not a unique 'health legacy outlives the regime' signal
verdict.notes:
EVERY number reproduced exactly: CORR 0.734 / n=179 / 2020; 5-yr stability 0.684,0.670,0.734,0.713,0.626; positive residuals Lebanon +9.9, Venezuela +8.8, Syria +8.7, NK +8.4, Libya +8.3; negative CAF -16.1, Lesotho -16.0, Nigeria -13.4. Data hygiene clean: TRY_CAST drops ZERO rows, no duplicate ISO codes in either table, clean 1:1 join on ISO3 (a HARD geographic key, so the JOIN is FACT-grade). LE range 51-86 = plausible period life-exp at birth, single-metric table, no unit mixing. Pearson 0.734 vs Spearman 0.742 nearly identical => correlation is real and not leverage-driven.

WHY DOWNGRADED (not rejected -- numbers are solid; the STORY is overstated):
1) Linear-fit-misfit artifact. The CPI~LE relationship saturates: by CPI band, LE stddev is 5.3-5.6 at the corrupt end (CPI<35, range 50.6-76.3) but only 2.8 at the clean end (CPI 65+, range 72.3-84.7). A single straight line systematically misfits, so the 'biggest positive residuals' are largely just the highest-variance band, not a special signal. The candidate's clean cases (Denmark/Singapore 'on-trend') sit where variance is tiny by construction.
2) The 'rebels live like clean middle-income states' hook is misleading. Among ALL 33 countries at CPI 14-26, the named 5 are merely the top of a band spanning 50.6-76.3yr. Iran 74.1, Uzbekistan 71.5, Bangladesh 71.4, Honduras 70.9, Nicaragua 70.8, Cambodia 70.1, Guatemala 70.0 sit right with them. The 5 are cherry-picked from the upper tail; the same band's bottom is CAF 50.6 / Nigeria 53.1.
3) Development-confounded by the candidate's own admission -- the all-Sub-Saharan negative cluster is the same point: this is a development/regional gradient, not a corruption->lifespan mechanism. 'Health legacy outlives the regime' is a LEAD-grade interpretation, never a FACT.
TIER: the correlation + residual table are FACT/reproducible, but the entity-level editorial claim about WHY those 5 states are exceptional is LEAD only.

kept.title: Inequality-breeds-violence confirmed in the panel: Gini vs homicide = +0.53 across 254 country-years, and the 6 most murderous nations all carry high Gini (0.40-0.63)
kept.sql: WITH gini AS (SELECT CODE iso, YEAR yr, TRY_CAST(GINI_COEFFICIENT AS DOUBLE) gini FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_OWID_GINI WHERE LENGTH(CODE)=3), hom AS (SELECT CODE iso, YEAR yr, TRY_CAST(HOMICIDE_RATE_PER_100_000_POPULATION AS DOUBLE) hom FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_OWID_HOMICIDE WHERE LENGTH(CODE)=3) SELECT CORR(gini.gini,hom.hom), COUNT_IF(gini.gini IS NOT NULL AND hom.hom IS NOT NULL) n FROM gini JOIN hom ON gini.iso=hom.iso AND gini.yr=hom.yr WHERE gini.yr IN ('2017','2018','2019','2020');
kept.evidence: Pooled ISO3+year join 2017-2020: CORR(Gini, homicide_rate) = +0.528 (n=254 country-years). Top homicide countries with their latest-year Gini all confirm: Jamaica 51.9/100k (Gini 0.399, 2021), Ecuador 45.7 (0.446, 2023), Venezuela 45.0 (0.447, 2006), South Africa 43.7 (0.541, 2022), Lesotho 43.2 (0.630, 1994), Belize 37.6 (0.399, 2018). Note Gini is stored 0-1 here (0.30-0.63 range), not 0-100.
kept.why_matters: A moderate-strong, multi-year-stable correlation tying income inequality to lethal violence -- the central hypothesis of inequality criminology -- reproduced cleanly on the Library's own joined panels. It also demonstrates the cross-country ISO3 reconciliation works end-to-end across three independent OWID sources, which is the foundation for every future cross-country detector.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: Gini vs homicide is a real, robust positive panel correlation (Pearson +0.53 / Spearman +0.56, n=254 country-years, 2017-2020, stable each year and outlier-insensitive) -- but the 'top 6 murderous nations all carry high Gini' anecdote is cherry-picked and false
verdict.notes:
CORE STAT REPRODUCES EXACTLY: ran the candidate's SQL verbatim -> CORR=0.5281, n=254. No fan-out: zero duplicate iso-year rows in either table for 2017-2020, so n=254 is genuine 1:1 country-years. Gini confirmed stored 0-1 (min 0.177, max 0.711, mean 0.374, 0 nulls of 2389) -- the units note is correct.

ROBUSTNESS (all strengthen the correlation, not break it): per-year corr is positive and stable every year (2017:0.49, 2018:0.49, 2019:0.60, 2020:0.68). Spearman rank corr = 0.559 ~ Pearson, so it is NOT a few extreme-value leverage points. Dropping the 13 highest-homicide country-years (>=25/100k) RAISES corr to 0.694 (n=241) -- the signal is broad-based across ordinary countries, not an artifact of Latin-American outliers. The aggregate finding is solid.

WHY DOWNGRADED, NOT CONFIRMED -- the anecdotal half of the claim is wrong/cherry-picked:
(1) The actual top-8 homicide countries by latest year (ISO3) are TCA 76.3, KNA 64.2, VCT 51.3, VIR 49.9, JAM 49.4, ECU 45.7, ZAF 43.7, HTI 41.2 -- NOT the candidate's list (which named Jamaica 51.9, Venezuela, Lesotho, Belize). Jamaica's real latest figure is 49.4, not 51.9.
(2) 4 of the real top-8 (the Caribbean micro-states TCA/KNA/VCT/VIR) have NO Gini row at all -- the candidate silently dropped them.
(3) Of the top countries that DO have Gini: JAM=0.399 and ECU=0.459 are MODERATE, contradicting 'all carry high Gini'. Only ZAF (0.541) and HTI (0.605) are genuinely high.
(4) The Lesotho 0.630 / Belize / Venezuela entries pair a recent-ish homicide rate with a stale Gini (Lesotho Gini year = 1994, 30 yrs old) -- definitionally inconsistent matching.

TIER: Pearson +0.53 is a clean statistical-aggregate result (publishable as a correlation, with the caveat it is ecological/country-level and the join is OWID-to-OWID on a shared ISO3 CODE column, which is fine -- not a fuzzy name match). But 'FACT' was applied to the whole claim including the false top-6 anecdote, so the headline overstates. Keep the correlation as a LEAD-grade panel signal; strike the 'most murderous nations all high Gini' sentence entirely.

kept.title: DATA-QUALITY TRAP: INTL_OWID_MILSPEND is absolute current US$, not %GDP -- any raw correlation with it is just 'economy size', and it silently poisons the military-vs-welfare analysis the track asked for
kept.sql: SELECT ENTITY, CODE, MILITARY_EXPENDITURE FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_OWID_MILSPEND WHERE YEAR='2020' AND CODE IN ('USA','SAU','ISR','OMN','CRI','ISL') ORDER BY TRY_CAST(MILITARY_EXPENDITURE AS DOUBLE) DESC;
kept.evidence: MILITARY_EXPENDITURE for 2020 is raw dollars: USA 943,446,560,000; Saudi Arabia 70,946,460,000; Israel 22,281,728,000; Oman 6,452,070,400; Costa Rica 0; Iceland 0 (the last two are demilitarized -- a true 0, not a null). Because the variable scales with GDP, CORR(milspend, life_exp)=+0.13 (n=153) and CORR(milspend, CPI)=+0.15 (n=151) are meaningless artifacts of country size, NOT a guns-vs-butter signal. To answer the intended question you must normalize by GDP or population (a column this table does not carry).
kept.why_matters: The track explicitly wants 'high military spend but low life expectancy' outliers -- but the milspend table only ships absolute dollars, so naive joins produce a fake weak-positive correlation and would mislead any analyst. Flagging the unit (and the genuine 0s for Costa Rica/Iceland) prevents a wrong published claim and tells the catalog this source needs a GDP companion before it is analytically usable.
kept.implies: new source to load: a GDP or population series (e.g. World Bank NY.GDP.MKTP.CD) to convert milspend to %GDP/per-capita before any military-vs-welfare correlation

verdict.corrected_title: INTL_OWID_MILSPEND is absolute current US$ with NO GDP/population column -- raw correlations with it are economy-size artifacts (CORR with life-exp is ~0.055, not 0.13 as claimed); normalize before any guns-vs-butter analysis
verdict.notes:
CORE THESIS CONFIRMED, ONE RECEIPT WRONG.

What holds (re-ran all):
- Units: MILITARY_EXPENDITURE is absolute current US$. MAX = 2,771,294,000,000 (~2.77T), MIN = 0. A %GDP column would top out ~5-15, so this is unambiguously dollars. Table carries ONLY ENTITY/CODE/YEAR/MILITARY_EXPENDITURE/WORLD_REGION (+ provenance) -- confirmed via DESCRIBE, no GDP or population column. So the 'naive correlation = country size' critique is valid.
- The 6 spot values reproduce EXACTLY: USA 943,446,560,000; SAU 70,946,460,000; ISR 22,281,728,000; OMN 6,452,070,400; CRI 0; ISL 0.
- Genuine zeros, not nulls/blanks: 0 blanks, 0 uncastable rows, 180 exact '0' values. Costa Rica and Iceland are '0' consistently across 2018-2021 -- real demilitarized zeros. Claim is correct.
- CPI correlation reproduces: CORR(milspend, CORRUPTION_PERCEPTIONS_INDEX) = 0.149 (claimed 0.15), n=152 (claimed 151). Note: 'CPI' here = Corruption Perceptions Index, NOT consumer prices -- the candidate's 'CPI' label is ambiguous and could mislead.

What FAILS (why downgraded from FACT):
- CORR(milspend, life_exp) for 2020 = 0.0554, n=155 -- NOT the claimed +0.13, n=153. Checked 2018-2022: correlation is a stable 0.051-0.058, n=155-156 every year. The claimed 0.13/n=153 is not reproducible under any reasonable filter; it's roughly 2x the true value. (The unfiltered join including blank-CODE regional aggregates gives 410 rows and corr 0.012 -- also not 0.13.) The conclusion is unchanged or even reinforced -- 0.055 is an even weaker artifact -- but a published FACT-grade finding cannot carry a wrong number.

Join hygiene is fine: both joins are CODE(ISO3)+YEAR exact matches, no fuzzy/name match, no tier inflation. But note the raw join double-counts blank-CODE aggregate rows -- must filter CODE<>'' (the candidate's own n=153 hints it did filter, but the magnitude still doesn't match).

Net: data-quality flag is genuinely useful and the unit/zero claims are bulletproof, but fix the life-exp correlation to ~0.055 and clarify 'CPI'=corruption index before publishing.

kept.title: Conflict-death spikes drive refugee OUTFLOW: Syria deaths peak 2014, refugees keep climbing to 6.85M
kept.sql: WITH u AS (SELECT UPPER(TRIM(COUNTRY)) c, TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT) y, SUM(TRY_CAST(BEST AS INT)) deaths FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_UCDP_GED GROUP BY 1,2), r AS (SELECT UPPER(TRIM(ENTITY)) c, TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT) y, TRY_CAST(REFUGEES_BY_COUNTRY_OF_ORIGIN AS DOUBLE) refs FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_OWID_REFUGEES) SELECT u.c country, u.y year, u.deaths conflict_deaths, r.refs refugees_origin FROM u JOIN r ON u.c=r.c AND u.y=r.y WHERE u.c='SYRIA' ORDER BY u.y;  -- lag test:  WITH ... j AS (SELECT u.c,u.y,u.deaths, r.refs-LAG(r.refs) OVER(PARTITION BY u.c ORDER BY u.y) delta_refs FROM u JOIN r ON u.c=r.c AND u.y=r.y) SELECT ROUND(CORR(deaths,delta_refs),3), COUNT(*) FROM j WHERE deaths>0 AND delta_refs IS NOT NULL
kept.evidence: Cross-source bridge on normalized country NAME (fuzzy join, hence LEAD): 112 of 124 UCDP country names match OWID entities (90%). Syria: deaths jump from 4,347 (2011) to 78,689 (2014), and refugees of Syrian origin climb 19,931 (2011) -> 3.89M (2014) -> 6.85M (2021), continuing to rise even as deaths fall to ~2k by 2022. Same-year deaths-vs-refugee-LEVEL correlation is weak (0.20, n=1725) but deaths-vs-refugee-GROWTH (year-over-year delta) is 0.31 (n=1628) -- new killing produces new displacement, while the standing refugee stock accumulates and lags.
kept.why_matters: Cleanly demonstrates the lagged, cumulative mechanics of forced displacement: refugee totals are a stock that integrates past violence, not a mirror of current-year deaths. Useful framing for any displacement story -- and a reminder that correlating levels-on-levels understates conflict's demographic footprint.

verdict.corrected_title: Refugees are a lagging stock, not a mirror of current deaths: Syria deaths peak 2014 (78,689) while Syrian-origin refugees keep climbing to 6.85M (2021) — but the UCDP×OWID name-join silently drops the #1 conflict country (DR Congo, 139k deaths)
verdict.notes:
RE-RAN EVERYTHING — core numbers reproduce exactly. Syria series identical to claim: deaths 4,347 (2011) -> 78,689 (2014) -> 1,948 (2021); refugees 19,931 (2011) -> 3.89M (2014) -> 6.85M (2021), then decline to 5.95M (2024). Level corr = 0.20, growth (YoY delta) corr = 0.311 with deaths>0, n=1628 — matches claim to 3 decimals. (Without the deaths>0 filter: level 0.20/n=1776, growth 0.311/n=1669.)

ROBUSTNESS CHECKS I RAN (candidate didn't, these STRENGTHEN it): (a) Excluding Syria entirely, growth corr (0.275) STILL beats level corr (0.187) — so 'growth>level' is not a single-country artifact. (b) Spearman rank corr of deaths-vs-refugee-LEVEL = 0.45, more than double the Pearson 0.20 — confirms the weak Pearson is outlier/scale distortion, and directly supports the candidate's framing that 'levels-on-levels understates conflict's footprint'. The mechanism claim (stock integrates past violence, lags current deaths) is genuinely supported.

WHY DOWNGRADED (the real flaw): the fuzzy NAME join is materially worse than the '112/124 = 90%' stat implies. The 12 unmatched UCDP countries are NOT random tail — they include the SINGLE HIGHEST-DEATH country in the whole dataset: 'DR CONGO (ZAIRE)' = 139,381 deaths, which fails to match OWID's 'DEMOCRATIC REPUBLIC OF CONGO'. Also dropped: Yemen-North (67,779), Bosnia-Herzegovina (63,535), Myanmar/Burma (31,560), Russia/Soviet-Union (33,700). Cause: UCDP appends historical parentheticals '(ZAIRE)/(NORTH YEMEN)/(BURMA)' that OWID omits. Worse, the bare 'CONGO' DOES match — so the join keeps the small Republic of Congo but loses giant DR Congo, a silent geography mismatch. So '90% of names match' is true by count but misleading by analytic weight; several of the most conflict-heavy countries are excluded.

TIER: LEAD is correct and must stay — name-only fuzzy join, no shared key (UCDP uses Gleditsch-Ward country_id, OWID uses ISO3 CODE; the join here ignores both and matches on text NAME). No casting artifacts found: TRY_CAST handles the all-TEXT raw fine, blanks null out cleanly, no double-counting (UCDP rows are events summed by country-year). Net: real, reproducible, well-grounded mechanism, but title/evidence overstate coverage and bury the DRC drop-out — fix the wording, keep LEAD.

kept.title: 1,390 physicians who self-disclosed an ownership stake collected $113.2M -- a built-in conflict-of-interest cohort
kept.sql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_records, COUNT(DISTINCT COVERED_RECIPIENT_PROFILE_ID) AS n_physicians, ROUND(SUM(TRY_CAST(TOTAL_AMOUNT_OF_PAYMENT_USDOLLARS AS DOUBLE)),0) AS total_usd FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS WHERE PHYSICIAN_OWNERSHIP_INDICATOR = 'Yes'
kept.evidence: PHYSICIAN_OWNERSHIP_INDICATOR='Yes': 3,761 records, 1,390 distinct physician profile IDs, $113,231,048 total in 2024. These are doctors who both prescribe/use a manufacturer's product AND hold an equity/ownership interest in that manufacturer -- the strongest single self-reported conflict-of-interest flag in the dataset, and it includes the $91M Goodis/Edge Endo and $25M Goyal/Zimmer acquisitions.
kept.why_matters: This column is a pre-labeled COI cohort sitting unused. Join the 1,390 NPIs to Part D Prescribers (do owner-physicians prescribe their own company's drug more?) and to the prescriber opioid outliers above for a high-value cross-table detector: 'physician owns the maker AND is a top prescriber of its product.' FACT-grade via NPI/profile ID.

verdict.corrected_title: 1,390 physicians self-disclosed an ownership stake in a manufacturer in 2024 Open Payments — a clean, NPI-joinable COI cohort — but the headline $113.2M is 84% one-time M&A buyouts (a single $91M Edge Endo acquisition = 80% of it); recurring conflict money is ~$18.6M, median physician got $160
verdict.notes:
REPRODUCIBILITY: Core numbers reproduce exactly — 3,761 records, 1,390 distinct COVERED_RECIPIENT_PROFILE_ID, $113,231,048, all PROGRAM_YEAR=2024. The 'Yes' flag is genuinely rare (3,761 of 15.4M; 13.2M 'No', 2.2M blank). NPI column (named NPI) is 100% populated on these rows — 1,390 distinct NPIs, 0 nulls, clean 1:1 with profile IDs. So the NPI/profile-ID join is genuinely FACT-grade and the cohort is real and usable. That part stands.

WHY DOWNGRADED (framing overstates):
1) CONCENTRATION ARTIFACT: 'collected $113.2M' is dominated by one record — Charles Goodis / Edge Endo LLC, $91,082,706, nature='Acquisitions' = 80% of the entire cohort total. By NATURE_OF_PAYMENT, 'Acquisitions' = 48 records / $94.66M (84% of total). The recurring product-influence money (Royalty $6.16M + Consulting $8.23M + other-comp $2.75M + the long tail) excluding Acquisitions is only $18.57M across 1,355 physicians.
2) DISTRIBUTION: per-physician median total = $160; mean = $81,461 (skewed by the $91M); 67.3% of physicians collected UNDER $1,000 total. Calling it a '$113.2M cohort' implies broad large-dollar conflict; it's a fat-tailed distribution where most members are tiny.
3) CONCEPTUAL MISMATCH: 'Acquisitions' = a manufacturer buying the physician's company (one-time M&A event), not a payment for using/prescribing the maker's product. The candidate's own thesis ('owns the maker AND is a top prescriber of its product') is undercut by the fact that 84% of the dollars are buyouts, not influence-on-prescribing.
4) FABRICATED RECEIPT: the '$25M Goyal/Zimmer acquisition' does NOT exist. No 'Goyal' appears in the ownership='Yes' cohort at all; the largest Zimmer Biomet payment to ANY owner-physician is $14,722 (James Dowd). This claimed receipt is false and must be struck. The $91M Goodis/Edge Endo record IS real and verified.

VERDICT: cohort is real, NPI-joinable, FACT-grade as an entity set — keep it and build the cross-table detector. But re-word: it's a 1,390-physician COI cohort whose recurring conflict money is ~$18.6M; drop the $113.2M headline (M&A-inflated) and delete the non-existent Goyal/Zimmer claim. Tier stays FACT for the cohort membership itself; the $113.2M 'collected' wording is the part that fails.

kept.title: DATA QUALITY: fed_doj_crt_cases is tagged 'landed' but is a 1-row scrape stub with every entity field empty
kept.sql: SELECT CASE_TITLE, SECTION, CASE_TYPE, PERSON_NAME, DATE, DESCRIPTION, STATUS FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_DOJ_CRT_CASES
kept.evidence: FED_DOJ_CRT_CASES has exactly 1 row: CASE_TITLE='Appellate', DESCRIPTION='Appellate Cases Disability Cases Educational Opportunitie...' (a scraped page-section header), and SECTION/CASE_TYPE/PERSON_NAME/DATE(only 'Updated June 5, 2025')/STATUS all blank. The registry (LIBRARY_META.REGISTRY.CATALOG) classifies it as lifecycle='landed', implying real usable data. The scraper captured a navigation header, not case records.
kept.why_matters: A source masquerading as 'landed' (usable) when it has zero real records will silently break any DOJ civil-rights join or count. The CATALOG is the navigation source of truth per CLAUDE.md, so a mislabeled lifecycle pollutes the moat. Should be reclassified to 'empty'/stub and the scraper re-pointed at the actual case listing.

verdict.corrected_title: DATA QUALITY: fed_doj_crt_cases is CATALOG lifecycle='landed' but holds exactly 1 row that is a scraped nav-header, not a case record — every entity field is blank
verdict.notes:
Every claim reproduces exactly. COUNT(*)=1. The single row: CASE_TITLE='Appellate', DATE='Updated June 5, 2025', and SECTION/CASE_TYPE/COMPANY_ID/PERSON_NAME/STATE/DOCUMENT_URL/STATUS all = empty string (blank, not NULL — confirmed via =''-tests; SECTION etc. are blanks). DESCRIPTION (427 chars) is the full DOJ CRT sidebar concatenated: "Appellate Cases Disability Cases Educational Opportunities ... Rights Cases Special Litigation Cases Voting Cases Sect..." — unmistakably a scraped page navigation/index header, not a case. CATALOG: lifecycle='landed', trust_layer='staging', domain_primary='justice_courts', landing_fqn correct.

No casting artifact (pure text inspection, no math). No definitional/units issue. No entity/name match involved, so zero libel exposure — this is clean data-quality, legitimately FACT grade and publishable.

Two honest caveats that bound the rating (not refutations): (1) Per CLAUDE.md, lifecycle is a DERIVED field; 'landed' almost certainly just means 'table has >=1 row', so this is the derivation rule being naive rather than a hand-mislabel — the *effect* (a nav-stub presenting as usable) is real, but framing it as a deliberate misclassification slightly overstates. (2) The investigator-prompt itself already warns 'several modeled sources are 1-row stubs', so the existence of stub tables is a known class — what's marginally novel here is that this one is tagged 'landed' (not 'modeled') and the lone row is a literal navigation header rather than even a partial record. Importance is bounded because no downstream model currently depends on this source; the harm is potential (silent break of any future DOJ civil-rights count/join), not active. Recommendation in the candidate (reclassify to empty/stub, re-point scraper at the real case listing) is sound.

kept.title: Ransomware victim disclosures exploded ~30x in five years: 270 in 2020 to 7,939 in 2025, and the US alone is 45% of all named victims
kept.sql: SELECT LEFT(PUBLISHED,4) yr, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_RANSOMWARELIVE_VICTIMS WHERE PUBLISHED<>'' GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1;  -- and: SELECT COUNT(*) total, SUM(CASE WHEN COUNTRY='US' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) us, ROUND(100.0*SUM(CASE WHEN COUNTRY='US' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/COUNT(*),1) us_pct FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_RANSOMWARELIVE_VICTIMS WHERE COUNTRY<>''
kept.evidence: Victim leak-site posts by publish year: 2020=270, 2021=1816, 2022=3168, 2023=5410, 2024=6055, 2025=7939, 2026=4391 (partial -- only to 2026-06-27, already pacing past 2024). Of 21,282 victims with a non-blank country, 9,529 (44.8%) are US; next are CA 1004, GB 975, DE 937, FR 626. Top groups: lockbit3 2016, qilin 1947, akira 1532, play 1271, clop 1254.
kept.why_matters: A beautiful, clean exponential -- ransomware-extortion leak-site disclosures grew nearly 30x in five years, with America bearing almost half the global load. The 2026 partial already exceeds full-year 2024 by mid-year, signaling no plateau. This is the macro context for the CISA-KEV appliance finding above (the vulns being exploited to produce these victims).

verdict.corrected_title: Ransomware leak-site victim posts grew ~29x from 2020 (270) to 2025 (7,939); US is 45% of victims-with-a-known-country (33% of all victims). Growth is flattening, not still exponential: H1-2026 vs H1-2025 is only +8.5%.
verdict.notes:
All raw numbers reproduce EXACTLY. Year trend 2020=270...2025=7939 (29.4x); US=9529/21282 non-blank-country=44.8%; top countries CA1004/GB975/DE937/FR626; top groups lockbit3 2016/qilin 1947/akira 1532/play 1271/clop 1254 -- all confirmed. Dates are clean ISO timestamps (0 unparseable via TRY_TO_TIMESTAMP), Jan-Dec bounds clean, so LEFT(PUBLISHED,4) text-sort is NOT a casting artifact. Single-source descriptive count, no fuzzy/cross-ID match, so FACT grade on the raw numbers stands.

Three overstatements force DOWNGRADE:
(1) DENOMINATOR ERROR -- 'US is 45% of all named victims' is wrong. 7,911 of 29,193 rows (27%) have BLANK country. 44.8% is US share of the 21,282 with a known country; US is only 32.6% (9529/29193) of all victims. The word 'all' is inaccurate.
(2) PACING CLAIM IS APPLES-TO-ORANGES -- '2026 partial already pacing past 2024, signaling no plateau' compares a half-year (4391, to 2026-06-27) against full years. The honest like-for-like: H1-2026=4391 vs H1-2025=4046 = only +8.5% YoY. Growth has essentially flattened.
(3) 'beautiful clean exponential' overstates -- it's an S-curve decelerating: 2023->2024 +11.9%, 2024->2025 +31.1%, H1-YoY +8.5%. Real and large, but no longer exponential.

SQL (all re-run via helper): year trend = SELECT LEFT(PUBLISHED,4) yr,COUNT(*) n ... WHERE PUBLISHED<>'' GROUP BY 1; US = SELECT COUNT(*),SUM(CASE WHEN COUNTRY='US'...)... WHERE COUNTRY<>''; blanks = SELECT SUM(CASE WHEN COUNTRY='' OR COUNTRY IS NULL...) -> 7911 blank country, 2 blank pub, 29193 total; H1 pacing = SELECT COUNT(*) WHERE PUBLISHED>='2025-01-01' AND PUBLISHED<'2025-06-28' -> 4046 vs 2026 H1 4391.

kept.title: crime_security shows '0 data' but real crime data exists -- it's all dumped in UNCLASSIFIED, so the domain map lies
kept.sql: SELECT SOURCE_ID, DOMAIN_PRIMARY, LIFECYCLE FROM LIBRARY_META.REGISTRY.CATALOG WHERE SOURCE_ID IN ('fed_fbi_nics_checks','xc_wapo_fatal_force','xc_ransomwarelive_victims','fed_cisa_kev','xc_owid_terrorism_deaths','fed_fec_bulk')
kept.evidence: All six are LIFECYCLE='landed' yet DOMAIN_PRIMARY='UNCLASSIFIED': fed_fbi_nics_checks (16,445 rows), xc_wapo_fatal_force (10,430), xc_ransomwarelive_victims (29,193), fed_cisa_kev (1,629), xc_owid_terrorism_deaths (10,481), fed_fec_bulk (20,938). Meanwhile the crime_security domain reports 0 landed/modeled and elections_voting reports 0 -- even though FBI gun-checks, WaPo police killings, ransomware victims, and FEC campaign-finance bulk are all sitting landed under UNCLASSIFIED.
kept.why_matters: The 'zero-data domain' map cannot be taken at face value: at least crime_security and elections_voting are FALSE holes -- the data is landed but mis-tagged to UNCLASSIFIED. Any browse-by-domain UI or 'what can we investigate' query built on DOMAIN_PRIMARY will tell users we have no crime or elections data when we do. Re-classifying the 49 UNCLASSIFIED landed sources is the highest-leverage catalog fix.

verdict.corrected_title: crime_security and elections_voting report 0 landed data by DOMAIN_PRIMARY, but the data is landed under UNCLASSIFIED -- a browse-by-domain false hole (catalog backlog, not deception)
verdict.notes:
Every number reproduces exactly. All 6 sources are LIFECYCLE='landed' with DOMAIN_PRIMARY='UNCLASSIFIED'; landing row counts match the claim precisely (fed_fbi_nics_checks 16,445; xc_wapo_fatal_force 10,430; xc_ransomwarelive_victims 29,193; fed_cisa_kev 1,629; xc_owid_terrorism_deaths 10,481; fed_fec_bulk 20,938). crime_security shows 0 landed/modeled (25 scouted), elections_voting shows 0 (5 scouted); confirmed zero via direct query of both domains. Both domains exist in FACET_VOCAB (governed vocab), so this isn't a missing-category issue. UNCLASSIFIED holds 49 landed/modeled of 111 total UNCLASSIFIED sources.

NOT AN ARTIFACT: claim is string equality on classification columns (no casting/math/blank-as-zero risk). DOMAIN_SECONDARY, THEMES, ENTITY_TYPES are all empty arrays for these 6, ruling out 'classified elsewhere.' So the contradiction (browse-by-DOMAIN_PRIMARY returns false zeros for crime/elections while data sits landed) is fully real.

WHY DOWNGRADED (wording, not substance): the candidate frames UNCLASSIFIED as the map 'lying.' It is actually a known, honest backlog -- all 6 sit in V_REVIEW_QUEUE (704 sources awaiting human topic/classification per CLAUDE.md). So it's a catalog-completeness gap, not deception. Also definitional nit: fed_fec_bulk is arguably money_in_politics (which already has 1 landed) rather than elections_voting -- the 'elections is a false hole' line leans on FEC, which is campaign-finance, not vote returns; xc_owid_terrorism_deaths is arguably crime_security-adjacent but global, not US crime. Core claim survives on FBI NICS + WaPo fatal-force + ransomware for crime_security regardless.

TIER stays FACT: reproducible string-equality evidence on hard catalog columns, no entity/person attribution involved (libel risk = none). Surprise low (UNCLASSIFIED-as-backlog is semi-expected); importance moderate (any DOMAIN_PRIMARY browse UI genuinely misrepresents coverage until the review queue is worked). The 'highest-leverage fix' framing is opinion, not data-supported.

kept.title: Cross-source sanity holds: UCDP 'conflict' deaths are ~30% of Mexico's total homicides -- coherent, and it quantifies how much Mexican violence is cartel-conflict-classified
kept.sql: SELECT o.YEAR, o.HOMICIDE_RATE_PER_100_000_POPULATION AS owid_rate, u.ucdp_total_conflict_deaths, u.ucdp_civilian_deaths FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_OWID_HOMICIDE o JOIN (SELECT YEAR, SUM(TRY_CAST(DEATHS_A AS INT)+TRY_CAST(DEATHS_B AS INT)+TRY_CAST(DEATHS_CIVILIANS AS INT)+TRY_CAST(DEATHS_UNKNOWN AS INT)) ucdp_total_conflict_deaths, SUM(TRY_CAST(DEATHS_CIVILIANS AS INT)) ucdp_civilian_deaths FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_UCDP_GED WHERE COUNTRY='Mexico' GROUP BY 1) u ON o.YEAR=u.YEAR WHERE o.ENTITY='Mexico' AND TRY_CAST(o.YEAR AS INT)>=2018 ORDER BY o.YEAR
kept.evidence: Mexico (~127M pop), 2018: OWID homicide rate 29.4/100k implies ~37,000 total homicides; UCDP logs 11,247 conflict deaths same year (2019: 29.2 vs 10,486; 2020: 29.0 vs 13,431; 2021: 28.0 vs 15,119; 2022: 25.9 vs 12,555; 2023: 24.9 vs 10,899). UCDP < OWID every year (direction correct), and UCDP conflict deaths run ~30-40% of implied total homicides. UCDP civilian-only deaths are tiny by comparison (28-159/yr) -- the bulk is non-state (cartel vs cartel). LEAD-tier: the OWID<->UCDP join is on country NAME, not a hard key (UCDP labels countries 'Russia (Soviet Union)', 'DR Congo (Zaire)', 'Myanmar (Burma)' -- they share NO key column with OWID's ISO3).
