kept.why_matters: Two independent sources are internally coherent (no contradiction) AND together produce a publishable number: roughly a third of Mexican killings are organized-armed-conflict-classified violence, almost all non-state cartel conflict rather than civilian-targeted. Separately, the UCDP parenthetical country names ('Russia (Soviet Union)' etc.) are a concrete join hole -- any cross-country UCDP join needs a name->ISO3 crosswalk first or it silently drops the biggest conflict countries.

verdict.corrected_title: UCDP conflict deaths ≈ 30-34% of an *estimated* Mexican homicide total (OWID has only a RATE, not a count — the denominator is hand-derived from external population) — internally coherent, and exposes a real UCDP parenthetical-country-name join hole
verdict.notes:
RE-RAN AND REPRODUCES EXACTLY. All numbers hold: 2018 OWID rate 29.448, UCDP 11,247; 2023 24.859 / 10,899, etc. Casting is clean — BEST sum == component sum (DEATHS_A+B+CIVILIANS+UNKNOWN) to the row, zero NULL casts in 15,125 Mexico rows, so no silent TRY_CAST loss or double-count.

WHY DOWNGRADED (not rejected): The headline "~30% of total homicides" is real arithmetic but the denominator is NOT in the Library. Verified: XC_OWID_HOMICIDE carries ONLY HOMICIDE_RATE_PER_100_000_POPULATION — there is no count column. The "~37,000 implied homicides" is rate × an EXTERNAL ~127M population (not a Library table). So this isn't really "two sources internally coherent" in the clean sense — it's one source's count (UCDP) divided by a hand-computed estimate off another source's rate. Ratio math confirmed: 2018=30.1%, 2023=34.3% (using 127M/128M pop). Plausible but population-sensitive and not a cross-source data match — frame it as a sanity-check estimate, not a published "third of all killings" fact without citing the population source.

CONFIRMED sub-claims: (1) Direction holds — UCDP < implied OWID every year. (2) Composition: 2018-2023 Mexico = 73,506 type-2 (non-state) deaths vs only 231 type-3 (one-sided/civilian) and ZERO type-1 (state-based) — so "almost all non-state cartel conflict, not civilian-targeted" is accurate (99.7% non-state). (3) JOIN HOLE FULLY CONFIRMED and genuinely useful: 9 UCDP countries carry parenthetical legacy names — 'Russia (Soviet Union)', 'DR Congo (Zaire)', 'Myanmar (Burma)', 'Cambodia (Kampuchea)', 'Yemen (North Yemen)', 'Serbia (Yugoslavia)', 'Zimbabwe (Rhodesia)', 'Madagascar (Malagasy)', 'Kingdom of eSwatini (Swaziland)'. OWID uses plain 'Russia'/'Myanmar'/'Yemen'/'Cambodia' — a naive NAME join silently drops the biggest conflict countries. This part deserves to be the lead, not a footnote.

TIER: LEAD is correct (name-only join, no hard ISO3 key shared). Surprise low (rate-vs-count sanity checks are expected to roughly line up). Importance moderate — the join-hole warning is the most actionable piece.

kept.title: fed_fbi_nics_checks is a perfectly balanced 55-jurisdiction monthly panel -- 16,445 = 55 x 299 with zero gaps
kept.sql: SELECT 16445.0/55 AS rows_per_state, (SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT MONTH) FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_FBI_NICS_CHECKS) distinct_months, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_FBI_NICS_CHECKS WHERE STATE IN ('Guam','Puerto Rico','Virgin Islands','Mariana Islands','District of Columbia')) territory_rows;  -- support: MIN/MAX MONTH = 1998-11 / 2023-09, 55 distinct STATE, 299 distinct MONTH, every STATE = 299 rows
kept.evidence: 16,445 rows = exactly 55 jurisdictions x 299 months (1998-11 through 2023-09), rows_per_state=299.0, distinct_months=299. 55 'states' = 50 + DC + 4 territories (PR, GU, VI, MP = 1,495 territory rows). TOTALS fully numeric (bad_totals=0), range 0 to 1,427,917. No missing month for any jurisdiction -- a complete, regular cross-sectional time series.
kept.why_matters: Rare in this warehouse: a genuinely gap-free balanced panel with clean numeric measures, ready for time-series/seasonality work straight out of landing. Worth flagging as a model-now candidate and a reference for what 'good' ingest looks like next to the broken ones above.
kept.implies: none (model-ready as-is)

verdict.corrected_title: fed_fbi_nics_checks is a perfectly balanced 55-jurisdiction monthly panel -- 16,445 = 55 x 299, gap-free (50 states + DC + 4 territories; the 4 territories are 1,196 rows, not 1,495)
verdict.notes:
Core claim reproduces exactly and is structurally airtight. Verified independently: COUNT(*)=16,445; 55 distinct STATE; 299 distinct MONTH (1998-11 to 2023-09). Every state has exactly 299 rows (per-state COUNT min=max=299, states_not_299=0). Crucially, COUNT(DISTINCT STATE||'|'||MONTH)=16,445 with dup_pairs=0, so it is a TRUE balanced panel, not duplicate rows masking gaps. Every state shares the identical window (all start 1998-11, all end 2023-09). TOTALS fully numeric: TRY_CAST bad=0, null/blank=0, range 0 to 1,427,917; max (Illinois 2021-03) is a real numeric value, not a text-sort artifact.

ONE ERROR in supporting detail -> downgrade reason: the claim states 'PR, GU, VI, MP = 1,495 territory rows.' Actual 4-territory count = 1,196 (= 4 x 299). The claimed 1,495 = 5 x 299 erroneously included District of Columbia, which is NOT a territory. The claim's own prose contradicts itself ('50 + DC + 4 territories' then labels the DC-inclusive number 'territory rows'). Verified decomposition: 50 states=14,950, DC=299, 4 territories=1,196, sum=16,445.

Tier stays FACT (this is structural/arithmetic, no entity claim, no fuzzy match). But surprise/importance are modest: this is the canonical FBI NICS monthly background-check series (the well-known BuzzFeed-cleaned 55xN panel). A clean balanced panel from a professionally-maintained gov series is expected, not anomalous -- it's a legit 'model-now / good-ingest reference' flag, not a discovery. Downgraded for the 1,495 vs 1,196 factual slip in the headline-supporting detail; corrected_title fixes the number.

kept.title: ClinicalTrials sample carries ZERO trials with posted results (HAS_RESULTS true) yet 257 are COMPLETED/TERMINATED
kept.sql: SELECT OVERALL_STATUS, HAS_RESULTS, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CLINICALTRIALS GROUP BY 1,2 ORDER BY n DESC
kept.evidence: Of 500 trials, only 62 have HAS_RESULTS=True (49 COMPLETED + 11 TERMINATED + 1 UNKNOWN + 1 ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING). 208 COMPLETED and 28 TERMINATED trials have HAS_RESULTS=False - i.e. 236 finished trials never posted results. RESULTS_FIRST_POSTED_DATE is correspondingly blank for them. ENROLLMENT also clusters hard on round numbers: top values are 30 (16x), 20 (16x), 100 (14x), 0 (14x), 10/50/40/80/60/200 - classic 'planned/target' round-number enrollment, plus 14 trials reporting 0 enrolled.
kept.why_matters: Two real signals in one tiny table: (1) the well-documented results-reporting gap (FDAAA non-compliance) shows up even in a 500-row pull - 236/264 completed-or-terminated trials with no results; (2) enrollment is dominated by round target numbers and 14 zero-enrollment trials, so ENROLLMENT cannot be read as actual accrual without care. Good story hook and a casting caveat.
kept.implies: new angle: results-reporting-gap detector (COMPLETED + HAS_RESULTS=false past due) once a fuller ClinicalTrials pull lands (this is only 500 rows)

verdict.corrected_title: ClinicalTrials 500-row sample: 236 of 296 completed/terminated trials have NO posted results (HAS_RESULTS=False), and ENROLLMENT clusters hard on round target numbers (plus 14 zero-enrollment trials)
verdict.notes:
SQL reproduces exactly (14 status x has_results rows, identical counts). Body signal is REAL: COMPLETED+TERMINATED = 296 finished trials, 60 with results, 236 without -> the FDAAA results-reporting gap is visible even in 500 rows. RESULTS_FIRST_POSTED_DATE tracks HAS_RESULTS perfectly (62 has_date = 62 True; 438 blank = 438 False) -- internally consistent, no contradiction. Enrollment round-number clustering reproduces (30:16, 20:16, 100:14, 0:14, 10/40/50/60/80/200...) and is NOT a casting artifact: HAS_RESULTS is clean text 'True'/'False' (exactly 2 distinct, no nulls/blanks/casing), and zero-enrollment (14, via TRY_CAST=0) is genuinely separate from blank-enrollment (7) -- blanks are not being counted as zero.

WHY DOWNGRADED, not CONFIRMED: the TITLE is flatly false and self-contradictory. It claims 'ZERO trials with posted results (HAS_RESULTS true)' while the data -- and the candidate's own evidence body -- show 62 trials with HAS_RESULTS=True. The headline states the opposite of the data. That alone disqualifies the stated claim. Also the evidence's '236/264 completed-or-terminated' denominator is loose: actual finished total = 296 (not 264); 236 without-results is the correct numerator but over the wrong base. Corrected title states 236/296.

TIER: all evidence is within ONE landing table on its own native columns (no cross-source / name / fuzzy match), so it is FACT-grade as a data-quality observation -- the downgrade is for the false headline and loose denominator, not for a libel/tier problem. Surprise/importance modest: the results-reporting gap is well-documented (the finding even says so) and it's a single 500-row truncated pull (TOTAL=500 looks like a hard API LIMIT, so this is a sample, not the full registry).

kept.title: fed_cms_pos_other: 'STATE_CD' field carries non-US-state junk codes -- CN (263 rows), MX (Mexico, 7), plus territories
kept.sql: SELECT STATE_CD, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_POS_OTHER GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY n DESC
kept.evidence: 44,429 rows across 58 distinct STATE_CD values. Beyond the 50 states + DC + standard territories (PR 212, VI 7, GU 5, MP 5, AS 1), there are non-state codes: 'CN' = 263 rows (not a US postal state -- legacy CMS code / data error) and 'MX' = 7 rows (Mexico) for what is a US Provider-of-Service file. TX (3852) leads, not CA.
kept.why_matters: Any per-state aggregation or FIPS join on this CMS file will leak 270+ rows into bogus 'states' (CN, MX) that won't match a US state crosswalk, silently dropping or misclassifying providers. Small but the kind of dirty-key issue that breaks geographic joins downstream.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: fed_cms_pos_other STATE_CD carries 270 FOREIGN-provider rows (CN=Canada 263, MX=Mexico 7) plus 230 territory rows — legit CMS codes, but they break any 50-state/FIPS crosswalk
verdict.notes:
Numbers reproduce EXACTLY: 44,429 rows, 58 distinct STATE_CD, 0 null/blank, TX=3852 leads (not CA=3622), CN=263, MX=7, PR=212, VI=7, GU=5, MP=5, AS=1. Buckets: 43,929 state/DC + 270 foreign + 230 territory. No casting artifact (clean 2-char categorical, no nulls, no numeric-sort issue).

KEY CORRECTION — the candidate mischaracterizes CN/MX as 'junk codes / legacy CMS code / data error'. They are NOT junk. Pulling the records: every CN city is unambiguously Canadian (Montreal, Winnipeg, Calgary, Vancouver, Edmundston, Sherbrooke, Lethbridge, Saint John...) and every MX city is Mexican (Tijuana, Mexicali Baja California, Nogales Sonora, Coahuila). CMS's State Code reference table legitimately uses CN=Canada and MX=Mexico for foreign-affiliated providers. All 270 foreign rows have BLANK ZIP_CD (vs PR/GU/TX which all have populated ZIPs), consistent with real foreign records lacking US ZIPs — further evidence they're intentional, not corrupt.

So: the data observation is FACT-grade and the receipts hold, but the WORDING overclaims ('junk', 'data error', 'not a US postal state'). Downgraded for framing, not for accuracy. The genuinely useful, correct part survives: 270 foreign + 230 territory = 500 rows that will silently drop or misclassify on a naive 50-state/DC FIPS crosswalk. Mild importance/surprise — anyone who has touched a CMS POS file knows it includes foreign and territory providers; this is a known data property, not a discovery. Title corrected to call them foreign/legit codes rather than junk.

kept.title: SERCOP (Ecuador procurement) OCID is a flawless unique key (132,995/132,995) but the 'panel' is secretly only 2025-2026
kept.sql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_rows, SUM(CASE WHEN TRY_CAST(AWARD_VALUE_AMOUNT AS DOUBLE)=0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS zero_award, SUM(CASE WHEN TRY_CAST(AWARD_VALUE_AMOUNT AS DOUBLE) < 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS neg_award, COUNT(DISTINCT OCID) AS dist_ocid, COUNT(DISTINCT BUYER_NAME) AS dist_buyer, MIN(LEFT(DATE,4)) AS min_yr, MAX(LEFT(DATE,4)) AS max_yr FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_EC_SERCOP
kept.evidence: 132995 rows, 132995 distinct OCID (perfect 1:1 primary key), 0 negative awards, only 1 zero-value award, 3206 distinct buyers. But DATE year range is just 2025-2026. Two currency buckets: USD 119393 rows (awards up to 115.99M) and 13602 rows with blank currency.
kept.why_matters: Rare for a raw landing table: a genuinely clean, fully-unique OCID key with no negatives -- ideal for joins -- yet the temporal scope is a single recent window, not the multi-year history the 132k row count implies. Good key, narrow time.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: SERCOP (Ecuador): OCID is a flawless unique release key (132,995/132,995, all 'ocds-' prefixed) but the table is a single recent window (2025-01-01 to 2026-06-05), and OCID is a per-RELEASE id, not a contract id (AWARD_ID/CONTRACT_ID are NOT unique)
verdict.notes:
CORE CLAIM HOLDS, EVIDENCE NUMBERS PARTLY WRONG.

REPRODUCES: n_rows=132,995; dist_ocid=132,995 (perfect 1:1, zero blank/null OCID, all 'ocds-' prefixed); dist_buyer=3,206; year range 2025-2026 (2025=76,441; 2026=56,554, max DATE 2026-06-05). DATE is full ISO timestamp '2025-01-01T00:00:00-05:00', length 25. Single ~17-month window confirmed -- the 'good key, narrow time' headline is real.

DOES NOT REPRODUCE -- currency split is flatly wrong. Candidate claimed USD 119,393 rows + 13,602 blank-currency; actual AWARD_VALUE_CURRENCY buckets are USD 127,299 and blank 5,696. (Candidate also referenced a column 'AWARD_CURRENCY' that does not exist; real col is AWARD_VALUE_CURRENCY.) Numbers off by ~7,900.

ARTIFACT in the 'clean awards' framing: 'zero_award=1, neg_award=0' is computed via TRY_CAST(AWARD_VALUE_AMOUNT AS DOUBLE), which SILENTLY NULLS 5,696 rows (4.3%) that have a blank award amount -- and those are exactly the 5,696 blank-currency rows. So it's '0 negatives / 1 zero among the 127,299 rows that have a parseable USD amount,' not across the whole table. Max award 115,992,214.01, min 0.0.

KEY CAVEAT for joins (downgrades 'ideal for joins'): OCID is a per-RELEASE identifier (OCDS model), not a contract grain. Distinct counts: OCID 132,995 (unique) but TENDER_ID 129,161, AWARD_ID 127,223, CONTRACT_ID 122,925 -- i.e. the same tender/award/contract recurs across multiple OCIDs (amendments/releases). Joining on OCID is clean; treating one OCID = one contract is wrong (~10k duplicate contracts).

Tier stays FACT (it's a HARD structural property of the table, no entity/name match involved), surprise/importance modest -- an OCDS release table being release-unique and recently scoped is expected behavior, not an anomaly. Downgraded for: two non-reproducing evidence numbers and the missing OCDS-release / blank-amount caveats.

kept.title: VA all-cause mortality is a broken scrape, not data: 244 rows, COL_1 is 100% blank, the 'data' column is 93% blank and the rest are figure CAPTIONS ('1. Leading Causes of Death in 2023...')
kept.sql: SELECT COUNT(*) total, SUM(CASE WHEN COL_1='' OR COL_1 IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) col1_blank, SUM(CASE WHEN COL_2='' OR COL_2 IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) col2_blank, SUM(CASE WHEN FIGURES_AND_TABLES='' OR FIGURES_AND_TABLES IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) c0_blank FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_VA_ALLCAUSE_MORTALITY
kept.evidence: 244 rows. COL_1 blank in 244/244 (single-valued empty column). COL_2 blank in 236/244 (the 8 non-blank are literally 'Go to Top' nav text). FIGURES_AND_TABLES blank in 228/244; the 16 non-blank values are PDF figure titles like '1. Leading Causes of Death in 2023 and Suicide, Veterans...' and '5. VHA Veterans 15 Leading Causes of Death in 2023: Rankings'. There is no mortality count, rate, or year-by-row in the table at all -- the columns FIGURES_AND_TABLES/COL_1/COL_2 prove the scraper grabbed a table-of-figures page, not the data.
kept.why_matters: This source is cataloged as landed but is effectively empty -- a stub masquerading as a real dataset, same failure class as the known 1-row modeled stubs. It should be flagged for re-ingest (the actual VA suicide/mortality numbers are in a different PDF table) and excluded from any 'VA mortality' analysis until then.
kept.implies: new source to load: re-scrape the actual VA National Veteran Suicide Prevention data tables; flag this row in registry as empty

verdict.corrected_title: FED_VA_ALLCAUSE_MORTALITY is a broken scrape of a PDF list-of-figures page, not mortality data: 244 rows, 227 fully blank, COL_1 100% empty, and the only content is 16 figure CAPTIONS plus 8 'Go to Top' nav links
verdict.notes:
Verified end to end; every number reproduces.

RE-RUN of candidate SQL: total=244, COL_1 blank=244/244, COL_2 blank=236/244, FIGURES_AND_TABLES blank=228/244. Exact match.

Content check (SELECT non-blank rows): the 16 non-blank FIGURES_AND_TABLES are PDF figure titles like '1. Leading Causes of Death in 2023 and Suicide, Veterans...' and '5. VHA Veterans 15 Leading Causes of Death in 2023: Rankings' (12 DISTINCT, duplicated because the scrape captured two passes of the 8-figure list). The 8 non-blank COL_2 values are literally 'Go to Top'.

Fully-blank-row check: 227/244 rows are blank across all three data columns; only 17 carry anything.

ARTIFACT RULED OUT: not a casting/blank-counting issue. NULLIF(TRIM(COL_1),'') real count = 0 and MAX(LENGTH(COL_1)) = 0 -> COL_1 is genuinely empty, not whitespace. TRY_CAST AS DOUBLE on COL_2 and FIGURES_AND_TABLES both return 0 numeric cells -> there is no mortality count, rate, or numeric year anywhere in the table. So the 'broken scrape' read is correct; it grabbed the list-of-figures / table-of-contents page, not the data table.

Tier FACT is appropriate (pure data-quality observation about one table, no entity/person claim, no cross-source inference). No libel exposure.

Minor wording: candidate's '93% blank' for the data column = 228/244 = 93.4% (FIGURES_AND_TABLES) is accurate; COL_2 is actually 96.7% blank. corrected_title tightened to lead with the structural fact (227/244 fully blank) and to clarify the 16 captions are duplicated from 8 figures.

Importance is moderate, not high: this is a known failure class (the brief already notes 'several modeled sources are 1-row stubs'); value is the explicit re-ingest flag and the instruction to exclude it from any 'VA mortality' analysis. Surprise low -- a 244-row all-text stub being empty is the expected failure mode, not a non-obvious signal.

kept.title: NADAC drug-pricing data is clean and rich (1.5M rows, 32.9k NDCs, 52 weekly snapshots, no non-positive prices) — a rare fully-usable big table in this batch
kept.sql: SELECT COUNT(*) total, COUNT(DISTINCT NDC) ndcs, MIN(TRY_CAST(EFFECTIVE_DATE AS DATE)) min_d, MAX(TRY_CAST(EFFECTIVE_DATE AS DATE)) max_d, MIN(TRY_CAST(NADAC_PER_UNIT AS DOUBLE)) min_p, MAX(TRY_CAST(NADAC_PER_UNIT AS DOUBLE)) max_p, SUM(CASE WHEN TRY_CAST(NADAC_PER_UNIT AS DOUBLE)<=0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) nonpos, COUNT(DISTINCT AS_OF_DATE) asof FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_NADAC
kept.evidence: 1,497,925 rows, 32,881 distinct NDCs, EFFECTIVE_DATE spans 2022-11-23 to 2024-12-25 across 52 distinct weekly AS_OF_DATE snapshots. NADAC_PER_UNIT min=0.00145, max=26968.37967, with ZERO non-positive prices (nonpos=0). PRICING_UNIT is a clean 3-value enum: EA=1,244,349 / ML=167,874 / GM=85,702. Carries NDC, the FDA/drug join key.
kept.why_matters: Contrast with the rest of this batch (broken scrape, hollow settlements, dirty grades): NADAC is a textbook weekly price panel — no negatives, no blanks in the price, a tidy 52-week cadence, and a real NDC join key. It is one of the few tables here that is publication-ready, and its NDC key bridges to FDA drug data (note: FDA enforcement's own PRODUCT_NDC is 100% empty, so the bridge must run NADAC->NDC->elsewhere).
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: FED_CMS_NADAC is a clean, publication-ready weekly drug-price panel: 1.5M rows, 32.9k 11-digit NDCs, 52 weekly AS_OF snapshots (all 2024-01-03 to 2024-12-25), zero non-positive/null/blank prices, NDC+AS_OF a perfect unique grain
verdict.notes:
All headline numbers reproduce exactly: 1,497,925 rows, 32,881 NDCs, price min 0.00145 / max 26968.37967, nonpos=0, 52 AS_OF snapshots, enum EA=1,244,349 / ML=167,874 / GM=85,702.

ARTIFACT CHECKS (all passed -- nonpos=0 is NOT a TRY_CAST mask): on NADAC_PER_UNIT raw_null=0, raw_blank=0, cast_null=0, so every one of 1.5M prices genuinely casts to a positive double. NDC: 0 blank, uniform LENGTH=11 (single variant). Weekly cadence verified real -- all 51 inter-snapshot gaps = exactly 7 days. Grain integrity: COUNT(DISTINCT NDC||'|'||AS_OF_DATE)=1,497,925 = total rows, so NDC+AS_OF is a perfect unique key, zero dups. FDA bridge supporting claim verified: FED_FDA_DRUG_ENFORCEMENT PRODUCT_NDC is 5000/5000 empty.

WHY DOWNGRADED (wording, not artifact): the candidate conflates two date columns. The 52 weekly snapshots are AS_OF_DATE and span 2024-01-03 to 2024-12-25 (357 days, all in 2024). The 2022-11-23 start belongs to EFFECTIVE_DATE (price-effective dates, a different column that predates the published files). So 'a tidy 52-week cadence spanning 2022-11-23 to 2024-12-25' mis-attributes the range -- the panel is a 2024 weekly panel. Cadence claim itself is true. Tier stays FACT (this is a data-quality observation on one table's own columns -- a HARD-ID/structural fact, no cross-source name match involved). Not surprising (clean govt reference table is expected); moderate importance (a usable bridge table, but the NDC->FDA bridge is dead on the enforcement side per the empty PRODUCT_NDC).

kept.title: intl_ch_zefix isn't a company registry -- all 18 rows are scraped website nav-bar chrome, zero actual companies
kept.sql: SELECT COUNT(*) total, COUNT(DISTINCT UID) uids, COUNT(DISTINCT CHID) chids, COUNT(DISTINCT STATUS) statuses FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_CH_ZEFIX;  -- then: SELECT UID, CHID, NAME, STATUS, ADDRESS_CANTON, COUNTRY FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_CH_ZEFIX
kept.evidence: 18 rows total but UID=1 distinct (blank), CHID=1 distinct (blank), STATUS=1 distinct (blank), COUNTRY=1 (CH). The NAME column holds only UI labels: 'Search for a business name', 'SOGC search', 'Registries of commerce', 'Localities', 'Contact', and a footer '(c) 2026 Federal Department of Justice and Police (FDJP)'. The same 6 menu items repeat once per canton (ZH, BE, LU) = 18 rows. No business UID, no CHID, no legal form, no status -- the scraper captured the Zefix homepage navigation, not the registry.
kept.why_matters: The inventory lists intl_ch_zefix as a Swiss company source carrying a UID join key. In reality it carries zero entities -- any join or detector built on it would silently match nothing, and the catalog overstates Swiss-registry coverage. This is a dead source that looks alive.
kept.implies: new source to load (re-scrape Zefix API properly, or mark source empty/broken in catalog)

verdict.corrected_title: intl_ch_zefix is a dead source: all 18 rows are scraped Zefix homepage nav/footer chrome (6 UI labels x 3 cantons), zero companies and zero UIDs
verdict.notes:
Fully reproduced against LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_CH_ZEFIX. Counts: 18 rows; COUNT(NULLIF(TRIM(col),'')) = 0 for UID, CHID, STATUS, and LEGAL_FORM (every business field is blank, not null vs zero ambiguity — confirmed via TRIM). 6 distinct NAME values, all site chrome: 'Search for a business name', 'SOGC search', 'Registries of commerce', 'Localities', 'Contact', and footer '© 2026 Federal Department of Justice and Police (FDJP)'. ADDRESS_CANTON = 3 values (ZH, BE, LU); 6 labels x 3 cantons = 18. COUNTRY constant 'CH'. No casting artifact (everything is genuinely blank text, not a TRY_CAST nulling issue). No definitional/scope confusion — it is simply empty.

Claim holds as FACT-grade data quality. Two minor caveats, neither material: (1) one of the 6 labels is a copyright footer, so 'nav-bar chrome' is slightly loose — it's general site chrome including footer, hence the corrected title. (2) Surprise/importance are modest, not high: the inventory itself already warns 'several modeled sources are 1-row stubs,' and sibling micro-registries (intl_es_borme:25, intl_ie_cro:3, intl_gr_gemi:40) are similarly thin — so a dead 18-row Swiss stub fits a known pattern rather than being a shock. Real and worth flagging to fix the catalog's UID-join-key overstatement, but it's one dead micro-source, not investigation-reshaping.

kept.title: Beautiful signal: industry-wide physician payments are flat to the dollar YoY -- $3.3140B (2023) vs $3.3138B (2024), 0.008% apart
kept.sql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS n, ROUND(SUM(TRY_CAST(TOTAL_AMOUNT_OF_PAYMENT_USDOLLARS AS DOUBLE)),0) AS total_usd, ROUND(AVG(TRY_CAST(TOTAL_AMOUNT_OF_PAYMENT_USDOLLARS AS DOUBLE)),2) AS avg_usd, MAX(TRY_CAST(TOTAL_AMOUNT_OF_PAYMENT_USDOLLARS AS DOUBLE)) AS max_usd FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS_2023; -- and same on FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS for 2024
kept.evidence: 2023: 14,700,786 records summing to $3,314,058,093 (avg $225.43). 2024: 15,385,047 records summing to $3,313,801,737 (avg $215.39). Total dollars differ by only ~$256k on a $3.31B base = 0.008%, even though 2024 has 684,261 MORE individual transactions. The aggregate envelope of pharma/device money to US physicians is essentially constant year over year while transaction count grows ~4.6%.
kept.why_matters: Clean publishable stat: 'pharma paid US doctors the same $3.31 billion two years running, to within a quarter-million dollars.' The flat total + rising transaction count means average payment is shrinking (more small Food & Beverage transfers). Strong viz candidate.

verdict.corrected_title: Coincidence, not signal: total CMS GENERAL payments are flat YoY ($3.3140B 2023 vs $3.3138B 2024) only because a +$104M rise in physician payments is offset by a -$132M drop in teaching-hospital payments — physician-only payments actually ROSE +4.5%
verdict.notes:
The aggregate numbers reproduce EXACTLY: 2023 = 14,700,786 rows / $3,314,058,093 / avg $225.43; 2024 = 15,385,047 rows / $3,313,801,737 / avg $215.39; difference $256,356 = 0.008%. Both tables cast cleanly (n == n_castable, zero silent TRY_CAST nulling). PROGRAM_YEAR is 100% pure in each table (2023 table = all 2023, other = all 2024), so no year contamination. So the arithmetic 'flat to a quarter-million' is a true fact.

BUT three problems sink it as stated:

1) TITLE IS WRONG. It says 'industry-wide PHYSICIAN payments are flat.' Physician-only payments are NOT flat: $2,304,871,723 (2023) -> $2,408,975,004 (2024) = +$104.1M / +4.5%. The flatness only appears at the all-recipients level, and it's a COINCIDENCE of offsetting components: Physician +$104.1M, Teaching Hospital -$131.9M ($797.8M->$665.9M, -16.5%), Non-Physician Practitioner +$27.5M. The +$104M and -$132M nearly cancel. So the 'beautiful flat signal' is an accident of summing three classes moving in opposite directions, not a stable physician envelope. SQL: GROUP BY COVERED_RECIPIENT_TYPE on each table.

2) CAUSAL STORY UNSUPPORTED. The claim 'flat total + rising txn count => shrinking average from more small F&B transfers' is not the real driver. The real driver of the flat aggregate is the teaching-hospital collapse offsetting physician growth. Avg did fall ($225.43->$215.39) but that's not why the total held.

3) SCOPE OVERSTATED. These are GENERAL payments only (schema has NATURE_OF_PAYMENT/COVERED_RECIPIENT_TYPE = the General file). CMS Open Payments also has Research and Ownership/Investment payments (~$8-9B more program-wide). 'Pharma paid US doctors $3.31B two years running' undersells total industry-to-physician flows by ~3-4x and conflates 'general payments' with all payments.

Tier downgraded FACT->LEAD: it's a coincidental aggregate identity, not a structural fact, and the framing misattributes the mechanism. A clean publishable line would be the OPPOSITE: 'physician payments rose 4.5% in 2024 but teaching-hospital payments fell 16.5%, leaving the general-payments total flat by coincidence.' Surprise 5 (the offset is mildly interesting), importance 3 (coincidental, easy to mis-publish, weak as a standalone stat).

kept.title: Court unanimity has RISEN over time (34% to 47%) even as the 5-4 era peaked, against the 'more polarized than ever' narrative
kept.sql: WITH c AS (SELECT DISTINCT CASEID, TRY_CAST(TERM AS INT) term, TRY_CAST(MAJVOTES AS INT) maj, TRY_CAST(MINVOTES AS INT) minv FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_SCDB) SELECT FLOOR(term/10)*10 decade, COUNT(*) cases, ROUND(100.0*SUM(CASE WHEN minv=0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/COUNT(*),1) pct_unanimous, ROUND(100.0*SUM(CASE WHEN maj=5 AND minv=4 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)/COUNT(*),1) pct_5to4 FROM c WHERE term IS NOT NULL GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
kept.evidence: Unanimous-case share rose steadily: 1940s 33.7%, 1960s 36.0%, 1990s 43.2%, 2010s 47.5%. Meanwhile 5-4 cases peaked in the 2000s at 22.0% (vs 8.3% in the 1960s) before falling back to 12.2% in the 2020s. So the Court is BOTH more unanimous AND went through a 5-4 spike -- the middle ground (6-3, 7-2) shrank.
kept.why_matters: Directly contradicts the common belief that the modern Court is uniformly more divided. Unanimity is at a multi-decade high (~47%); what actually changed is the bimodal split -- more 9-0 AND a 2000s bulge of 5-4 -- as moderate-margin decisions hollowed out. A nuance the data forces that op-eds miss.

verdict.corrected_title: SCOTUS unanimity rose from a ~33-37% plateau (1940s-80s) to a 2010s peak of 47.5%, then fell back to 42.4% in the 2020s -- and 6-3 rulings are now at a record 24.1%, so the modern court is NOT at a multi-decade unanimity high
verdict.notes:
Numbers reproduce exactly; data is clean. Verified: MINVOTES is integers 0-4 with NO nulls/blanks (no casting artifact), DISTINCT CASEID is a valid per-case grain (each of 9,341 CASEIDs maps to exactly one MAJVOTES/MINVOTES combo -- the 83,644 raw rows are justice-vote rows, correctly deduped). minv=0 unanimity count (3,572 cases) includes 5-0/6-0/7-0/8-0 short-bench unanimous decisions, which is the standard SCDB definition -- fine.

WHY DOWNGRADED (three real problems with the framing, not the math):
1. Title cherry-picks the 2010s PEAK (47.5%) as the endpoint. The actual MOST RECENT decade (2020s) is 42.4%. Claim 'unanimity is at a multi-decade high (~47%)' is FALSE -- it's off peak. By-decade unanimity: 1940s 33.7, 1950s 32.5, 1960s 36.0, 1970s 36.2, 1980s 36.9, 1990s 43.2, 2000s 43.7, 2010s 47.5, 2020s 42.4.
2. 'Rose steadily' is false -- flat ~33-37% for four decades (1940s-1980s), then a jump in the 1990s, then a fall in the 2020s. It's plateau-jump-peak-decline, not a steady climb.
3. The 'middle ground (6-3, 7-2) shrank' claim is OUTRIGHT CONTRADICTED by the latest data: 6-3 rulings surged to 24.1% in the 2020s -- the HIGHEST of any decade (vs 9.4% in 2010s). The hollowing-out was a 2010s phenomenon that reversed hard (consistent with a 6-3 conservative majority court). So the 'bimodal split' story is wrong for the modern court.

Confirmed sub-claims: 5-4 peaked in 2000s at 22.0% (reproduces); 1960s 5-4 low at 8.3% (reproduces).

Scope caveats: both endpoint decades are PARTIAL -- 1940s is only terms 1946-1949 (487 cases), 2020s is only terms 2020-2024 (311 cases), so endpoint percentages are noisier. No filtering to orally-argued cases: DECISIONTYPE mixes type-1 signed opinions (7,323) with per curiam type-2 (1,001) and others; per curiam skew unanimous, a minor definitional choice that isn't disclosed.

Tier set to LEAD because the headline conclusion ('more unanimous than ever, contradicting the polarized-court narrative') is NOT supported by the most recent decade, and the supporting 'middle ground shrank' claim is contradicted by 2020s data. The trend is real but the story an investigator would publish is the corrected one.

kept.title: fed_wpa_slave_narratives is NOT slave narratives -- all 100 rows are photographic-print catalog stubs, zero narrative text
kept.sql: SELECT PERSON_NAME, STATE_FIPS, MIN(FULL_TEXT) sample_text, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_WPA_SLAVE_NARRATIVES GROUP BY 1,2;  -- and: SELECT FULL_TEXT, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_WPA_SLAVE_NARRATIVES GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC LIMIT 10;
kept.evidence: 100 rows. PERSON_NAME has 1 distinct value (blank), STATE_FIPS 1 distinct value (blank). FULL_TEXT min/max length = 49/57 chars; every value is a photo dimension caption e.g. '1 photographic print : gelatin silver ; 5 x 3 1/4 in.' (70 of 100). Zero actual interview/narrative text, zero named formerly-enslaved people, zero FIPS. The source_id promises WPA slave narratives; the landed data is image-metadata stubs.
kept.why_matters: A source named for one of the most historically important US oral-history collections contains none of it -- only photo catalog captions. Anyone querying this for narrative content gets nothing. It is a mislabeled/empty source masquerading as 100 usable rows.
kept.implies: new source to load (re-ingest the actual Born in Slavery / WPA narrative full text from LOC; current landing is unusable)

verdict.corrected_title: fed_wpa_slave_narratives has a column-mapping defect -- FULL_TEXT holds the photo-format caption (not narrative text) and PERSON_NAME/STATE_FIPS are blank, but the named formerly-enslaved people ARE present in TITLE and SUBJECTS
verdict.notes:
Re-ran the SQL; the numeric claims reproduce exactly: 100 rows, PERSON_NAME 1 distinct (blank), STATE_FIPS 1 distinct (blank), FULL_TEXT length 49-57 (avg 52.7), and FULL_TEXT is the physical-format caption '1 photographic print : gelatin silver ; N x N in.' on 100/100 rows (candidate undercounted as 70/100 -- it's actually all 100).

BUT the candidate's headline conclusions are false because it only inspected PERSON_NAME and FULL_TEXT and never looked at the other 11 columns. Profiling the full table: STATE has 17 distinct values, TITLE has 92 distinct, and TITLE/DIGITAL_URL/SUBJECTS are 100% populated. 87/100 TITLE values are plain person names (Richard Amerson, Hannah Crasson, Georgia Flournoy, Josephine Hill, John C. Becton...), and SUBJECTS repeats the name plus tags 'freedmen' (87/100) and 'portrait photographs' (88/100). DIGITAL_URL points to real LOC catalog items (e.g. http://www.loc.gov/item/99615425/ for Richard Amerson). So 'zero named formerly-enslaved people' is flatly wrong, and 'not slave narratives / mislabeled / empty source masquerading' is overstated -- this is the photographic/image-record portion of the LOC 'Born in Slavery: Slave Narratives' collection, just not the transcribed interview text.

Tier dropped FACT->LEAD: the surviving real finding is a parsing/column-mapping bug (narrative text was never landed; FULL_TEXT got the format caption; identity lives in TITLE/SUBJECTS not PERSON_NAME), which needs a human eyes-on call, not a publishable fact. Verdict DOWNGRADED rather than REJECTED because the underlying observation -- this table is image metadata, not narrative transcripts -- is genuinely true and a legit data-quality flag for anyone expecting narrative content.

SQL run:
DESCRIBE TABLE LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_WPA_SLAVE_NARRATIVES  -- revealed TITLE, SUBJECTS, DIGITAL_URL, FORMAT_LABEL columns the candidate ignored
SELECT COUNT(*), COUNT(DISTINCT PERSON_NAME), COUNT(DISTINCT STATE_FIPS), COUNT(DISTINCT STATE), COUNT(DISTINCT TITLE), MIN/MAX/AVG(LENGTH(FULL_TEXT)) ... -> 100,1,1,17,92,49,57,52.67
SELECT COUNT(CASE WHEN TITLE NOT LIKE '[%' ...), ... freedmen, portrait -> plain_title 87, freedmen 87, portrait 88
SELECT TITLE, DIGITAL_URL WHERE TITLE NOT LIKE '[%' -> Richard Amerson http://www.loc.gov/item/99615425/ etc.

kept.title: intl_it_istat: a 213k-row national-statistics table where UNIT_MEASURE is 100% blank -- you can't tell what OBS_VALUE measures
kept.sql: SELECT DATAFLOW_ID, UNIT_MEASURE, COUNT(*) n, SUM(CASE WHEN OBS_VALUE IS NULL OR OBS_VALUE='' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) blank_val FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_IT_ISTAT GROUP BY 1,2 ORDER BY n DESC
kept.evidence: 213,284 rows but only 3 distinct DATAFLOW_IDs (101_1015=186191, 101_1033=23817, 101_1030=3276). UNIT_MEASURE is blank for all rows in all 3 dataflows (SDMX unit attribute never populated). DATE granularity is mixed: 101_1015 has only 4 annual dates (2020-2023) while 101_1033 has 41 monthly dates ('2023-01' style). 101_1030 also has 244 blank OBS_VALUEs.
kept.why_matters: Italy's entire ISTAT presence in the library is 3 statistical cubes, and the unit-of-measure metadata that would make OBS_VALUE interpretable is empty -- the numbers are uninterpretable without re-decoding DIMENSION_KEYS against the SDMX codelists. The mixed annual/monthly DATE also means a naive time join across dataflows will misalign.
kept.implies: new detector / source: re-pull ISTAT with SDMX unit + dimension labels resolved

verdict.corrected_title: intl_it_istat (213k rows, 3 ISTAT SDMX cubes): the dedicated UNIT_MEASURE and UNIT_MULT columns are 100% blank, and dataflow 101_1033 silently mixes annual + monthly series -- so OBS_VALUE needs DIMENSION_KEYS/FREQ decoded, not the unit columns
verdict.notes:
Numbers reproduce exactly. 3 DATAFLOW_IDs (101_1015=186191, 101_1033=23817, 101_1030=3276). UNIT_MEASURE: all 213,284 rows are empty-string (non-null, COUNT(UNIT_MEASURE)=213,284, COUNT(DISTINCT)=1, all ''). UNIT_MULT is ALSO 100% blank -- candidate missed this, it strengthens the finding. 244 blank OBS_VALUEs, all in 101_1030, confirmed; the other 213,040 OBS_VALUE cast cleanly to DOUBLE (0 non-castable non-blank), so OBS_VALUE itself is clean numeric.

WHY DOWNGRADED (wording, not substance): The headline 'you can't tell what OBS_VALUE measures' is OVERSTATED. The measure/indicator code IS carried in DIMENSION_KEYS and SERIES_KEY -- e.g. row 'A.SPINNGREEN.ALL.ITC4.TP_QUIN_EXT' where TP_QUIN_EXT / FODUNIT_EXT / HP_Q_EXT are the SDMX indicator codes and ITC4/ITF4 are NUTS region codes. FREQ (A vs M) also disambiguates. So the unit IS recoverable from the keys; only the dedicated SDMX attribute column is empty. The candidate's own 'why it matters' concedes this ('re-decoding DIMENSION_KEYS against the SDMX codelists'), contradicting its own headline. DIMENSION_KEYS has 56,096 distinct values = exactly equal to distinct SERIES_KEY, so they are 1:1 series identifiers.

DATE granularity claim is actually STRONGER than stated, candidate had a detail wrong: 101_1033 is not '41 monthly dates' -- it is a SPLIT dataflow: 13,357 annual rows (FREQ=A, dates 2020-2023) + 10,460 monthly rows (FREQ=M, dates 2020-01..2023-01). FREQ has 2 distinct values overall only because of this. The mixed-granularity time-join misalignment warning is valid.

Tier stays FACT: structural property of one landing table, no entity/person/libel exposure. Surprise low: blank SDMX attribute columns are a routine, expected artifact of flattening SDMX-JSON (the loader kept the column but the source response omitted the attribute), not a surprising signal. Importance moderate: a real usability caveat on one mid-size foreign stats table, but not a cross-source contradiction or a wrongdoing lead.

kept.title: INTL_ES_BORME is a hollow shell: 25 rows but COMPANY_ID, COMPANY_NAME, SECTION and ACT_TYPE are all 100% blank
kept.sql: SELECT COMPANY_ID, COMPANY_NAME, SECTION, ACT_TYPE, COUNTRY, COUNT(*) n_rows FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_ES_BORME GROUP BY 1,2,3,4,5
kept.evidence: All 25 rows collapse to a single group: COMPANY_ID='', COMPANY_NAME='', SECTION='', ACT_TYPE='', COUNTRY='Spain'. COUNT(DISTINCT COMPANY_ID)=1, COUNT(DISTINCT COMPANY_NAME)=1, every DATE='2026-06-01'. The only signal is PROVINCE (13 distinct). This is the Spanish official company-registry gazette (BORME) -- the table that should carry company identities -- and the identity columns landed empty.
kept.why_matters: BORME is the de-silo's would-be Spanish company join source (company name/ID to cross-reference against OpenSanctions, ZEFIX, CRO). With name and ID blank it carries zero join value -- it is a broken/stub load, not a usable source. Should be marked empty in the catalog, not 'landed'.

verdict.corrected_title: INTL_ES_BORME (25 rows, one day's gazette) has COMPANY_ID/COMPANY_NAME/SECTION/ACT_TYPE 100% blank — but the company names+registry IDs landed in the wrong column (ACT_DESCRIPTION), so it's a mis-parsed load, not a hollow one
verdict.notes:
Reproduced exactly. The candidate's literal facts all hold: COMPANY_ID, COMPANY_NAME, SECTION, ACT_TYPE are 0/25 populated (COUNT(NULLIF(TRIM(col),''))=0); COUNT(DISTINCT COMPANY_ID)=COUNT(DISTINCT COMPANY_NAME)=1 (the empty string); all DATE='2026-06-01'; PROVINCE has 13 distinct values.

WHY DOWNGRADED (the conclusion is overstated, two specific errors):

1. "Carries zero join value / broken stub, mark empty." REFUTED. 12 of 25 rows DO carry full company identity — it just landed in ACT_DESCRIPTION instead of the ID/NAME columns. Verbatim values: '258980 - DROMO GESTION 2026 SOCIEDAD LIMITADA. Constitución...', '258991 - EZRIC FREEMAN HOLDING SL...', '259120 - WITNESS GESTION DE LA INFORMACION S.L...'. Each string = registry number + company name + act type packed together. A staging regex (split on ' - ' and '. ') recovers name + ID. This is a column-mapping/parser bug, not absent data.

2. "The only signal is PROVINCE." FALSE. Per-column populated counts (of 25): ACT_DESCRIPTION 12, PDF_URL 12 (working https://www.boe.es/borme/... links to the official gazette PDFs), PROVINCE 12, CVE 2. ACT_DESCRIPTION is the RICHEST signal, not PROVINCE.

Also: the '25 rows' overstates the content — only 12 are real company records; the other 13 are near-empty (DATE only, 2 with just a CVE like 'BORME-A-2026-102-08'). So it's a half-populated scrape of a SINGLE day (BORME issue 102, 2026-06-01), tier FACT on the column-blank facts since all are exact aggregates on a 25-row table with no casting involved.

Net: the named identity columns ARE blank (FACT), but 'broken/zero-value/mark-empty' is wrong — there are ~12 extractable Spanish company names + working source PDFs. Right call is 'fix the parser to populate COMPANY_NAME/COMPANY_ID from ACT_DESCRIPTION', not 'mark empty'. Low importance either way: 12 usable rows from one day is tiny, and it would only ever be a LEAD-grade name join to OpenSanctions/ZEFIX/CRO (no hard Spanish company ID extracted yet).

kept.title: DPRK missile-tests load swallowed its own header row: all 19 cols are COL_N and row 1 IS the column names
kept.sql: SELECT COUNT(*) AS n_rows, SUM(CASE WHEN COL_0='F1' OR COL_1='Date' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS header_rows_embedded, MIN(TRY_TO_DATE(LEFT(COL_1,10),'YYYY-MM-DD')) AS min_launch, MAX(TRY_TO_DATE(LEFT(COL_1,10),'YYYY-MM-DD')) AS max_launch FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_NTI_CNS_DPRK_MISSILE_TESTS
kept.evidence: Row 1 = (COL_0='F1', COL_1='Date', COL_2='Date Entered/Updated', COL_3='Launch Time (UTC)', COL_4='Missile Name', COL_5='Missile Type', COL_6='Launch Agency/Authority') -- the literal header landed as data (header_rows_embedded=1). Schema is COL_0..COL_18, no real names. Treating COL_1 as launch date, real launches span 1984-04-09 to 2024-11-04 over the remaining 303 data rows.
kept.why_matters: The column meanings are entirely lost in the catalog (every column is COL_N) and the header pollutes every aggregate as a phantom 'F1/Date' row. Any consumer must hard-code positional column mapping AND filter WHERE COL_0<>'F1' to use it. A re-load with header=0 would fix both.
kept.implies: re-load INTL_NTI_CNS_DPRK_MISSILE_TESTS with header parsing so columns get real names

verdict.corrected_title: DPRK missile-tests load swallowed its own header row: all 19 cols are COL_N and row 1 IS the column names
verdict.notes:
Every claim reproduces exactly. DESCRIBE confirms 19 columns COL_0..COL_18, all VARCHAR(16777216), zero real names (plus the 3 provenance stamps). The candidate SQL returns n_rows=304, header_rows_embedded=1, min_launch=1984-04-09, max_launch=2024-11-04 — identical to the claim. Pulling the embedded header row (WHERE COL_0='F1') returns the literal text COL_0='F1', COL_1='Date', COL_2='Date Entered/Updated', COL_3='Launch Time (UTC)', COL_4='Missile Name', COL_5='Missile Type', COL_6='Launch Agency/Authority' — the file header landed as data. Excluding it (WHERE COL_0<>'F1') leaves 303 genuine data rows; COL_0 is a sequential index (1,2,3...), COL_1 is a launch datetime, and data is real (e.g. Scud-B / SRBM, 1984). 

No artifacts found: (1) not a casting issue — TRY_TO_DATE nulls exactly 1 of 304 rows and that 1 is the header, which is the finding, not a silent-null trap; col1_not_date=1 confirms. (2) Not a fuzzy/name match — this is a HARD structural check (DESCRIBE + literal string equality), correctly FACT-grade. (3) Not overstated — both remediation steps it prescribes (positional column mapping + WHERE COL_0<>'F1', or a header=0 reload) are exactly what the data requires. 

Downgrade considerations on impact only, not validity: this is one of the smallest sources in the library (304 rows, an inventory tail-ender), so blast radius is tiny and the fix is trivial. The finding is genuine and publishable but low-novelty (header-as-data is a textbook loader bug) and low-stakes (small table, no analytical conclusions ride on it). Hence surprise=3, importance=4 — verdict CONFIRMED, tier and title unchanged.

kept.title: The demographic-transition law holds ferociously (life expectancy vs fertility = -0.81, n=236) -- and the countries that break it are the HIV belt: Lesotho, Eswatini & South Africa die 11-16 years young for their birth rate
kept.sql: WITH le AS (SELECT CODE iso, ENTITY name, TRY_CAST(LIFE_EXPECTANCY AS DOUBLE) le FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_OWID_LIFE_EXPECTANCY WHERE YEAR='2020' AND LENGTH(CODE)=3), fert AS (SELECT CODE iso, TRY_CAST(TOTAL_FERTILITY_RATE AS DOUBLE) fert FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_OWID_FERTILITY WHERE YEAR='2020' AND LENGTH(CODE)=3), j AS (SELECT le.iso,le.name,le.le,fert.fert FROM le JOIN fert ON le.iso=fert.iso WHERE le.le IS NOT NULL AND fert.fert IS NOT NULL), reg AS (SELECT REGR_SLOPE(le,fert) m, REGR_INTERCEPT(le,fert) b FROM j) SELECT j.name,j.iso,j.fert,ROUND(j.le,1) life_exp,ROUND(j.le-(reg.m*j.fert+reg.b),1) resid FROM j,reg ORDER BY resid ASC LIMIT 6;
kept.evidence: CORR(life_exp, fertility) on ISO3 2020 = -0.809 (n=236) -- the strongest pairwise relationship found. The largest negative residuals (live shorter than their fertility predicts) are Lesotho -15.9yr (TFR 2.85, life 55.1 vs predicted 71.0), Eswatini -10.9 (TFR 2.89, life 59.9), Nigeria -9.0, Bolivia -9.0, South Africa -8.7 (TFR 2.26, life 65.2 vs predicted 73.8), Armenia -8.0. The Southern-Africa cluster has already completed the fertility transition (TFR ~2.3-2.9) yet life expectancy is crushed -- the HIV/AIDS fingerprint.
kept.why_matters: A textbook macro-law reproduced cleanly in the Library validates the OWID panels for analytic use. The residual outliers isolate a specific cause: in Southern Africa, fertility fell but the AIDS epidemic decoupled lifespan from the transition, so the country sits far below the global curve. This is exactly the kind of off-trend signal that points to a real exogenous shock rather than slow development.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: Life expectancy vs fertility = -0.81 (R2 0.65, n=236 OWID entities, 2020); the largest negative residuals are the HIV belt -- Lesotho -15.9yr, Eswatini -10.9, South Africa -8.7 -- where fertility fell but AIDS decoupled lifespan from the transition
verdict.notes:
Reproduced EXACTLY. CORR=-0.8085 (claimed -0.809), n=236, slope -4.8 yr/child, intercept 84.6, REGR_R2=0.654. Residual table matches to the decimal: Lesotho -15.9 (TFR 2.85, life 55.1), Eswatini -10.9, Nigeria -9.0, Bolivia -9.0, South Africa -8.7, Armenia -8.0.

Adversarial checks, all passed:
- JOIN is clean 1:1 on ISO3: 236 rows both sides, 236 distinct both sides, no fan-out.
- No casting artifact: of 236 LENGTH(CODE)=3 rows in 2020, 0 blanks and 0 TRY_CAST failures. No silent nulling.
- No aggregate leakage: no World/income-group/OWID-region codes among the 3-letter codes; OWID_WRL (8 char) is excluded by LENGTH=3.
- HIV attribution is CORRECT and appropriately scoped. The candidate names only Lesotho/Eswatini/South Africa as the HIV story -- all three have completed the fertility transition (TFR 2.26-2.89) yet life exp 55-65, the textbook AIDS decoupling. Botswana(67.6/2.86), Namibia(64.1/3.35), Zimbabwe(61.5/3.75) round out the belt with smaller residuals. The candidate does NOT wrongly attribute Nigeria/Bolivia/Armenia to HIV.

Two precision nits (basis for keeping surprise/importance low, not for downgrade):
1) 'n=236 countries' is really 236 OWID entities -- the top POSITIVE residuals are Monaco(+12.9), French Guiana, Mayotte, Saint Martin, Reunion, Gibraltar, Palestine: microstates/French overseas territories, not UN countries (~195). Doesn't bias the correlation, but 'countries' overstates the unit.
2) 'strongest pairwise relationship found' is an unverifiable superlative scoped to the prior investigator's own search, not a data fact.

Tier stays FACT: this is a within-source aggregate correlation on a hard ISO3 key, no cross-ID/name matching, no entity-level libel exposure. The verdict is CONFIRMED but the finding is LOW-VALUE: the demographic transition (life exp up as fertility falls) is one of the most famous relationships in social science -- reproducing it 'validates the OWID panels' (the genuine utility here) but is not itself a discovery. Surprise=3, importance=4.

kept.title: Treasury Debt-to-Penny ships 4 fully-blank shadow columns duplicating the real fiscal/calendar fields
kept.sql: SELECT SUM(CASE WHEN NULLIF(RECORD_FISCAL_YR,'') IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) fyr_blank, SUM(CASE WHEN NULLIF(RECORD_FISCAL_QTR,'') IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) fqtr_blank, SUM(CASE WHEN NULLIF(RECORD_CALENDAR_YR,'') IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) cyr_blank, SUM(CASE WHEN NULLIF(RECORD_CALENDAR_QTR,'') IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) cqtr_blank, SUM(CASE WHEN NULLIF(RECORD_FISCAL_YEAR,'') IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) fy_blank, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_TREASURY_DEBT_TO_PENNY
kept.evidence: All 8,329 rows: RECORD_FISCAL_YR, RECORD_FISCAL_QTR, RECORD_CALENDAR_YR, RECORD_CALENDAR_QTR are 100% blank (8329/8329 each). The populated twins are RECORD_FISCAL_YEAR and RECORD_CALENDAR_YEAR (0 blank). Date span is real and clean: 1993-04-01 to 2026-06-15, 8,329 distinct daily records, no dup dates.
kept.why_matters: Four dead duplicate columns from the Treasury API's redundant schema (YEAR vs YR, QUARTER vs QTR). Harmless but a trap: an analyst who picks the _YR/_QTR variant gets all-NULL fiscal periods. Staging should drop the blank twins and keep RECORD_FISCAL_YEAR / RECORD_CALENDAR_YEAR.
kept.implies: none -- staging hygiene: drop the 4 blank shadow columns

verdict.corrected_title: Treasury Debt-to-Penny ships 4 fully-blank shadow columns (_YR/_QTR) duplicating the populated _YEAR/_QUARTER fiscal/calendar fields
verdict.notes:
Reproduced exactly. All 8,329 rows: RECORD_FISCAL_YR / RECORD_FISCAL_QTR / RECORD_CALENDAR_YR / RECORD_CALENDAR_QTR are 100% blank (8329/8329 each). The twins RECORD_FISCAL_YEAR and RECORD_CALENDAR_YEAR are 0 blank, each with 34 distinct values spanning 1993-2026. Verified the blanks are TRUE empty strings, not whitespace: RECORD_FISCAL_YR has exactly 1 distinct value with MIN/MAX length 0 -- so NULLIF(col,'') correctly catches them, no casting artifact. Date span reproduces: 1993-04-01 to 2026-06-15, 8,329 distinct RECORD_DATE, 0 dup-excess, 0 unparseable via TRY_CAST AS DATE -- clean daily series.

Not an artifact, not a contradiction, no cross-ID/name match involved -- pure schema-introspection fact, FACT tier is correct. Candidate slightly UNDER-claimed: the verbose schema also carries RECORD_FISCAL_QUARTER, RECORD_CALENDAR_QUARTER, RECORD_CALENDAR_MONTH, RECORD_CALENDAR_DAY -- I checked all four and they are 0 blank (fully populated), so the _YR/_QTR abbreviated pair are the ONLY dead columns. The naming pattern is consistent (full word = populated, abbreviation = dead), which strengthens the 'pick the wrong twin and get all-NULL' staging-footgun point.

Tempered surprise/importance: this is a benign API redundancy on one small 8k-row table, a staging-layer cleanup note rather than an investigative lead -- correctly tagged data-quality. Remediation advice (drop the _YR/_QTR twins in staging, keep _YEAR/_QUARTER) is sound.

kept.title: fed_cdc_suicide_rates: 'panel' year-range is huge (1950-2018, 69 years) but the INDICATOR column is single-valued
kept.sql: SELECT INDICATOR, UNIT, COUNT(*) n, MIN(YEAR) min_y, MAX(YEAR) max_y FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CDC_SUICIDE_RATES GROUP BY 1,2 ORDER BY 3 DESC
kept.evidence: 6,390 rows. INDICATOR has exactly 1 distinct value ('Death rates for suicide') -- so the column carries no information. Real YEAR spans 1950-2018 (the YEAR_NUM 1-42 column is just a row-index, NOT a year -- a classic trap). UNIT splits cleanly into 'crude' (5578 rows) vs 'age-adjusted' (812). STUB_LABEL is a clean demographic cross-tab (Male/Female x race x age, ~86 obs each), making this one of the tidiest panels in the batch.
kept.why_matters: Beautiful, analysis-ready long-format CDC panel covering 7 decades -- but two traps for the unwary: (1) YEAR_NUM is an index not a year (use YEAR), and (2) INDICATOR is dead weight. Worth wiring into a mart; the crude-vs-age-adjusted split is already structured for it.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: fed_cdc_suicide_rates is a tidy long-format CDC panel (6,390 rows, YEAR 1950-2018, single INDICATOR, crude vs age-adjusted split) -- but YEAR_NUM is a year-ORDINAL (1:1 with 42 distinct years), the panel is irregularly spaced (1950/1960/1970 then annual), and 14% of rows have suppressed/blank ESTIMATE (FLAG '...'/'*')
verdict.notes:
All headline numbers reproduce EXACTLY: 5578 crude + 812 age-adjusted = 6390; INDICATOR has exactly 1 distinct value; YEAR spans 1950-2018. STUB_LABEL is a clean demographic cross-tab (~84-87 obs per group). So the 'beautiful tidy panel' framing is fair.

THREE corrections, hence DOWNGRADED:

(1) YEAR_NUM is NOT 'just a row-index'. It is a 1:1 year ORDINAL: 42 distinct YEAR_NUM (1-42) map perfectly to 42 distinct YEAR values (verified COUNT(DISTINCT YEAR)=COUNT(DISTINCT YEAR_NUM)=42). The real trap is subtler: the panel is IRREGULARLY spaced -- 1950, 1960, 1970 (decade gaps), then annual 1980-2018 -- so YEAR_NUM is a compressed index, not a calendar year, and not a true row index either. Calling it a 'classic row-index trap' mischaracterizes it.

(2) 'One of the tidiest panels' is slightly overstated: 906 / 6390 rows (14%) have a BLANK ESTIMATE. This is fully explained by FLAG: '...' = data not available (645 rows) and '*' = unreliable/suppressed (261 rows) -- 645+261=906 exact. The 5484 unflagged rows all TRY_CAST to DOUBLE cleanly (0 non-castable). So it's clean and documented, but not drop-in analysis-ready -- you must handle CDC suppression. Blanks cluster in Hispanic/race-detail breakdowns for early decades.

(3) The headline 'huge year-range but single-valued INDICATOR' is technically true but low-value: this is a single-indicator extract, so INDICATOR being constant is expected, not surprising. No casting artifact, no contradiction, no fuzzy/cross-ID match -- tier stays FACT (it's a pure structural observation on one table), but surprise and importance are low. Worth wiring to a mart with explicit handling of the irregular year spacing and the suppression flags.

kept.title: fed_cms_irf carries certification dates 3-4 months in the future (Sep/Oct 2026) -- impossible as of today
kept.sql: SELECT CCN, PROVIDER_NAME, STATE, CERTIFICATION_DATE FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_IRF WHERE TRY_TO_DATE(CERTIFICATION_DATE,'MM/DD/YYYY') > '2026-06-27' ORDER BY CERTIFICATION_DATE
kept.evidence: Today is 2026-06-27. Three inpatient rehab facilities show certification dates in the future: 10TA10 KINDRED HOSPITAL-SOUTH FLORIDA-FT LAUDERDALE (09/01/2026), 10TA25 KINDRED HOSPITAL THE PALM BEACHES (09/01/2026), 10T286 PHYSICIANS REGIONAL MEDICAL CENTER - PINE RIDGE (10/01/2026) -- all in FL. Table max CERTIFICATION_DATE = 2026-10-01 vs min 1983-10-01. CCN is a perfect key (1222 distinct = 1222 rows).
kept.why_matters: A Medicare certification date cannot post-date the snapshot. Either CMS published prospective certification effective-dates (likely) or these rows are corrupted -- either way any 'days since certification' metric goes negative on these 3 facilities, and two are the same operator (Kindred). Worth a flag before modeling.
kept.implies: new detector (future-dated certification check across all CMS provider tables)

verdict.corrected_title: fed_cms_irf CERTIFICATION_DATE is an administrative effective-date (62% land on the 1st of a month); 3 FL facilities carry future effective-dates (Sep/Oct 2026) that will break any naive 'days since certification' metric
verdict.notes:
Every literal claim reproduces exactly. SQL re-ran: the 3 rows are identical (10TA10 & 10TA25 KINDRED 09/01/2026, 10T286 PHYSICIANS REGIONAL 10/01/2026, all FL). Profile confirms 1222 rows = 1222 distinct CCN (perfect key), 0 null parses, 0 blanks, min 1983-10-01, max 2026-10-01. Two of three are Kindred (For profit). No casting artifact: TRY_TO_DATE nulls nothing.

WHY DOWNGRADED (tier + wording, not the numbers):
- The word 'impossible/corrupted' is wrong. CERTIFICATION_DATE is a CMS administrative EFFECTIVE date (the date a provider is approved to begin/continue Medicare participation), not a snapshot or observation timestamp. Signature evidence: 761 of 1222 rows (62%) fall on day-of-month = 1 (next highest day-of-month is only 28 rows). Prospective effective-dates are normal and expected in CMS provider-of-services data, so a future date is not impossible.
- Reinforcing: other Kindred FL facilities carry 09/01 effective-dates in PAST years (09/01/2023, 09/01/2021), i.e. 09/01 is this operator's recurring administrative effective-date, not a one-off corruption.
- It is HARD-ID grade (CCN is a perfect key), so the rows themselves are FACT-publishable, but the candidate's stated conclusion ('impossible/corrupted') overstates what the data supports. The candidate already hedged ('likely prospective effective-dates') yet kept the 'impossible' title and FACT tier -- those need to match the hedge.

VALUE THAT SURVIVES: legitimate data-hygiene flag -- any 'days since certification' / tenure metric goes negative on these 3 CCNs as of 2026-06-27. Worth a guard before modeling, but it's a routine effective-date quirk, not an anomaly/scandal. Low surprise, low importance.

kept.title: Over half of FEC committees (53%) have a blank party affiliation -- the field that makes the table useful is mostly empty
kept.sql: SELECT CMTE_PTY_AFFILIATION, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_FEC_BULK GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 2 DESC LIMIT 10;  -- plus: SELECT COUNT(*) total, COUNT(DISTINCT FEC_CMTE_ID) d, SUM(CASE WHEN NULLIF(TRIM(FEC_CAND_ID),'') IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) null_cand FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_FEC_BULK
kept.evidence: 20938 committees, FEC_CMTE_ID a perfect key (20938 distinct). CMTE_PTY_AFFILIATION is blank in 11160 rows (53.3%); next values REP 3891, DEM 3482, IND 658. Also FEC_CAND_ID is blank in 12717/20938 (60.7%) -- i.e. most committees aren't tied to a candidate (PACs, party orgs), which is expected, but the party-blank rate is the surprise.
kept.why_matters: If you filter FEC committees by party you silently drop the majority of them. The blank isn't random noise -- it reflects FEC's own coding gaps -- but a naive 'group by party' viz would mislead. The party label needs derivation (from CMTE_TP / connected org), not the raw column.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: FEC party-affiliation is blank for 53% of committees ONLY because PACs have no party by design -- for actual candidate committees (House/Senate/Pres) it is 97% populated
verdict.notes:
All raw numbers reproduce exactly: 20938 rows = 20938 distinct FEC_CMTE_ID (perfect key, FACT-grade); CMTE_PTY_AFFILIATION blank in 11160 (53.3%); REP 3891 / DEM 3482 / IND 658; FEC_CAND_ID blank in 12717 (60.7%). So the literal claim is true and reproducible.

BUT the framing is wrong and the finding overstates the problem. Cross-tab by candidate-link: of 11160 blanks, 10999 (98.6%) are in committees with NO candidate ID. Cross-tab by committee type is decisive: candidate committees (CMTE_TP IN H/S/P) are 8895 rows with only 229 blank = 2.6% blank (97.4% populated). All other types (N non-party PAC, Q qualified non-party PAC, O super PAC, I independent-expenditure, V/W/C/E etc.) are 12043 rows, 90.8% blank.

That blank is NOT an 'FEC coding gap' as the candidate claims -- it is the CORRECT value. A non-party PAC has no party affiliation by definition, so blank is accurate, not missing. The candidate's 'why it matters' is therefore partly false: the column does NOT 'need derivation'; you just filter to candidate committees where it is already ~97% complete. The 53% headline is a population-mixing artifact, not a data defect.

Tier stays FACT (numbers are exact, on a hard key), but surprise/importance are low: this is a routine analyst trap, not a real signal. Anyone who knows FEC committee types expects PACs to lack party. Verdict DOWNGRADED -- real numbers, misleading interpretation, corrected title supplied.

kept.title: A clean, beautiful signal: global AI-incident reports went near-exponential — 8 in 2012 to 362 in 2025, a 45x climb with a sharp 2022 inflection
kept.sql: SELECT ENTITY, YEAR, $4 FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_OWID_AI_INCIDENTS_ANNUAL ORDER BY TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT)
kept.evidence: All 14 rows are ENTITY='World', YEAR 2012-2025, one metric. Series: 8, 6, 13, 24, 41, 50, 45, 43, 90, 77, 104, 168, 281, 362. Flat-ish ~40-50/yr 2016-2019, then doubles to 90 in 2020, and after ChatGPT's late-2022 launch jumps 104 (2022) -> 168 (2023) -> 281 (2024) -> 362 (2025). 45x from the 2012 base.
kept.why_matters: A rare instant-publishable clean line: a single-entity, single-metric, no-nulls 14-point series that tells the generative-AI-era story by itself (the post-2022 kink lines up with the LLM boom). Perfect Plotly candidate. The caveat to state: it's 'reported incidents,' so growth conflates real incidents with growth in the reporting database itself — but the inflection is still striking.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: Reported AI incidents/controversies (OWID/AIID) climbed near-monotonically from 8 (2012) to 362 (2025), ~45x — but the single biggest doubling is 2020 (43→90), not the post-ChatGPT 2022 kink
verdict.notes:
Re-ran positionally ($4 = the metric col, whose real name is truncated by DESCRIBE: ANNUAL_REPORTED_ARTIFICIAL_INTELLIGENCE_INCIDENTS_AND_CON...). Series reproduces EXACTLY: 8,6,13,24,41,50,45,43,90,77,104,168,281,362. Profile confirms 14 rows, 1 ENTITY=World (CODE=OWID_WRL), 14 distinct years 2012-2025, 0 blanks, 0 uncastable. No casting artifact: all values are small clean ints, ORDER BY is numeric (TRY_CAST(YEAR AS INT)), so no text-sort issue. 45x checks (362/8=45.25). The data and structural claims are solid and instantly publishable.

Three honest caveats that justify a wording fix, not a tier downgrade:
1) NARRATIVE OVERSTATEMENT: candidate ties the 'sharp inflection' to ChatGPT (late Nov 2022). But the LARGEST single-year jump is 43->90 in 2020 (2.09x), BEFORE ChatGPT. 2022 (104) is only +35% over 2021 (77); the real acceleration is 2023->2025. So the kink is more 'generative-AI era broadly' than 'ChatGPT specifically.' Pulling the causal claim back is warranted.
2) 2025 is almost certainly a PARTIAL / still-accruing year (today is 2026-06-27), so 362 may understate the true 2025 count or, conversely, be a fresh-data snapshot — either way the top point is the least stable. Plot it but flag it.
3) The metric is 'reported incidents AND controversies' from the AI Incident Database — the candidate's own caveat is correct: growth conflates real incidents with growth/coverage of the reporting DB itself. This is a reporting-volume series, not an incidence rate.

Tier stays FACT: single HARD source, exact reproduction, no cross-ID/name match, no nulls. Surprise/importance are low — it's a clean Plotly line but it's a known public OWID series telling a widely-understood story; it surfaces no hidden signal an investigator didn't already expect.

kept.title: FED_DOJ_CRT_CASES is a 'modeled' source but is a 1-row scrape of a navigation page -- zero actual cases
kept.sql: SELECT * FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_DOJ_CRT_CASES
kept.evidence: Exactly 1 row. It is not a case: CASE_TITLE='Appellate', SECTION/CASE_TYPE/COMPANY_ID/PERSON_NAME/STATE/DOCUMENT_URL/STATUS all blank, DATE='Updated June 5, 2025', and DESCRIPTION is a raw run-on of the site's menu categories ('Appellate Cases Disability Cases Educational Opportunitie...'). The scraper captured the page chrome / category index, not the case listing.
kept.why_matters: Confirms build-state's flag. A source that reads 'modeled' in the catalog is a broken stub carrying zero DOJ Civil Rights cases -- it will silently contribute nothing to any justice-domain analysis while appearing complete. Same shape afflicts the European company-registry stubs: INTL_IE_CRO (3 rows), INTL_CH_ZEFIX (18), INTL_ES_BORME (25), INTL_GR_GEMI (40) -- national business registries that should hold hundreds of thousands to millions of companies are API-probe stubs.

verdict.corrected_title: FED_DOJ_CRT_CASES is a 1-row nav-page scrape carrying zero actual cases -- but it is correctly tagged 'landed' (NOT 'modeled') in the catalog
verdict.notes:
DATA FACT REPRODUCES EXACTLY (FACT-grade, hard table read, no casting involved): COUNT(*)=1. The single row: CASE_TITLE='Appellate', DATE='Updated June 5, 2025', and SECTION/CASE_TYPE/COMPANY_ID/PERSON_NAME/STATE/DOCUMENT_URL/STATUS all blank ('' or NULL). DESCRIPTION is a 427-char run-on of the site's category menu ('Appellate Cases Disability Cases Educational Opportunitie...'). So yes -- it is a scrape of the DOJ CRT page chrome/category index, zero actual cases. That part holds.

WHY DOWNGRADED -- the central framing is FALSE. The title says it is a 'modeled' source and the 'why it matters' says 'a source that reads modeled in the catalog is a broken stub... while appearing complete.' I checked LIBRARY_META.REGISTRY.CATALOG: source_id='fed_doj_crt_cases' has LIFECYCLE='landed', TRUST_LAYER not 'mart'. It is NOT among the 25 'modeled' sources (queried WHERE lifecycle='modeled' -- fed_doj_crt_cases is absent; the list is fed_cms_nppes, fed_scdb, fed_oyez, etc.). So the catalog does NOT misrepresent it as complete -- 'landed' correctly means raw-only, no models built. The alarming part of the claim (silently masquerades as complete/modeled) is unsupported; the catalog is telling the truth about this source.

SAME CORRECTION on the spillover claim: the European registry stubs (intl_ie_cro:3, intl_ch_zefix:18, intl_es_borme:25, intl_gr_gemi:40) are ALL lifecycle='landed' too, not 'modeled'. They are thin landed stubs, not falsely-modeled sources.

NET: real but minor data-quality note (a near-empty landed scrape), stripped of its overclaimed 'modeled/appears-complete' framing. Surprise/importance low: build-state already flags 1-row stubs, the catalog already classifies it as merely landed, and a justice-domain analyst querying CATALOG would see lifecycle='landed' and know not to trust it as a case corpus. SQL receipts: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_DOJ_CRT_CASES -> 1; SELECT * (all blanks + 'Appellate'/'Updated June 5, 2025'); SELECT source_id,lifecycle FROM LIBRARY_META.REGISTRY.CATALOG WHERE source_id='fed_doj_crt_cases' -> 'landed'.

kept.title: Wayback DOJ deep-pages table is ~4x over-replayed: 2,542 rows but only 653 distinct URLs (max 37 captures of one page)
kept.sql: WITH d AS (SELECT RECORD:url::string url, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.XC_WAYBACK_REPLAY_DOJ_DEEP_PAGES GROUP BY 1) SELECT COUNT(*) distinct_urls, SUM(n) total_rows, MAX(n) max_dupes, ROUND(AVG(n),2) avg_per_url, SUM(CASE WHEN n>1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) urls_with_dupes FROM d
kept.evidence: distinct_urls=653, total_rows=2,542, avg_per_url=3.89, max_dupes=37, urls_with_dupes=394. So 60% of distinct URLs have multiple rows and the table is ~3.9x larger than its unique-page count. _SRC_SHA256 has only 1 distinct value across all 2,542 rows (single ingest hash).
kept.why_matters: Row count overstates coverage by ~4x. Any 'how many DOJ Epstein pages were archived' count read straight off this table is inflated; it needs a DISTINCT url (or timestamp) before being used as a denominator. The single shared SHA256 also means provenance can't distinguish the duplicates.
kept.implies: none

verdict.corrected_title: Wayback DOJ deep-pages: 2,542 rows = 653 URLs x multiple captures each (one row per timestamped snapshot, not duplicates) - use DISTINCT url if counting distinct pages
verdict.notes:
Numbers reproduce EXACTLY: distinct_urls=653, total_rows=2,542, max_dupes=37, avg=3.89, urls_with_dupes=394. The candidate's SQL re-ran identically. BUT the interpretation is wrong, so I downgraded from FACT/data-quality to LEAD.

WHY THE 'OVER-REPLAY' FRAMING IS WRONG: The table is a VARIANT/RECORD column (not all-TEXT). Each repeated-URL row is a DISTINCT Wayback Machine capture at a DISTINCT capture_timestamp. For https://www.justice.gov/epstein/search the 37 rows = 37 distinct capture_timestamps spanning 2026-02-15 to 2026-06-04, each with a different page_digest. (url, capture_timestamp) is PERFECTLY UNIQUE: SELECT COUNT(*), COUNT(DISTINCT url||'|'||capture_timestamp) returns 2542 and 2542 -- ZERO true duplicate rows. A 'Wayback replay' table is by design one row per (url, capture); multiple snapshots of an evolving DOJ Epstein page is the whole point of archiving, not a defect.

SHA256 CLAIM IS A RED HERRING: 1 distinct _SRC_SHA256 is just the file-level ingest hash (one bulk JSON file -> one SHA). It is normal and says nothing about row-level duplication. Calling it 'provenance can't distinguish duplicates' misreads what the stamp is.

ONE THING THE CANDIDATE GOT BACKWARDS: page_digest is NOT a per-page content hash -- each of the 37 digests appears across ~69 different URLs (37x69~=2553), so it's a per-crawl-batch digest. Irrelevant to the dup claim but shows the source wasn't fully understood.

VALID KERNEL (why DOWNGRADED not REJECTED): the denominator caveat is genuine -- anyone reading raw row count as 'distinct DOJ pages archived' overstates page coverage ~3.9x and should DISTINCT on url. That's a useful usage note, but it's a benign expected property of a temporal archive table, not a data-quality problem. Captures span 16 distinct days, Feb 15 - Jun 4 2026. Surprise low: this is exactly what a Wayback table should look like.

kept.title: FHFA House Price Index ships 1,198 rows dated 2026 -- a 'future' year relative to the 2026-06-27 load
kept.sql: SELECT YR, COUNT(*) n_rows FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_FHFA_HPI WHERE TRY_CAST(YR AS INT) >= 2025 GROUP BY YR ORDER BY YR
kept.evidence: YR=2025 -> 4,792 rows, YR=2026 -> 1,198 rows. The 2026 rows are quarterly (FREQUENCY='quarterly', PERIOD=1 -> 1,168 of them) i.e. Q1 2026 prints that already exist at load time, plus a few monthly. MIN_YR=1975, MAX_YR=2026, 52 distinct years, 184,807 rows total. Not corrupt -- but any 'latest complete year' logic that picks MAX(YR) lands on a partial 2026 (Q1 only) and will look like a price crash.
kept.why_matters: A panel that silently includes a single partial quarter of the current year is a classic trap for trend marts -- year-over-year or 'latest year' aggregates break. Worth flagging in staging so 2026 is treated as partial.

verdict.corrected_title: FHFA House Price Index is a quarterly+monthly panel whose latest year (2026) is partial -- Q1 only (1,198 rows vs ~4,792 for a full year) -- so MAX(YR) 'latest year' logic lands on one quarter and can fake a price crash
verdict.notes:
All raw numbers reproduce exactly: WHERE TRY_CAST(YR AS INT)>=2025 GROUP BY YR -> 2025=4792, 2026=1198. Table totals: 184,807 rows, MIN_YR=1975, MAX_YR=2026, 52 distinct years (contiguous), 0 NULL YR -- no casting artifact, YR casts clean. 2026 breakdown confirmed: FREQUENCY=quarterly/PERIOD=1 -> 1168, monthly PERIOD 1/2/3 -> 10 each (30), total 1198. Compared against 2024 and 2025, both of which are COMPLETE years (monthly 12 periods=120 rows, quarterly 4 periods=4672 rows each); 2026 has only monthly periods 1-3 and quarterly period 1 -- genuinely partial, exactly Q1 + Jan-Mar.

WHY DOWNGRADED (two problems):
(1) FALSE FRAMING. The title calls 2026 a 'future' year relative to the load. It is not. Ingest epoch 1782615445917690 -> 2026-06-28 02:57 UTC. Q1 2026 (Jan 1 - Mar 31) ended ~3 months BEFORE load. These are normal, already-published past-quarter prints, not future-dated data. The 'future year' / 'prints that already exist at load time' phrasing is wrong and self-contradictory.
(2) NOT AN ANOMALY. A quarterly price index loaded mid-year always trails -- the latest available year is partial by construction. This is the expected shape of FHFA HPI, not a quirk of this load.

WHAT SURVIVES (legitimately useful, hence not REJECTED): the partial-latest-year is a real trap for any trend mart that picks MAX(YR) or does naive YoY -- 2026 has 1 of 4 quarters, so a 'latest complete year' aggregate over 2026 would be built on one quarter and could look like a crash. That staging caveat (treat the current/max year as partial until 4 quarters land) is worth keeping. Numbers are FACT-grade (own-column row counts, hard IDs not involved), but surprise/importance are low because this is standard panel behavior, not a data defect.

rejected.title: intl_ipc_food_insecurity_global is beautifully self-consistent: phase shares sum to 1.0, and every country carries 3 nested time-views
rejected.sql: SELECT COUNTRY, DATE_OF_ANALYSIS, ROUND(SUM(CASE WHEN PHASE IN ('1','2','3','4','5') THEN TRY_CAST(PERCENTAGE AS DOUBLE) ELSE 0 END),3) sum_phases, MAX(CASE WHEN PHASE='all' THEN PERCENTAGE END) all_val FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.INTL_IPC_FOOD_INSECURITY_GLOBAL GROUP BY 1,2 ORDER BY sum_phases DESC LIMIT 10
rejected.evidence: For each country/analysis-date, PHASE 1+2+3+4+5 percentages sum to ~3.0 (2.99-3.01), PHASE='all' is exactly 1.0, and VALIDITY_PERIOD splits as current=315, first projection=343, second projection=77. So the sum of 3.0 = three stacked snapshots (current + 2 projections), each a clean partition summing to 1.0. 735 rows, 50 countries, population 0..256.7M.
rejected.why_matters: Rare clean signal in the library: the data validates itself (mutually-exclusive phase shares partition to 100%), and it ships forward-looking projections, not just current state -- you can compare IPC's predicted vs realized food-insecurity by lining up a country's 'first projection' against its later 'current' snapshot. That's a built-in forecast-accuracy backtest.
rejected.implies: new detector (join each country's 'first projection' to its subsequent 'current' DATE_OF_ANALYSIS to score IPC projection accuracy)

verdict.corrected_title: intl_ipc_food_insecurity_global is a single-snapshot panel (50 countries, each one analysis date); phase shares sum to ~1.0 per validity period as expected, but it has NO time-series and cannot backtest projections
verdict.notes:
SQL reproduces (BDI/HND 3.01, SDN/AFG/SLV 3.0; validity split first=343, current=315, second=77). But the finding is built on artifacts and false claims:

1) "Every country carries 3 nested time-views" is FALSE. Of 50 country/date analyses: only 10 have all 3 validity periods, 35 have 2, 5 have 1. The headline 3.0 sum is NOT universal -- it is an artifact of the candidate's GROUP BY COUNTRY,DATE collapsing however many validity periods a country has (1 view -> ~1.0, 2 -> ~2.0, 3 -> ~3.0). The ORDER BY sum_phases DESC LIMIT 10 cherry-picked the rare 3-view countries. The cited 343/315/77 split actually DISPROVES uniform nesting (they are unequal).

2) PHASE='all'=1.0 is a hardcoded constant, not self-validation. It is 1.0 in 105/105 partitions. Proven degenerate: GMB Mar 2025 'current' has every phase percentage=0 and NUMBER=0, yet 'all'=1.0. So 'all'=1.0 carries zero validating information.

3) A 7th phase label '3+' exists (105 rows) = cumulative rollup of phases 3+4+5 (SDN current: 0.30+0.11+0.00=0.41='3+'). The finding ignores it; the raw PHASE column does NOT cleanly partition -- you must manually drop '3+' first.

4) 2.99-3.01 is rounding noise: source percentages are stored at 2-decimal precision (0.01 steps), so summing rounded shares gives 0.99/1.00/1.01 per view. Per-validity-period sums: 0.99 x20, 1.00 x67, 1.01 x17, 0.00 x1 (GMB). Nothing 'beautiful', just expected float behavior.

5) The flagship value -- a 'built-in forecast-accuracy backtest' comparing a 'first projection' to a later 'current' -- is IMPOSSIBLE: all 50 countries appear at exactly 1 analysis date (n_dates=1 for all 50). No country has a second snapshot to realize a projection against. The backtest cannot be run with this data.

Tier downgraded to LEAD: every entity-level statement (GMB, SDN) is fine descriptively but the 'self-consistent / backtestable' framing is unsupported. Population min=0.0 (at least one country has TOTAL_COUNTRY_POPULATION='0'), a quirk the finding's '0..256.7M' glosses as a clean range.

rejected.title: FED_NARA_WRA_AAD is a 36-row table of pure blanks - every business column empty in all rows
rejected.sql: SELECT PERSON_NAME, RECORD_ID, CAMP_LOCATION, FIPS, CITIZENSHIP_STATUS, AGE, SEX, DATE, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_NARA_WRA_AAD GROUP BY 1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8 ORDER BY n DESC LIMIT 10
rejected.evidence: GROUP BY on all 8 business columns collapses to a SINGLE group of 36 identical rows, every field blank ('' / NULL). Aggregate confirms: COUNT(DISTINCT RECORD_ID)=1, COUNT(DISTINCT PERSON_NAME)=1, COUNT(DISTINCT FIPS)=1, COUNT(DISTINCT CITIZENSHIP_STATUS)=1, MIN/MAX(AGE) both NULL. This is a WWII Japanese-American incarceration camp roster (NARA WRA Form 26) that landed with 36 empty rows - the loader caught the row count but zero actual content. Worse than the known '1-row stubs' - it presents as data but holds nothing.
rejected.why_matters: A source flagged as landed/usable in the catalog actually carries ZERO content. Any join or count against it silently returns nothing or 36 phantom rows. Needs to be re-flagged empty (its real LIFECYCLE) before anyone trusts a 'WRA camp records' source in a story about Japanese-American internment.
rejected.implies: new source to load: re-ingest FED_NARA_WRA_AAD properly (header/parse bug stripped all values); re-classify current load as empty

verdict.corrected_title: FED_NARA_WRA_AAD is NOT empty - it's a mislabeled 36-row NARA AAD series catalog (SERIES_ID 36/36, NOTES_FIELD 24/36 carry real WWII record-group listings); the "person" columns are blank because it was never a camp roster
verdict.notes:
Re-ran the candidate's exact SQL: it reproduces (collapses to one group of 36, all-blank). BUT the claim "every business column empty in all rows / ZERO content / holds nothing" is FALSE. The table has 12 business columns; the candidate cherry-picked 8 that happen to be blank and OMITTED the two that carry content.

Receipts (DESCRIBE TABLE shows 12 business cols incl. GEO, SERIES_ID, FAMILY_NUMBER, NOTES_FIELD - candidate's SQL named only 8):
- COUNT non-blank per col: SERIES_ID = 36/36 (all "623"), NOTES_FIELD = 24/36 non-blank. Every other business col = 0/36.
