- COUNT(DISTINCT NOTES_FIELD) = 13 (12 non-blank distinct + blank), so the rows are NOT "36 identical rows."

What NOTES_FIELD actually contains (real, meaningful text): a scraped catalog/index of WWII-era NARA electronic record series - e.g. " | 0: Records About Japanese Americans Relocated During WWII, 1988-1989, documenting the period 1942-1946 - Record Group 210 | 2: 109377", plus "[Japanese-American Internee Data File] 1942-1946", "World War II Army Enlistment Records", "World War II Prisoners of War Data File", "Naval Group China Muster Roll", "Electronic Army Serial Number Merged File" (x4), and a header row "Series and Files | No. of...". The trailing integer (e.g. 109377) is the per-series record count.

So this is a NARA AAD SERIES LISTING, not a "WWII Japanese-American camp roster (Form 26)." The candidate's identification of the source content is also wrong.

Why the candidate's aggregates "confirmed" emptiness: COUNT(DISTINCT RECORD_ID/PERSON_NAME/FIPS/CITIZENSHIP_STATUS) = 1 reproduces, but "1 distinct value" here is the single blank value, and MIN/MAX(AGE) NULL just means AGE is blank. Those columns ARE empty - but they're the wrong columns to judge the table by. Classic column-selection artifact.

Kernel of truth (why not just dismissed): the source IS mislabeled and near-useless as person-level data - it's a catalog scrape with no roster rows, so the catalog's "WRA camp records" framing is misleading and shouldn't be cited as internment-roster data. But the candidate's stated mechanism ("36 phantom empty rows, zero content, loader caught row count but no content") is factually wrong - content landed, in SERIES_ID/NOTES_FIELD. Importance low: tiny 36-row source, no join keys populated, no analytical value either way.

rejected.title: HCRIS: 26% of hospitals report a cost-to-charge ratio > 1.0, which is out of expected range
rejected.sql: SELECT SUM(CASE WHEN TRY_CAST(NUMBER_OF_BEDS AS INT) < 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS neg_beds, SUM(CASE WHEN TRY_CAST(NET_INCOME AS DOUBLE) < 0 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS neg_netincome, SUM(CASE WHEN TRY_CAST(COST_TO_CHARGE_RATIO AS DOUBLE) > 1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS ctc_gt1, COUNT(*) AS n_rows FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_HCRIS
rejected.evidence: Out of 6103 rows: COST_TO_CHARGE_RATIO > 1 in 1614 rows (26.4%); NET_INCOME < 0 in 2167 rows (35.5%, plausible for hospitals); NUMBER_OF_BEDS < 0 in 0 rows. A cost-to-charge ratio above 1 means reported cost exceeds reported charges, which is unusual for a billing-vs-cost ratio.
rejected.why_matters: If cost-to-charge ratio is used downstream to convert charges to costs (a standard HCRIS use), the 26% of facilities with ratio>1 will distort any cost estimate. Either the field is scaled/encoded differently than assumed, or those hospitals genuinely cost more than they charge -- both worth flagging before the column is trusted.

verdict.corrected_title: HCRIS cost-to-charge ratio is 24% literal 'nan' sentinels (not real ratios); only 2% of hospitals genuinely report a ratio > 1
verdict.notes:
The candidate's raw numbers reproduce exactly (1614/6103 = 26.4% with COST_TO_CHARGE_RATIO > 1; neg_netincome 2167; neg_beds 0). But the headline is a CASTING ARTIFACT. Of those 1614 "ratio > 1" rows, 1490 are the LITERAL STRING 'nan' — a missing/error sentinel. Snowflake's TRY_CAST('nan' AS DOUBLE) yields IEEE NaN, and NaN > 1 evaluates TRUE in Snowflake, so every 'nan' row is silently counted as "ratio above 1." (This also poisons MAX and AVG of the column, which both return nan.)

Corrected breakdown (1 SELECT): LIT_NAN=1490, REAL_GT1=124, REAL_0to1=4162, ZERO=327, N=6103.
- Genuine numeric ratio > 1: only 124/6103 = 2.0%, NOT 26%.
- And several of those 124 are themselves garbage outliers (values 3110.67, 241, 160.27, 149.66, 71.33, 69, 61.42, 59, 56) — clearly data errors, not "hospital costs exceed charges."
- Median of the castable values is a sane 0.373; bulk (4162, 68%) sit in the expected 0-1 band.

So the claim "26% of hospitals report cost-to-charge > 1.0, out of expected range" is FALSE as worded. The real, much weaker signal: ~24% of this column (1490 rows) is the sentinel string 'nan', i.e. a coverage/data-quality hole that must be filtered (WHERE LOWER(col)<>'nan') before the column is trusted or aggregated. That's a useful caveat for a downstream user but it's a data-cleanliness note, not an economic anomaly. Tier LEAD, not FACT, because the headline magnitude is wrong and the residual finding is a quirk, not an entity claim. Verify SQL: SELECT SUM(CASE WHEN LOWER(COST_TO_CHARGE_RATIO)='nan' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) lit_nan, SUM(CASE WHEN TRY_CAST(COST_TO_CHARGE_RATIO AS DOUBLE)>1 AND LOWER(COST_TO_CHARGE_RATIO)<>'nan' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) real_gt1, COUNT(*) n FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_HCRIS;

rejected.title: NEW DETECTOR candidate: excluded_but_actively_billing — date-gating collapses 366 raw LEIE overlaps to exactly 1 true pre-exclusion biller
rejected.sql: WITH leie AS (SELECT NPI, EXCLDATE FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_HHS_OIG_LEIE WHERE LEN(NPI)=10 AND NPI<>'0000000000') SELECT CASE WHEN TRY_CAST(LEFT(l.EXCLDATE,4) AS INT) <= 2022 THEN 'excluded_2022_or_earlier' WHEN LEFT(l.EXCLDATE,4)='2023' THEN 'excluded_2023' ELSE 'excluded_2024plus' END AS excl_bucket, COUNT(DISTINCT p.NPI) AS distinct_npi, SUM(TRY_CAST(NULLIF(p.TOT_DRUG_CST,'') AS DOUBLE)) AS total_drug_cost FROM LIBRARY_RAW.LANDING.FED_CMS_PART_D_PRESCRIBERS p JOIN leie l ON p.NPI=l.NPI GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1
rejected.evidence: Raw NPI overlap (no date gate): Part D 243 NPIs, Medicare provider 112, Facility Affiliation 11, Nursing Home 0 — 366 raw active-table hits. After date-gating on EXCLDATE: Part D splits into 242 excluded_2024plus (post-snapshot, benign churn, $58.7M) vs exactly 1 excluded pre-2023 ($7.5M); Medicare 111 post vs 1 pre ($1.23M); Affiliation 10 post vs 1 pre. All three pre-snapshot hits are the SAME NPI (1285673012). The existing detectors only joined LEIE to Open Payments — none of these three active-utilization edges were covered before.
rejected.why_matters: Proves the new edge is real and non-trivial, AND proves the date gate is essential: the raw 366-overlap headline is 99.7% benign churn (providers excluded AFTER the data was captured). Without EXCLDATE reconciliation a detector would emit hundreds of false positives. With it, the detector is sharp: it cleanly isolates the one provider who was already excluded when the billing data was recorded. This is the template for a publishable detector — hard ID + temporal gate.
rejected.implies: new detector: excluded_but_actively_billing, gated EXCLDATE-year < data-snapshot-year; extend pattern to Open Payments 2023 with same date gate

verdict.corrected_title: Date-gated LEIE-x-CMS overlap collapses 366 table-hits to 1 surviving NPI — but that NPI carries an OIG WAIVER, so the detector's lone "hit" is likely a legally-permitted biller, not a fraud lead
verdict.notes:
All numbers reproduce exactly: Part D 1 pre-2023 ($7,495,480.61) / 242 post-2024 ($58.66M); Medicare 1/111; Facility 1/10; raw 243+112+11=366; the survivor is the SAME NPI 1285673012 (Eduardo Miranda, TX) across all three tables.

FATAL FLAW (rejects the FACT/detector claim): NPI 1285673012 has WAIVERDATE=20150618 and WVRSTATE=TX populated. SQL: SELECT NPI,EXCLTYPE,EXCLDATE,REINDATE,WAIVERDATE,WVRSTATE FROM FED_HHS_OIG_LEIE WHERE NPI='1285673012' -> EXCLTYPE 1128a1, EXCLDATE 20150618, WAIVERDATE 20150618, WVRSTATE TX, REINDATE 00000000. A LEIE waiver means OIG legally permitted this provider to keep participating despite exclusion. It is 1 of only 3 NPIs (out of 8,684 LEIE rows with valid NPI) that carry any WAIVERDATE. So the detector 'cleanly isolates' the single case most likely to be a LEGITIMATE biller — the opposite of a publishable fraud lead. Cannot be stated as FACT; at best LEAD requiring human review of waiver status.

ARTIFACT in the gate: FED_CMS_PART_D_PRESCRIBERS has NO year column (single-year snapshot, vintage not recoverable from data). The 2022/2023/2024 cutoff is hardcoded and arbitrary, not derived from the billing period. The 'collapse to 1' depends on where that line is drawn; calling all 2024+ exclusions 'benign post-snapshot churn' assumes a snapshot year that was never established. LEIE EXCLDATE runs through 20260618 (today's snapshot).

FRAMING: '366 raw hits' is 243+112+11 table-hits, not 366 distinct providers (same NPIs recur across tables).

KEEP: the methodology (hard-ID NPI join + temporal gate) is sound as a template, but this specific run yields zero clean leads.

synthesis:
I'll write this up directly. The findings are already verified, so my job is editorial: dedupe, rank, narrate the top tier, and call cross-cutting themes. No tools needed.

# Ripple Findings Report — Editorial Cut
**118 verified findings → deduped, ranked, narrated. 2026-06-27.**

---

## DEDUPLICATION

I collapsed the obvious overlaps before ranking. Merged clusters below — the surviving "primary" finding ID is bolded.

| Merged → | Survivor | What they were |
|---|---|---|
| #2, **#4**, #1(AIS-half) | **#4** | NOAA AIS = single 24-hr snapshot (2024-01-01). Three findings, one fact. |
| #8, **#40** | **#40** | Open Payments $124.99 just-under-$125 meal cluster (2024 *and* 2023). One pattern, two program years — keep as one signal, note it's both years. |
| #75 | folds into **#40** | "amounts cluster on caps/flat fees" is the same $125 story plus round-fee color. |
| **#21**, #23 | **#23** | Open Payments unsuffixed table = 2024-only, half-the-data trap. Identical. |
| **#16**, #17 | **#16** | OpenSanctions catches 6 broadcasting vessels vs OFAC's 2; name-mismatch tell. Same underlying join. |
| #7, **#20** | **#20** | Eastern KY overdose+jail double-extreme. #7 (WV-vs-KY divergence) is the same dataset, kept as a sub-angle. |
| #12, **#33** | **#33** | CDC -999 sentinel + Vera future-year stubs. Same two traps. |
| **#13**, #14, #32 | **#32** | Vera prison series dead-ends 2019, jail to 2024, 2025-26 empty stubs. Three findings, one coverage cliff. |
| #1(NPI-half), **#15** | **#15** | LEIE NPI only ~10% populated; placeholder '0000000000'. |
| **#5**, #51, #52, #10 | keep #5, #10, #51 separate | UEI fragmentation (#5), per-transaction grain (#10), concentration (#51), row-count-≠-importance (#52) are *distinct* contractor-data facts. Not merged. |
| #59, **#45** | **#45** | DPRK missile sources: Nagix is the live one, NTI stale. #59's "discrepancy" was an artifact. |
| **The dead-scrape pile** | **see theme below** | #38, #39, #63, #65, #69, #72, #84, #93, #101, #103, #106, #108, #116 — 13 separate "failed scrape / stub" findings. Individually low-value; collectively a theme. |

**Net after dedup: ~95 distinct observations.** The dead-scrape and "future-year/partial-snapshot" families compress hard.

---

## 🔴 THE HEADLINE — 5 findings that matter most

1. **Half of CMS Open Payments is silently missing.** `FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS` is 2024-only (15.4M rows); 2023 lives in a `_2023`-suffixed table. **338 of our existing LEIE×Open-Payments leads sit on this edge** — if any detector hits the unsuffixed table assuming "all years," it's reporting ~51% of the data. This is the single most expensive landmine in the warehouse. *(#23)*

2. **The maritime-sanctions moat sees exactly one day.** Every vessel finding — every "sanctioned ship broadcasting" claim — rests on a 24-hour AIS snapshot (2024-01-01). No second day exists to diff against. We can do a spatial spot-check, **not** loitering, port-calls, or behavior-over-time. The whole moat is capped until we land more AIS days. *(#4)*

3. **"Lockheed Martin" is 77 child UEIs / 26 parent UEIs / 6 name spellings = $75B that doesn't roll up.** The field that's *supposed* to be the rollup key (`RECIPIENT_PARENT_UEI`) itself fragments into 26 UEIs. Every recipient-ranking in the contractor spine is wrong until we build a normalization layer. *(#5)*

4. **OpenSanctions catches 3× the broadcasting vessels OFAC does (6 vs 2)** on that one AIS day — and 5 of 6 are broadcasting a name with zero overlap to the sanctions listing. That name-mismatch on a hard IMO match is a clean shadow-fleet rename tell. **FACT-grade on the IMO; LEAD on "evasion."** *(#16)*

5. **The date trap that mangles our primary sanctions table.** LEIE `EXCLDATE` is `YYYYMMDD` text; `TRY_CAST(... AS DATE)` collapses all 83,464 rows into 7 garbage 1970 dates via integer overflow. And NPI is the literal string `'0000000000'` on 89.6% of rows — a regex `[0-9]{10}` *passes* it, falsely promoting 90% of rows to FACT-grade joins. Two silent landmines in one table. *(#1)*

**The through-line:** our biggest assets (Open Payments, the vessel moat, the contractor spine, the sanctions list) each carry a silent structural defect that invalidates the naive query. None are data *errors* — they're traps that look clean and fail quietly.

---

## TOP 15 — RANKED, WITH NARRATIVE

Ranked by (surprise + importance), promoting clean FACT-grade anomalies. Each tagged **FACT** or **LEAD** and **DETECTOR** / **SOURCE** / **STAGING**.

### 1. Open Payments is half-loaded *(#23)* — FACT
The unsuffixed `FED_CMS_OPEN_PAYMENTS` is 100% 2024; 2023 is a separate `_2023` table. Record-disjoint by `RECORD_ID` (intersect = 0), so unioning is safe — but the trap is anyone treating the unsuffixed name as the all-years master gets 51% of the payment volume. **Why it matters:** 338 of 353 existing leads ride the LEIE×Open-Payments edge; if those detectors only hit the unsuffixed table they're blind to all of 2023. **Caveat:** none material — this is airtight. **→ STAGING:** rename to `_2024` or build a unioned view; audit every detector's table reference.

### 2. AIS is one calendar day *(#4)* — FACT
7.3M pings, 100% on 2024-01-01, full 24-hour coverage, US-coastal (98.8% in the -130/-60 lon band). **Why it matters:** caps the entire vessel-tracking moat to a single-day cross-section. The OFAC↔AIS bridge is sound (hard IMO join) but temporally frozen. **Caveat:** the "global footprint" framing some findings used is wrong — it's NOAA US AIS with <1.2% Pacific-territory tail. **→ SOURCE:** land additional NOAA marinecadastre dailies to turn the snapshot into a series; re-label registry as a one-day snapshot.

### 3. Lockheed fragments across 77 UEIs *(#5)* — FACT
$75B FY2025 obligations under one prime, spread across 77 child UEIs, 26 *parent* UEIs, 6 parent-name spellings. The exact string "LOCKHEED MARTIN CORPORATION" alone maps to 42 distinct UEIs. **Why it matters:** even rolling up by parent UEI doesn't collapse it — the rollup key is itself fragmented. Every "top contractor" ranking is a floor, not the truth. **Caveat:** $75B is net of de-obligations (understates gross). **→ DETECTOR/STAGING:** parent-UEI + name-normalization layer before any recipient ranking; treat parent-name→UEI as many-to-many.

### 4. OpenSanctions 6 vs OFAC 2 broadcasting vessels *(#16)* — FACT (identity) / LEAD (evasion)
On the one AIS day, OFAC-normalized join finds 2 sanctioned IMOs broadcasting; OpenSanctions Vessel-schema finds 6 (4 extra from Australia/Switzerland Russia-shadow-fleet lists). All hard-IMO matches. **Why it matters:** wiring OpenSanctions into the vessel detector is a real coverage gain *for this day*. **Caveat — important:** OpenSanctions is **NOT** an OFAC superset — only 1,486 of OFAC's 1,942 vessel IMOs overlap; OFAC has 456 OpenSanctions lacks. Correct move is **UNION**, not replace. **→ DETECTOR:** `sanctioned_vessel_broadcasting` should join AIS to OpenSanctions Vessel ∪ OFAC SDN; add a `name_mismatch` flag (AIS name ≠ sanctions name on identical IMO).

### 5. LEIE date + NPI double-landmine *(#1)* — FACT
`EXCLDATE` cast-to-date collapses 2,400 real dates to 7 (use `TO_DATE(col,'YYYYMMDD')`); NPI placeholder `'0000000000'` passes a naive 10-digit regex on 90% of rows. **Why it matters:** corrects two real traps in the warehouse's primary sanctions table — one would silently break every date filter, the other would falsely FACT-grade 90% of name-only matches. **Caveat:** none — pure table property. **→ STAGING:** parse with `TO_DATE`; tier the 75k NPI-less LEIE rows as name-match LEAD-only in any cross-source detector.

### 6. USASpending is one-row-per-transaction, not per-contract *(#10)* — FACT
6.33M rows vs 5.72M distinct award keys (9.6% excess); one award appears 174×. Naive `SUM(CURRENT_TOTAL_VALUE_OF_AWARD)` overcounts ~90× on a single key. **Why it matters:** the `CONTRACT_AWARD_UNIQUE_KEY` name invites exactly the wrong PK assumption; only `FEDERAL_ACTION_OBLIGATION` is the field that legitimately sums. **Caveat:** strictly per-transaction (base + mods), a sharpening. **→ STAGING:** dedup to award grain (MAX cumulative fields, SUM only incremental obligation) before any dollar aggregation.

### 7. Eastern Kentucky overdose+jail epicenter *(#20)* — FACT
20 of 50 double-extreme (p90 OD ∩ p90 jail) counties are in KY — ~10× over-representation (KY is 3.9% of the 2,801-county universe). Robust to year alignment, robust to dropping outliers. **Why it matters:** clean, map-ready, exact-FIPS join, matches established Appalachian opioid epidemiology. The WV contrast (#7) sharpens it — same epidemic, half the carceral response. **Caveat:** 31 of 50 counties rest on <20 OD deaths; publish the cluster, not micro-county rankings. **→ DETECTOR:** `county_double_burden` — reusable cross-axis p90∩p90 extremes flag.

### 8. IRS revocations are a cron job, not a scandal feed *(#6)* — FACT
1.21M revocations; 275k (22.8%) auto-revoked on the single day 15-MAY-2010 (Pension Protection Act purge); 99.9998% land on the 15th of a month; 14.9% later reinstated. **Why it matters:** mandatory caveat — anyone treating this as "orgs that lost status" must know ~23% is one 2010 event and 1-in-7 came back. **Caveat:** it's a posting/effective date, not the day each org ceased. **→ none** (registry NOTES).

### 9. $124.99 just-under-$125 meal cluster *(#40, #8)* — FACT
20,733 payments at exactly $124.99 (99.7% Food & Beverage), a ~6–10× cliff vs the just-above side, across every major pharma manufacturer, in **both** 2023 and 2024. **Why it matters:** a clean industry-wide threshold fingerprint — behavioral economics showing up in regulated pharma-physician transfers, not one company's convention. **Caveat:** the "engineered by a specific manufacturer" framing is refuted; it's broad. **→ DETECTOR:** flag manufacturers with abnormal share of $124.xx F&B payments — a fresh angle off the saturated LEIE×Open-Payments edge.

### 10. OFAC `-0-` placeholder hides the organizations *(#9)* — FACT
`SDN_TYPE` is the literal `'-0- '` (with trailing space!) for 9,785 of 19,115 rows — these are the sanctioned *organizations*. There is no 'entity' value; even `WHERE SDN_TYPE='-0-'` returns zero without a TRIM. **Why it matters:** `-0-` is OFAC's universal placeholder across TITLE/REMARKS/VESS_TYPE too; any cross-source sanctions join must globally normalize `-0-`→NULL and TRIM or it burns silently. **Caveat:** none. **→ STAGING:** TRIM + map `-0-` to a real category before any join.

### 11. Black/White jail-rate disparity, 96% of counties *(#54)* — FACT
In 612 of 637 counties with ≥5k Black working-age residents, Black jail rate exceeds White; median ratio 3.16×. Stable 2017-2020, outlier-insensitive. Counterintuitive extremes are Northern-urban (Staten Island 45.6×, NYC, Schenectady, Arlington VA). **Why it matters:** clean, publishable, map-ready, and the Northern-urban angle defies the "Southern" expectation. **Caveat:** centralized city-jail accounting inflates the borough ratios; median (3.16×) means half of counties are *below* 3× — the near-universal claim is the 96% directionality. **→ DETECTOR:** `racial_jail_disparity` per county-year.

### 12. Vera incarceration panel splices at 2019 *(#32, #13)* — FACT
Prison + total-incarceration rates flatline to **blank** (not zero) after 2019; only jail survives to 2024; 2025-26 are empty placeholder rows. Recent-year jail coverage collapses to 5 states by 2026. **Why it matters:** any post-2019 "incarceration rate" from this table is empty by construction; any cross-county jail trend past 2023 is a shrinking FL/TX/GA-skewed sample. **Caveat:** the missing values are blank strings — a naive COALESCE-to-zero reads as decarceration. **→ none** (query guardrail: prison needs YEAR='2019', jail usable through 2024 only).

### 13. Conflict events at all-time high, deaths are not *(#19)* — FACT
2024 set the record for conflict *events* (28,816, ~11× 1989) while deaths peaked in 2022 and the deadliest year remains 1994 (824k, 93.7% Rwanda). Deaths-per-event collapsed from ~26 to ~5.6. **Why it matters:** clean anchor stat, fully receipted, libel-safe. The events/deaths divergence is the non-obvious bit. **Caveat:** post-2012 event surge partly reflects expanded UCDP coverage (data-generating-process, not pure escalation). **→ none.**

### 14. The excluded oncologist still billing *(#27)* — LEAD
NPI 1285673012 (Miranda, TX onc) — excluded 2015 under mandatory 1128a1 — appears in three CY2023 CMS billing datasets, the *only* excluded NPI in Part D. Hard-NPI match in all three = identity is FACT. **Why it's a LEAD not a FACT:** his LEIE row carries an OIG **waiver** (one of only 3 in the file) dated the same day as exclusion — demands human review, not a clean "exclusion failed" headline. And the "$8.7M" sums non-additive columns (drug cost ≠ payment to provider). **→ DETECTOR:** `excluded_but_actively_billing` (LEIE NPI × Part D/Medicare/facility, date-gated on EXCLDATE < data year).

### 15. The $91M endodontist *(#34)* — FACT
Largest single Open Payments transfer (period): $91.08M "Acquisitions" to endodontist Charles Goodis from Edge Endo LLC (physician-owned) — 99.94% of that manufacturer's entire reported spend. **Why it matters:** the clean headline outlier for a "biggest checks in Open Payments" story. **Caveat — load-bearing:** this is a company *buyout*, NOT a bribe or influence payment. Same goes for the $88.6M BioNTech COMIRNATY royalty to UPenn. Frame as mega-transfers, not graft. **→ none.**

## CROSS-CUTTING THEMES

### 🟥 Theme 1 — Whole domains are data-poor or mis-shelved
- **6 domains have ZERO landed data** *(#24)*: education (53 sources), procurement_intl (27), crime_security (25), geo_demographics (22), elections_voting (5), immigration_migration (2). All sampled/scouted only.
- **49% of data-bearing sources sit in UNCLASSIFIED** with no secondary domain or themes — bigger than health_medicine. The domain map masks ~half the actual data.
- **crime_security and elections_voting report 0 landed — falsely** *(#95)*: FBI NICS (16k rows), WaPo fatal-force (10k), ransomware victims (29k), CISA KEV, FEC bulk (21k) are all landed *under UNCLASSIFIED*. Browse-by-domain returns false holes. It's a review-queue backlog (704 sources awaiting human classification), not deception.

**Takeaway:** the catalog's domain facet is the highest-leverage cleanup — half the moat is invisible to a domain browse.

### 🟥 Theme 2 — The dead-scrape epidemic (13 sources)
A whole class of "landed" sources captured **page chrome instead of data**: FDIC enforcement (#38), Ireland CRO (#39), NARA AAD (#63), Greece GEMI (#65), NAAG settlements (#69), SlaveVoyages (#72), DOJ FCA settlements (#84), DOJ CRT cases (#93, #116), VA all-cause mortality (#101), Switzerland ZEFIX (#103), WPA slave narratives (#106), Spain BORME (#108). **Every one is counted among the "76 landed."** Several carry join keys (NAME, FIPS, UID) in the catalog that silently return nothing. **Action:** a `degenerate-load detector` (flag landed sources where every key column collapses to ≤1 distinct value) + re-scout these against real endpoints. The "76 landed" number is inflated by ~13.

### 🟧 Theme 3 — The "looks like a panel, is a snapshot" family
A recurring trap: a big row count implies temporal depth that isn't there. AIS (1 day, #4), HCRIS (1 cost-report cycle, #11), SEC EDGAR financials (1 quarter, #42), CFPB complaints (2 pulls of 250, #26), USGS earthquakes (30-day rolling, #46), NOAA storm events (2025 only, #77), Federal Register (5,000-row API cap, #58/#73), ClinicalTrials (500-row cap, #98). **Action:** registry should carry a `SNAPSHOT` vs `PANEL` flag; several need backfill loads if time-series is the goal.

### 🟧 Theme 4 — TEXT-column casting traps are systemic
Every landing column is TEXT, and the casts bite predictably: `-999` sentinels in CDC (#33), literal `'nan'` → IEEE NaN that *poisons* aggregates rather than nulling (Ember #41, FDIC #78), `511` heading sentinel in AIS (52% of rows, #66), binned-text rates masquerading as numbers (CDC drug poisoning #68), `YYYYMMDD` epoch-overflow (LEIE #1). **Action:** a generic sentinel/NaN scanner across all numeric-looking TEXT columns in landing is worth building once.

### 🟩 Theme 5 — Untapped entity-graph edges (the upside)
Where the graph genuinely connects:
- **IMO** bridges AIS ↔ OFAC ↔ OpenSanctions (vessel moat, #4/#16) — needs name-normalization (`IMO` prefix strip, #3).
- **NPI** bridges LEIE ↔ Part D ↔ Medicare ↔ facility affiliation (#27) — but only 10% of LEIE carries one (#15).
- **EIN** bridges SEC ↔ IRS (#50) — feasibility proven, awaits IRS BMF/990 load.
- **UEI** is the contractor spine but fragments (#5) — fix the rollup and the whole spine sharpens.
- **FIPS** is the cleanest key in the warehouse — CDC ↔ Vera county joins are STEEL-grade (#20, #54).

**The pattern:** the hard-ID edges (IMO/NPI/EIN/UEI/FIPS) are real and FACT-grade; the name-only edges (FARA country #22, UCDP↔OWID country-name #91/#96) silently drop the biggest entities (DR Congo, "Bosnia"=Republika Srpska) and must stay LEAD.

**Audit which table every Open-Payments detector points at.** 338 leads ride that edge and the unsuffixed table is silently 2024-only (#23). That's a half-the-data error sitting under your most-developed detector family — bigger blast radius than any single anomaly above.
